Field Manual
Electrical Assessments, SPOF Analysis & Recommendations
for MV/LV Process Plants

Rev 3 — Saudi (SEC) Edition
Medium-Voltage / Low-Voltage Industrial Plant — Indicative ~2 MW Demand

Standards: SASO-adopted IEC / SEC / SBC 401 Voltage: 13.8 kV MV / 400–230 V LV (TN-S) Frequency: 60 Hz Ambient: 50 °C design Demand: ~2 MW / ~2.35 MVA
Indicative / Training Material. All values, equipment ratings, and configurations in this manual are indicative only and are provided for training and familiarisation purposes. They do not constitute a design, specification, or safety document. All numeric values must be confirmed by detailed engineering studies before use in any real installation. Follow all applicable national and IEC standards, site safety rules, and employer procedures at all times.

About This Manual

This field manual is a self-paced reference for a junior electrical engineer assigned to assess the main electrical equipment of a medium-voltage / low-voltage (MV/LV) industrial process plant with an indicative demand of approximately 2 MW. It covers equipment identification, core electrical calculations, single-point-of-failure (SPOF) analysis, and practical recommendations.

How to Use This Manual

Work through the parts in order. Each part builds on the previous: start by identifying the equipment and understanding its function (Part 1), apply the key calculations (Part 2), then conduct the SPOF analysis (Part 3) and formulate recommendations (Part 4).

Use the sidebar to navigate between sections. Checklists in each part track your progress — tick items as you complete them; your progress is saved automatically in the browser. Print the manual (use the Print button) for field use; the sidebar is hidden and each section starts on a new page.

This manual is a single offline HTML file. It requires no internet connection and no installed software beyond a modern web browser. Checklist state is stored in the browser's local storage — use the same browser session to preserve progress.

Scope and Plant Configuration

The indicative plant configuration used throughout this manual is:

Supply13.8 kV SEC incomer, 60 Hz, metered at MV level
MV switchgear13.8 kV switchgear (17.5 kV class) with numerical protection relays
Transformers2 × 1600 kVA, Dyn11, 13.8 kV / 0.4 kV, Z = 6 % (TX-1 and TX-2)
LV main switchboardSplit-bus, 400/230 V TN-S, with normally-open bus-tie (LV-MSB)
Motor controlMultiple MCCs fed from LV-MSB sections; 4-pole motors at 1800 rpm
Standby generationDiesel generator 1250 kVA ESP (DG) with automatic transfer switch (ATS)
EarthingMV resistance-earthed via NER (400 A, 19.9 Ω); LV TN-S
Environment50 °C design ambient, dust-laden desert site (Saudi Arabia)
Total demand~2 MW (indicative); apparent power ~2.35 MVA at pf 0.85

Refer to the master single-line diagram (SLD) throughout for equipment locations and tag references.

What's New in Rev 3

  • Saudi (SEC) localization — electrical basis changed to 13.8 kV MV, 400/230 V LV (TN-S), 60 Hz, 50 °C design ambient; standards cited as SASO-adopted IEC plus SEC Distribution Code, Saudi Grid Code and SBC 401.
  • Part 2 rewritten as deep-dive tutorials — every calculation topic now follows an eight-step teaching pattern (why it exists → failure example → feel for the numbers → worked calculation → settings → technology → control → sizing), with new topics: per-unit, breakers, arc flash, lightning/SPD, UPS and ATS.
  • NEW Part 6 — Equipment Catalogues — commercial vendor families (switchgear, transformers, breakers, MCC/VFD, UPS/DC, gensets/ATS, relays and auxiliaries) with "how to read the datasheet" guides and selections for this plant. All catalogue data is indicative; no part numbers.
  • New orientation sections — the Saudi electrical context (SEC, SASO, SABER, SBC 401) and designing for 50 °C and dust.
  • Concepts companion — a separate self-contained file (concepts-rev3.html) explaining the core concepts behind each calculation.

Revision History

RevDateDescription
12026-05Initial field manual (assessment, calculations, SPOF).
22026-05Expanded edition: orientation, philosophies, field tools, case study.
32026-06Saudi (SEC) edition: 13.8 kV / 400 V / 60 Hz / 50 °C basis, 4× expansion — Part 2 deep-dive rewrite, NEW Part 6 equipment catalogues, Saudi orientation, concepts companion file.
Drawing basis. The SVG single-line diagrams in this manual were drawn for the original 13.8 kV / 400 V / 60 Hz reference design and have not been re-rendered. A note under each diagram explains the conversion: read 13.8 kV as 13.8 kV, 400 V as 400 V and 60 Hz as 60 Hz. Topology, tags and protection philosophy are unchanged.

The Assignment

Where you are in the workflow: before anything else, understand what the campaign is and what you are being asked to deliver.

The SPOF Assessment Campaign

You are one of two Electrical Engineers on a four-month campaign to deliver an Electrical Single Point of Failure (SPOF) Assessment across 21 groundwater desalination plants in the Central & Eastern sector — 19 in Riyadh Province and 2 in the Eastern Province (no plants in Madinah or Qassim), 16 sites, ~1.66 M m³/day total. The core team is:

Role Count Primary responsibility
Project Manager (Electrical) 1 Programme, quality, HSE, client interface; reviews all deliverables before submission.
Electrical Engineer 2 Per-plant SPOF assessment, plant calculation packages, concept modifications, assessment forms. That is you.
Electrical Technician Optional Non-intrusive field surveys and asset inventory support only — not part of the core team.

What you are asked to do per plant

For each of the 21 plants you are expected to work autonomously through the following:

  • Desk study: review the existing single-line diagrams, equipment schedules and prior studies.
  • Plant calculation package: produce load-flow (verifying bus voltages and cable loadings under normal and N-1 conditions), short-circuit (prospective fault current at each bus), protection coordination (relay grading), and earthing & lightning assessments with power-system software. Arc-flash per IEEE 1584 (incident energy at each working position) and RAM/RBD — Reliability, Availability, Maintainability / Reliability Block Diagram availability modelling (Part 3) — are advantageous.
  • SPOF assessment: inventory and classify electrical single points of failure; assess criticality using FMECA — Failure Mode, Effects and Criticality Analysis per IEC 60812 (Part 3) — which ranks each failure mode by Risk Priority Number (RPN = Severity × Occurrence × Detection, max 125 on the 1–5 scales used here).
  • SLD verification: red-line the single-line diagrams against the as-found plant during site visits.
  • Concept modifications: for each recommended change, prepare a marked-up SLD, equipment outline specification, scheme description, sequence of operation and high-level bill of materials. Concept level only — not detailed design, procurement or construction.
  • Assessment forms: complete the four per-plant deliverable forms (see Section — The Four Deliverable Forms).
Note — indicative figures: All ratings, capacities and figures in this manual are indicative and tentative. They illustrate method and typical practice. Confirm every value against the plant's actual load list, short-circuit study, utility connection agreement and governing specifications before acting on them.

Site logistics — Central & Eastern sector

The 21 plants sit at 16 sites — 19 in Riyadh Province (clustered around Riyadh, with outliers from Al-Aflaj in the south to Al-Artawiyah in the north) and 2 in the Eastern Province (Al-Hani and Hafar Al-Batin), ~1.66 M m³/day total. The map combines a national overview with a Riyadh-province zoom. City-level positions are indicative; marker size ~ capacity.

Key takeaway: You are an autonomous assessment engineer. Your job is to identify electrical single points of failure, quantify their risk, and recommend practicable mitigations — plant by plant, across 21 sites in four months.

The Four Deliverable Forms

Where you are in the workflow: understand the exact deliverables you must produce for every plant before you step on site.

For each of the 21 plants, the primary deliverables are four completed assessment forms plus the plant calculation package and any concept modification packages. The forms are described below. Blank templates are in Appendix F — Blank Forms.

Form What it contains When completed Who owns it
1. Document Inventory List of all electrical documents obtained or requested: single-line diagrams (revision, date, verified), equipment schedules, protection setting records, prior studies, maintenance records, drawings. Records what was available, what was missing, and what was verified on site. Started in desk study; updated on site visit; finalised after site. Lead Engineer (you) — PM reviews.
2. FMECA Form Failure Mode, Effects and Criticality Analysis (IEC 60812). One row per electrical item of significance: failure mode, local effect, system effect, existing controls, severity (S), occurrence (O), detectability (D), RPN or criticality number, and recommended action. Identifies and ranks the SPOFs. Populated during analysis phase after desk study and site visit; updated iteratively. Lead Engineer — PM quality-gates before submission.
3. Recommendations Register Ranked list of recommended modifications or improvements. One row per recommendation: reference number, description, priority (high/medium/low), estimated scope, applicable standard, link to FMECA row, and status (open / in progress / closed). The basis for the concept modification packages. Drafted after FMECA is complete; updated as concept modifications are developed. Lead Engineer — PM approves priority ranking before issue.
4. Site-Visit Report Factual record of the site visit: date, attendees, areas accessed, equipment observed, as-found conditions (nameplate data, breaker/switch status, visible condition concerns, any thermographic hot-spots if permitted), discrepancies against the SLD, and photographs index. Non-intrusive only — see the warning below. Completed on the day of the site visit; reviewed by PM before issue. Lead Engineer (with optional technician support for data capture).
Tip — form discipline: Open all four forms as soon as a plant is assigned. Fill the Document Inventory and Site-Visit Report header during desk study so the template is ready on site. Do not wait until after the visit to create the form.
Tip — traceability: Every row in the FMECA form should carry a unique reference that you can cite in the Recommendations Register. The PM uses this linkage when quality-checking your work.
Form & row-ID legend. SF-01 = Document Inventory; SF-02 = FMECA Worksheet; SF-03 = Recommendations Register; SF-04 = Site-Visit Report. Within each form, rows carry the following prefixes: DOC-xx (SF-01 document rows) | FM-xx (SF-02 FMECA rows) | REC-xx (SF-03 recommendation rows) | OBS-xx (SF-04 observation rows) | PH-xx (SF-04 photolog rows) | ACT-xx (SF-04 action rows). These prefixes are consistent across the blank forms in Appendix E and the worked Jubail example in Part 5.
Key takeaway: Four forms, completed in sequence — Document Inventory, FMECA, Recommendations Register, Site-Visit Report — are the non-negotiable deliverables for every plant. Start them early; update them iteratively.

Assessment Workflow at a Glance

Where you are in the workflow: map the full sequence so you can plan your time plant by plant.

Stage What you do Form(s) updated
1 — Pre-site desk study Request and review all available documents: SLDs (confirm revision / date), equipment schedules, prior calculations, protection setting records. Identify missing documents to chase on site. Prepare calculation model shell. Mark up a working copy of the SLD with questions. Brief the optional technician if deployed. Document Inventory (columns: document received, revision, status)
2 — Site visit (non-intrusive) Verify SLD against as-found installation; red-line discrepancies. Capture nameplate data (ratings, make/model, serial numbers) for all main equipment. Record breaker/switch configuration (normally open / closed). Note visible condition, housekeeping and reliability concerns. Photograph (where permitted) and index photos. Collect any documents not previously received. Strictly observe host permit-to-work and QHSE rules. Site-Visit Report (as-found data);
Document Inventory (documents obtained on site)
3 — Analysis (office) Update the calculation model with as-found data. Run load-flow, short-circuit, protection coordination and earthing/lightning studies. Identify and classify SPOFs from the verified SLD. Populate FMECA rows: failure mode, effects, controls, criticality (S × O × D). Rank items by criticality / risk. FMECA form (all columns)
4 — Recommendations For each high-criticality FMECA item, develop a concept modification: marked-up SLD, equipment outline specification, scheme description / sequence of operation, and high-level bill of materials. Re-run affected calculations on the modified arrangement. Populate the Recommendations Register with priority, scope and FMECA cross-reference. Recommendations Register (all columns)
5 — Report & issue PM reviews and quality-gates all four forms and the calculation package. Finalise the Site-Visit Report. Assemble the per-plant deliverable pack (forms + calculation package + concept modification packages + red-lined SLDs). Submit to PM for consolidation into the campaign report. All four forms finalised; calculation package issued.
Tip — iterate freely: The workflow above is a logical sequence, not a strict one-pass process. You will often loop back: a site finding may invalidate a desk-study assumption and require you to update the FMECA and re-run a calculation. Return to earlier stages whenever new information warrants it. The PM expects updated forms up until the submission milestone.
Key takeaway: Five stages — desk study, site visit, analysis, recommendations, report. The forms are living documents throughout; complete and finalise them in order.

How to Use This Manual

Where you are in the workflow: orient yourself to the manual before reading the technical content.

Manual structure — Parts 0 to 5 and Appendices

This manual is a single self-contained HTML file designed for field use (offline, printable). It is organised into six parts and a set of appendices:

Part Title What it gives you
Part 0 Orientation (this part) Campaign context, deliverable forms, assessment workflow, conventions.
Part 1 Foundations How to read a MV/LV SLD; every equipment tag and its function; power-flow walk-through; equipment schedule and applicable IEC standards.
Part 2 Calculations All core electrical calculations — load, transformer sizing, full-load current, short-circuit, voltage drop, PFC, cable selection, protection coordination, arc-flash, earthing — with worked examples and a formula card.
Part 3 SPOF Analysis SPOF identification method, a symbol library of failure points, FMECA and RBD techniques, reliability metrics, and a printable site checklist.
Part 4 Control Philosophy & Modifications DCS/SCADA/PMS integration, best-practice modification templates, grid-instability mitigations, modification concept packages.
Part 5 Field Tasks Step-by-step site procedures: desk study, site-visit checklist, drawing verification, analysis workflow, report assembly, and the first-assignment walk-through.
Appendices A – G Glossary, standards list, formula card, full tag table, blank forms, hardening guidance, MTBF reference data.

How to use the manual on a real assignment

  1. First time through: read Parts 0 and 1 in full to internalise the workflow and the tag scheme. Skim Part 2 to confirm you are comfortable with the calculation methods.
  2. Starting a new plant: open Part 5 — Desk Study Procedure and follow the checklist. Open the four blank forms from Appendix F and fill the header information.
  3. On site: use the Visual Inspection Checklist and the Drawing Verification Checklist in Part 5. The SPOF symbol library in Part 3 helps you spot failure points in real switchrooms.
  4. During analysis: use Part 2 for calculations and Part 3 for the FMECA method. Cross-reference to the Formula Card and MTBF Reference Data as needed.
  5. New to this type of assignment? Start with the first-assignment walk-through in Part 5 — it takes you step-by-step through one complete plant cycle.
Tip — sidebar navigation: Use the sidebar (left panel) to jump between sections. Checkboxes in checklist sections persist across sessions in the same browser — use them to track progress per plant.
On-screen vs field use. Checkbox tick-state is stored in the browser's localStorage — it persists only in the same browser on the same device, and only while on-screen at a desk. For field use, print the relevant checklist section (use the Print button or Ctrl+P) and tick on paper. Transfer your paper marks back to the on-screen form in the office.
Key takeaway: Parts 0 and 1 are the foundation — read them first. Parts 2–4 are your reference library. Part 5 is your task guide on every assignment. Use the first-assignment walk-through in Part 5 if you are new to this type of work.

Conventions Used in This Manual

Where you are in the workflow: agree the ground rules before diving into the technical content.

IEC basis, voltages and frequency

This manual follows IEC conventions throughout:

Parameter Value used in this manual Note
Standards basis SASO-adopted IEC (primary), applied together with the SEC Distribution Code, the Saudi Grid Code and SBC 401 (Saudi Building Code — Electrical); IEEE referenced where relevant (e.g. IEEE 1584, IEEE 493) ANSI device numbers (52, 87T, etc.) are used in SLD breaker notation as industry practice. See The Saudi Electrical Context for the regulatory map.
MV supply voltage 13.8 kV (SEC standard distribution voltage, indicative) 13.8 kV is the normal SEC MV distribution level for industrial customers of this size; confirm the actual connection voltage per plant from the SEC connection agreement.
LV distribution voltage 400 / 230 V, 3-phase SEC / SASO low-voltage utilisation voltage. Confirm per plant.
Frequency 60 Hz The Saudi national grid operates at 60 Hz — not the 60 Hz used in most IEC-basis countries. Confirm per plant; 4-pole induction motors therefore run at a synchronous speed of 1800 rpm.
MV earthing Resistance-earthed (NER) Typical for industrial MV; confirm per plant.
LV earthing TN-S (solid) Confirm per plant.

Equipment tag scheme

Every piece of equipment is identified by a tag. Use only the tags in the table below; do not invent new tags without updating the project reference document. The full tag table with functions is in Part 1 — Equipment Function Table and Appendix D — Tag Table.

Tag Equipment Voltage level
MV-METUtility revenue metering cubicle13.8 kV (MV)
MV-SWGRMV metal-clad switchgear13.8 kV (MV)
MV-NERNeutral Earthing Resistor13.8 kV (MV)
TX-1 / TX-2MV/LV power transformers (2 × 1600 kVA, indicative)13.8 kV / 400 V boundary
LV-MSBMain LV switchboard / PCC (split bus A/B)400 V (LV)
BTLV bus-tie circuit breaker (normally open)400 V (LV)
MCC-1 … MCC-nMotor Control Centres400 V (LV)
VFDVariable Frequency Drive(s)400 V (LV)
PFCPower factor correction (detuned capacitor bank)400 V (LV)
DGDiesel generator set400 V (LV)
ATSAutomatic Transfer Switch400 V (LV)
UPSUninterruptible Power Supply400 V AC / battery
DCDBDC battery and charger system (110 V DC)DC (MV switchgear supply)
DB / EDBDistribution board / Essential distribution board400 V (LV)

The "indicative figures" rule

Every numerical value in this manual — voltages, currents, kVA ratings, cable sizes, fault levels, reliability figures — is indicative and tentative. The values are sized to a representative ~2 MW industrial plant to illustrate calculation method and typical equipment selection. They are not a substitute for:

  • the plant's actual load list and demand study,
  • a site-specific short-circuit and load-flow study,
  • the utility connection agreement and network data,
  • the governing project specification and local regulations.

Always confirm figures against actual plant data before acting on them.

Warning — NON-INTRUSIVE surveys only. All site surveys under this campaign are non-intrusive. You must not open energised panels, operate switches or breakers, perform live electrical tests, or take measurements that require direct contact with live conductors. Visual inspection, nameplate reading, photograph capture and accessible meter readings are permitted. Strictly observe the host permit-to-work procedure, lock-out/tag-out rules and QHSE requirements (ISO 9001 / 14001 / 45001) at every plant. The NDA and applicable national cybersecurity data-handling requirements apply to all data collected on site.
Key takeaway: SASO-adopted IEC basis, 13.8 kV / 400–230 V / 60 Hz, the tag scheme above, all figures indicative — and every site survey is non-intrusive. These four rules underpin everything in this manual. Part 1 now walks you through the standard MV/LV plant architecture you will encounter on most of these 21 sites.

The Saudi Electrical Context

Where you are in the workflow: before the first desk study, understand who the utility is, what the connection looks like, and which rulebooks govern every plant in this campaign.

Every plant you will assess is connected to the Saudi grid and operates under the Saudi regulatory framework. Even though the engineering inside the fence is recognisably "IEC-style" MV/LV practice, the surrounding context — the utility, the connection voltage, the codes, the certification regime — is specific to the Kingdom. This section gives you the working map. As everywhere in this manual, regulatory details evolve; treat the descriptions below as indicative and confirm the current editions of all codes and the actual connection agreement per plant.

SEC — the utility you connect to

The Saudi Electricity Company (SEC) is the dominant transmission and distribution utility in the Kingdom and the supply authority for the plants in this campaign. From your point of view as an assessment engineer, SEC matters in four practical ways:

  • Connection agreement: each plant has (or should have) a connection agreement with SEC stating the connection voltage, the agreed supply capacity, the metering arrangement and the network fault-level data. This document belongs in your Document Inventory (form SF-01) for every plant — chase it during the desk study.
  • Network data: the utility fault level at the point of connection is an SEC-supplied figure. In this manual we assume a 25 kA design value at 13.8 kV (SEC networks are commonly quoted in the 25–40 kA range, indicative) — confirm per plant before relying on any short-circuit result.
  • Metering and tariffs: revenue metering (MV-MET) is on the SEC side of the ownership boundary; tariff structure and any power-factor or maximum-demand clauses come from the SEC supply contract.
  • Compliance codes: SEC publishes the SEC Distribution Code, which — together with the national Saudi Grid Code — sets the technical conditions a connected customer must meet (see below).
Note — who regulates whom. SEC is the utility, not the regulator. The sector regulator (the Water & Electricity Regulatory Authority, WERA — formerly ECRA) licenses utilities and approves the codes; SEC applies them at the connection point. For your assessments the practical interface is SEC: the connection agreement, the Distribution Code and the network data all come through the utility.

Connection at 13.8 kV distribution

A ~2 MW industrial plant in Saudi Arabia is normally connected at the 13.8 kV SEC distribution level — too large for an LV service, far too small for a transmission connection. The typical arrangement, and the one assumed throughout this manual, is:

  • SEC feeder(s) terminate in a utility metering cubicle (MV-MET) at the plant boundary — this is the ownership and metering demarcation point;
  • the customer's 13.8 kV metal-clad switchgear (MV-SWGR, 17.5 kV class, indicative 31.5 kA / 3 s rating) distributes to the two 13.8/0.4 kV, 1600 kVA transformers (TX-1/TX-2, Dyn11, Z = 6 %, MV FLC ≈ 66.9 A each, indicative);
  • everything downstream of MV-MET is the customer's responsibility — which is exactly the scope of your SPOF assessment.

Other SEC distribution voltages exist (33 kV and legacy levels in some areas), and a few plants may have non-standard arrangements. Always confirm the actual connection voltage and the demarcation point from the connection agreement and the as-found installation — a red-line on the SLD at the boundary is one of the most common site-visit findings.

Saudi Grid Code essentials for an industrial customer

The Saudi Grid Code and the SEC Distribution Code define the technical envelope of the supply and the customer's obligations. For an industrial customer of this size the essentials are (all values indicative — verify against the current code edition and the connection agreement):

  • Frequency: the national grid operates at 60 Hz. Normal operation is held within a narrow band around 60 Hz (roughly ±0.5 %, i.e. ~59.7–60.3 Hz under normal conditions, indicative), with wider excursions permitted briefly during disturbances. Your underfrequency/overfrequency protection and any generator controls must coordinate with these bands.
  • Voltage: distribution-level supply voltage is typically maintained within about ±5 % of nominal at the connection point under normal conditions (indicative); the customer must keep its own disturbances (flicker, harmonics, unbalance) within code limits.
  • Power factor: a contractual expectation of power factor ≥ 0.9 (lagging) at the connection point is the typical basis (indicative — the exact threshold and any penalty mechanism are tariff/contract matters). This is the commercial driver behind the PFC bank: this plant's design targets 0.95, i.e. ≈ 580 kvar of detuned capacitors, comfortably inside the expectation.
  • Harmonics and power quality: emission limits at the point of connection follow the codes (aligned with the IEC 61000 series); VFD-heavy plants like these deserve attention here — hence the 7 % detuning reactors on the PFC bank.
  • Protection coordination at the boundary: the customer's incoming protection must grade with the SEC feeder protection; setting changes at the incomer normally require utility agreement.
Tip — what to actually check on a plant. You are not auditing Grid Code compliance in detail. But three quick checks belong in every assessment: (1) does the metered power factor honour the contractual basis, (2) do the incomer relay settings match the SEC-agreed settings record, and (3) is the declared fault level in the short-circuit study consistent with the SEC network data? A mismatch on any of these is a finding.

SASO and the adoption of IEC standards

The Saudi Standards, Metrology and Quality Organization (SASO) is the national standards body. Rather than writing electrical standards from scratch, SASO largely adopts IEC standards, issuing them as Saudi national standards (typically referenced as SASO IEC xxxxx), sometimes with national deviations (notably the 60 Hz frequency, 230/400 V utilisation voltage and the 50 °C climatic context). The practical consequences for you:

  • your IEC knowledge transfers almost directly — switchgear is still IEC 62271-series equipment, transformers are still IEC 60076 machines, LV assemblies still follow IEC 61439;
  • when citing a standard in a deliverable, cite the SASO-adopted edition where one exists, alongside the SEC codes and SBC 401;
  • watch for the national deviations: ratings stated for 60 Hz or 40 °C ambient in a generic IEC catalogue sheet do not automatically hold at 60 Hz and 50 °C (see Designing for 50 °C and Dust).

The table below maps the IEC standards you will use most often to their SASO adoptions. The mapping is indicative — adoption status, edition and numbering change over time; confirm the current SASO catalogue entry before citing in a formal deliverable.

Subject IEC standard SASO adoption (indicative)
HV/MV switchgear & controlgear — common specsIEC 62271-1SASO IEC 62271-1
MV metal-enclosed switchgear (1–52 kV)IEC 62271-200SASO IEC 62271-200
Power transformersIEC 60076 seriesSASO IEC 60076 series
LV switchgear & controlgear assembliesIEC 61439 seriesSASO IEC 61439 series
LV circuit-breakersIEC 60947-2SASO IEC 60947-2
LV electrical installationsIEC 60364 seriesSASO IEC 60364 series (basis of SBC 401)
Power cables 1–30 kVIEC 60502 seriesSASO IEC 60502 series
Short-circuit current calculationIEC 60909 seriesSASO IEC 60909 (method reference)
Degrees of protection (IP code)IEC 60529SASO IEC 60529
Rotating electrical machinesIEC 60034 seriesSASO IEC 60034 series
EMC / power quality limitsIEC 61000 seriesSASO IEC 61000 series
Failure mode and effects analysis (FMEA/FMECA)IEC 60812SASO IEC 60812 (method reference)

SABER — the conformity certification platform

SABER is the Saudi online conformity-assessment platform through which regulated products imported into or sold in the Kingdom obtain their Product Certificate of Conformity and per-consignment Shipment Certificate, issued via SASO-approved certification bodies. Many categories of electrical equipment are regulated products. Why an assessment engineer should care:

  • when your Recommendations Register leads to a concept modification with new equipment, the procurement phase will require SABER certification for regulated items — flag this in the outline specification so it is not discovered at customs;
  • vendor "typical" data sheets are not evidence of Saudi acceptance — ask for the certified data sheet and SASO/SABER status (see the catalogue disclaimer convention in Part 6);
  • lead times for certification are real schedule items in any modification plan.

SBC 401 — the Saudi Building Code, Electrical

SBC 401 is the electrical part of the Saudi Building Code. It governs electrical installations in buildings and facilities in the Kingdom and is grounded in the IEC 60364 installation rules (with national adaptations for 230/400 V, 60 Hz and the Saudi climate). For the plants in this campaign, SBC 401 is most relevant to:

  • LV installation practice: earthing arrangement (TN-S in this manual's basis), protection against electric shock, RCD requirements for final circuits, cable installation and identification;
  • Buildings on the plant: switchrooms, control rooms, admin buildings — their wiring, lighting, socket circuits and emergency systems fall squarely under SBC 401;
  • Modification packages: any concept modification touching an LV installation should cite SBC 401 alongside the SASO-adopted IEC standards in its outline specification.
Note — how the three layers stack. Think of the regulatory stack in three layers: SASO-adopted IEC standards define what good equipment and installations are; SBC 401 defines how installations in buildings must be designed and erected; the SEC Distribution Code / Saudi Grid Code define the rules at the connection point with the utility. A compliant plant satisfies all three simultaneously. In your deliverables, cite them together.
Key takeaway: SEC supplies at 13.8 kV / 60 Hz under the SEC Distribution Code and the Saudi Grid Code (power factor ≥ 0.9 typical contractual basis, indicative); SASO adopts the IEC standards you already know; SABER certifies the products you specify; SBC 401 governs the building installations. Cite all of them together, and confirm current editions and the per-plant connection agreement before issuing any deliverable.

Designing for 50 °C and Dust

Where you are in the workflow: calibrate your engineering instincts to the Saudi environment before you size, check or inspect anything — the climate quietly invalidates many "standard" ratings.

Most IEC equipment ratings are written for a 40 °C maximum ambient (often 35 °C average). The plants in this campaign live in a different world: 50 °C design ambient, intense solar radiation, wind-blown dust and sand, and — at coastal and high-water-table sites — aggressive humidity and salinity. Almost every reliability and sizing judgement you make must pass through this filter. All figures below are indicative; site climatic data and manufacturer derating curves govern.

50 °C ambient — the dominant design driver

Heat is the single most important environmental variable for electrical equipment, because almost every failure mechanism — insulation ageing, contact deterioration, electronics drift, battery decay — accelerates with temperature. A useful rule of thumb: every 8–10 °C of additional operating temperature roughly halves the life of organic insulation (the classic Arrhenius/Montsinger behaviour). Running gear designed for 40 °C at a true 50 °C ambient is therefore not a marginal note — it can halve insulation life and erode every thermal margin in the plant.

What it means in practice on these plants:

  • Transformers: an IEC 60076 transformer's rated temperature rise assumes a standard ambient; at 50 °C it must either be purchased with a suitably reduced rise (the usual Saudi practice) or operated below nameplate. Check the nameplate ambient on site — a "standard ambient" unit at full load in a Saudi summer is a finding.
  • Switchgear and busbars: continuous current ratings are ambient-referenced; a board rated 2500 A at 40 °C may carry meaningfully less at 50 °C switchroom temperature. The LV main board carrying ≈ 2309 A per transformer (indicative) has little slack to waste.
  • The diesel generator: engine and alternator outputs fall with intake air temperature and altitude — which is exactly why this plant's standby set is specified as 1250 kVA ESP at 50 °C (indicative), not at the brochure's reference conditions.
  • Electronics (VFDs, relays, UPS): most drives derate above 40 °C (typically a few percent of output current per °C, indicative) and their cooling fans become life-limiting components.
Warning — nameplate ≠ usable rating. On site, never accept a nameplate rating at face value. Ask: at what ambient was this rating declared, and what is the real ambient where the equipment lives? A piece of equipment that is "adequately rated" on paper but thermally over-driven every summer afternoon is a slow-motion failure — and a legitimate FMECA entry.

Solar gain on outdoor enclosures

Air temperature is only part of the story. Direct solar radiation on an outdoor enclosure adds heat the ambient figure does not capture: an unshaded metal kiosk, feeder pillar or marshalling box in the Saudi sun commonly runs 10–15 °C above the surrounding air internally (indicative) — so a 50 °C day can mean 60–65 °C inside the box, beyond the working range of much electronics and battery equipment.

  • Prefer shaded or indoor locations for anything electronic; a simple sunshade canopy over an outdoor panel is one of the cheapest reliability upgrades available.
  • Specify light-coloured / reflective finishes and, where applicable, double-skin (ventilated) roofs on outdoor enclosures.
  • On site, note the orientation of outdoor panels: a west-facing door in afternoon sun is the worst case. Touch-test (back of hand, carefully) and record suspiciously hot enclosures in the Site-Visit Report.

Dust and sand — ingress, filters and creepage

Wind-blown dust and occasional sandstorms are routine in the Central sector. Dust attacks electrical equipment in three distinct ways, each with its own countermeasure:

  • Ingress: dust inside enclosures blankets components (worsening heat), abrades moving parts and contaminates contacts. Specify minimum IP54 for outdoor enclosures (IP65 for instruments and small boxes is common practice, indicative) per IEC 60529, and keep switchroom doors and gland plates properly sealed. On site, dust drifts inside a panel mean the IP integrity is already lost — record it.
  • Ventilation and filters: any forced-ventilated equipment (switchroom HVAC, generator radiator and intake, VFD cooling paths) needs sand-trap louvres and serviceable filters — and a maintenance regime that actually changes them. A clogged filter quietly converts a ventilation system into a heat trap; ask to see the filter-change records.
  • Creepage and tracking: dust layers on insulators, combined with the morning condensation events of desert climates, create conductive films that promote surface tracking — especially at MV. Outdoor MV insulation in dusty/saline areas is normally specified with increased creepage distance (heavy pollution class per IEC/TS 60815, indicative), and periodic insulator cleaning belongs in the maintenance plan.

Humidity and corrosion — coastal C4/C5 conditions

The Eastern Province sites (and any site near sabkha/salt flats or with saline groundwater — common around groundwater desalination plants by definition) combine heat with chloride-laden humidity. In ISO 12944 atmospheric-corrosivity terms these environments are typically category C4 (high) to C5 (very high) — indicative; classify per site.

  • Specify C4/C5 paint systems (or hot-dip galvanising plus duplex coating) for outdoor steelwork, cable trays, ladder racks and enclosures; stainless fasteners and hinges.
  • Inside panels, look for green/white corrosion bloom on copper and aluminium terminations — a classic precursor to hot joints. Anti-condensation heaters must actually be working (a failed panel heater is a frequent and easy site finding).
  • Beware dissimilar-metal joints (aluminium lug on copper bar) in humid salty air — galvanic corrosion is accelerated; bimetallic connectors and inhibitor compound are the standard defence.

Cable derating at 50 °C — the ≈ 0.82 factor

Published current-carrying capacities for XLPE cables are tabulated at a reference ambient (30 °C in air for many IEC 60364-5-52 tables). At a 50 °C air ambient, the correction factor for 90 °C XLPE insulation is approximately 0.82 (indicative) — a cable "good for" 100 A in the table is good for about 82 A here, before any grouping or solar-exposure corrections, which stack multiplicatively on top.

Quick feel — what 0.82 does to a feeder

I_permissible = I_table × k_ambient = 100 A × 0.82 = 82 A

Stack a grouping factor of, say, 0.8 for several touching cables on a tray and the same cable is down to ≈ 66 A — two-thirds of its table rating. This is why cables sized "by the table" in a temperate-climate design routinely turn out undersized in the Kingdom. Full method and worked examples in Part 2 — Cable Selection.

On site, treat any cable route in direct sun, any heavily-bundled tray, and any duct bank in hot ground as derating hot-spots, and check whether the plant's cable schedule states the ambient on which its sizing was based.

Batteries — life halves every 8–10 °C above 20 °C

Stationary batteries (the UPS bank and the 110 V DC DCDB system that trips the MV switchgear) are the plant components most punished by heat. The design life of a VRLA battery is quoted at 20–25 °C; as a rule of thumb, service life halves for every 8–10 °C above 20 °C (indicative). A "10-year" VRLA block living at 35 °C is realistically a 2–3 year battery; at 40 °C+, worse.

  • Battery rooms/areas must be air-conditioned and held near 20–25 °C; verify the actual room temperature on every site visit, not just the set-point.
  • Check battery installation date vs design life corrected for the real room temperature — an aged battery in a hot room is a high-criticality FMECA entry, because the DC system is the classic hidden SPOF: when it fails, MV protection cannot trip (see Part 3).
  • Where rooms cannot be reliably cooled, consider NiCd chemistry (more temperature-tolerant, indicative) in the Recommendations Register.

HVAC as a reliability-critical system

Put the threads together and an uncomfortable conclusion emerges: in this climate the switchroom air-conditioning is part of the electrical protection system. If the HVAC fails on a summer afternoon, room temperature can climb past equipment limits within hours — VFDs trip or derate, batteries cook, relays and UPS electronics drift toward failure. A single non-redundant air-conditioning unit serving a critical switchroom is a genuine single point of failure and must appear in your SPOF inventory like any breaker or transformer.

  • Look for N+1 redundancy (two units, each able to carry the room) on critical switchrooms and battery rooms; record the as-found arrangement.
  • Check for a high-temperature alarm in the room, routed to a place where somebody actually responds — an unalarmed HVAC failure is found only when equipment starts tripping.
  • Ask what happens on power failure: is the HVAC on the essential (DG-backed) supply? A diesel-backed plant whose switchroom cooling is on the non-essential bus can ride through the outage and then overheat during it.
Tip — the afternoon test. Whenever practicable, time part of your site visit for the early afternoon. Equipment, rooms and enclosures show their true thermal behaviour at 14:00 in July, not at 08:00 in January. A thermal camera (where permitted, non-intrusively) earns its keep in this hour.

Field checklist — environment

  • Record switchroom and battery-room temperatures (actual, not set-point) and the HVAC arrangement (single / N+1, essential-supply backed, high-temperature alarm).
  • Check nameplate ambient ratings of transformers, switchgear and the DG against the 50 °C design ambient; flag any standard-ambient equipment running near full load.
  • Inspect outdoor enclosures for solar exposure, IP integrity (seals, gland plates, dust drifts inside) and condition of sand-trap louvres / filters.
  • Inspect for corrosion: coating condition (C4/C5 environments), terminal bloom inside panels, working anti-condensation heaters, dissimilar-metal joints.
  • Note cable routes in direct sun or heavy bundling; confirm the cable schedule states its sizing ambient (expect ≈ 0.82 air derating at 50 °C).
  • Verify battery installation dates against temperature-corrected life expectancy; record room temperature next to the battery age.
Key takeaway: 50 °C ambient is the dominant design driver — it derates cables (≈ 0.82), transformers, switchgear, drives and the DG; solar gain adds another 10–15 °C inside outdoor enclosures; dust demands IP54+, filters and creepage margin; coastal salt demands C4/C5 protection; heat halves battery life every 8–10 °C above 20 °C. Treat the switchroom HVAC as reliability-critical — in this climate, cooling failures are electrical failures. All figures indicative; site data and manufacturer curves govern.

The MV/LV Plant at a Glance

Where you are in the workflow: before you can assess a plant, you must be able to read its single-line diagram and name every item on it. Part 1 builds that foundation.

Design basis of the reference plant

Throughout this manual, calculations and examples are illustrated using a representative ~2 MW industrial process plant (water / desalination service, typical of the sites you will assess). The design basis is summarised below; all figures are indicative and tentative.

Parameter Indicative value Note
Total plant maximum demand ~2.0 MW Confirm against actual load list.
Overall power factor (before correction) 0.85 lagging Indicative; varies with motor mix and VFD loading.
Apparent power (uncorrected) ~2.35 MVA S = P / pf = 2000 / 0.85
Installed transformer capacity (with margin) 2 × 1600 kVA +20–25 % design/diversity margin; next standard size above 2 × ~1175 kVA.
Utility incoming voltage (MV) 13.8 kV, 3-phase, 60 Hz (SEC) SEC standard distribution voltage; confirm per the connection agreement.
Distribution / utilisation voltage (LV) 400 / 230 V, 3-phase, 60 Hz SASO-adopted IEC / SEC standard utilisation voltage; confirm per plant.
MV system earthing Resistance-earthed via NER (7.97 kV, 400 A / 10 s → 19.9 Ω) Limits earth-fault current to ~400 A (indicative).
LV system earthing TN-S (solid) Confirm per plant.
Design ambient temperature 50 °C, dust/sand exposure Saudi inland design basis; drives the deratings in Derating: the 50 °C Tax.
Governing standards framework SASO-adopted IEC; SEC Distribution Code / Saudi Grid Code; SBC 401 Cited together throughout; project specification governs the applicable revisions.
Redundancy philosophy N-1 partial — 2 × transformers + N.O. bus-tie; single DG for essential loads One transformer can carry the essential load if the other is lost.

Worked example — Transformer sizing from demand

Given ~2 MW demand at 0.85 power factor, size the transformer bank:

S = P / pf = 2000 kW / 0.85 ≈ 2353 kVA

Apply a design and diversity margin of +20–25 %:

S_design = 2353 × 1.25 ≈ 2941 kVA

Split across two transformers (N-1 split-bus), select next standard size:

S_each = 2941 / 2 ≈ 1471 kVA → select 2 × 1600 kVA

Result: 2 × 1600 kVA, Dyn11, 13.8 kV / 0.4 kV (tags TX-1 and TX-2). Values are indicative.

Power architecture — the master SLD

The master single-line diagram below shows the complete reference power architecture. Study it before proceeding — every section of Part 1 refers to items on this drawing.

The architecture has three tiers:

  1. MV (13.8 kV) tier: Utility feed → metering (MV-MET) → MV switchgear (MV-SWGR) with protection IEDs (MV-PROT) and neutral earthing resistor (MV-NER).
  2. Transformation tier: Two independent transformer feeders supply TX-1 and TX-2, each stepping 13.8 kV down to 400 V.
  3. LV (400 V) tier: Main switchboard (LV-MSB) with a split bus (A and B) joined by a normally-open bus-tie (BT); Motor Control Centres (MCC-1, MCC-2), PFC bank, distribution boards, essential board (EDB), diesel generator (DG), ATS, UPS and DC battery system (DCDB).
Key takeaway: The ~2 MW reference plant is the template for everything in this manual. Two 1600 kVA transformers on a split LV bus with a normally-open bus-tie give partial N-1 resilience — understanding why this architecture is used, and where it is still vulnerable, is the core of the SPOF assessment.

How to Read an SLD

Where you are in the workflow: before the site visit, you must be comfortable reading any MV/LV SLD you are handed, not just the reference drawing.

Power-flow direction and voltage levels

Read an SLD top to bottom — energy flows from the utility source at the top to the loads (motors, boards) at the bottom. On the reference drawing there are two clearly separated horizontal zones:

  • MV zone (upper band): 13.8 kV — from the utility intake down to and including the transformer primaries. MV busbars are drawn in firebrick red on colour drawings.
  • LV zone (lower band): 400 V — from the transformer secondaries to all distribution equipment and loads. LV busbars are drawn in navy blue.

The two transformers (TX-1, TX-2) are the physical and electrical boundary between the MV and LV zones.

IEC 60617 symbols used on the reference SLD

Note — IEC 60617: This is the international standard defining graphical symbols for diagrams. The symbols used on the reference SLD are drawn in accordance with IEC 60617 series. If you encounter a drawing using ANSI/IEEE symbols (common in North America), the layout is the same but some symbols differ — the most common example is the circuit-breaker symbol (IEC: a solid square; ANSI: two diverging lines). Always check the drawing legend before interpreting unfamiliar symbols.
Symbol / notation Meaning Where seen on reference SLD
Heavy horizontal line (═══) Busbar — the common copper to which feeders and incomers connect. 13.8 kV bus in MV-SWGR; LV Bus A and Bus B in LV-MSB.
Solid square on conductor Circuit breaker (closed / in service). IEC 60617-07. All MV and LV breakers.
Hollow / dashed square Circuit breaker normally open (N.O.). Bus-tie BT; ATS open position.
[52-I], [52-T1], etc. MV circuit breaker — ANSI device number 52. Suffix identifies the function (I = incomer, BS = bus-section, T1/T2 = transformer feeder). MV-SWGR breakers.
[ACB] Air Circuit Breaker — large LV breaker used for main incomers and bus-tie. LV-MSB incomers from TX-1 and TX-2; bus-tie BT.
[MCCB] Moulded-Case Circuit Breaker — smaller LV breaker used for outgoing feeders. LV-MSB outgoing feeders to MCC-1, MCC-2, PFC, DB, EDB.
Two interlinked circles with a line between ((│ │) with 8) Two-winding transformer (IEC 60617). The winding configuration label (e.g. Dyn11) shows delta primary (D), star secondary (y, solidly earthed), and 30° phase shift (clock position 11). TX-1 and TX-2.
Resistor symbol to earth at neutral point ([N]) Neutral Earthing Resistor — MV neutral earthed through a resistor. MV-NER at the 13.8 kV neutral.
Three diminishing bars to earth Earth / ground connection. LV transformer secondary neutral; earthing points on equipment.
Circle with CT or VT Current Transformer / Voltage Transformer — instrument transformers feeding meters and relays. MV-MET (revenue CTs/VTs); MV-PROT (protection CTs).
Circle with M Motor load. Process motors at the bottom of each MCC feeder.
Two parallel plates Capacitor bank (PFC). PFC on LV Bus A feeder.
Labelled rectangle (block) Equipment assembly — used for MCC, VFD, UPS, ATS, DB, DCDB where a single-symbol representation is appropriate. MCC-1, MCC-2, VFD, UPS, ATS, DCDB, DB, EDB.
DOL / S/S / VFD label on motor feeder Starter type: Direct-on-Line / Soft-Starter / Variable Frequency Drive. Within MCC blocks.

Busbar convention and the split bus

The LV main switchboard (LV-MSB) is drawn as a split bus: Bus A (left, fed by TX-1) and Bus B (right, fed by TX-2) are two physically separate copper bars in the one board. They are joined only through the normally-open bus-tie (BT). In normal operation each transformer supplies its own half of the plant independently. The split-bus arrangement:

  • limits the fault current that a single transformer must interrupt,
  • contains the blast zone of any bus fault to one section, and
  • allows the bus-tie to close automatically on loss of one incomer, maintaining supply to both buses from the surviving transformer.

Red-lining on site

On a site visit, you compare the SLD against the as-found installation and mark discrepancies in red on a working print or a PDF annotation tool. Common discrepancies to look for:

  • Breaker labelling or device number differs from drawing.
  • An extra or missing feeder — a circuit on site not shown on the drawing.
  • Bus-tie in a different normal position (open or closed) from the drawing.
  • Equipment substituted (e.g. RMU instead of metal-clad switchgear).
  • Protection relay type or settings different from what is listed.
  • Cables or transformers replaced with a different rating.
Key takeaway: Read SLDs top-to-bottom (source to load), know the IEC 60617 symbols, and understand the split-bus concept. On site, every discrepancy between drawing and reality is a red-line — and potentially a SPOF waiting to be found.

PCC vs MCC

Where you are in the workflow: this distinction is fundamental — confusing the two is one of the most common errors on assessment forms.

Both the PCC and the MCC are LV assemblies built to IEC 61439-1/-2, and they can look similar in a switchroom. They do very different jobs:

Feature PCC (Power Control Centre) MCC (Motor Control Centre)
Tag in reference plant LV-MSB MCC-1, MCC-2
Primary function Receives transformer output(s); distributes bulk LV power to downstream boards and feeders. The LV power hub. Houses individual motor starters — DOL, star-delta, soft-starter, VFD — that control and protect specific process motors.
Incomers Air Circuit Breakers (ACBs) from transformers; motorised bus-tie ACB. Single MCCB incomer from the PCC (LV-MSB) outgoing feeder.
Outgoing feeders MCCBs feeding MCCs, distribution boards, PFC bank, large individual loads. Per-motor buckets: MCCB or fuse-contactor, contactors, overload relay, local/remote control, ammeter, run/trip indication.
Bus rating (indicative) ~2500–3200 A; 50–65 kA fault withstand. ~1000–2000 A; fault withstand matched to upstream board.
Form of segregation (IEC 61439) Form 4b — busbars separated from functional units; terminals separated from other functional units. Form 3b/4b — functional units (starters) separated from each other.
Where on the SLD The central LV switchboard spanning Bus A and Bus B — immediately below the transformers. On outgoing MCCB feeders from the LV-MSB Bus A/B; one MCC per bus section in the reference plant.
One-line description "The LV power hub — it feeds the MCCs." "Where the motors are started — it feeds the loads."
Note — vocabulary variation: In different organisations, the PCC is also called the Main LV Switchboard (MLVSB), Main Distribution Board (MDB), or simply the Main Board. The MCC may be called a Motor Control Panel (MCP) or a starter board. The function — bulk distribution vs. individual motor control — is the reliable distinguishing criterion, regardless of what the nameplate says.
Tip — SPOF implication: Because the PCC is the single point through which all transformer power passes before reaching the MCCs, a fault or loss of the PCC is a plant-level SPOF. The split bus with bus-tie is specifically designed to limit this vulnerability. When assessing a plant, check whether the PCC is truly split-bus or is effectively a single-bus board with cosmetic segregation.
Key takeaway: PCC (LV-MSB) = bulk distribution hub, fed by transformers, feeds MCCs. MCC = motor starter assembly, fed by PCC, feeds individual motors. The PCC feeds the MCCs; the MCCs feed the loads.

Equipment Function Table

Where you are in the workflow: this is the core reference table for the assessment — you will return to it every time you encounter a tag on site or in a document.

The table below covers all 17 tags in the reference plant (including MV-PROT). For each tag it states the equipment name, its function (what it does and why it is there), the indicative rating, and where it appears on the master SLD. All ratings are indicative; confirm against actual plant nameplates.

Tag Equipment Function / purpose Indicative rating Where on the SLD
MV-MET Utility revenue metering cubicle Measures energy and maximum demand at the 13.8 kV intake via utility-grade CTs and VTs feeding a tariff kWh/kVAh meter. The commercial boundary between utility and plant — SEC reads this meter for billing (time-of-use tariff and power-factor clause; see metering and tariff basics). Utility CT/VT and tariff meter; class 0.2S or better. MV section, first item on the 13.8 kV supply side (top of drawing).
MV-SWGR 13.8 kV metal-clad switchgear Receives and distributes the 13.8 kV supply. Houses the MV incomer breaker [52-I], bus-section breaker [52-BS], and two transformer-feeder breakers [52-T1] / [52-T2], so that MV circuits can be switched and fault current cleared quickly. The 13.8 kV busbar inside this board is the MV power hub. Typical product families in the MV switchgear catalogue. 13.8 kV system (17.5 kV class), 630–1250 A bus, 31.5 kA / 3 s withstand (≥ 25 kA SEC design fault level); vacuum CBs; SASO-adopted IEC 62271-200. MV section, central board below metering.
MV-PROT MV protection and control IEDs Detects MV faults and trips the correct breaker to isolate them quickly, minimising damage and restoring supply elsewhere. Functions: overcurrent (50/51), earth-fault (50N/51N), transformer differential (87T), under/over-voltage (27/59). Relays are supplied from the DCDB so they remain operative even on AC failure. Typical relay families in the protection relay catalogue. Numerical IEDs; one per breaker or combined; SASO-adopted IEC 60255 series. MV section, relay panels associated with MV-SWGR breakers.
MV-NER Neutral Earthing Resistor Earths the 13.8 kV transformer star neutral through a resistor that limits the earth-fault current to a safe, detectable level (~400 A indicative). Without it, an undetected earth fault could cause equipment damage and personnel hazard. A neutral CT allows the protection relay to detect and measure the fault current. 7.97 kV (13.8 kV/√3), 400 A / 10 s → 19.9 Ω (indicative); with neutral CT. MV section, at the [N] neutral point — connected to the neutral of the 13.8 kV supply transformer or to the MV bus neutral where provided.
TX-1 MV/LV power transformer No. 1 Steps 13.8 kV down to 400 V to supply Bus A of the LV-MSB. One of the two parallel sources that give the plant its partial N-1 resilience. If TX-2 is lost, the bus-tie closes so TX-1 can carry both buses. Typical units in the transformer catalogue. 1600 kVA, 13.8 / 0.4 kV, Dyn11, Z = 6 %; MV FLC 66.9 A, LV FLC 2309 A; SASO-adopted IEC 60076; cast-resin or oil-immersed. Between MV feeder [52-T1] and LV incomer [ACB-A] on Bus A.
TX-2 MV/LV power transformer No. 2 Steps 13.8 kV down to 400 V to supply Bus B of the LV-MSB. Mirrors TX-1 on the Bus B side. If TX-1 is lost, the bus-tie closes so TX-2 can carry both buses. 1600 kVA, 13.8 / 0.4 kV, Dyn11, Z = 6 %; MV FLC 66.9 A, LV FLC 2309 A; SASO-adopted IEC 60076. Between MV feeder [52-T2] and LV incomer [ACB-B] on Bus B.
LV-MSB Main LV switchboard / PCC (Power Control Centre) The 400 V power hub: receives the two transformer outputs on a split bus (A and B) and distributes bulk LV power via MCCB feeders to the MCCs, PFC bank, distribution boards and large individual feeders. ACB incomers from both transformers; motorised normally-open bus-tie (BT) between the two bus sections. Typical ACB/MCCB families in the LV breaker catalogue. 400/230 V, 60 Hz, split bus A/B, ~2500–3200 A bus, 50–65 kA rated (Isc ≈ 38.5 kA one transformer; ≈ 77 kA prospective if tie closed with both incomers — see Part 2); ACB incomers + motorised bus-tie; Form 4b; SASO-adopted IEC 61439-1/-2. LV section — the main board spanning both Bus A and Bus B, directly below the transformers.
BT LV bus-tie circuit breaker (normally open) Normally open during operation so the two bus sections are independent. Automatically closes on loss of either LV incomer (trip of ACB-A or ACB-B), allowing the surviving transformer to supply both buses. This is the single most important N-1 feature in the LV distribution — its correct operation (auto-close logic armed, correct setting) must always be verified. ACB, motorised/automatic; matching LV-MSB rating. LV-MSB — the link between Bus A and Bus B (drawn with hollow/N.O. symbol).
MCC-1 Motor Control Centre No. 1 (Bus A) Houses the motor starters (DOL, star-delta, soft-starter and/or VFD buckets) that control and protect the Bus-A process motors. Each bucket contains: MCCB or fuse-contactor, contactor(s), thermal/electronic overload relay or motor protection relay, local/remote control switch, run/trip indication and ammeter. Typical assemblies in the MCC catalogue. 400 V, 60 Hz; bus rating matched to group motor demand; SASO-adopted IEC 61439-1/-2; Form 3b/4b. LV-MSB Bus A, on an outgoing MCCB feeder.
MCC-2 Motor Control Centre No. 2 (Bus B) Mirrors MCC-1 on Bus B — houses the starters for Bus-B process motors. Distributing motors across two bus sections means the plant retains some motor availability even if one transformer section is lost. 400 V; bus rating matched to group motor demand; IEC 61439-1/-2. LV-MSB Bus B, on an outgoing MCCB feeder.
VFD Variable Frequency Drive(s) Provides continuously variable speed control of pumps, fans, compressors and mixers by varying output voltage and frequency. Reduces energy consumption and mechanical starting stress compared with DOL starting. May be installed as individual MCC buckets or as standalone panels. Input chokes and EMC filters required to limit harmonic injection into the LV bus. Typical drive families in the VFD catalogue. Rated per driven motor (note 50 °C ambient derating, ~1–1.5 %/°C above 40 °C); input choke and EMC filter; SASO-adopted IEC 61800 series. Within MCC-1 / MCC-2 buckets, or as standalone panels; feeding speed-controlled motors.
PFC Power factor correction (detuned capacitor bank) Supplies reactive power locally to correct the plant power factor from ~0.85 to 0.95+ lagging, reducing demand charges and freeing transformer and cable capacity. Automatic switching (contactor-controlled steps) matches reactive output to load variation. Detuned (7 % tuning reactor in series) to prevent resonance with VFD-generated harmonics. ≈580 kvar automatic (indicative); detuned 7 % (tuning ≈ 227 Hz at 60 Hz); corrects ~0.85 → 0.95; SASO-adopted IEC 60831. LV-MSB Bus A, on a dedicated MCCB feeder.
DB Distribution board(s) Distributes LV power to general (non-essential) loads — lighting, small power, HVAC, sockets — via MCBs and RCBOs. Fed from the LV-MSB via MCCB feeders. These loads are not critical: they are shed on a utility outage before or when the generator takes over the essential bus. 400/230 V; MCB/RCBO outgoing ways; IEC 60439 / IEC 61439. LV-MSB Bus A (and/or Bus B), on MCCB outgoing feeders.
EDB Essential / emergency distribution board Feeds loads that must remain energised through a utility outage — escape lighting, fire systems, critical control auxiliaries, communication systems. Supplied from the LV-MSB Bus B via an MCCB feeder in normal operation, and backed by the ATS so that on utility loss it is transferred to the generator within the ATS changeover time. 400/230 V; MCB/RCBO outgoing; IEC 61439. LV section — fed from Bus B (normal) and backed via ATS from DG.
DG Diesel generator set Standby generation sized to carry the essential load on utility failure — not necessarily the full 2 MW plant demand. Starts automatically (auto-start logic) on detection of utility loss; the ATS transfers the essential bus once the generator has reached rated voltage and frequency. Equipped with AVR, fuel day-tank, acoustic canopy and exhaust system. Typical sets in the genset catalogue. 1250 kVA ESP at 50 °C ambient, indicative (confirmed by essential-load list); 400 V, 60 Hz, 1800 rpm, AVR. Standby power path (lower band of drawing).
ATS Automatic Transfer Switch Automatically transfers the essential bus between the utility feed (normal source) and the generator (standby source) on utility loss, and retransfers when utility supply is restored and stable. Mechanically interlocked to prevent both sources feeding the bus simultaneously — an important safety feature. Changeover time is the key performance parameter (open-transition, ≤ 10 s total for diesel start + transfer, indicative). Typical switches in the ATS catalogue. Rated for essential bus load; open-transition, ≤ 10 s; mechanically interlocked; SASO-adopted IEC 60947-6-1. Standby path — between DG output and EDB incoming supply.
UPS Uninterruptible Power Supply Provides clean, uninterrupted AC power to the DCS, PLC, SCADA, instrumentation and critical communications with no break — not even the ~10 s (indicative) it takes the diesel generator to start and the ATS to transfer. Operates from internal batteries during the outage bridge; static bypass allows maintenance without interrupting the load. Battery autonomy 30 min (indicative; confirm per spec — and see the battery-temperature note). Typical units in the UPS catalogue. 100 kVA / 30 min (indicative); static bypass; SASO-adopted IEC 62040. LV-MSB Bus B feeder, dedicated UPS output to DCS/control system.
DCDB DC battery and charger system (110 V DC) Provides secure 110 V DC power to trip and close the MV switchgear breakers, and to power the protection relay IEDs and their communications. This DC supply must remain available even when the entire AC system (both utility and generator) is dead — battery float charging maintains the battery at full charge continuously, and the battery alone sustains protection and switching capability during a complete AC blackout. 110 V DC (typical); battery sized per IEC 62485-2; float/boost charger; IEC 60896. DC control/protection supply band — feeds MV-SWGR trip/close circuits and MV-PROT IEDs.
Note — tag MV-PROT: The protection IEDs are sometimes shown as part of the MV-SWGR cubicle rather than as a separate tag, depending on the drawing convention. On site, check whether the relay panel has its own tag plate. For FMECA purposes, MV-PROT is treated as a distinct item because its failure mode (relay malfunction or loss of DC supply) is independent of the switchgear mechanism failure.
Key takeaway: Seventeen tags, two voltage levels, three functional layers — generation of the supply (MV), distribution (LV-MSB), and control/protection (DCDB/UPS/DG/ATS). Memorise the function of each tag in one sentence; you will need that knowledge to populate the FMECA without hesitation.

Power-Flow Walk-Through

Where you are in the workflow: tracing the three power paths is what you do mentally at every site visit — it reveals where the architecture is robust and where it is not.

Keep the master SLD (Fig 1-1) open alongside this section. There are three distinct paths through the plant — trace each one on the drawing as you read.

Path 1 — Main (normal) path: utility to process motor

  1. Utility 13.8 kV feed arrives at the site and passes through the revenue metering cubicle (MV-MET) where energy is metered for tariff billing.
  2. The supply enters the MV switchgear (MV-SWGR) via incomer breaker [52-I]. The protection IEDs (MV-PROT) watch this incomer and the transformer feeders for faults. The 13.8 kV neutral is earthed through the resistor (MV-NER).
  3. Two transformer feeder breakers [52-T1] and [52-T2] supply the two transformers (TX-1 and TX-2), which step 13.8 kV down to 400 V.
  4. Each transformer secondary feeds its LV incomer ACB: TX-1 → ACB-A → Bus A, TX-2 → ACB-B → Bus B. The bus-tie (BT) between them is normally open, so the two bus sections are independent.
  5. Outgoing MCCB feeders from Bus A and Bus B supply the Motor Control Centres (MCC-1, MCC-2), the PFC bank (PFC), distribution boards (DB) and the essential board (EDB).
  6. Inside an MCC, the appropriate starter (DOL, soft-starter or a VFD) energises the process motor — pump, fan, conveyor or mixer.
Tip — tracing on a real SLD: On a site SLD that may be larger or more complex, follow the same top-to-bottom discipline and identify: (a) the metering point, (b) the MV incomer and switchgear, (c) the transformer(s), (d) the LV main board and its bus arrangement, (e) the MCC feeders, and (f) the standby and DC paths. This sequence works on any MV/LV plant drawing.

Path 2 — Standby path: on utility loss

When the utility supply is lost (or falls outside acceptable voltage/frequency limits):

  1. The ATS detects utility loss and issues an auto-start signal to the diesel generator (DG).
  2. The DG starts, ramps to rated speed, and the AVR brings the voltage to 400 V ± tolerance.
  3. The ATS transfers the essential bus from the utility feed to the DG output — the transfer is mechanically interlocked to ensure only one source feeds the EDB at a time.
  4. The essential distribution board (EDB) remains energised — escape lighting, fire systems and critical process auxiliaries stay live.
  5. Within the LV-MSB, if one incomer ACB trips (loss of one transformer), the bus-tie (BT) auto-closes so the surviving transformer can supply both Bus A and Bus B (at reduced capacity — load shedding may be needed).
Note — ATS changeover time: The reference plant assumes an open-transition ATS with a total changeover (diesel start + transfer) of ≤ 10 s (indicative); older or poorly maintained sets may take 10–30 s. During this window, only the UPS-backed loads remain energised. The EDB is de-energised for this interval unless the ATS uses a static (electronic) transfer or the EDB has its own small UPS. Verify the actual changeover time on site and confirm it is acceptable for the essential loads.

Path 3 — DC / control path: always alive

The control and protection path is designed to be available at all times — even during a complete AC blackout:

  1. The DCDB (DC battery and charger) is float-charged from the LV supply and holds its battery at full charge continuously. On loss of AC, the battery sustains the DC bus.
  2. The 110 V DC bus from the DCDB feeds the trip and close coils of the MV switchgear breakers and the MV protection relay IEDs. The protection system remains operative — it can still detect faults and trip breakers — even with no AC available.
  3. The UPS is float-charged from the LV AC bus (typically Bus B). On loss of AC it switches seamlessly to its own battery (static changeover, no break). It supplies the DCS, PLC, SCADA and instrumentation at clean regulated AC.
  4. Together, the DCDB and UPS ensure that both protection (MV DC) and control (AC to DCS/instruments) remain available for the full battery autonomy period, giving the operator visibility and the ability to operate the MV switchgear during a blackout.
Tip — SPOF on the DC path: A common finding is that both the UPS and the DCDB charger are supplied from the same LV bus section or even the same feeder, removing their independence. On site, verify that the UPS and DCDB have segregated sources. Also check the battery condition and last test date — an aged or untested battery is a hidden SPOF that does not show up on the SLD.
Key takeaway: Three paths — normal (utility to motors), standby (DG via ATS to essential loads), and DC/control (DCDB to MV switchgear; UPS to DCS). Trace all three on every SLD you review; the points where paths converge or where independence is lost are your SPOF candidates.

Equipment Schedule and Standards

Where you are in the workflow: the equipment schedule is the quantitative companion to the function table — use it during the desk study to frame your calculations and check nameplate data on site.

Indicative equipment schedule

The schedule below summarises the principal electrical equipment for the reference ~2 MW plant. All ratings are indicative and tentative; confirm against the actual plant documents and nameplate data.

Tag Equipment Indicative rating / specification IEC standard
MV-MET Utility metering cubicle 13.8 kV; tariff-grade CTs/VTs (class 0.2S); kWh + kVAh meter; per SEC specification. SASO-adopted IEC 62053; SEC metering specification.
MV-SWGR MV metal-clad switchgear 13.8 kV (17.5 kV class); 630–1250 A bus (indicative); 31.5 kA / 3 s fault withstand — covers the 25 kA SEC design fault level (declared range 25–40 kA); vacuum CBs; 4 panels (incomer, bus-section, 2 × transformer feeder). IEC 62271-200; IEC 62271-100.
MV-NER Neutral Earthing Resistor 7.97 kV (13.8 kV/√3), 400 A / 10 s → 19.9 Ω (indicative); with neutral CT; outdoor or indoor housing. IEC 60099-6 (guidance); manufacturer's specification.
TX-1 / TX-2 MV/LV power transformers 2 × 1600 kVA (indicative); 13.8 / 0.4 kV; Dyn11; Z = 6 %; MV FLC 66.9 A / LV FLC 2309 A; cast-resin (dry-type) or oil-immersed (ONAN); off-load taps ±2 × 2.5 %; check 50 °C ambient rating (see transformer derating). IEC 60076 series.
LV-MSB Main LV switchboard / PCC 400 V; split bus A/B; ~2500–3200 A main bus (indicative); 50–65 kA fault withstand; ACB incomers + motorised N.O. bus-tie ACB; MCCB outgoing feeders; Form 4b segregation. IEC 61439-1; IEC 61439-2.
MCC-1 / MCC-2 Motor Control Centres 400 V; horizontal bus 1000–2000 A (indicative); 50 kA fault withstand; withdrawable or fixed starter buckets: DOL, star-delta, soft-starter, VFD; MCCB or fuse-contactor, contactor, electronic overload/MPR; Form 3b/4b. IEC 61439-1; IEC 61439-2.
VFD Variable Frequency Drives Rated per motor; 400 V input; input choke + EMC filter; integrated or remote keypad; RS485/PROFIBUS/PROFINET fieldbus interface. IEC 61800-3; IEC 61800-5.
PFC Power factor correction bank Automatic; detuned (7 % tuning reactor); sized to correct ~0.85 → 0.95+ (indicative); contactor-switched capacitor steps. IEC 60831; IEC 60831-1/-2.
DB Distribution boards 400/230 V; MCB and RCBO outgoing ways; surface or flush-mounted; IP rating per environment. IEC 61439-3.
EDB Essential distribution board 400/230 V; fed via ATS; rated for essential load; MCB/RCBO outgoing. IEC 61439-3.
DG Diesel generator set ~800–1250 kVA (indicative — sized by essential load list); 400 V, 3-phase, 60 Hz; AVR; fuel day-tank; acoustic canopy; auto-start panel. IEC 60034 (generator); ISO 8528 (genset performance).
ATS Automatic Transfer Switch Rated for essential bus full load; mechanically interlocked; open-transition or closed-transition (confirm per plant); changeover time per DG start time (indicative 10–30 s). IEC 60947-6-1.
UPS Uninterruptible Power Supply Online double-conversion; rated per DCS/instrument load; static bypass; battery autonomy 15–30 min (indicative); 400 V or 230 V single-phase output as required. IEC 62040-1; IEC 62040-3.
DCDB DC battery and charger system 110 V DC (typical for MV switchgear); float/boost charger; VRLA or vented lead-acid battery; battery sized for ~8 h autonomy (confirm per spec) plus interruption allowance. IEC 60896-11/-21; IEC 62485-2.

Applicable IEC standards summary

Equipment category Primary standard(s)
MV switchgear (metal-clad)IEC 62271-200; IEC 62271-100 (vacuum CBs)
Power transformersIEC 60076 series
LV switchgear and MCC assembliesIEC 61439-1; IEC 61439-2
LV circuit breakers (ACB, MCCB, MCB)IEC 60947-2; IEC 60947-3
MotorsIEC 60034 series
Variable speed drives / VFDsIEC 61800-3; IEC 61800-5
Power factor correction capacitorsIEC 60831
Uninterruptible power suppliesIEC 62040-1; IEC 62040-3
Automatic transfer switchesIEC 60947-6-1
DC battery systemsIEC 60896; IEC 62485
Earthing and bonding (general installation)IEC 60364 series
Lightning protection systemsIEC 62305 series
Protection relays / IEDsIEC 60255 series
FMECA methodologyIEC 60812
Reliability / availability (RAM)ISO 14224; ISO 20815
Arc-flash incident energyIEEE 1584 (US basis, widely adopted)
Note — standards confirmation: The standards listed are indicative. The governing standards for each plant are determined by the client specification and the applicable national regulations. Check the project specification and confirm with the client which revision of each standard applies.
Key takeaway: The equipment schedule gives you the quantitative framework for the desk study — use it to check whether the nameplate data you collect on site matches the design intent. Any significant discrepancy (wrong rating, different vector group, different impedance) is a calculation input that may change your load-flow or short-circuit result and should be noted in the Document Inventory and potentially the FMECA. Part 2 now shows you how to run the calculations.

Part 1 — Self-Check

Where you are in the workflow: test yourself before moving to the calculations in Part 2. If you cannot answer these confidently, re-read the relevant section of Part 1.

Attempt all six questions before revealing the answers. Use only the master SLD (Fig 1-1) and your memory — no notes.

Question 1

Which two tags mark the boundary between the 13.8 kV and the 400 V parts of the plant? State what each one does.

Show answer

TX-1 and TX-2 — the two MV/LV power transformers (1600 kVA each, indicative; 13.8 kV / 0.4 kV, Dyn11). They step the 13.8 kV utility supply down to 400 V for the LV distribution system. TX-1 feeds Bus A and TX-2 feeds Bus B of the LV-MSB. Everything above them is MV (13.8 kV); everything below them is LV (400 V).

Question 2

What is the difference between LV-MSB and MCC-1? Which one feeds which?

Show answer

LV-MSB is the PCC (Power Control Centre) / Main LV Switchboard — it receives the transformer outputs and distributes bulk 400 V power. Think "the LV power hub."

MCC-1 is a Motor Control Centre — an assembly of motor starters (DOL, soft-starter, VFD) that control and protect individual process motors. Think "where the motors are started."

The LV-MSB feeds MCC-1 (via an outgoing MCCB feeder from Bus A). MCC-1 feeds the individual motors.

Question 3

The bus-tie BT is normally open. What event causes it to close automatically, and why is that desirable?

Show answer

The bus-tie closes automatically on the trip or loss of either LV incomer ACB (i.e. loss of one transformer or its MV feeder). The auto-close logic detects the incomer trip and closes BT so the surviving transformer can carry both Bus A and Bus B loads.

This is desirable because it prevents loss of supply to half the plant whenever one transformer is lost — it is the primary N-1 resilience feature of the LV distribution. Without it, loss of TX-1 would immediately shed all Bus A loads (MCC-1, PFC, DB-A and any Bus A feeders).

Question 4

Name the three tags on the standby/essential power path and state the order in which energy flows through them on a utility outage.

Show answer

DG → ATS → EDB.

On a utility outage: the DG starts (auto-start signal from the ATS), ramps to rated voltage and frequency; the ATS transfers the essential bus from the utility source to the DG output; the EDB (essential distribution board) remains energised, supplying escape lighting, fire systems and critical process auxiliaries.

Question 5

Why does the plant need a separate DCDB when it already has a UPS? What does each supply?

Show answer

They serve two different and independently critical supply circuits:

  • UPS supplies AC power (400 V or 230 V) to the DCS, PLC, SCADA and instrumentation systems. These are AC-powered electronics that need a clean, regulated, uninterrupted supply.
  • DCDB supplies 110 V DC to the trip and close coils of the MV switchgear breakers and to the MV protection relay IEDs. These must operate even when the AC system is completely dead — a DC battery is the only supply that can guarantee this.

If you replaced the DCDB with a UPS, the MV switchgear would lose its ability to trip or close once the UPS itself lost its input feed. The DCDB's battery is the last line of protection operability during a full AC blackout.

Question 6

What is the purpose of MV-NER, and what would happen if it were removed (neutral left floating)?

Show answer

MV-NER (the Neutral Earthing Resistor) earths the 13.8 kV system neutral through a resistor to limit earth-fault current to approximately 300–400 A (indicative). This value is high enough for earth-fault protection relays to detect the fault reliably, but low enough to limit damage to equipment and reduce the step/touch voltage hazard at the fault location.

If the NER were removed and the neutral left floating (isolated neutral system):

  • An earth fault would not produce a reliable fault current — the protection relay could not detect it dependably, meaning the fault might persist and worsen.
  • The healthy phases would rise to line-to-line voltage above earth, stressing cable insulation and equipment rated for line-to-earth voltage.
  • Transient overvoltages (ferro-resonance) could develop on the unearthed network, potentially damaging voltage transformers and other equipment.
Key takeaway: If you answered all six correctly without hesitation, you are ready for Part 2 — Calculations. If any question exposed a gap, go back to the relevant section of Part 1 now. The calculations in Part 2 assume you know what every tag does and how the three power paths connect.

Why 60 Hz Changes Things

Where you are in the workflow: this Saudi (SEC) edition of the manual is written for a 60 Hz grid. If your previous experience is on 60 Hz plants — Europe, most of Asia and Africa, the rest of the GCC — this section tells you exactly which of your instincts still hold and which must be recalibrated before you touch a calculation or a nameplate.

What frequency physically means

System frequency is the number of complete alternating-current cycles per second, in hertz. On the SEC grid the voltage on every conductor swings from positive peak, through zero, to negative peak and back 60 times every second — a full cycle takes 1/60 s ≈ 16.7 ms (compared with 20 ms on a 60 Hz grid).

Frequency is the heartbeat of the grid.
