It is set by the rotational speed of the synchronous generators feeding the network, and — unlike voltage, which differs from busbar to busbar — frequency is the same everywhere on an interconnected AC system at any instant. Every generator in the Kingdom spins in lock-step; every transformer core magnetises and demagnetises 60 times a second; every induction motor chases a magnetic field rotating at a speed fixed by that same 60 Hz. When load exceeds generation the whole grid slows together (frequency droops); when generation exceeds load it speeds up. That is why frequency is the universal balance indicator used by the grid operator — and why nothing inside your plant can choose its own frequency (except behind a VFD or a UPS inverter, which synthesise their own).

Note — voltage is local, frequency is global. A voltage problem (sag, drop, unbalance) can exist on one feeder of your plant only. A frequency excursion is a system-wide event affecting the entire SEC grid — if you see 58 Hz on a plant meter, either the grid is in serious trouble or (far more likely) you are looking at the output of an islanded generator or a drive. This distinction matters when diagnosing power-quality complaints on site.

Because frequency enters directly into the physics of rotating machines, magnetic cores and reactive components, moving a design from 60 Hz to 60 Hz changes real numbers — speeds, fluxes, reactances, kvar. The changes are systematic and predictable. The table in the next sub-section is the master list; the sub-sections after it explain the three consequences that cause real trouble in the field: motor shaft power, transformer flux, and capacitor-bank tuning.

60 Hz vs 60 Hz — the consequences table

Quantity At 60 Hz At 60 Hz Change Practical consequence
Synchronous speed, 4-pole motor (n = 120·f/p) 1500 rpm 1800 rpm +20 % Every direct-on-line motor runs ~20 % faster. All speed-stamped data (pump curves, fan curves, gearbox ratios) must be re-read for 60 Hz.
Shaft power of a centrifugal pump/fan at fixed impeller (affinity laws, P ∝ n³) 100 % (design) up to ~173 % up to +73 % A 60 Hz-selected pump motor moved to 60 Hz without impeller change can be overloaded by up to 73 % — see the motor sub-section. The single most expensive 50/60 Hz mistake.
Transformer core flux at same applied voltage (Φ ∝ V/f) 100 % (design) ~83 % −17 % A 60 Hz-designed transformer at 60 Hz runs at lower flux — magnetically safe. The reverse direction is dangerous: a 60 Hz-designed unit at 60 Hz saturates — see below.
Inductive reactance X = 2πfL (cables, reactors, transformer leakage in ohms) 100 % 120 % +20 % Reactive volt-drop on long feeders slightly higher; per-unit (%) impedances of transformers are unchanged because they are restated on the 60 Hz base.
Capacitor reactive output Q = 2πfCV² at same voltage 100 % (rated) 120 % +20 % A capacitor can carries +20 % kvar and +20 % current at 60 Hz — its series reactor, contactors and fuses may be overloaded. PFC banks must be ordered for 60 Hz, not adapted.
Detuned PFC filter resonant point, 7 % reactor (f_res = f/√0.07) ≈189 Hz ≈227 Hz +20 % Tuning stays safely below the 5th harmonic (260 Hz at 60 Hz; 300 Hz at 60 Hz) only if reactor and capacitor are matched as a 60 Hz set.
Harmonic frequencies (5th, 7th…) 250, 360 Hz… 300, 420 Hz… +20 % Harmonic studies, filter designs and power-quality meter settings must use the 60 Hz harmonic ladder.
Transformer/motor iron losses at design flux basis slightly higher per cycle count, lower flux ≈ neutral Properly 60 Hz-designed equipment has the same efficiency class; only cross-frequency misapplication causes loss problems.
Warning — nameplates decide, not assumptions. Much imported equipment is dual-rated "50/60 Hz", but not all of it, and dual-rated machines often carry two different output figures, one per frequency. On site, read the frequency line of every nameplate. Equipment carrying only "60 Hz" on a 60 Hz SEC plant (or vice versa) is an immediate red-line finding.

Motors: +20 % speed can mean +73 % power

An induction motor's speed is locked to frequency by the synchronous-speed formula n = 120 × f / p (p = number of poles). A 4-pole machine therefore runs at 1500 rpm synchronous on a 60 Hz grid and 1800 rpm on the 60 Hz SEC grid (actual shaft speed is 2–3 % lower because of slip — typically ~1470 rpm vs ~1775 rpm). The motor itself does not mind: a 60 Hz-wound motor is designed for that speed.

The trap is the driven machine. Centrifugal pumps and fans obey the affinity laws at fixed impeller/blade geometry:

  • Flow ∝ speed — at +20 % speed the pump moves +20 % flow;
  • Head (pressure) ∝ speed² — (1.2)² = 1.44, i.e. +44 % head;
  • Shaft power ∝ speed³ — (1.2)³ = 1.728, i.e. up to +73 % power.

Read that last line again, because it surprises everyone the first time: a "modest" 20 % speed increase nearly doubles the power a centrifugal load demands. (The exact increase depends on where the new operating point lands on the system curve — the cube law is the worst case at fixed system resistance — but +50 to +73 % is realistic.) This is why a pump set engineered for a 60 Hz country can never simply be shipped to a 60 Hz site: either the impeller is trimmed for the higher speed, a different (larger) motor is fitted, or a VFD is installed to run the pump back down to its design speed.

Worked comparison — the same pump at 60 Hz and 60 Hz

A centrifugal pump absorbs 70 kW at 1470 rpm on a 60 Hz grid, driven by a 75 kW 4-pole motor. The skid is relocated to a 60 Hz plant without modification. Estimate the new shaft power.

New speed: n₂ ≈ n₁ × (60/50) = 1470 × 1.2 ≈ 1764 rpm Affinity (power): P₂ = P₁ × (n₂/n₁)³ = 70 × (1.2)³ = 70 × 1.728 ≈ 121 kW

The 75 kW motor is now asked for ≈121 kW — an overload of about 61 %:

Loading = 121 / 75 ≈ 161 % of motor rating

Result: the thermal overload relay trips within minutes — or, if someone has "solved" the nuisance tripping by raising the overload setting, the winding insulation cooks instead. Sanity check: flow is +20 % and head +44 %, so the process side also receives far more than design duty — the hydraulic numbers confirm the electrical ones. All values indicative.

Tip — what to look for on site: on any pump or fan skid, compare the motor nameplate frequency and speed (e.g. "60 Hz / 1775 rpm") against the driven-machine data plate. A 60 Hz pump curve pinned in the file with a 60 Hz motor on the skid is a finding even if nothing has tripped yet — it usually means the margins have been silently consumed.

Transformers: flux, and the one-way compatibility rule

A transformer core's peak magnetic flux is set by the voltage-per-cycle it must absorb: Φ_max = V / (4.44 × f × N) (N = turns). Voltage in the numerator, frequency in the denominator. Hold the voltage constant and raise the frequency, and the flux falls; lower the frequency and the flux rises. This gives a simple, asymmetric compatibility rule:

  • 60 Hz-designed unit operated at 60 Hz, same voltage: flux drops to 50/60 ≈ 83 % of design — the core runs further from saturation. Magnetically safe (verify accessories: fans, tap-changer motors, instrumentation rated for 60 Hz).
  • 60 Hz-designed unit operated at 60 Hz, same voltage: flux rises to 60/50 = 120 % of design. Transformer cores are deliberately designed close to the knee of the magnetising curve to save iron, so +20 % flux drives the core into saturation: the magnetising current jumps from ~1 % to tens of percent, it becomes sharply distorted, the core overheats and hums loudly, and the unit can fail in hours. A 60 Hz-designed transformer must never be used on a 60 Hz system at the same voltage.
Warning — second-hand and relocated equipment. The dangerous direction (60 Hz design → 60 Hz grid) is exactly the direction equipment travels when a Saudi plant is decommissioned and its transformers are resold into 60 Hz markets — or when a "spare" sourced from a 60 Hz fleet is fitted in a 60 Hz sister plant. Within the Kingdom you are safe; across borders, the nameplate frequency is a hard constraint. For our plant, TX-1 / TX-2 are specified as 60 Hz units (13.8/0.4 kV, Dyn11, Z = 6 %) per the transformer catalogue.

Reactance, capacitors and the detuned PFC bank

Inductive reactance is directly proportional to frequency — X = 2πfL — so every reactance expressed in ohms is 20 % higher at 60 Hz for the same physical inductance: cable reactance, current-limiting reactors, transformer leakage reactance in ohms. (Percent impedances quoted on nameplates, like the Z = 6 % of TX-1/TX-2, are already stated on the equipment's own rated-frequency base and carry through unchanged into per-unit fault calculations.) Capacitors mirror this: reactive output Q = 2πfCV² is +20 % at 60 Hz for the same capacitance and voltage, and so is the capacitor current.

Where both effects meet is the detuned power-factor-correction bank (PFC, ≈580 kvar indicative). Each capacitor step has a series reactor sized at 7 % of the capacitor reactance, creating a series-resonant circuit tuned below the lowest characteristic harmonic so the bank cannot resonate with VFD harmonics:

f_res = f_supply / √0.07 → 60 Hz system: 189 Hz | 60 Hz system: 227 Hz

On the 60 Hz SEC grid the tuning point is ≈227 Hz — comfortably below the 5th harmonic at 300 Hz, exactly as 189 Hz sits below 260 Hz on a 60 Hz grid. But this only works when the reactor and capacitors are engineered as a 60 Hz set: pairing a 60 Hz reactor with 60 Hz capacitors (or vice versa) shifts the tuning point unpredictably and can park it near a real harmonic, turning the protection device into an amplifier. Order PFC equipment explicitly for 13.8 kV-network/400 V, 60 Hz service and check the step nameplates on site.

Why Saudi Arabia is a 60 Hz country

The Kingdom's 60 Hz standard is a legacy of who built the first big power systems. Electrification at scale began in the Eastern Province with the oil industry from the late 1930s onward, engineered to North American practice — 60 Hz. As regional utilities grew and were later consolidated into SEC, the Kingdom standardised nationally on 60 Hz with 230/400 V utilisation (the voltage was unified by royal decision in the 1990s, with conversion of older 127/220 V areas phased over many years). This makes Saudi Arabia — together with North America, parts of Japan and a few other states — one of the minority of 60 Hz countries, and notably different from the rest of the GCC, which is 60 Hz. That is why the GCC Interconnection grid ties into the Saudi system through back-to-back HVDC converters (at Al Fadhili): direct AC interconnection between a 60 Hz and a 60 Hz system is impossible. All historical details indicative — interesting context, not assessment input.

Frequency checks for the site visit

  • Confirm the plant supply frequency on the intake metering display reads 60 Hz (SEC nominal).
  • Check the frequency line on every motor nameplate: 60 Hz, or dual 50/60 Hz with a separate 60 Hz rating column.
  • For each pump/fan skid, verify motor speed (≈1800 rpm 4-pole) matches the driven-machine data plate and curve.
  • Verify transformer nameplates (TX-1, TX-2) state 60 Hz; flag any 60 Hz-only or relocated unit.
  • Confirm the PFC bank steps and detuning reactors are nameplated for 400 V / 60 Hz (tuning ≈227 Hz, 7 %).
  • Check VFD, UPS and protection-relay settings files use the 60 Hz base and 60 Hz harmonic ladder (300/420 Hz…).
  • Flag any imported spare (motor, transformer, capacitor can) carrying only a 60 Hz rating as a red-line finding.
Key takeaway: 60 Hz changes four numbers by exactly +20 % — motor speed, inductive reactance, capacitor kvar, and the harmonic ladder — and one number by up to +73 %: centrifugal shaft power, via the cube law. Transformer flux moves the other way (safe at 60 Hz for a 60 Hz design; dangerous in reverse). Check the frequency line on every nameplate; on a SEC plant it must say 60 Hz or 50/60 Hz with a 60 Hz rating column.

The SEC Network Upstream

Where you are in the workflow: your assessment boundary starts at the 13.8 kV intake, but the design numbers at that boundary — voltage, fault level, tariff, reliability — are all dictated by what the Saudi Electricity Company (SEC) operates upstream. This section explains where the power comes from, so the numbers in the connection agreement make sense.

Where the energy comes from — generation mix

The Saudi grid is supplied predominantly by thermal generation burning natural gas and liquid fuels — gas turbines, combined-cycle blocks and steam plant, much of it co-located with desalination on the coasts — with a rapidly growing fleet of utility-scale solar PV and the first large wind farms. National programmes target a far higher renewable share (an indicative aspiration of ~50 % of generation) by 2030, with gas covering most of the remainder as liquid fuels are displaced. All figures indicative — the mix changes year on year and does not alter how you assess the plant.

What the mix does change is grid behaviour at the connection point. Synchronous thermal machines provide the rotating inertia that holds 60 Hz steady and the fault current your protection relies on; inverter-based solar provides neither in the same way. As the mix shifts, utilities tighten grid codes — ride-through requirements, power-factor windows, harmonic limits — which is why this manual cites the Saudi Grid Code and SEC Distribution Code alongside SASO-adopted IEC standards, and why any on-site generation or large drive installation needs checking against the current code revision.

The voltage ladder — from 380 kV to your motor terminals

Energy reaches the plant by stepping down a voltage ladder. Each rung exists for one reason: moving the same power at higher voltage means lower current, and losses fall with the square of current — so bulk energy travels far at very high voltage and is only reduced close to the consumer. The indicative Saudi ladder:

Level (indicative) Role Typical equipment What our plant sees
380 kV EHV national transmission backbone — bulk transfer between generation centres, regions and the GCC interconnection Overhead lines, EHV substations, series/shunt compensation, HVDC converter stations (GCC tie) Nothing directly — but a 380 kV event can ripple down as a region-wide voltage dip
132 kV / 115 kV (110 kV in some areas) HV sub-transmission — feeds bulk supply points around cities and industrial areas (115 kV is the Eastern-Province heritage level; 132 kV elsewhere) HV substations, 132(115)/33 kV or 132(115)/13.8 kV power transformers, GIS in urban areas Our primary substation hangs from this layer; an outage here takes out multiple feeders at once
33 kV Primary distribution in some networks; direct intake voltage for very large industrial customers (above roughly 10–20 MVA demand) 33 kV switchgear, 33/13.8 kV transformers Not used at our ~2 MW size — but you will meet 33 kV intakes on larger plants you assess
13.8 kV MV distribution — the standard SEC feeder voltage for industrial and commercial customers in our demand class Distribution feeders (cable or overhead), ring main units, the customer intake: MV-MET, MV-SWGR This is our connection point — the top of the master SLD
400 / 230 V, 60 Hz LV utilisation — three-phase 400 V between phases, 230 V phase-to-neutral TX-1/TX-2 secondaries, LV-MSB, MCCs, boards, loads Everything below the transformers — created and owned by the plant itself
Note — whose equipment is whose. SEC owns and operates everything down to (typically) the revenue metering point MV-MET; the plant owns everything from the MV switchgear inward. The exact ownership/maintenance boundary is defined in the connection agreement — locate it during the desk study, because it decides which equipment you may assess hands-on and which you may only observe.

The 13.8 kV feeder(s) to our plant

From the primary substation, the plant is served by one or more 13.8 kV feeders — usually XLPE underground cable in industrial cities, sometimes overhead line in remote areas. Three arrangements matter for your reliability assessment:

  • Single radial feeder: one cable, one path. Cheapest, and a plain single point of failure at the utility level — any feeder fault is a full plant outage for the duration of the repair or switching time.
  • Ring / loop connection (RMU): the plant tees into a ring that can be fed from either direction. A cable fault is isolated by switching and supply is restored in minutes-to-an-hour rather than repair time. Common in SEC industrial-city networks.
  • Dual dedicated feeders: two independent feeders onto the two sections of MV-SWGR with the bus-section breaker [52-BS] — the premium arrangement, giving genuine utility-side N-1. Reserved for larger or more critical plants.

The reference plant assumes a single utility intake (with the [52-BS] giving internal sectionalising only) — which is precisely why the standby path (DG → ATS → EDB) exists. On site, establish which arrangement you actually have: it is the first row of the utility-side SPOF table in Part 3.

The declared fault level: 25–40 kA, design for 25 kA minimum

When SEC connects a customer at 13.8 kV it declares a design fault level at the point of connection — the prospective short-circuit current the customer's equipment must withstand and interrupt. For SEC 13.8 kV networks this declaration falls in the range 25–40 kA (indicative; the connection agreement states the binding figure). Two things about this number routinely confuse newcomers:

  • It is a planning ceiling, not today's measurement. The actual fault current available today might be 12 or 15 kA. But the utility will add transformers, parallel feeders and generation over the equipment's 30-year life, and every addition raises the fault level. The declared value is the level the network is allowed to grow to without the customer having to replace switchgear.
  • The customer must design to at least the declared minimum — 25 kA — even if local measurements suggest less. Under-rated switchgear that "has always been fine" is a latent bomb: the first fault after a network reinforcement can exceed its interrupting capacity, and a breaker that fails to clear a 13.8 kV fault fails catastrophically.

This is why the reference MV-SWGR is specified at 31.5 kA / 3 s (17.5 kV class): it covers the 25 kA SEC design fault level with margin inside the declared range, at the standard IEC rating step above it. See the MV switchgear catalogue for typical product families, and Part 2 for how the MV fault level cascades into the LV figures (38.5 kA at one transformer, ≈77 kA prospective tie-closed — all indicative).

Warning — never infer the fault level from old single-line diagrams. SLDs show what was declared at design time, decades ago. Always obtain the current declared fault level in writing from SEC (or from the latest connection-agreement revision) before judging switchgear adequacy. A plant connected in 1995 against 20 kA may now sit on a 31.5 kA network.

Metering and tariff basics — why MV-MET matters commercially

The revenue meter in MV-MET is the commercial boundary: everything the plant consumes — including the losses inside TX-1/TX-2, since metering is at MV — is billed by SEC against it. The elements of an industrial bill, indicatively:

  • Energy charge (SAR/kWh): the bulk of the bill, at the industrial tariff category rate. For larger customers SEC applies time-of-use (TOU) structures — higher rates in the summer afternoon system peak (air-conditioning driven), lower off-peak. Indicative: shifting deferrable load (pumping to storage, batch processes) out of the peak window is often the cheapest energy saving available.
  • Demand-related charges: linked to the contracted/recorded maximum demand (kVA or kW). Exceeding contracted capacity triggers penalties and may require a formal connection upgrade.
  • Power-factor clause: SEC requires industrial customers to maintain power factor above a threshold (indicatively 0.9 lagging at the metering point), with a surcharge proportional to the shortfall below it. This clause is the commercial justification for the PFC bank: correcting from 0.85 to a target of 0.95 keeps the plant clear of the penalty band with margin, and simultaneously frees transformer and cable capacity (≈580 kvar, indicative — sizing worked through in Part 2).
Tip — read twelve months of bills. During the desk study, request a year of SEC invoices. They reveal, at zero site effort: the real maximum demand vs the 2353 kVA design figure, the load's seasonality, whether power-factor surcharges are being paid (a failed or undersized PFC bank shows up in money before it shows up in measurements), and any demand-limit exceedances. Bills are the cheapest condition-monitoring data you will ever get.

Outage escalation — what failure upstream looks like

Utility-side disturbances arrive in escalating sizes, and the plant's defence ladder (Part 1, power-flow walk-through) is built to match:

  • Voltage dips and momentary interruptions (cycles to ~1 s): remote faults cleared by utility protection, auto-reclosing on overhead sections. The plant rides through on drive DC-bus inertia and the UPS; the classic casualty is contactor drop-out in the MCCs tripping motors that the process then needs restarted in sequence.
  • Sustained feeder outage (minutes to hours): cable fault, feeder breaker lockout. The ATS starts the DG and transfers the essential bus (≤ 10 s, indicative); non-essential load is shed by design. Ring-connected plants may be back-fed within the hour by SEC switching.
  • Primary substation / sub-transmission outage (hours): multiple feeders lost simultaneously; no back-feed available. The plant runs on the DG for the duration — which is why DG fuel autonomy (day-tank plus bulk storage) belongs in the assessment.
  • Regional / system event (rare, potentially long): grid-scale disturbance. The DG, the UPS (30 min, indicative) and the DCDB battery are the plant's entire world; orderly process shutdown is the success criterion.

Operationally, every plant should hold a current SEC escalation contact list (emergency/outage number, account manager for the connection agreement) and an outage log. The log matters technically as well as commercially: a pattern of dips clustered at certain hours often identifies a network cause SEC can fix — and it tells you which power-quality mitigations (ride-through relays, delayed-dropout contactors) would actually pay back.

Key takeaway: the plant hangs from a ladder — 380 kV backbone, 132/115 kV sub-transmission, 13.8 kV feeder — and the connection agreement compresses that whole upstream world into a few binding numbers: 13.8 kV, 60 Hz, a declared fault level of 25–40 kA (design to 25 kA minimum — our switchgear is 31.5 kA), a contracted demand, and a power-factor clause. Find the connection agreement and twelve months of bills in the desk study; they anchor half of your assessment. All values indicative.

Derating: the 50 °C Tax

Where you are in the workflow: every rating you will read on a nameplate or in a catalogue was promised at a reference temperature — usually 30 or 40 °C. Our design ambient is 50 °C. The gap between those two numbers is a tax levied on the capacity of nearly every item in the plant, and this section teaches you to compute it before Part 2 asks you to.

One physical idea behind every derating factor

All electrical equipment is ultimately limited by insulation temperature. A cable, a winding, a breaker contact or a capacitor can carry current only until its hottest point reaches the temperature its insulation system can survive for its design life — 90 °C for XLPE cable, 155 °C (class F) for typical motor and dry-type transformer insulation, and so on. The operating temperature is always the same sum:

hot-spot temperature = ambient temperature + temperature rise caused by load

The insulation limit is fixed by chemistry. So when the ambient term grows — from the 30–40 °C assumed by international standards to the 50 °C Saudi design ambient (see the environment section in Part 0) — the allowable rise, and therefore the allowable load, must shrink. That is the whole theory of derating. Each equipment class merely converts the lost rise into a capacity factor in its own way, which is why the factors below differ in size but never in direction. Two general rules before the specifics:

  • Derating compounds. A cable that loses 18 % to ambient and 20 % to grouping keeps 0.82 × 0.80 ≈ 66 % of its table rating — the factors multiply, they do not add.
  • The reference temperature is part of the rating. "400 A" means nothing until you know "400 A at what ambient". Two data sheets quoting the same current at different reference temperatures are quoting different cables.
Note — heat also arrives from outside. At 50 °C air temperature, direct solar gain on outdoor enclosures and cable trays adds further heating on top of ambient (sun-exposed steel surfaces can far exceed air temperature). Shading, ventilation and light-coloured enclosures are not cosmetic — they are part of the thermal design, alongside the dust/IP54 requirements of the same environment.

Cables: the ≈0.82 factor

IEC current-carrying-capacity tables (IEC 60364-5-52 / IEC 60287 basis) for cables in air are referenced to a 30 °C ambient. The correction factor for another ambient follows directly from the insulation-temperature logic above:

k = √( (θ_conductor − θ_ambient) / (θ_conductor − θ_reference) )

For 90 °C XLPE insulation in 50 °C air instead of 30 °C:

k = √( (90 − 50) / (90 − 30) ) = √(40/60) = √0.667 ≈ 0.82

So every XLPE cable in this plant delivers, as a rule of thumb, about 82 % of its table rating before any other factor is applied. PVC-insulated cable (70 °C conductor) fares worse — k ≈ √(20/40) ≈ 0.71 — one reason XLPE dominates in the Kingdom. Buried cables use a different reference (soil temperature, indicatively 25–35 °C here) and are further punished by dry desert sand, whose thermal resistivity can be several times the standard 2.5 K·m/W assumption; specify thermally stabilised backfill or derate hard. The full sizing procedure, with grouping and voltage-drop checks, is worked in Part 2 — cable sizing.

Worked example — the "400 A" cable that is really a 328 A cable

A single-core XLPE copper cable is tabulated at 400 A in free air (30 °C reference). It is installed on a cable ladder at the 50 °C design ambient. What may it actually carry?

I_z = I_table × k_ambient = 400 A × 0.82 = 328 A

If it also runs bunched with two other loaded circuits on the same ladder (grouping factor ≈ 0.80, indicative):

I_z = 400 × 0.82 × 0.80 ≈ 262 A

Result: the "400 A" cable is a ~328 A cable at 50 °C — and only a ~262 A cable when grouped. Sanity check: the protective device and the design current must both sit at or below the derated capacity (I_b ≤ I_n ≤ I_z = 328 A), not the table figure; a 400 A breaker on this cable at 50 °C would be a finding. All values indicative.

Transformers: insulation-class margin and the fan (AF) boost

IEC 60076 rates a transformer for an ambient of 40 °C maximum (30 °C monthly average, 20 °C yearly average). At a 50 °C site, the same logic as cables applies to the oil or winding temperature rise: roughly, each kelvin of ambient above the standard basis costs about 1 % of continuous loading capability (indicative), so a standard-design 1600 kVA unit would only be good for ~1440–1480 kVA here. Three standard remedies, usually combined at specification stage rather than bolted on later:

  • Specify the 50 °C ambient on the order — the manufacturer designs the unit with reduced guaranteed temperature rises so the nameplate kVA is honest at site conditions. This is the approach assumed for TX-1/TX-2 (1600 kVA each at 50 °C, indicative).
  • Insulation-class margin (dry-type): build with class F or H insulation (155 / 180 °C capability) but limit the design rise to a lower class's values — the unused thermal headroom absorbs the extra 10 K of ambient instead of being spent on load.
  • Forced-air (AF/ONAF) boost: fans on a cast-resin AN unit or radiator fans on an ONAN oil unit typically add +25–40 % capacity (indicative). Useful as contingency margin (e.g. for the bus-tie-closed, one-transformer-carries-all condition) — but note the fans, their supply and their controller become reliability items in their own right for the FMECA.
Tip — read the rise, not just the kVA. On the transformer nameplate, check the guaranteed temperature-rise figures and the stated design ambient. A unit stamped "1600 kVA, 40 °C ambient" installed at a 50 °C site is not a 1600 kVA unit here — that single line on the nameplate is a desk-study finding before you ever measure anything. Typical product families in the transformer catalogue.

LV breakers in boards: 0.8–0.9

An ACB or MCCB is rated (IEC 60947-2) at a reference air temperature — typically 40 °C around the device. Installed inside a Form 4b switchboard in a 50 °C switchroom, the air around the breaker is the room's 50 °C plus the board's internal rise (heat from busbars, other devices and the breaker itself), easily reaching 55–65 °C at the device. Manufacturers publish derating tables for exactly this; the working rule is that a breaker inside a board at our ambient delivers 80–90 % of its frame/trip rating (indicative). Consequences for the reference plant:

  • The LV-MSB incomer ACBs must carry the transformer LV FLC of 2309 A continuously. A 2500 A frame at a 0.85 in-board factor is only ~2125 A — not enough; the specification therefore moves to a 3200 A frame (3200 × 0.85 ≈ 2720 A ≥ 2309 A, indicative) or relies on the manufacturer's certified temperature-derating curve for the actual board. This is why the schedule shows a ~2500–3200 A bus.
  • Electronic trip units are set to the derated continuous current, and the board's ventilation (or air conditioning of the room) is part of the breaker's rating — a failed switchroom HVAC silently converts adequate breakers into overloaded ones.

Typical ACB/MCCB families and their published derating data are referenced in the LV breaker catalogue.

VFDs and UPS: 1–1.5 %/°C above 40 °C

Power-electronic equipment derates fastest, because its limiting components — IGBTs, DC-bus electrolytic capacitors — are the most temperature-sensitive items in the plant. Typical manufacturer rule for VFDs: full rating up to 40 °C, then 1–1.5 % output current per °C above, i.e. 10–15 % lost at 50 °C (indicative); many vendors simply cap operation at 50 °C entirely. UPS units behave similarly, and their batteries are a separate, harsher story (below). Practical responses: select drives one frame size up, and treat the switchroom air-conditioning as process-critical equipment — for drive rooms, the HVAC is the derating mitigation, and its failure mode belongs in the SPOF analysis of Part 3.

Diesel generators: 0.5–1 %/°C above 40 °C

Genset site rating (ISO 8528) falls with intake-air temperature — hot, thin air means less oxygen per stroke and weaker alternator cooling — by an indicative 0.5–1 % per °C above 40 °C, plus altitude derating where applicable. A set quoted "1250 kVA" at ISO standard conditions delivers perhaps 1130–1190 kVA at a 50 °C site. The reference DG avoids this trap by being specified as 1250 kVA ESP at 50 °C ambient — the derating is already inside the number. On site, always determine whether a genset nameplate states standard-condition or site-condition output, and check radiator sizing (a "tropical" high-ambient radiator is a distinct option). Typical sets in the genset catalogue.

Batteries: life halves every 8–10 °C

Batteries are the exception that proves the rule: heat barely reduces their capacity (it slightly increases it) — instead it destroys their life. Internal corrosion and grid degradation are chemical reactions that follow Arrhenius behaviour: reaction rate, and therefore ageing, roughly doubles for every 8–10 °C above the reference temperature (20–25 °C for VRLA). Indicatively, a "10-year design life" VRLA block achieves:

  • ~10 years at 20–25 °C (air-conditioned battery room),
  • ~5 years at ~30–33 °C,
  • ~2–3 years at ~40 °C,
  • little more than a year in an unconditioned 50 °C enclosure.

This applies equally to the UPS battery (the 30 min autonomy bridging the DG start — see the power-flow walk-through) and the DCDB battery (the last line of MV protection operability). A dead battery room air-conditioner is therefore a hidden plant-level SPOF: nothing trips, nothing alarms, the batteries simply age out years early and fail on the first real outage. On site: check the battery room temperature log, the A/C condition, and the date and result of the last discharge test. Typical UPS systems in the UPS catalogue.

The 50 °C tax — summary table

Equipment Rating reference Indicative factor / penalty at 50 °C Standard basis Field check
XLPE cables in air 30 °C air × ≈0.82 (PVC ≈0.71); grouping factors multiply on top IEC 60364-5-52 / IEC 60287 Cable schedule uses 50 °C column; breaker rating ≤ derated I_z
Buried cables Soil 20–25 °C, ρ = 2.5 K·m/W Soil temp + dry-sand resistivity penalties; site-specific IEC 60287 Backfill specification; thermal resistivity assumption in calcs
Transformers (TX-1/TX-2) 40 °C max ambient ≈ −1 %/K above basis if standard design; zero if ordered for 50 °C; AF fans recover +25–40 % IEC 60076 Nameplate design ambient and rise figures; fan operation
LV breakers in boards (ACB/MCCB) 40 °C around device, free air × 0.8–0.9 installed in board at 50 °C room IEC 60947-2 + manufacturer tables Frame size vs continuous load (incomers ≥ 2309 A duty); room HVAC
VFDs / UPS electronics 40 °C −1 to −1.5 %/°C above 40 °C → −10–15 % at 50 °C IEC 61800 / IEC 62040 + manufacturer Drive frame margin; drive-room A/C status and alarms
Diesel generator (DG) ISO standard conditions (≈25–40 °C basis) −0.5 to −1 %/°C above 40 °C unless site-rated (ours: 1250 kVA ESP at 50 °C) ISO 8528 Nameplate: standard vs site rating; tropical radiator fitted
Batteries (UPS, DCDB) 20–25 °C Service life halves per 8–10 °C above reference (capacity barely affected) IEC 60896 / IEC 62485 Battery room temperature, A/C condition, last discharge-test record
Warning — the meta-SPOF of a hot-climate plant is the HVAC. Almost every factor in this table silently assumes a working air-conditioning or ventilation system: switchroom, drive room, battery room. At 50 °C ambient, cooling-plant failure does not announce itself as an electrical fault — it quietly removes the margins that every rating in the plant depends on. Treat switchroom and battery-room HVAC as electrical-reliability equipment in the Part 3 SPOF analysis, not as a building service.

50 °C field checklist

  • Record switchroom, drive-room and battery-room temperatures; compare against the design assumptions.
  • Verify the cable schedule states its ambient basis and that 50 °C correction (≈0.82) plus grouping factors were applied.
  • Check transformer nameplates for the stated design ambient and guaranteed temperature rises; confirm AF fans (if fitted) run on demand.
  • Compare LV-MSB incomer ACB frame/trip settings against the 2309 A continuous duty with the in-board derating (0.8–0.9) applied.
  • Confirm VFD and UPS room A/C is duty/standby or alarmed; note any drive already running above 40 °C cabinet inlet.
  • Check whether the DG nameplate output is ISO standard-condition or 50 °C site-rated, and whether a high-ambient radiator is fitted.
  • Obtain the battery-room temperature log and the date/result of the last UPS and DCDB battery discharge tests.
  • Inspect outdoor enclosures and cable trays for direct solar exposure; note missing sunshades on sun-facing equipment.
Key takeaway: every rating is a promise made at a reference temperature, and at the 50 °C Saudi design ambient (see Part 0 — environment) that promise is taxed: cables × 0.82, breakers-in-boards × 0.8–0.9, drives and UPS −10–15 %, gensets −5–10 %, transformers fixed only if ordered for 50 °C, and battery life halved per 8–10 °C. The factors multiply, the deratings are invisible on the SLD, and the air-conditioning that holds them all together is itself a single point of failure. All values indicative; manufacturer data and detailed studies govern.

Part 2 — Calculations

Where you are in the workflow: you have identified the equipment and read the SLD (Part 1); now you run the numbers that tell you whether the existing equipment is correctly rated before you write a single recommendation.

Assessment calculations are verification, not green-field design. Your job in the field is to ask: "Is this transformer / breaker / cable / genset still adequate for what the plant is actually doing today, in this climate, on this grid?" — and to back the answer with arithmetic, not opinion.

Why Calculations Matter in an Assessment

Every rating you see on the SLD — a transformer kVA, a breaker breaking capacity, a busbar bracing figure, a cable cross-section, a capacitor bank, a genset — is the output of a calculation somebody performed years ago. That calculation is the audit trail that justifies the rating. In an assessment you work in the opposite direction: you start from the nameplate, reverse-engineer the assumption that was made, and check whether reality still matches it.

Calculations are not a one-time design activity. They appear at four distinct moments in the life of a plant, and a field engineer must recognise which moment they are standing in:

Moment in plant lifeQuestion the calculation answersTypical trigger
1. Design "What rating do we buy?" — sizing from a load list that does not yet exist as physical equipment. New plant, major expansion. Owned by the design consultant; produces the original studies.
2. Verification (this manual's focus) "Is what was bought still adequate for what the plant actually does?" — the same arithmetic run backwards against nameplates and meter readings. Condition assessment, due diligence, insurance survey, SPOF/FMECA study.
3. Modification "Can we add this 250 kW compressor without re-equipping the substation?" — delta calculations layered on the as-found base case. Debottlenecking, new packages, PFC retrofit, DG addition. Requires the management-of-change file to carry the revised studies.
4. Incident investigation "Why did the breaker explode / the cable burn / the relay not trip?" — forensic re-calculation of fault duty, loading and settings as they were at the moment of failure. After a trip, fire, arc-flash event or unexplained outage. The most unforgiving audience your arithmetic will ever have.

The two as-found failure modes you are hunting in moment 2:

  • Under-rating — cables and busbars that overheat (fire risk), switchgear that cannot interrupt a fault (catastrophic failure — see short-circuit withstand), protection that mis-coordinates or nuisance-trips (coordination), motors that stall, process voltage too low to run.
  • Over-rating — capital tied up in oversized transformers, switchgear frames, cables and gensets the plant will never use; this matters most when the client is deciding whether to add load or replace equipment.

The Saudi (SEC) Basis — What Changes and Why It Matters

This Rev 3 edition localises every calculation to a plant connected to the Saudi Electricity Company (SEC) distribution network. Three things change relative to the generic IEC textbook case, and each one flows through the arithmetic of every later section:

  • Voltage levels: 13.8 kV MV, 400/230 V LV. SEC's standard distribution voltage is 13.8 kV (a North-American-heritage level adopted across the Kingdom), not the 13.8 kV common in 60 Hz countries. The LV utilisation voltage is 400/230 V three-phase TN-S. Both levels appear inside every √3 × V denominator in Part 2 — get the voltage wrong and every current is wrong by 20–25 %.
  • Frequency: 60 Hz. The Saudi grid runs at 60 Hz. Consequences: a 4-pole motor runs at 1800 rpm (not 1500), reactances are 20 % higher for the same inductance (X = 2πfL), and imported 60 Hz equipment must be explicitly verified for 60 Hz duty. Frequency does not appear in the FLC formula — but it silently sits inside every impedance, every motor speed and every capacitor rating.
  • Climate: 50 °C design ambient, dust and sand. The "derating culture" is the defining habit of good Saudi electrical engineering. Cable tables, busbar ratings, transformer ONAN ratings and DG outputs are all published at 30–40 °C reference ambients; at a 50 °C design ambient an XLPE cable in air keeps only about 0.82 of its tabulated ampacity, and a diesel genset is bought against its ESP rating at 50 °C, not its 25 °C brochure figure. Enclosures outdoors are minimum IP54 against dust. Any rating you read off a generic table and do not derate is a finding waiting to happen.
Standards basis (cite together): SASO-adopted IEC standards (Saudi Standards, Metrology and Quality Organization adopts IEC with national deviations), the SEC Distribution Code and Saudi Grid Code for connection, power factor and fault-level requirements, and SBC 401 (Saudi Building Code — Electrical) for installation practice. Where this manual cites "IEC 60909" or "IEC 60364", read the SASO-adopted edition.

The indicative Rev 3 design basis used throughout Part 2:

ParameterIndicative valueSource / derivation
Plant maximum demand~2.0 MWBasis of design (load estimate)
Power factor (uncorrected)0.85Basis of design; PFC target 0.95 (PFC sizing)
Apparent power~2 353 kVA2 000 kW / 0.85
MV supply13.8 kV, 60 Hz, 3-ph (SEC)SEC Distribution Code; switchgear 17.5 kV class
Utility fault level25 kA design (SEC range 25–40 kA)Confirm with SEC connection agreement
Transformers2 × 1600 kVA, 13.8/0.4 kV, Dyn11, Z ≈ 6 %Basis of design (N-1 check)
LV system400/230 V, 60 Hz, TN-SSBC 401 / SASO-IEC 60364
LV FLC per transformer≈ 2 309 A§c-flc
MV FLC per transformer≈ 66.9 A§c-flc
Total LV load current≈ 3 396 A at 400 V§c-flc
LV prospective Isc (single tx)≈ 38.5 kA (tie closed ≈ 77 kA)§c-perunit / c-shortcircuit
Design ambient50 °C, dust/sand (min IP54 outdoor)Site climate basis

The master SLD is embedded here so every sub-section below can refer back to the equipment being sized:

Drawing basis note. This diagram was drawn for the original 13.8 kV / 400 V / 60 Hz reference design and has not been re-rendered. For this Saudi (SEC) Rev 3 edition read 13.8 kV as 13.8 kV, 400 V as 400 V and 60 Hz as 60 Hz. Topology, tags and protection philosophy are unchanged.
Constants used throughout Part 2: sqrt(3) = 1.732  |  LV line voltage V = 400 V  |  MV line voltage = 13.8 kV  |  60 Hz  |  50 °C design ambient. Every numeric result is indicative and tentative — it illustrates method and typical practice, not a substitute for project-specific load lists, short-circuit / load-flow studies, and the governing standards (SASO-adopted IEC, SEC Distribution Code / Saudi Grid Code, SBC 401).
If you master only four, master these: on most assessments, four calculations carry the most evidential weight: (1) Transformer sizing / N-1 check (§2) — confirms the fundamental redundancy claim; (2) Full-load current (FLC) (§3) — the basis for every breaker, cable and relay sizing check; (3) Short-circuit level (§4, with the per-unit machinery in §15) — verifies that switchgear can interrupt faults safely; (4) Protection coordination (§10) — confirms that faults isolate the minimum zone.
  • Confirm the SEC connection voltage on the supply agreement: 13.8 kV, 60 Hz (do not assume from drawings drawn on another basis).
  • Obtain the SEC-declared fault level at the point of connection (design value here: 25 kA; SEC range 25–40 kA).
  • Record the site design ambient used in the original studies; flag any rating table referenced at 30–40 °C without a 50 °C correction.
  • Locate the original design calculations / studies; their absence is itself a documentation finding.
  • Identify which "moment" you are in (design / verification / modification / incident) and scope the calculation set accordingly.

1 — Load & Demand Estimate Core

Goal: confirm the ~2 MW plant demand and convert it to apparent power (kVA) — the anchor point for every sizing calculation that follows.

From Load List to Maximum Demand

Why this exists

No plant ever draws the sum of its nameplates. Motors are oversized for their pumps, standby drives sit idle, heaters cycle, and not everything runs at once. The load estimate exists to bridge the gap between two very different numbers: the connected load (sum of every nameplate — here ≈ 2.7 MW) and the maximum demand (the highest simultaneous draw the supply must actually deliver — here ≈ 2.0 MW). The bridge is built from two factors applied per feeder or per group:

  • Demand factor — the ratio of a group's actual peak draw to its connected nameplate total (captures partial loading and idle standby units).
  • Diversity factor — recognition that different groups do not peak at the same instant, so the system peak is less than the sum of group peaks.

Everything downstream hangs off the result: transformer kVA (§2), busbar and breaker frames via FLC (§3), cable sizes (§6), the PFC bank (§7) and the SEC connection capacity itself. The SEC Distribution Code requires a declared maximum demand at the point of connection — this calculation is where that declaration comes from.

What goes wrong without it

Two opposite disasters, both common:

  • Sizing on nameplates ("everything on, all the time"): the plant buys 2 × 2000 kVA transformers, a 3200 A switchboard and an SEC connection it never uses. Capital is wasted, transformers idle at 40 % loading for twenty years burning no-load losses, and the over-stiff system masks creeping load growth until it surprises someone.
  • Sizing on optimism (or never re-checking): the original 2005 load list said 1.6 MW. Fifteen years of "small" additions — a 90 kW air compressor here, a chiller there — and the true peak is now 2.3 MW. Nobody re-ran the estimate. The first symptom is a transformer winding-temperature alarm during the August peak, at 50 °C ambient, when cooling margin is already at its thinnest. The second symptom is an incomer trip during the most profitable production week of the year.

In the Saudi climate the second failure mode has a vicious twist: plant demand peaks (HVAC and chilled-water load) at exactly the moment equipment capability is at its lowest (50 °C ambient derating). The load estimate and the derating culture meet head-on in mid-August.

Feel for the numbers

Anchors for 2.0 MW, computed honestly:

  • ≈ 900 household kettles boiling at once (2 000 kW / 2.2 kW per kettle = 909).
  • ≈ 2 700 horsepower (2 000 kW / 0.746 kW per hp = 2 681 hp) — roughly twenty family cars at full throttle, running continuously.
  • ≈ 130 large Saudi villas at summer peak (taking ≈ 15 kW per villa with every AC unit running: 2 000 / 15 ≈ 133).
  • Energy per day at peak: 2 MW × 24 h = 48 MWh/day — at an indicative industrial tariff of 0.18 SAR/kWh that is ≈ 8 600 SAR per day, ≈ 3.2 million SAR per year. Demand estimation errors are measured in millions of riyals, in both capex and opex directions.

Worked calculation

Worked example — Load table for the ~2 MW plant (indicative)

Step 1 — Build the load table (group connected loads by feeder, apply a demand factor per group):

Feeder / group Connected (kW) Demand factor Demand (kW) Notes
MCC-A — process motors (train A)9500.75712Includes idle standby pumps
MCC-B — process motors (train B)7500.70525Batch duty, cycling
HVAC & chilled water4500.80360Peaks in summer — coincident with 50 °C ambient
Compressed air2500.85212Lead/lag compressors
Lighting & small power1600.70112230 V single-phase, spread across phases
Water / utility pumps900.7063Intermittent
UPS, control & comms500.8040Via UPS-100 kVA
Totals2 7000.75 overall2 025≈ 2.0 MW maximum demand

Step 2 — Active demand:

P_demand = SUM(connected × demand factor) = 2 025 kW ≈ 2.0 MW

(Equivalently: 2 700 kW × 0.75 overall = 2 025 kW.)

Step 3 — Apparent power at pf 0.85 (uncorrected):

S = P / pf = 2 000 kW / 0.85 = 2 353 kVA

Step 4 — Total LV current at 400 V (preview of §3):

I = S / (sqrt(3) × V) = 2 352 941 VA / (1.732 × 400 V) = 3 396 A

Step 5 — Installed transformer base (margin for growth and N-1):

S_installed = 2 353 kVA × 1.20 to 1.25 ≈ 2 800 – 2 950 kVA

This drives the 2 × 1600 kVA selection (3 200 kVA installed) — verified for normal and N-1 loading in §2.

Sanity check: overall demand factor 2 025 / 2 700 = 0.75 — within the typical 0.7–0.8 band for a continuous process plant. Each transformer carries 2 353 / 2 = 1 176 kVA ≈ 73.5 % of nameplate in normal split operation — comfortable. All values indicative.

Equipment settings

A load estimate has no relay pickup of its own, but it sets numbers on other people's equipment, and you should verify each one against the table above:

  • Maximum-demand declaration in the SEC connection agreement (and the tariff's demand charge) — should match the calculated ≈ 2.0 MW / 2 353 kVA within reason.
  • Demand alarm thresholds in the power-management system (PMS) — typically alarm at 90 % of declared demand.
  • Load-shedding priority table — the essential / non-essential split used in the N-1 scheme (§2) and DG sizing is drawn directly from this load table's rows.
  • Incomer trip-unit long-time setting (Ir) — must sit above the per-transformer share of this demand (breaker deep-dive).

Equipment technology

The data behind a believable load estimate comes from hardware:

  • Tariff metering at the SEC intake — class 0.2S/0.5S CT-operated meters recording kW, kvar and maximum demand (typically 15- or 30-minute integrating windows). The MD register is the single most trustworthy demand number on site.
  • Multifunction power meters on the LV-MSB incomers and major feeders — modern digital meters log demand profiles to memory or to the PMS.
  • MCC ammeters and motor protection relays — per-feeder currents for building a bottom-up profile where no meter exists.
  • Portable power-quality analysers — a 7-day logging campaign on the incomers is the standard assessment move when historical data is missing.

Control

In a well-instrumented plant the PMS/SCADA continuously trends kW, kvar and kVA per incomer and per major feeder, time-stamped, with the demand window aligned to the tariff meter. The assessment question is whether anyone looks at it: ask for the 12-month demand trend and the all-time peak. If the PMS exists but trends are not archived, raise it — demand growth is invisible without a trend, and the load-shedding controller (which acts on these same measurements) cannot be validated either.

Sizing method

  1. Obtain (or build) the load list: every feeder with nameplate kW, duty (continuous / intermittent / standby), and pf.
  2. Group by MCC / distribution board; apply a demand factor per group from duty knowledge (continuous process 0.7–0.9; intermittent 0.3–0.6; standby 0).
  3. Apply diversity between groups if their peaks demonstrably do not coincide (be conservative: in a Saudi summer, HVAC and process peaks usually do coincide).
  4. Sum to maximum demand P (kW); divide by pf for S (kVA); add 20–25 % growth margin for the installed transformer base.
  5. Cross-check top-down against 12 months of tariff-meter MD readings — the bottom-up table and the meter should agree within ≈ 10 %; investigate any larger gap before using either number.
Field tip — what to capture: obtain the latest load list or motor schedule from the client. If none exists, read each MCC ammeter at peak shift and back-calculate kW per feeder as sqrt(3) × V × I × pf. Sum the branch demands to build your own diversity estimate, then compare with the SEC tariff meter's maximum-demand register — the cheapest cross-check on site.

Assessment interpretation

If as-found…Likely meaningAction
Demand > transformer capacity Plant has grown since original design Flag overload risk; recommend load-flow study (§9)
Demand < 50 % capacity for years Oversized original design or load reduction Check transformer no-load losses; note in report
No load list available Documentation gap Raise as finding; capture ammeter readings and meter MD register
Summer MD ≫ winter MD (> 25 % seasonal swing) HVAC-dominated profile — peak coincides with 50 °C derating window Verify all thermal checks at the summer-peak / 50 °C corner, not the annual average
Tariff MD and bottom-up table disagree > 10 % Stale load list, metering error, or unmetered additions Walk down feeders; reconcile before relying on either number
  • Obtain the load list / motor schedule and its revision date.
  • Record 12 months of SEC tariff maximum-demand readings.
  • Read all MCC and incomer ammeters at peak shift; note time and ambient.
  • Compare bottom-up demand table with tariff MD; reconcile gaps > 10 %.
  • Confirm the declared maximum demand in the SEC connection agreement.
  • Confirm PMS demand trends are archived and the all-time peak is known.
  • Note any load added since the last study revision (management-of-change file).
The demand estimate sets the size of the entire electrical plant: ≈ 2.0 MW / 0.85 pf = 2 353 kVA, ≈ 3 396 A at 400 V. Every other calculation in Part 2 is downstream of this number. If the load list is wrong or absent, all downstream ratings are unverifiable. All values indicative.

3 — Full-Load Current (LV & MV) Core

Goal: convert every kVA rating on the SLD into the ampere figure the conductors actually carry — the 400 V full-load current (FLC) of a 1600 kVA transformer, its 13.8 kV primary current, and the total plant LV current — because amperes, not kVA, are what breakers, busbars, cables and CTs are rated in.

From kVA to Amperes

Why this exists

Power equipment is bought in kVA and MW, but it fails in amperes. Heat in a conductor is I²R; mechanical force between busbars is proportional to I²; a breaker contact welds or survives according to the current through it. The full-load current calculation is the universal translator between the power world of the load estimate (§1) and the current world of every physical conductor on site. It is the single most-used formula in this manual:

I = S / (sqrt(3) × V) [A, with S in VA and V in volts, line-to-line]

The sqrt(3) appears because in a balanced three-phase system the power is shared across three phases whose line voltage is sqrt(3) times the phase voltage. You do not need to re-derive it in the field; you do need to never forget it — omitting the 1.732 makes every answer 73 % too high.

FLC matters twice over on this plant because the same kVA produces two wildly different currents on the two sides of each transformer: at 13.8 kV a 1600 kVA unit draws a modest 66.9 A; at 400 V the same power becomes 2 309 A — a 34.5 : 1 ratio (13 800 / 400), which is exactly the turns ratio. This is why MV switchgear (catalogue) is physically small and LV switchboards are rooms full of copper.

What goes wrong without it

A real-world composite, seen on more than one assessment: a plant adds load over the years and the LV incomer trip unit starts nuisance-tripping in summer. The electrician's "fix" is to raise the long-time pickup Ir — first to 100 % of the 2500 A sensor rating, then someone defeats it further. Nobody recalculates FLC. The transformer is now permitted to run above its 2 309 A rating indefinitely; at 50 °C ambient its insulation life halves for roughly every 6–8 K of extra winding temperature. Two summers later the winding-temperature trip operates during the August peak — or worse, the unit fails internally and the plant discovers what an unplanned 12-week transformer lead time costs.

The mirror-image error sits on the MV side: a CT ratio chosen without an FLC calculation. A 200/1 CT on a feeder that carries 66.9 A runs at 33 % of rated primary current — accuracy and relay sensitivity suffer, and the earth-fault pickup, set as a percentage of CT primary, may be too coarse to detect a high-resistance fault. Wrong FLC → wrong CT → blind protection (§10).

Feel for the numbers

  • 2 309 A is an enormous current by everyday standards — about 175 domestic 13 A socket circuits drawing flat-out simultaneously, flowing in each of three phases. At 400 V it is carried by parallel busbars roughly the cross-section of a hardcover book per phase.
  • 66.9 A on the MV side is, by contrast, less current than a domestic cooker circuit carries — but at 13.8 kV. Same power, 34.5 times the voltage, 1/34.5 of the current. High voltage exists precisely to make current (and therefore copper and I²R loss) small.
  • Heat check: the LV incomer connection has resistance of the order of tens of micro-ohms. Even at 20 µΩ, 2 309 A produces I²R ≈ 2 309² × 20e-6 ≈ 107 W in a single bolted joint — a soldering-iron's worth of heat in one connection, continuously. This is why thermography of LV incomers (one loose bolt → hundreds of watts → discoloured, cooked joint) is a standard assessment activity.
  • Rule of thumb worth memorising for 400 V: FLC ≈ 1.44 A per kVA (1000 / (1.732 × 400) = 1.443). For 13.8 kV: FLC ≈ 0.0418 A per kVA, or ≈ 42 A per MVA.

Worked calculation

Worked example — FLC set for the Rev 3 plant (indicative)

(a) MV FLC of one 1600 kVA transformer at 13.8 kV:

I_MV = S / (sqrt(3) × V) = 1 600 000 VA / (1.732 × 13 800 V) I_MV = 1 600 000 / 23 902 = 66.9 A

This sizes the MV transformer-feeder breakers [52-T1] / [52-T2] in MV-SWGR (a 630 A panel is bored at 66.9 A — normal and fine for MV) and points to a CT primary of 75–100 A on the feeder protection.

(b) LV FLC of the same transformer at 400 V:

I_LV = 1 600 000 VA / (1.732 × 400 V) = 1 600 000 / 692.8 = 2 309 A

This sets the LV-MSB incomer ACB frame (next standard frame ≥ 2 500 A, often 3 200 A for margin) and the incomer busbar/riser rating.

(c) Total LV load current at plant maximum demand (2 000 kW / 0.85 pf = 2 353 kVA, from §1):

I_total = 2 352 941 VA / (1.732 × 400 V) = 2 352 941 / 692.8 = 3 396 A

Shared across two incomers with the bus-tie open: ≈ 1 698 A per transformer — that is 1 698 / 2 309 = 73.5 % loading, matching the N-1 check (§2).

Sanity checks:

  • Turns-ratio check: I_LV / I_MV = 2 309 / 66.9 = 34.5 = 13 800 / 400. ✓ The two answers are mutually consistent — if your LV and MV FLCs do not divide to the voltage ratio, one of them is wrong.
  • Rule-of-thumb check: 1 600 kVA × 1.44 A/kVA ≈ 2 304 A ≈ 2 309 A. ✓
  • Order of magnitude: total plant current (3 396 A) exceeds one transformer's FLC (2 309 A) — confirming that one transformer alone cannot carry the whole plant without load shedding, which is the entire premise of the N-1 scheme.

All values indicative — confirm nameplate kVA and measured voltage on site.

Equipment settings

FLC is not itself a setting — it is the reference quantity that almost every setting on the plant is expressed against. On an assessment, walk the chain and verify each one:

  • LV incomer ACB long-time pickup Ir — a pickup modestly above transformer FLC (up to ≈ 1.05 × FLC) is normal practice: it gives close overload protection while absorbing metering tolerance and load variation without nuisance tripping. The manual's reference setting study (the breakers chapter) sets Ir = 2 375 A (0.95 × a 2 500 A sensor), i.e. 1.03 × FLC. What the assessor should flag is Ir above ≈ 1.1 × FLC (≈ 2 540 A) or above the cable/busbar ampacity — that genuinely permits sustained transformer overload (see the breaker deep-dive).
  • MV feeder relay overcurrent pickup — typically 1.1–1.25 × MV FLC, i.e. ≈ 74–84 A primary on the 66.9 A feeder, graded with the LV incomer (§10).
  • CT ratios — MV feeder ≈ 75/1 or 100/1 (FLC at 67–89 % of primary rating: healthy); LV incomer 2 500/5 or 3 200/5.
  • Metering and alarm thresholds — PMS high-current alarms typically at 90 % of FLC per incomer; demand alarms per §1.
  • Motor feeder settings are referenced to motor FLC, not transformer FLC — same formula with motor kW, efficiency and pf (§5).

Equipment technology

Where the FLC numbers physically live, and what carries them:

  • Nameplates — the transformer nameplate (catalogue) states rated kVA per cooling stage and usually prints rated current per winding directly: read it, do not trust drawings. The breaker rating plug, busbar label and cable schedule are the other primary sources.
  • Busbars and risers — at 2 309 A the LV incomer is hard-drawn copper bar, typically 2–3 laminations per phase, sized for both continuous current (at 50 °C ambient inside an IP-rated board — derate!) and short-circuit bracing (§4).
  • Current transformers — ring or bar-primary CTs scale thousands of amperes down to 1 A or 5 A for relays and meters; separate cores for metering (accuracy class 0.2S/0.5S, saturates early to protect instruments) and protection (class 5P/10P, stays linear into fault current).
  • Trip units and digital relays — measure current continuously via internal or external CTs; modern units display true-RMS per-phase current, which is your most accessible on-site FLC reading.

Control

Current is the most-monitored quantity on the plant. The LV trip units and MV relays stream per-phase amperes to the PMS/SCADA over the substation network; the tariff meter integrates it into demand. Assessment use: pull the 12-month per-incomer current trend and overlay the 2 309 A line — sustained operation above ≈ 0.85 pu (≈ 1 960 A) at 50 °C ambient deserves a finding even before the FLC line is crossed, because the derating culture says published ratings assume kinder ambients. Where no trending exists, a clamp-meter reading at peak shift, divided by 2 309 A, gives instant per-unit loading.

Sizing method

  1. Take rated S (kVA) from the nameplate — not the drawing.
  2. Take actual line voltage V — measure it; SEC LV systems sit at 400 V nominal but the as-found voltage matters for current.
  3. Compute I = S × 1000 / (1.732 × V).
  4. Select the next standard conductor/frame rating above FLC, then apply the 50 °C / grouping / enclosure derating factors (§6) and re-check that the derated rating still exceeds FLC.
  5. Cross-check: LV FLC / MV FLC must equal the voltage ratio; FLC ≈ 1.44 A/kVA at 400 V; measured current / calculated FLC = per-unit loading.

Quick Reference — FLC at Rev 3 Voltages

For any three-phase rating S (kVA) at line voltage V: I = S × 1000 / (sqrt(3) × V). Indicative values:

Rating (kVA)VoltageFLC (A) indicativeTypical use on this plant
2 353400 V3 396Total plant demand current (both incomers)
1 600400 V2 309TX-1 / TX-2 secondary; LV-MSB incomer
1 60013.8 kV66.9TX-1 / TX-2 primary; MV feeder [52-T1]/[52-T2]
1 250400 V1 804DG-1250 alternator output (before 50 °C ESP check)
1 000400 V1 443—
630400 V909Large LV sub-distribution feeder
100400 V144UPS-100 input/output frame sizing
Quick sanity check on site: clamp-meter (or trip-unit display) on each LV incomer; divide by 2 309 A for per-unit loading. Consistently above 0.85 pu in summer → investigate before the transformer does it for you. With the bus-tie open, the two incomer readings should also sum to roughly the tariff-meter demand converted at 1.44 A/kVA — a free three-way cross-check.
On the forms →
(a) Document inventory: LV-MSB incomer row — ACB frame (A), rating plug In, trip-unit Ir setting, measured current at peak; compare with 2 309 A.
(b) AS-FOUND site data required: transformer nameplate kVA and rated currents; measured LV line voltage; incomer ACB frame and Ir; busbar rated current from board label; MV feeder CT ratio and relay pickup.
  • Record transformer nameplate kVA and printed rated currents (MV and LV).
  • Verify LV incomer Ir ≤ 2 309 A (per 1600 kVA unit); flag any raised-pickup "fix".
  • Verify MV feeder CT primary is 75–100 A class, not grossly oversized.
  • Read per-phase incomer currents at peak shift; compute per-unit loading.
  • Thermography of LV incomer joints carrying > 1 500 A (hot-joint hunt).
  • Check the two incomer currents + tariff demand reconcile via 1.44 A/kVA.
On the Rev 3 (SEC 13.8 kV) basis: MV FLC ≈ 66.9 A and LV FLC ≈ 2 309 A per 1600 kVA transformer; total LV ≈ 3 396 A at 2 353 kVA. Every conductor, breaker, CT and relay pickup on the plant is referenced to these three numbers. Memorise 1.44 A per kVA at 400 V. All values indicative.

9 — Load Flow & Voltage Profile Reference

Goal: understand what a load-flow (power-flow) study is, follow the voltage from the SEC intake down to a motor terminal by hand, know what the software adds beyond hand arithmetic, and recognise when to demand a study.

What a Load Flow Actually Is

A load flow answers one deceptively simple question: with every load drawing its normal power, what is the voltage at every bus and the current in every branch? It is the "steady-state weather map" of the plant — no faults, no starting transients, just the plant running. Think of the network as a water system: SEC is the reservoir at fixed pressure (13.8 kV), every cable and transformer is a pipe with friction (impedance), and every load is a tap drawing flow. Open all the taps and the pressure at the far end of the system is inevitably lower than at the reservoir. The load flow computes exactly how much lower, everywhere, simultaneously.

Why it cannot be fully done by hand: each voltage drop depends on the current, but each load's current depends on its voltage (a constant-power motor draws more current as voltage falls). The equations are circular, so the solution is found by iteration — guess voltages, compute currents, recompute voltages, repeat until the numbers stop moving. For a radial two-bus system one iteration by hand is plenty (below); for a real plant model with dozens of buses, motors and the PFC bank, the iteration is what the software is for.

Three outputs matter to an assessor:

  • Bus voltages — every bus within limits (typically ± 5 % at utilisation points, per SASO-IEC 60364 and the SEC Distribution Code at the point of connection)?
  • Branch loadings — every transformer, cable and busbar below its (derated) continuous rating in both normal and N-1 configurations?
  • Reactive flows and losses — power factor at the SEC intake ≥ 0.95 with the PFC bank in service (§7), and losses not flagging an inefficient operating split?

The Voltage Profile Through This Plant

Follow one electron's-eye view from the grid to a pump motor, at summer peak, bus-tie open, ≈ 73 % transformer loading (all values indicative):

Point in systemIndicative voltageWhat moved it
SEC point of connection13.8 kV ± a few %Grid-controlled; varies with regional load
MV-SWGR bus≈ 13.7–13.8 kVShort MV intake cable — drop negligible at 66.9 A
TX-1 secondary (no-load)≈ 410 V with +2.5 % tapOff-load tap selection compensates expected drop
LV-MSB bus (at 73 % load)≈ 400 V (≈ 1.00 pu)Transformer regulation ≈ −2.7 % (worked below)
MCC bus≈ 396–398 VSub-main cable drop ≈ 0.5–1 %
Motor terminals (worst feeder)≈ 390 V (≈ 0.97 pu)Final cable drop ≈ 1.5–2.5 % (§6)

The pattern to internalise: voltage only ever falls as you move toward the load (capacitors and taps are the two deliberate exceptions), and the design game is to spend the ± 5 % budget so that the last motor on the worst feeder at the summer peak still sees ≥ 0.95 pu. The off-load tap changer is the coarse correction (set once, at shutdown); the PFC bank is the dynamic one — reactive current is what drags voltage down through the predominantly reactive transformer impedance, so correcting pf at the bus also props up the voltage.

A Two-Bus Load Flow by Hand

The simplest possible load flow — source bus, one impedance, load bus — is exactly the SEC-to-LV-MSB path, and it is worth doing by hand because it is the hand check you will run against any software study you are handed.

Worked example — Voltage drop SEC bus → LV-MSB (indicative)

System: stiff 13.8 kV source → TX-1 (1600 kVA, Z = 6 %) → LV-MSB bus. Load: ≈ 73 % of transformer rating (1 176 kVA) at pf 0.90 with the PFC bank in service (cos φ = 0.90, sin φ = 0.436).

Step 1 — Split the transformer impedance. Z = 6 % is mostly reactance; take R ≈ 1.2 %, X ≈ 5.9 % (so that sqrt(1.2² + 5.9²) ≈ 6.0 — typical for a 1600 kVA distribution unit; nameplate load loss gives the exact R).

Step 2 — Apply the regulation formula (valid for small drops):

dV% ≈ load_pu × (R% × cos φ + X% × sin φ) dV% ≈ 0.73 × (1.2 × 0.90 + 5.9 × 0.436) dV% ≈ 0.73 × (1.08 + 2.57) = 0.73 × 3.65 = 2.7 %

Step 3 — Place the result on the voltage profile. With the off-load tap at +2.5 % the no-load secondary sits at ≈ 410 V; subtracting the 2.7 % drop (≈ 11 V) lands the LV-MSB bus at ≈ 399–400 V at full normal load — on nominal. Adding the worst downstream feeder drop of ≈ 2.4 % (§6) puts the farthest motor terminal at ≈ 0.97 pu — inside the ± 5 % limit. ✓

Sanity checks: (1) at zero load the drop is zero, so the bus would sit at the tapped 410 V — lightly-loaded night-shift voltage visibly higher than day-shift voltage is normal and confirms the model. (2) In N-1 (one transformer at ≈ 100 %+ with load shed): dV% ≈ 1.0 × 3.65 ≈ 3.7 % — the surviving bus dips a further ≈ 1 %, still workable. (3) Worsen the pf to 0.85 (PFC out of service): sin φ = 0.527, dV% ≈ 0.73 × (1.02 + 3.11) ≈ 3.0 % — the PFC bank is quietly worth ≈ 0.3–0.5 % of bus voltage. All values indicative.

Loss check, same hand level: copper loss scales with loading². A 1600 kVA unit with ≈ 17 kW full-load copper loss dissipates ≈ 0.73² × 17 ≈ 9 kW at normal load, plus ≈ 2–3 kW constant iron loss — per transformer, continuously, which is why "two units at 73 %" versus "one unit at 100 % + one energised idle" is a real operating-cost question the load flow can answer.

What the Software Adds

Commercial power-system tools (ETAP, DIgSILENT PowerFactory and similar — named here generically, with no endorsement) do nothing conceptually beyond Step 2 above. What they add is scale and rigour:

  • Simultaneity — they solve every bus at once by iteration (Newton–Raphson and variants), correctly capturing the circular voltage-depends-on-current-depends-on-voltage coupling that hand methods linearise away.
  • Load models — constant-power motors, constant-impedance heaters and lighting, and the PFC bank's voltage-squared sensitivity are modelled distinctly; at depressed voltage these behave very differently.
  • Scenario sweeps — normal / N-1 / tie-closed / DG-island / minimum and maximum grid voltage, each at several tap positions, in minutes. The tap-selection table in a good study report is the direct product.
  • A validated network model — the same model then feeds the short-circuit (§4), motor-starting (§5), protection-coordination (§10) and arc-flash studies. The load flow is the foundation study: if its input data are wrong, every downstream study inherits the error.
Garbage in, garbage out. A software study is only as good as the cable lengths, transformer test-sheet impedances and load data typed into it. Your hand check above should land within ≈ 1 % of the software's transformer drop; if it does not, question the model's inputs before questioning your arithmetic.

When to Demand a Study (and How to Read One)

Recommend (or require) a fresh load-flow study whenever any of these is true:

  • No study exists, or it predates the as-built plant — the plant then has no formal basis for its tap settings, cable ratings or transformer loading limits. Absence is itself a finding.
  • Load has grown > ≈ 10 % since the study revision, or any feeder ≥ ~150 kW has been added (management-of-change trigger).
  • Voltage complaints — flicker, motors running hot, drives tripping on undervoltage, contactors dropping out on big-motor starts.
  • Configuration changes — PFC retrofit, DG addition or re-rating, proposal to operate tie-closed, or a new SEC connection agreement.
  • As-found measurements disagree with the study — measured bus voltage or incomer current more than a few % from the study's normal case.

When handed a study report, check it in this order:

Report outputWhat to checkAcceptable limit (indicative)
Study basisRevision date, SLD revision modelled, source fault level and voltage range usedMatches as-built plant and current SEC connection data
Bus voltage (pu)All buses, peak load, normal and N-1 cases± 5 % of nominal at utilisation points
Transformer loadingEach unit, both cases≤ 100 % continuous; short-time emergency loading only per IEC 60076-7 with the 50 °C ambient correction applied
Branch loadingAll cables/busbars vs derated ampacity≤ 100 % of 50 °C-derated rating, not the table rating
Reactive flow / pf at PCCWith PFC in and out of service≥ 0.95 in service (SEC Distribution Code expectation)
Tap positionsRecommended vs actual on the nameplate tap switchMust match what is physically set on site
LossesPer transformer, Cu + FeFlag if > ≈ 2 % of throughput
The one field check that catches most stale studies: read the actual off-load tap position on each transformer and compare with the study's recommended tap. A mismatch means either the study was never implemented or the plant has been retapped ad hoc since — both findings.
  • Locate the latest load-flow study; record date, revision and software basis.
  • Confirm the modelled SLD revision matches the as-built plant.
  • Run the two-bus hand check; compare with the study's transformer drop.
  • Read actual tap positions; compare with study recommendations.
  • Measure LV bus voltage at peak shift; compare with study normal case.
  • Check the N-1 case exists in the study and respects the 50 °C derating.
  • If no study, or load growth > 10 % since its date: raise a finding and recommend a new study.
A load flow is the steady-state map of voltages and flows; ± 5 % at the load is the budget, the transformer's reactive drop (≈ 2.7 % at normal load here) is the biggest single spender, and the tap + PFC bank are the two levers. The hand two-bus check validates any software study in five minutes — and the absence of a current study is a finding in its own right. All values indicative.

15 — Per-Unit System & the MVA Method Core

Goal: learn the per-unit (pu) system — the universal bookkeeping trick behind every impedance percentage on every nameplate — and its field-friendly cousin the MVA method, then use both to reproduce the plant's headline LV fault level (≈ 38.5 kA) from nothing but the transformer nameplate.

The Per-Unit Idea, From Scratch

Why this exists

A power system spans wildly different voltage levels — here 13.8 kV and 400 V, joined by a transformer. Working in ohms across that boundary is miserable: every impedance must be converted by the square of the turns ratio (34.5² ≈ 1 190) each time it crosses the transformer, and one slipped conversion ruins the whole study.

The per-unit system removes the problem by expressing every quantity as a fraction of a chosen base: pick one base power S_base for the whole system and the nominal voltage of each level as V_base, and then voltage, current, power and impedance all become dimensionless ratios. The magic: a transformer's impedance has the same per-unit value seen from either winding — the 34.5² conversion is baked into the bases and disappears from the arithmetic. The network becomes one flat circuit with no voltage levels in it.

You already use per-unit daily without naming it: the transformer nameplate "Z = 6 %" is a per-unit impedance (0.06 pu on the transformer's own kVA and voltage base); "the motor draws 6 × FLC at start" is per-unit current; "bus voltage 0.97 pu" is per-unit voltage. The system has only four rules:

quantity_pu = actual value / base value I_base = S_base / (sqrt(3) × V_base) [per voltage level] Z_base = V_base² / S_base Z_pu(new base) = Z_pu(old) × (S_base,new / S_base,old) [same voltage]

The last rule is the one used constantly in the field: nameplate impedances come on each machine's own rating, and you must move them all to one common base before adding them.

What goes wrong without it

The classic, expensive error: an engineer estimates the LV fault level for a switchboard extension by adding the transformer impedance in ohms to the upstream grid impedance in ohms — but takes the grid impedance at 13.8 kV without referring it through the transformer. The grid ohms are ≈ 1 190 times too large; the computed fault current collapses; the new board is bought at 25 kA where the true duty is ≈ 38.5 kA single-transformer (and ≈ 77 kA if anyone ever closes the tie). The error sleeps until the first real bolted fault, at which point an underrated breaker may fail to interrupt — the most violent failure mode an LV board has (§4).

The quieter failures: mixing impedances quoted on different kVA bases without the base-change rule (a 6 % on 1600 kVA is not the same animal as 6 % on 2000 kVA), or comparing relay settings across voltage levels in amperes instead of per-unit, which makes coordination reviews (§10) needlessly error-prone. Per-unit is not optional sophistication; it is the error-proofing that makes multi-voltage arithmetic survivable on a clipboard.

Feel for the numbers

  • Z = 6 % means exactly this: with the LV winding bolted shut, only 6 % of rated primary voltage is needed to push full-load current through the transformer. Flip it round: apply full voltage to that short and you get 1 / 0.06 ≈ 16.7 × full-load current. The fault level of a transformer-fed bus is hiding in plain sight on the nameplate.
  • 1.0 pu is always "rated, nominal, comfortable". Per-unit turns every quantity into a universal gauge: 0.97 pu voltage = slightly low anywhere from 400 V to 400 kV; 6 pu current = motor starting; 16.7 pu current = bolted fault. Your intuition transfers across the whole industry.
  • The MVA method makes "strength" additive-ish: every element is described by the short-circuit MVA it can pass (S_sc = S_rating / Z_pu). Sources in parallel: add. Elements in series: combine like parallel resistors (product over sum). The SEC grid here is a ≈ 600 MVA "reservoir"; the 1600 kVA transformer is a ≈ 27 MVA "pipe" — and a 27 MVA pipe fed from a 600 MVA reservoir passes ≈ 26 MVA: the skinniest pipe dominates. That single sentence is most of short-circuit engineering.

Worked calculation

Worked example — LV fault level by per-unit and by MVA method (indicative)

Chosen bases (stated explicitly, always): S_base = 1600 kVA (the transformer's own rating — the convenient choice here); V_base = 13.8 kV on the MV side and 400 V on the LV side.

Step 1 — Base currents (one per voltage level):

I_base,MV = 1 600 000 / (1.732 × 13 800) = 66.9 A I_base,LV = 1 600 000 / (1.732 × 400) = 2 309 A

Note these are exactly the FLCs from §3 — choosing the transformer's own rating as base makes 1.0 pu current = FLC, which is why it is the convenient choice.

Step 2 — Transformer impedance in per-unit: nameplate Z = 6 % on its own 1600 kVA base → Z_tx = 0.06 pu. No base conversion needed (we deliberately chose its base). Had we chosen S_base = 2 MVA instead: Z_tx = 0.06 × (2000 / 1600) = 0.075 pu — different number, same physics, identical final answer.

Step 3 — LV fault current, infinite-bus assumption (grid impedance ≈ 0, the standard conservative first pass):

I_fault,pu = V_pu / Z_pu = 1.0 / 0.06 = 16.7 pu I_sc = 16.7 × I_base,LV = 16.7 × 2 309 A ≈ 38 500 A ≈ 38.5 kA

Step 4 — Same answer by the MVA method (no currents until the very end):

S_sc,tx = S_rating / Z_pu = 1 600 kVA / 0.06 = 26 667 kVA ≈ 26.7 MVA I_sc = S_sc / (sqrt(3) × V) = 26 700 000 / (1.732 × 400) ≈ 38 500 A ≈ 38.5 kA

Step 5 — How much does the finite grid actually shave off? SEC design fault level 25 kA at 13.8 kV (SEC range 25–40 kA):

S_sc,grid = sqrt(3) × 13.8 kV × 25 kA ≈ 597 MVA

Grid and transformer in series — combine product-over-sum:

S_sc,LV = (597 × 26.7) / (597 + 26.7) ≈ 25.6 MVA → I_sc ≈ 36.9 kA

The infinite-bus 38.5 kA is ≈ 4 % conservative — which is exactly the right direction to err when checking breaker ratings.
