Sanity checks: (1) direct method: I_sc = FLC / Z_pu = 2 309 / 0.06 = 38.5 kA ✓ — three independent routes, one answer. (2) 16.7 × FLC "feels" right for a 6 % transformer (1/0.06). (3) Two transformers tie-closed: double the source → ≈ 77 kA, the Rev 3 tie-normally-open rationale (§4). All values indicative; motor contribution adds a further increment in a formal IEC 60909 study.

Where Per-Unit Numbers Live on Real Equipment

Equipment settings

Per-unit is not just a paper convention — most of the settings you will dial into real devices are entered in per-unit of something, and knowing the base is half the setting:

  • Transformer nameplate impedance is always per-unit (percent) on the transformer's own rating: Z = 6 % means 0.06 pu on 1600 kVA, 13.8/0.4 kV. Change the base, and the number changes — the physics does not.
  • Relay pickups are set in multiples of nominal (a 51 element pickup of "1.1 × In" is a per-unit statement), and CT secondary quantities are effectively per-unit on the CT ratio (§10).
  • LV trip units express L/S/I pickups as multiples of the frame or plug rating — "L = 0.95 × In, S = 4 × In" is per-unit language (§14).
  • Voltage bands in the Saudi Grid Code / SEC Distribution Code are stated as ± percent of nominal — per-unit voltage by another name.

Per-unit on the nameplate

Equipment technology

The "equipment" of per-unit is the data that feeds it. The numbers come from three physical places: nameplates (transformer Z%, rated kVA and voltages — stamped after the factory short-circuit test per SASO-adopted IEC 60076), type-test certificates (switchgear ratings expressed against rated values), and the utility intake declaration (SEC fault level in kA or MVA at 13.8 kV — ask for the design value in writing; the range is typically 25–40 kA). Treat all three as controlled documents: a per-unit study is only as good as the bases and impedances it was fed.

Per-unit in relays and studies

Control

Power-system software (load flow, short circuit, arc flash) works internally in per-unit; what you control is the model data discipline. The plant single-line in the software must carry the same bases, the same nameplate impedances and the same SEC intake fault level as the controlled documents — and every modification (new transformer, tie philosophy change, SEC reinforcement) triggers a model update and re-run. The PMS and metering supply the measured MW / Mvar / voltage values you compare against the model's per-unit predictions: persistent disagreement means the model, not the plant, is wrong.

Choosing the base values

Sizing method

The per-unit / MVA recipe you will reuse for the rest of Part 2 — pin it up:

  1. Choose bases and say so out loud: one S_base for the whole study (the transformer rating, or a round 10/100 MVA); V_base = nominal voltage of each level (13.8 kV, 400 V). Base currents follow: I_base = S_base / (sqrt(3) × V_base).
  2. Convert every impedance to the common base: Z_new = Z_nameplate × (S_base / S_nameplate). Grid: Z_grid,pu = S_base / S_sc,grid.
  3. Combine: series impedances add; parallel sources add their short-circuit MVA (or combine impedances product-over-sum).
  4. Read off the answers: I_sc = (V_pu / Z_pu) × I_base at the bus of interest; voltage drops and loadings fall out in percent directly.
  5. Sanity-check against the direct method (FLC / Z_pu for a single transformer) and against the previous study revision before trusting any new number.
Per-unit is the dialect the whole industry speaks: nameplates, relays, trip units and grid codes all quote per-unit numbers. Master the base conversion once and every fault, dip and loading question in this manual becomes one or two lines of arithmetic.

Self-check — Per-Unit & MVA Method

  • State the four base quantities and compute I_base on both sides of TX-1 from S_base = 1600 kVA (answers: 66.9 A / 2309 A — indicative).
  • Convert Z = 6 % on 1600 kVA to a 2 MVA base (answer: 0.075 pu) and explain why the fault current result is unchanged.
  • Compute the LV fault level by the MVA method including the 597 MVA SEC grid (answer: ≈ 25.6 MVA → ≈ 36.9 kA) and explain why the infinite-bus 38.5 kA is the conservative number to check breaker ratings against.
  • Explain in one sentence why two paralleled 1600 kVA transformers give ≈ 77 kA and what the normally-open bus-tie does about it.
  • Find the per-unit statements on a breaker trip-unit settings sheet (L/S/I multiples) and identify the base each multiple refers to.

4 — Short-Circuit Current & Switchgear Withstand Core

Goal: understand why the prospective short-circuit current is the single most consequential number in the plant, calculate it from the SEC utility connection down to the 400 V busbars (including the IEC 60909 refinements — voltage factor, X/R, peak factor, motor contribution), and verify that every switchboard, breaker and busbar between the 13.8 kV intake and the last MCC can both survive and interrupt the worst fault it can ever see.

This section feeds almost every other calculation in Part 2: the breaker rating checks in §14 — Circuit Breakers, the grading study in §10 — Protection Coordination, the incident-energy calculation in §11 — Arc-Flash, and the cable thermal withstand check in §6 — Cable Sizing. Get this number wrong and every one of those studies inherits the error.

Indicative data. Every numeric value in this section is indicative and chosen for teaching on the Rev 3 plant basis (SEC 13.8 kV, 60 Hz, design utility fault level 25 kA within the SEC-stated 25–40 kA range; 2 × 1600 kVA, Z = 6 % transformers; 400/230 V TN-S LV system; 50 °C design ambient). The plant's formal short-circuit study to the SASO-adopted edition of IEC 60909, performed with the fault level confirmed in writing by SEC, governs. Standards are cited as SASO-adopted IEC together with the SEC Distribution Code / Saudi Grid Code and SBC 401.

The One Number Every Rating Answers To

Why this exists

Walk through the plant and read the nameplates. The LV-MSB incomer ACB says something like Icu 65 kA. The MV switchgear label says 31.5 kA / 3 s. The busbar type-test certificate quotes a peak withstand in kA. The MCC door label, the fuse cartridges in the PFC panel, even the little MCBs in the lighting distribution board — every one of them carries a fault rating in kiloamps. None of those numbers describes anything the equipment does on a normal day. They all answer one question, and it is the question this section exists to ask: if a bolted short circuit occurs right here, how much current will the network push through this spot before something opens?

The answer is called the prospective short-circuit current (Isc, or Ik" in IEC 60909 language). "Prospective" is doing important work in that name: it is the current that would flow if the fault were a perfect zero-impedance connection and nothing intervened. It is a property of the network, not of the fault or of any protective device. You cannot reduce it by setting a relay differently, and you cannot wish it smaller — you can only make sure every piece of equipment in its path is rated for it.

The physics is humbling. Under normal load the network behaves like a polite negotiation: the load's impedance limits the current to what the process needs — roughly 2 309 A through each transformer at full load. A bolted fault removes the load from the circuit entirely. What remains between the SEC generators and the fault is only the series impedance of the supply path: the grid, the transformer, a few metres of busbar. On the Rev 3 plant that residual impedance is about 6 % on the transformer base — which means the fault current is about 1 / 0.06 ≈ 17 times the transformer's full-load current. Seventeen times. Every conductor, joint, support and contact in the path must carry that current — and the breakers must interrupt it — without converting the switchroom into the failure story told in the next sub-section.

Two distinct duties flow from the one number, and the distinction matters all through this section:

  • Withstand — every component the fault current flows through (busbars, cable, CTs, isolators, the closed contacts of breakers that are not tripping) must survive the thermal and magnetic punishment for as long as the fault persists. This is a passive, mechanical duty: it begins at the instant of the fault, milliseconds before any relay has even measured anything.
  • Interruption — the one device assigned to clear the fault must be able to break that current: stretch, cool and extinguish an arc carrying tens of kiloamps. This is the breaking-capacity duty (Icu at LV, rated short-circuit breaking current at MV) covered in detail in §14.

Both duties are sized from the prospective short-circuit current. That is why this calculation comes so early in Part 2 and why it is flagged Core: transformer sizing (§3) tells you what the plant needs; the short-circuit study tells you what the plant must survive.

The prospective short-circuit current is a property of the network, not of any device. It defines two duties at every point in the system: components in the fault path must withstand it, and the clearing device must interrupt it. Every kA rating on every nameplate in the plant is an answer to this one number.

A Failure Story: the Board That Was Never Asked

What goes wrong without it

The following narrative is fictional, but every element of it is assembled from failure modes documented across the industry, and every number is consistent with the Rev 3 plant basis. Read it as a rehearsal of what the checklist at the end of this section is protecting you from.

A process plant much like this one had grown in stages. The original 400 V switchboard was bought when the site had a single 1000 kVA transformer; the board was honestly rated 25 kA, comfortably above the 21 kA the old transformer could deliver, and the type-test certificate said so. Years later the site re-powered: two 1600 kVA, Z = 6 % units, a new bus-tie arrangement, a doubling of demand. The new transformers could each drive about 38.5 kA into a bolted fault on the board — and with the tie closed, towards 77 kA. Nobody re-ran the short-circuit study. The old board "had always been fine". The fault level had been raised; the board was never asked whether it could cope.

For six years nothing happened, because nothing happened: prospective fault current is invisible until there is a fault. Then, during a maintenance window, a forgotten bonding lead was left across two phases behind the incomer compartment, and the incomer was closed onto it. A bolted phase-to-phase fault, dead short, full network behind it.

What followed took less than a quarter of a second. Within the first half-cycle — 8.3 ms at 60 Hz — the current rose to its fully offset peak, close to 88 kA on this network (the worked calculation below shows where that number comes from). The electromagnetic force between parallel conductors scales with the square of the instantaneous current — F ∝ I² — so a board braced for a 25 kA duty (peak around 53 kA) suddenly experienced forces nearly three times what its supports were type-tested for. The busbars did what unbraced conductors carrying anti-parallel currents always do: they tried to fly apart. One support insulator cracked; a bar deflected; the phase-to-phase clearance collapsed; and the bolted fault became a three-phase arcing fault in open air inside the enclosure.

Now the second failure mode took over. An arc at this current level burns at temperatures of the order of 10 000–20 000 K — hotter than the surface of the sun — and it is not stationary: magnetic forces drive it along the bars (away from the source) like a blowtorch on rails. The enclosure was ordinary folded sheet steel, never designed for internal arc containment. The arc burnt through the rear panel in roughly 100 ms, vented plasma and vaporised copper into the cable trench behind the board, and ignited cable insulation. The upstream MV protection — graded slow precisely so the LV devices could act first, as explained in §10 — finally cleared the fault from the 13.8 kV side after several hundred milliseconds. The LV incomer breaker never got the chance: an early instant of the fault had already exceeded its 25 kA interrupting rating, its contacts welded on the first peak, and from then on it was just another conductor in the fault path.

Total damage: the switchboard destroyed, the cable trench fire-damaged, the plant down for weeks awaiting a replacement board on long lead time. The two electricians in the room survived because they happened to be standing beside, not in front of, the cubicle — the arc-flash consequences of exactly this scenario are quantified in §11. The investigation's finding fitted in one sentence: the installed fault level had been raised above the switchboard's rating, and no process existed to catch it.

Notice the chain of cause and effect, because it is general:

  • Underrated withstand fails first and fails mechanically — bracing, supports, clearances — within the first peak, before any protection can act. No relay setting can prevent it.
  • Underrated interruption fails next — a breaker asked to break more than its Icu may weld, restrike or rupture, converting the protective device itself into the failure.
  • Containment fails last — a non-arc-rated enclosure lets the arc out, escalating an electrical fault into a fire and a personnel hazard.
The trap of the quiet upgrade. Fault level rises whenever the network gets "stronger": a bigger or lower-impedance transformer, a second transformer paralleled, a closed bus-tie, a new SEC infeed, on-site generation paralleled to the bus. None of these changes look dangerous — they all improve supply. Every one of them must trigger a re-check of the short-circuit study against every downstream rating. The bus-tie case is the most common offender and has its own sub-section below.

How Violent Is 38.5 kA?

Feel for the numbers

Numbers in kiloamps slide past the eye. Before the formal calculation, let us honestly estimate two things: the energy released if the 400 V bus faults and arcs for 0.3 s, and the mechanical force the busbars feel at the first peak. Both follow from arithmetic you can redo on a phone.

Energy. The power the network can push into a three-phase bolted fault at the LV-MSB is:

Estimate — energy in a 0.3 s arcing fault at the LV-MSB

Apparent power feeding a bolted three-phase fault:

S_fault = sqrt(3) × V × Isc = 1.732 × 400 V × 38 500 A ≈ 26.7 MVA

For a bolted fault nearly all of that energy is dissipated out in the network impedance (mostly inside the transformer). The dangerous case is the arcing fault, where the arc itself is a resistor sitting at the fault point. Maximum power transfer theory says a resistive arc dissipates the most energy locally when its impedance matches the source impedance — at which point roughly half the bolted-fault power burns in the arc:

P_arc (upper estimate) ≈ 0.5 × 26.7 MW ≈ 13.3 MW

If protection clears the fault in 0.3 s (a realistic time if the incomer's short-time delay is involved — see §14):

E_arc ≈ 13.3 MW × 0.3 s ≈ 4.0 MJ

Sanity check: this is the crude upper-bound estimate; the IEEE 1584 method in §11 computes the realistic arcing current and the fraction of energy reaching a worker. But as an order of magnitude it is honest — and it is the right order of magnitude to be frightened of.

Now translate 4.0 MJ into everyday quantities, computed honestly:

  • TNT: TNT releases ≈ 4.6 MJ/kg, so 4.0 MJ ≈ 0.9 kg of TNT — a stick-of-dynamite-class energy release inside a sheet-steel cabinet at chest height. (The comparison is about energy, not blast dynamics — an arc releases it over 0.3 s rather than microseconds — but the pressure wave from an arc in an enclosure is still enough to blow doors off non-arc-rated boards.)
  • Car crash: a 1 200 kg car at 100 km/h (27.8 m/s) carries KE = 0.5 × 1 200 × 27.8² ≈ 0.46 MJ ≈ 0.5 MJ. The arc releases the kinetic energy of about eight highway-speed cars, in a third of a second, in a box.
  • Kettle: a 2 kW kettle would take 4 000 000 / 2 000 = 2 000 s ≈ 33 minutes to release the same energy that the arc releases in 0.3 s. The arc is roughly 6 600 kettles running at once.

Force. Parallel conductors carrying current exert forces on each other — anti-parallel currents (as between two phases at the moment of a phase-to-phase fault) repel. The force per metre between two long parallel bars is:

Estimate — magnetic force between LV busbars at the first peak

F/L = 2 × 10^-7 × i1 × i2 / d (N/m, currents in A, spacing d in m)

Take the first-peak current with DC offset, ip ≈ 88 kA (derived in the worked calculation below from kappa ≈ 1.61 at the transformer terminals), and a typical 100 mm phase spacing:

F/L = 2 × 10^-7 × (88 000)^2 / 0.1 = 2 × 10^-7 × 7.74 × 10^9 / 0.1 ≈ 15 500 N/m

That is ≈ 15.5 kN per metre ≈ 1.6 tonne-force on every metre of busbar — as if a family car were hung off each metre of bar, applied sideways, arriving in 8.3 ms. A busbar span between supports 0.5 m apart carries a transverse load of ≈ 7.7 kN (≈ 790 kg).

Now the tie-closed case, Isc ≈ 77 kA, ip ≈ 175 kA:

F/L = 2 × 10^-7 × (175 000)^2 / 0.1 ≈ 61 000 N/m ≈ 6.2 tonne-force per metre

Sanity check — the I-squared law: doubling the current (38.5 → 77 kA) quadrupled the force (15.5 → 61 kN/m), exactly as F ∝ I² demands. This is why a board rated 50 kA cannot be "probably OK" at 77 kA: it is not 54 % overstressed, the bracing forces are (77/50)² ≈ 2.4 times its tested duty.

Hold these two pictures — a kilogram of TNT of energy, and a car's weight of sideways force per metre of bar — while reading the rest of the section. They are what the words "breaking capacity" and "short-time withstand" actually mean.

A 0.3 s arcing fault at the 400 V bus releases of the order of 4 MJ — about 0.9 kg of TNT, or eight highway-speed car crashes — and the first current peak loads the busbars with about 1.6 tonne-force per metre. Force scales with I²: closing the bus-tie doesn't add 100 % to the mechanical duty, it adds 300 %.

From SEC Intake to the 400 V Bus, Step by Step

Worked calculation

We now compute the prospective short-circuit current at the two places where it matters most — the 13.8 kV switchgear bus and the 400 V LV-MSB — and then refine the raw numbers with the three IEC 60909 corrections a field engineer should be able to reproduce: the voltage factor c, the peak factor κ (DC offset), and the motor contribution. All arithmetic is doable on a phone. The per-unit machinery behind these shortcuts is developed in §2 — Per-Unit System; the transformer data come from §3 — Transformer Sizing.

Step (a) — fault level at the 13.8 kV intake. SEC does not hand you an impedance; it hands you (on request, in writing) a design fault level at the point of connection. For this plant the Rev 3 design basis is 25 kA at 13.8 kV, and SEC's stated planning range for this class of network is 25–40 kA — which is why the MV switchgear is bought at 31.5 kA (next sub-section) rather than barely above 25 kA. Convert the current into a short-circuit apparent power, because MVA is the most convenient currency for the next step:

Worked calculation — MV fault level as short-circuit MVA

Formula (three-phase apparent power at the fault):

S"k = sqrt(3) × U × Ik"

Substitution, with U = 13.8 kV and Ik" = 25 kA:

S"k = 1.732 × 13 800 V × 25 000 A = 597.6 MVA ≈ 598 MVA

It is also worth expressing the grid "strength" as an impedance on the transformer base, because that tells us whether the grid impedance matters at the 400 V bus. On the 1600 kVA base of one transformer:

Z_grid (pu, 1600 kVA base) = S_base / S"k = 1.6 MVA / 597.6 MVA ≈ 0.0027 pu ≈ 0.27 %

Sanity check: the grid contributes about 0.27 % of impedance in series with the transformer's 6 % — under one twentieth. The 400 V fault level is therefore dominated by the transformer, and treating the SEC grid as an "infinite bus" overstates the LV fault current by only a few percent — an error in the safe direction for rating equipment. At the upper SEC value of 40 kA (956 MVA) the grid term shrinks further, to ≈ 0.17 %. Indicative values; SEC's written confirmation and the formal IEC 60909 study govern.

Field habit. The MV fault level is an SEC planning number and can rise over the life of the plant as the SEC network is reinforced. Treat the 25 kA figure as today's floor, not a constant of nature: that is exactly why the MV board is specified at 31.5 kA, inside SEC's stated 25–40 kA range, and why the re-study triggers at the end of this section include "SEC notifies a change of fault level".

Step (b) — fault current at the 400 V bus through one transformer. The most useful shortcut in all of power engineering: for a fault at the LV terminals of a transformer fed from a strong grid, the fault current is simply the transformer's full-load current divided by its per-unit impedance. The logic is one sentence long — the impedance voltage Z % is, by definition, the fraction of rated voltage needed to push rated current through the short-circuited transformer, so applying full voltage to a short circuit pushes 1/Z times rated current.

Worked calculation — LV bus fault current, one 1600 kVA transformer

Transformer full-load current at 400 V (from §3):

I_FLC = S / (sqrt(3) × U) = 1 600 000 VA / (1.732 × 400 V) = 2 309 A

Infinite-bus fault current with Z = 6 % = 0.06 pu:

Isc = I_FLC / Z = 2 309 A / 0.06 ≈ 38 500 A = 38.5 kA

Refinement — include the SEC grid impedance from step (a) in series:

Isc = 2 309 / (0.06 + 0.0027) = 2 309 / 0.0627 ≈ 36.8 kA

Sanity check: the grid-included value is only ≈ 4 % lower than the infinite-bus value, confirming step (a)'s conclusion that the transformer dominates. For rating equipment we keep the conservative 38.5 kA; for protection settings the study value matters. Cross-check against the "17 times" intuition from the start of this section: 1/0.06 = 16.7, and 16.7 × 2 309 A ≈ 38.5 kA. Consistent.

The bus-tie case. The LV-MSB has two bus sections, one per transformer, with a normally-open bus-tie. Close the tie with both transformers energised and a fault anywhere on the bus now sees two 6 % impedances in parallel — i.e. 3 % — between itself and the (effectively infinite) grid:

Worked calculation — LV bus fault current, bus-tie closed

Z_parallel = 0.06 / 2 = 0.03 pu (two identical transformers in parallel) Isc = 2 309 A / 0.03 ≈ 77 000 A = 77 kA

Equivalently: each transformer independently drives its own 38.5 kA into the same fault, and the contributions add:

Isc = 38.5 kA + 38.5 kA = 77 kA

Sanity check: doubling the sources exactly doubles the fault current (the small grid term spoils this by only a couple of percent). But recall from "Feel for the numbers" that busbar force scales with current squared — the mechanical duty quadruples, from ≈ 15.5 kN/m to ≈ 61 kN/m.

This single calculation is the entire justification for the plant's tie-normally-open operating philosophy. With the tie open, each bus section sees at most ≈ 38.5 kA plus motor contribution (step (d)), comfortably inside a 50–65 kA board rating. With the tie closed and both incomers shut, the prospective current approaches 77 kA plus motor contribution — at or beyond the top of the board's rating. The tie exists for maintenance transfer: the switching sequence is close tie → open one incomer, so that both incomers and the tie are never all closed except for the few seconds of a make-before-break transfer, under a documented procedure (and, in many plants, an electrical interlock that enforces "two of three" — see §14).

One transformer: Isc ≈ 2 309 A / 0.06 ≈ 38.5 kA at the 400 V bus. Tie closed, both transformers: ≈ 77 kA — fault current doubles, busbar forces quadruple. That is why the bus-tie is normally open and why closing it is a controlled, interlocked, short-duration operation.

Step (c) — the IEC 60909 refinements. The raw 1/Z arithmetic above is the right first estimate, but the SASO-adopted IEC 60909 method adds three corrections that change purchasing decisions. Each is small to compute and large in consequence.

(c-1) The voltage factor c. Real buses do not sit at exactly nominal voltage; transformers have taps; the standard wants the maximum credible fault current for rating purposes. IEC 60909 therefore multiplies the nominal voltage by a factor c before calculating: for maximum fault currents, cmax = 1.10 at MV, and 1.05 at LV systems with the usual +6 % supply tolerance (1.10 where +10 % applies). A minimum set (cmin = 0.95–1.00) is used when you need the smallest fault current — for checking that protection still picks up at the far end of a long cable (§10). Applied to our LV bus:

Worked calculation — applying the voltage factor at the 400 V bus

Ik" = c × Isc(raw) = 1.05 × 38.5 kA ≈ 40.4 kA (one transformer) Ik" = 1.05 × 77 kA ≈ 80.9 kA (tie closed)

Sanity check: a 5 % voltage uplift gives a 5 % current uplift — fault current is linear in driving voltage. Note what this does at the margins: a board rated exactly 38.5 kA would already be non-compliant on the IEC 60909 maximum case. This is one reason the LV-MSB is specified at 50–65 kA.

(c-2) The peak factor κ and the DC offset. Everything so far is an RMS, steady-state-symmetrical number. But a fault that strikes at the wrong point on the voltage wave does not start symmetrically: the inductive network cannot change its current instantaneously, so a decaying DC component is superimposed on the AC fault current, and the very first peak can approach twice the symmetrical peak. How close it gets depends on how slowly the DC component decays — i.e. on the network's X/R ratio (decay time constant τ = X/(ωR) = L/R). High X/R = inductive, slow decay, high first peak. IEC 60909 captures this with the empirical peak factor:

Worked calculation — peak current ip at the 13.8 kV bus

Formula (IEC 60909 peak factor, then peak current):

kappa = 1.02 + 0.98 × e^(-3 / (X/R)) ip = kappa × sqrt(2) × Ik"

Substitution with a typical MV network X/R ≈ 15 and Ik" = 25 kA:

kappa = 1.02 + 0.98 × e^(-3/15) = 1.02 + 0.98 × e^(-0.2) = 1.02 + 0.98 × 0.819 ≈ 1.82 ip = 1.82 × 1.414 × 25 kA ≈ 64.4 kA peak

Sanity check: the theoretical ceiling is kappa = 2.0 (purely inductive, no decay), giving ip = 2 × 1.414 × 25 = 70.7 kA; a purely resistive network gives kappa = 1.02, ip ≈ 36 kA. Our 64.4 kA sits sensibly near, but below, the ceiling, as expected for X/R = 15. The MV switchgear's rated peak withstand is 2.6 × 31.5 kA ≈ 82 kA (the 2.6 factor is the 60 Hz standard ratio built into the IEC rating structure), so 64.4 kA passes with margin. At the 400 V transformer terminals the X/R is lower (≈ 6 for a 1600 kVA unit), giving kappa ≈ 1.61 and the ip ≈ 88 kA already used in "Feel for the numbers": 1.61 × 1.414 × 38.5 ≈ 87.7 kA.

(c-3) What the DC offset means in time. At 60 Hz one half-cycle lasts 1 / (2 × 60) = 8.33 ms — the fully offset first peak arrives about 8.3 ms after fault inception, long before any relay-plus-breaker chain can act. So the peak duty is unavoidable: busbar bracing and breaker making capacity must be rated for ip with no help from protection. Thereafter the DC component decays with τ = (X/R)/ω — for X/R = 15 at 60 Hz, τ = 15/377 ≈ 40 ms — so a breaker that opens its contacts at, say, 50–80 ms still interrupts a current with significant remaining asymmetry. This is why breaker ratings distinguish the symmetrical breaking current from the asymmetrical duty (expressed via the rated DC component percentage at contact separation), and why fast-tripping MV breakers on high-X/R networks must be checked for DC component, not just RMS amperes — taken up in §14.

Step (d) — motor contribution. Here is the refinement most often forgotten in hand calculations: during a fault, the plant's own running motors stop being loads and briefly become generators. A spinning induction motor has kinetic energy in its rotor and trapped flux in its magnetic circuit; when the bus voltage collapses, that flux drives current out of the motor and into the fault. The motor feeds the fault through its own subtransient impedance — which is essentially its locked-rotor impedance — so each running motor contributes roughly its starting current, 4–6 × FLC, decaying away over a few cycles as the trapped flux dies (induction motors have no excitation to sustain it).

Worked calculation — contribution of 500 kW of running LV motors

Assume an indicative 500 kW of LV motors running on one bus section at the moment of the fault (efficiency ≈ 0.94, pf ≈ 0.85). Aggregate full-load current:

I_FLC(motors) = 500 000 W / (1.732 × 400 V × 0.85 × 0.94) ≈ 900 A

Initial fault contribution at 4–6 × FLC (use 4 × as the typical aggregate figure — large motors near 6, small motors and those behind long cables nearer 4 or below):

I_motor ≈ 4 × 900 A ≈ 3 600 A ≈ 3.5–4 kA (up to ≈ 5.4 kA at 6×)

Total initial symmetrical fault current at the bus, one transformer:

Ik"(total) ≈ 38.5 kA + 4 kA ≈ 42–43 kA

Sanity check: the motor add-on is ≈ 10 % of the transformer contribution — small, but exactly the kind of margin that decides whether a 40 kA-rated device passes or fails. Note the direction of flow: motor current arrives at the fault from the load side, so it stresses outgoing feeder breakers "backwards" and inflates the first-cycle peak duty even when the upstream source is modest. IEC 60909 includes motor groups whenever their combined contribution exceeds about 5 % of the fault current — which it does here.

Pulling it together — the indicative Rev 3 fault-current picture (formal IEC 60909 study governs):

LocationCaseIk" sym (indicative)ip peak (indicative)Equipment rating to beat
13.8 kV busSEC design level25 kA (SEC range 25–40 kA)≈ 64 kA (kappa ≈ 1.82)MV swgr 31.5 kA / 3 s, peak ≈ 82 kA
400 V LV-MSBOne TX + motors≈ 38.5 + 4 ≈ 42–43 kA≈ 88 kA + motor peakLV-MSB 50–65 kA, ACB Icu ≥ 50 kA
400 V LV-MSBTie closed, both TX + motors≈ 77 + 4 ≈ 81 kA≈ 175 kA +Exceeds board duty — hence tie normally open
Four moves cover the whole plant: (a) SEC fault level → S"k ≈ 598 MVA at 13.8 kV; (b) Isc = FLC / Z = 2 309 / 0.06 ≈ 38.5 kA per transformer, 77 kA tie-closed; (c) IEC 60909 corrections — voltage factor c, and peak factor kappa = 1.02 + 0.98 e^(-3/(X/R)) giving the first-peak duty at 8.3 ms; (d) running motors add 4–6 × their FLC for a few cycles. Rate equipment for the sum, not for the transformer alone.

Turning the Fault Table into Nameplate Ratings

Equipment settings

Strictly, the short-circuit study produces ratings to buy more than knobs to set — the settings themselves (pickups, time delays, curves) belong to the grading study in §10 and the breaker section in §14. But every rating selection below is a decision made against the fault table from the worked calculation, and the field engineer should be able to audit each one:

  • MV switchgear short-time withstand — 31.5 kA / 3 s. The bus, CTs, isolating contacts and cable terminations of the 13.8 kV board must carry the full fault current for the rated short time while upstream/downstream grading plays out. 31.5 kA > the 25 kA design level with margin inside SEC's 25–40 kA planning range; the 3 s duration covers the slowest credible backup clearing time. The paired peak (making) withstand, ≈ 2.6 × 31.5 ≈ 82 kA, covers the computed ip ≈ 64 kA. If SEC ever confirms a level above 31.5 kA, the board — not a setting — is what must change.
  • MV breaker rated short-circuit breaking current. Selected equal to the board's 31.5 kA class, with the rated DC component percentage checked against the network X/R ≈ 15 (τ ≈ 40 ms at 60 Hz) at minimum contact-parting time.
  • LV-MSB rated short-time/withstand — 50–65 kA class. The board must beat the worst single-source case including motors (≈ 42–43 kA) with margin; it is not rated for the tie-closed 77 kA+ case, which is why the operating philosophy (tie normally open, two-of-three interlock, make-before-break transfer only) is itself a "setting" — an administrative and interlock configuration that holds the duty inside the rating.
  • LV ACB/MCCB breaking capacities — Icu vs Ics. Icu (ultimate breaking capacity) is the maximum fault the breaker can interrupt once, after which it may need overhaul; Ics (service breaking capacity, quoted as a % of Icu) is what it can interrupt and remain in service. Select Icu ≥ the prospective fault at the point of installation (incomer ACBs: ≥ 50 kA at 400 V; feeder MCCBs further out may legitimately use lower Icu where cable impedance has reduced the fault level — proven by calculation, never by assumption). Prefer Ics = 100 % Icu at the incomers, where a re-closeable board after a fault is worth the price. Catalogue families in Part 6 — LV ACB/MCCB catalogue.
  • Icw of the LV incomers (short-time withstand of the breaker itself). An incomer ACB that must stay closed while a downstream device clears needs its own 1 s withstand ≥ the through-fault current — the basis of time-graded selectivity in §10.
  • Minimum fault check. The same study, run with cmin and maximum impedance, yields the smallest fault current at the far end of each feeder — the number protection pickups must sit below. Both edges of the study are used: maximum for ratings, minimum for settings.
Never rate to the bare transformer number. 38.5 kA is the transformer's contribution alone. The rating decision is made against c × (transformer + grid refinement) + motor contribution at the relevant bus, on the worst credible switching configuration. All values here are indicative; the certified IEC 60909 study fixes the final figures.

How Hardware Survives: Bracing and Containment

Equipment technology

Two pieces of physical engineering stand between the numbers above and the failure story at the start of this section: busbar bracing (surviving the magnetic forces) and arc-resistant construction (containing the worst case when, despite everything, an arc occurs inside the enclosure). The breaker arc-interruption technologies — vacuum at MV, air-chute at LV — are covered in §14; here we deal with the passive structure.

Busbar bracing. "Feel for the numbers" computed the load: F/L = 2 × 10-7 × ip² / d — note that the structural duty is set by the peak current ip, not the RMS value, because F ∝ ip² and the worst blow lands on the very first offset peak at 8.3 ms. A switchboard manufacturer meets this duty with:

  • Support spacing. Each busbar span behaves as a beam under a distributed magnetic load; bending stress grows with the square of the span. Halving the distance between insulator supports quarters the stress — which is why a 65 kA board visibly has supports every 300–400 mm where a 25 kA board might have them every metre. This is the cheapest kA upgrade lever a manufacturer has, and it is why withstand rating is baked into the mechanical design and cannot be retrofitted in the field.
  • Support strength and bar stiffness. Moulded or cast-resin insulator supports rated for the computed cantilever load; bars oriented edge-on to the force where possible; phase spacing d chosen as a compromise (wider spacing cuts the force linearly but lengthens the board).
  • Resonance check. The magnetic force on a bar carrying a 60 Hz current pulsates at 120 Hz (force ∝ i² rectifies the waveform). The bar/support system's natural frequency must sit away from 120 Hz, or the fault shakes the structure at resonance and the type-tested margin evaporates.
  • Proof by type test, not by calculation. The Icw / ip withstand of an assembled board is demonstrated by an actual short-circuit type test on the design (IEC 61439 series at LV, IEC 62271-200 at MV). The certificate is design-specific: change the bar size, spacing or supports and the certificate no longer applies.

Arc-resistant enclosures. Withstand ratings assume the fault current flows in the bars. The nastier case is the internal arcing fault — a tool, a rodent, tracking across polluted insulation (a real consideration at 50 °C with wind-blown dust). For MV switchgear, IEC 62271-200 defines an Internal Arc Classification (IAC): the assembly is type-tested with a deliberately initiated internal arc at rated current (for this plant's class, an IAC rating of 31.5 kA / 1 s would match the board) and must satisfy criteria that amount to: doors stay shut, no fragments ejected, no burn-through of accessible surfaces, and cotton indicators placed where personnel would stand do not ignite. The classification letters state which sides are protected — A for authorised-personnel access, with F/L/R (front/lateral/rear) coverage. Construction features that achieve it: reinforced doors and hinges, pressure-relief flaps that vent the arc blast upward into a duct or plenum away from the operator, compartment segregation (busbar / breaker / cable boxes) so an arc in one compartment cannot involve the next, and in premium designs ultra-fast arc-detection that collapses or clears the arc in tens of milliseconds (§11). Equivalent LV practice uses arc-fault-contained assemblies tested to the IEC/TR guidance under the IEC 61439 umbrella. Catalogue families in Part 6 — MV switchgear catalogue.

Field check. The IAC rating, like Icw, is on the nameplate and in the type-test certificate — and it is only valid with the enclosure as tested: doors closed and latched, pressure flaps unobstructed, gland plates fitted, venting path clear. A propped-open door or a blocked relief duct quietly deletes the arc rating without changing a single nameplate.

Seeing the Fault: Records, Oscillography and the PMS

Control

Prospective fault current cannot be "controlled" in the feedback sense — it is set by the network. What the control and monitoring layer does is three things: configure the network so the duty stays inside ratings, record every fault that actually happens, and turn those records into verification of the study this whole section is built on.

  • Configuration enforcement. The plant's power management system (PMS) and the switchgear interlocking together hold the fault level down: the two-of-three incomer/tie interlock at the LV-MSB prevents the 77 kA parallel configuration except during a deliberate transfer, and the PMS mimic shows at a glance whether the tie is closed. The DG (which adds its own subtransient contribution when paralleled) is connected through the ATS in open transition, so it never feeds a bus in parallel with SEC. Any future closed-transition or parallel-running modification is, by definition, a fault-level modification.
  • Oscillography in relays and trip units. Every modern MV protection IED, and the electronic trip units of the LV incomer ACBs, capture a disturbance record on every trip: sampled current and voltage waveforms (typically COMTRADE format at MV), pre-fault and post-fault, plus an event log time-stamped to the station clock. On that record you can see everything this section calculated — the symmetrical RMS level, the DC offset and its decay (hence the real X/R), the first peak, the motor contribution as an initial bump that decays over the first few cycles, and the exact clearing time.
  • PMS event recording. The PMS aggregates relay events, breaker status changes and analogue snapshots across the plant onto one synchronised timeline, so the sequence "feeder relay picked up → feeder breaker opened in 65 ms → incomer never tripped" can be reconstructed without walking from panel to panel. Accurate time synchronisation (GPS/NTP to the relays) is what makes the sequence trustworthy.
  • Using fault records after an incident. The post-incident drill: (1) freeze and download the records from every device that saw the fault, before they are overwritten; (2) read the actual fault magnitude off the oscillography and compare it with the study's predicted value for that location — a real 30 kA fault where the study predicted 20 kA means the model (or the SEC fault level) is wrong, and every rating verified against the study is now in question; (3) verify the protection operated per the grading plan (§10); (4) check the cleared duty against the interrupting device's rating and its cumulative-duty maintenance counter — breakers earn inspection after heavy interruptions; (5) file the record with the study, so the next revision starts from measured reality.
Every real fault is a free, full-scale validation test of the short-circuit study. A plant that downloads, reads and archives its relay oscillography knows its true fault levels; a plant that just resets the flag and re-closes is operating on an unverified model.

The Bus-by-Bus Fault Table: How a Study Is Actually Built

Sizing method

"Sizing" for short-circuit means establishing the prospective fault duty at every bus, then proving every device on that bus survives and (where required) interrupts it. The procedure, step by step:

  1. Fix the sources. Get the SEC intake fault level in writing (design value here: 25 kA at 13.8 kV; SEC range 25–40 kA — use the declared maximum for ratings). Add on-site sources: DG subtransient contribution when it can be connected, and running-motor contribution at each LV bus.
  2. Model the impedances. Grid, transformers (Z = 6 % on 1600 kVA), significant cable runs. Convert to a common base (per-unit / MVA method).
  3. Compute per bus, worst credible configuration. For each bus: initial symmetrical Ik", peak ip (κ·sqrt(2)·Ik" with the X/R of that bus), and thermal equivalent for the protection clearing time. The Rev 3 headline figures: MV board ≈ 25 kA, LV-MSB ≈ 38.5 kA one transformer, ≈ 77 kA tie-closed — which is exactly why the tie is normally open and interlocked.
  4. Build the fault table (bus → Ik", ip, duty time) and check every device against it: switchgear Icw (MV: 31.5 kA / 3 s) and peak withstand, breaker Icu/Ics (§14), busbar bracing, cable adiabatic limits (§7), and the protection settings that assume these currents (§10).
  5. Margin and document. Select the next standard rating above the computed duty (here 50–65 kA class at the LV-MSB), state the configuration assumptions on the study cover sheet, and put the study under document control.
  6. Re-study triggers: any new source (DG paralleling, solar/BESS), any transformer change, any tie-philosophy change, or an SEC network reinforcement letter. A certified IEC 60909 (SASO-adopted) study by a competent engineer governs over the hand numbers in this manual — all values here are indicative.

Self-check — Short-Circuit

  • Compute the LV bolted-fault current for one 1600 kVA, Z = 6 % transformer (answer: 2309 / 0.06 ≈ 38.5 kA — indicative) and for the tie-closed case (≈ 77 kA).
  • Convert the SEC 25 kA at 13.8 kV intake into short-circuit MVA (answer: ≈ 598 MVA) and explain what the number means physically.
  • Explain why the first current peak is higher than the symmetrical RMS value and what X/R has to do with it; state the first half-cycle time at 60 Hz (8.33 ms).
  • List the three withstand/interrupt checks a fault table drives on one LV bus (breaker Icu/Ics, busbar/switchgear Icw and peak, cable adiabatic).
  • Name three plant changes that invalidate the current short-circuit study.

14 — Circuit Breakers — Settings, Technology & Sizing Core

Goal: understand what a circuit breaker actually does during a fault, set the LSIG trip unit of the LV-MSB incomer ACB from first principles, read a breaker nameplate without guessing (Icu, Ics, Icw, Icm), and select a breaker step by step for the Rev 3 plant basis (13.8 kV / 400-230 V, 60 Hz, 50 °C design ambient, 38.5 kA LV fault level).

This section is the deep dive behind two earlier ones: the fault currents come from §4 — Short-Circuit Current & Switchgear Withstand, and the grading philosophy comes from §10 — Protection Coordination & Selectivity. Here we go inside the device itself.

Indicative data. Every numeric value in this section — settings, clearing times, derating factors, fault magnitudes — is indicative and chosen for teaching on the Rev 3 plant basis. The plant's formal short-circuit, coordination and arc-flash studies govern. Standards cited are the SASO-adopted editions of IEC 60947-2 (LV circuit breakers) and IEC 62271-100 (MV AC circuit breakers), applied together with SBC 401 and the SEC Distribution Code.

The Contract Between Brains and Muscle

Why this exists

Almost every component in the plant is designed to do one thing well under normal conditions: a cable carries current, a transformer changes voltage, a busbar distributes power. The circuit breaker is the only device in the entire installation with a contradictory job description. For years at a time it must behave like a near-perfect conductor — carrying 2 309 A through the LV-MSB incomer with contact resistance measured in micro-ohms, generating almost no heat, asking for nothing. Then, perhaps once in its whole service life, it must do the opposite: deliberately destroy a current seventeen times larger than anything it has ever carried, inside a few hundredths of a second, and survive to tell the protection relay it has done so.

No other device can do this. A fuse can interrupt a fault, but only once, and it cannot be told when. A contactor can switch load current thousands of times, but ask it to break 38.5 kA and it will weld shut or explode. An isolator can carry fault current but must never attempt to break even load current. The circuit breaker is the unique combination: it carries, it makes (closes onto), and it breaks both load and fault current, repeatedly, on command.

That phrase "on command" is the heart of this section. A modern protection scheme is a partnership between two very different things:

  • The brains — protection relays and electronic trip units (§10, relay catalogue). They measure, compare, time, and decide. They handle milliamps and microjoules. A relay deciding to trip is an event of almost no physical consequence — a transistor changes state, an output contact closes.
  • The muscle — the circuit breaker. It does no thinking at all. When the trip command arrives, springs holding tens of kilograms of stored energy are released, contacts part at several metres per second, and an electric arc burning at thousands of kelvin is stretched, split, cooled and extinguished — all in roughly the time it takes a camera flash to fire.

The contract between them is brutally simple: the brains promise to ask only for what the muscle can deliver, and the muscle promises to deliver every time it is asked. Every calculation in this section serves one side of that contract or the other. The LSIG setting study (worked calculation below) makes sure the brains ask at the right current and the right time. The rating checks (Icu ≥ 38.5 kA, Icw through the short-time delay) make sure the muscle can physically honour the request. A coordination study that assumes a breaker clears in 50 ms is worthless if the breaker's mechanism is seized; a perfectly maintained breaker is equally worthless if its trip unit is set so high it never receives the command.

A circuit breaker is the plant's only device designed to both carry a fault current and deliberately destroy it. Protection settings are a promise made on the breaker's behalf — every pickup and delay you choose assumes the mechanism underneath will actually operate. Set the brains and verify the muscle; one without the other is a paper protection scheme.

A Failure Story: the Breaker That Couldn't

What goes wrong without it

The following narrative is fictional but assembled from real failure modes reported across the industry. Every number is consistent with the Rev 3 plant basis. Read it once for the drama, then a second time noting how many separate defences had already failed before the fault ever happened.

The setup. It is a Tuesday afternoon in August, 48 °C in the shade. The plant has run for nine years. The LV-MSB incomer ACB from TX-1 has not been exercised — opened and closed — in four years; "it works, don't touch it." Its lubricant has hardened in the heat and dust. Last year, during a transformer changeover, an operator closed the bus-tie BT and never reopened it, so both 1 600 kVA transformers have been feeding the bus in parallel for eleven months. Nobody noticed, because everything works — in parallel the bus simply runs stiffer and happier. The switchboard is rated 50 kA. The prospective fault level with the tie closed is ≈ 77 kA (§4).

The fault. A 25-year-old cable termination on an outgoing way, cooked by years of 50 °C ambient plus load heating, finally tracks across its degraded insulation. A phase-to-phase arc strikes inside the cable compartment, a few hundred millimetres from the main busbars.

TimeWhat is happening
t = 0 ms The arc strikes. Current climbs toward the prospective value within the first quarter-cycle. Because two transformers feed the bus, the driving impedance is half the design assumption: the fault current heads for ≈ 70+ kA symmetrical, not 38.5 kA.
t ≈ 4 ms First asymmetrical peak: with a typical X/R the peak approaches 2.1 × 70 kA ≈ 150 kA. Electromagnetic force between adjacent busbars spikes to tens of kilonewtons per metre — the bars try to tear free of their supports. The bracing was type-tested for a 50 kA board; it holds, barely, deforming.
t ≈ 8 ms The feeder breaker's trip unit has already decided — the current is far above its instantaneous pickup. The trip command fires. But the fault is on the supply side of the feeder breaker's terminals: opening it changes nothing. The fault belongs to the incomer zone.
t ≈ 15 ms Both incomer trip units (current sharing through the closed tie) see well above their 25 kA instantaneous pickups and fire their actuators. In incomer 1, the trip coil pushes the latch. The latch, riding on grease that has turned to varnish, moves 2 mm and stops. The mechanism does not unlatch. Incomer 2's mechanism does operate — its contacts part at t ≈ 45 ms.
t ≈ 60 ms Incomer 2 attempts to interrupt. But it is breaking a current near or beyond its 50 kA ultimate rating (its share of a 77 kA bus fault plus asymmetry). The arc in its chutes does not extinguish at the first current zero, nor the second. Arc chute plates erode; metal vapour fills the interrupter. At t ≈ 80 ms it finally clears — at its design limit, this breaker is now scrap (that is what "Icu, ultimate" means — see ratings decoded). Half the fault current is gone. The other half, through the stuck incomer 1, keeps flowing.
t = 100–500 ms The arc, fed now at ≈ 38 kA through the stuck breaker, burns freely in the cable compartment at around 9 MW (next sub-section shows where this number comes from). It vaporises copper — 1 cm³ of copper becomes roughly 60 000 cm³ of conductive vapour — which the pressure wave drives along the busbar chamber, turning a single-compartment fault into a whole-board fault. The compartment doors, never designed as arc-rated, bulge.
t ≈ 500 ms Upstream, the MV transformer-feeder relay [52-T] on MV-SWGR — the backup brains, graded at 0.5 s per the §10 ladder — times out and trips the MV vacuum breaker. That breaker, maintained and healthy, clears in ≈ 60 ms.
t ≈ 560 ms Current stops. Total arc energy delivered: on the order of 4–5 MJ — the energy of roughly a kilogram of TNT, released as heat, light, pressure and molten metal inside a sheet-steel enclosure. The LV-MSB is destroyed end to end. Replacement switchboard lead time: months. If anyone was standing at the board, the story is far worse — see §11 — Arc Flash.

Count the failed defences: a bus-tie left closed (fault level doubled past the board rating), a breaker mechanism seized through lack of exercise, and a healthy breaker asked to interrupt beyond its rating. The protection relays — the brains — performed flawlessly throughout. Every trip command was issued correctly and on time. The catastrophe happened entirely on the muscle side of the contract.

The two breaker failure modes that matter most are invisible in normal operation: a mechanism that will not open (no trip despite a healthy trip command), and a breaker applied above its interrupting rating (trips, but fails to clear). Neither announces itself until the one moment that matters. This is why breaker exercise, secondary-injection testing of trip units, and fault-level verification (§4) appear in every assessment checklist.

The Physics in Plain Numbers

Feel for the numbers

Before setting a single pickup, build an honest physical feel for what a breaker is asked to control. Four estimates, each one you can redo on a phone calculator. Basis: 400 V bus, prospective bolted fault 38.5 kA (one transformer, §4), 60 Hz, so one cycle = 1/60 s ≈ 16.7 ms.

Estimate 1 — Power and energy of the arc

A bolted fault dissipates little energy at the fault point itself (the voltage across a solid metallic short is near zero — the energy burns in the transformer and cables upstream). The dangerous case at the fault location is the arcing fault. Two honest, typical figures for a 400 V arc in air: arcing current ≈ 60 % of bolted (the arc adds impedance), and arc voltage ≈ 130 V per phase:

Iarc ≈ 0.6 × 38 500 A ≈ 23 000 A Parc ≈ 3 × Varc × Iarc = 3 × 130 V × 23 000 A ≈ 9.0 MW

Nine megawatts — about four and a half times the entire plant's 2 MW demand — concentrated in a plasma column a few centimetres long. Now the energy for two different clearing times:

One cycle (16.7 ms):   E = 9.0 MW × 0.0167 s ≈ 150 kJ Slow backup (0.3 s):   E = 9.0 MW × 0.3 s ≈ 2.7 MJ

Sanity check & comparison: 150 kJ is the kinetic energy of a 1.2-tonne car at 57 km/h (½ × 1 200 × 15.8² ≈ 150 kJ) — a serious crash, absorbed by the switchboard internals, but survivable for the equipment. 2.7 MJ is eighteen times that: the heat of a 2 kW kettle running for 22 minutes released in a third of a second, or the energy of roughly 0.6 kg of TNT (TNT ≈ 4.6 MJ/kg). The difference between those two outcomes is nothing but clearing time — which is why §11 calls clearing time "the lever", and why every intentional delay we add in the LSIG study below has to buy something worth its cost in energy.

Estimate 2 — Thermal let-through, I²t

For the conductors carrying the fault (not the arc itself), the damage currency is I²t — amp-squared-seconds — because conductor heating during a fault is adiabatic (no time to cool). At the full bolted current:

I²t (1 cycle) = (38 500)² × 0.0167 ≈ 2.5 × 10&sup7; A²s I²t (0.3 s)   = (38 500)² × 0.3 ≈ 4.4 × 10&sup8; A²s

Convert that to copper using the adiabatic equation from §6 — Cable Sizing (k ≈ 143 for XLPE/copper):

Amin (1 cycle) = √(2.5 × 10&sup7;) / 143 ≈ 4 980 / 143 ≈ 35 mm² Amin (0.3 s)   = √(4.4 × 10&sup8;) / 143 ≈ 21 100 / 143 ≈ 148 mm²

Sanity check: a fault cleared in one cycle can be survived by a 35 mm² copper conductor; the same fault held for 0.3 s needs 148 mm² — more than four times the cross-section — just to avoid melting its insulation. Every tenth of a second of intentional delay is paid for in copper somewhere downstream. This is the quantitative reason fast, current-limiting breakers allow smaller cables and why the short-time delay on an incomer is kept as short as grading permits.

Estimate 3 — Mechanical force on the conductors

Parallel conductors carrying current in opposite directions repel. The first asymmetrical peak at 60 Hz is about 2.1 × the symmetrical RMS value (typical LV X/R):

ipeak ≈ 2.1 × 38.5 kA ≈ 81 kA

Force per metre between two busbars spaced d = 60 mm apart:

F/L = 2 × 10⁻⁷ × i² / d = 2 × 10⁻⁷ × (81 000)² / 0.06 ≈ 21 900 N/m ≈ 22 kN per metre

Sanity check & comparison: 22 kN/m is equivalent to hanging a 2.2-tonne SUV from every metre of busbar — applied not gradually but in 4 milliseconds, and reversing direction 120 times per second. (Using 2 × 10⁻⁷ as μ₀/2π in SI units.) This is what the busbar bracing withstand and the breaker's own through-fault rating protect against, and why the breaker contact system needs the special geometry described under technology just to stay closed while carrying a fault it has been told not to interrupt yet.

Estimate 4 — Why interrupting in < 50 ms is remarkable

List what must happen, in series, for an ACB to clear a 38.5 kA fault "instantaneously":

sense & decide (≈ 10 ms) + unlatch & part contacts (≈ 25–40 ms) + arc to current zero (≈ 8–17 ms)

The trip unit must sample the current wave long enough to be sure (about half a cycle), fire an actuator, release spring energy that accelerates a contact assembly of around a kilogram to several metres per second, draw an arc whose core temperature reaches the order of 10 000–20 000 K (hotter than the surface of the sun at ≈ 5 800 K), drive that arc into a chute that stretches and splits it until its voltage exceeds the 400 V driving it, and then — at a current zero, which at 60 Hz arrives every 8.3 ms — win a microsecond-scale race between the recovering dielectric strength of the contact gap and the recovering system voltage trying to restrike the arc. Total: roughly 40–80 ms, all of it inside one human eye-blink (100–150 ms).

Sanity check: the breaker controls, in two cycles, an instantaneous power (9 MW arc) that the plant's entire generation contract cannot match, using stored spring energy of only a few hundred joules. It wins not by overpowering the arc but by out-engineering it — raising arc voltage and exploiting natural current zeros. That is why the interrupting medium and contact geometry (technology section) matter as much as the settings.

One number to carry in your head: at this plant's LV fault level, every additional 0.1 s of arcing adds roughly 0.9 MJ — a quarter-stick of dynamite — to the energy released in the switchboard. Settings buy selectivity with time; time costs energy. The whole LSIG study below is the management of that trade.

Worked LSIG Setting Study — LV-MSB Incomer ACB

Worked calculation

We now set, from first principles, the four protection elements — Long-time, Short-time, Instantaneous, Ground-fault — of the electronic trip unit on the LV-MSB incomer ACB. Every choice is tested against the things that must not trip it (load, motor re-acceleration) and the things that must (overload, bus faults), and against the downstream devices it must not race.

Drawing basis note. This diagram was drawn for the original 13.8 kV / 400 V / 60 Hz reference design and has not been re-rendered. For this Saudi (SEC) Rev 3 edition read 13.8 kV as 13.8 kV, 400 V as 400 V and 60 Hz as 60 Hz. Topology, tags and protection philosophy are unchanged.

The cast (indicative ratings):

DeviceLocationFrame / InTrip unitCarries
Incomer ACB LV-MSB incomer from TX-1 3 200 A frame, sensor/rating plug In = 2 500 A Electronic LSIG Transformer LV FLC = 2 309 A
Feeder MCCB LV-MSB outgoing to MCC-A 400 A, electronic LSI L + S + I MCC running load ≈ 300 A
MCC outgoing MCCB MCC-A, largest motor starter 160 A, thermal-magnetic Thermal + fixed magnetic 75 kW motor, FLC ≈ 135 A

Known fault levels (from §4 plus one cable-impedance estimate we make below): 38.5 kA bolted at the LV-MSB bus; ≈ 20 kA at the MCC-A bus; ≈ 5 kA at the 75 kW motor terminals (long motor cable). Bus-tie normally open, so 38.5 kA is the design case.

Step 0 — Fault current at the MCC bus (needed for the I-pickup check)

Source impedance per phase seen at the 400 V bus, from the 38.5 kA bolted level:

Zsource = U / (√3 × Isc) = 400 / (1.732 × 38 500) ≈ 6.0 mΩ (mostly reactive; take R ≈ 0.6, X ≈ 6.0)

Feeder cable to MCC-A, indicative: 100 m of 2 × 185 mm² Cu/XLPE per phase (sized per §6 with the 0.82 air-derating at 50 °C):

Rcable ≈ 0.099 Ω/km × 0.1 km / 2 ≈ 5.0 mΩ;   Xcable ≈ 0.08 Ω/km × 0.1 km / 2 ≈ 4.0 mΩ Ztotal = √((0.6+5.0)² + (6.0+4.0)²) = √(5.6² + 10²) ≈ 11.5 mΩ Isc,MCC = 231 V / 0.0115 Ω ≈ 20 kA

Sanity check: 100 m of LV cable roughly doubled the impedance and halved the fault current (38.5 → 20 kA) — typical of LV systems, where cable impedance dominates within tens of metres. This number decides whether the incomer's instantaneous element can "see into" the feeder's zone.

Step 1 — L (long-time) pickup Ir and band

The L element is the thermal guardian of the transformer and busbar. It must sit just above the legitimate full-load current and below the conductor capability.

Ir = 0.95 × In = 0.95 × 2 500 = 2 375 A Check vs load:  Ir / IFLC = 2 375 / 2 309 = 1.03 → 3 % margin above transformer FLC ✓

Why 0.95 and not 1.0? At 1.0 × (2 500 A) the breaker would permit an 8 % continuous overload of the 1 600 kVA transformer before responding. 0.95 gives close overload protection while the 3 % margin absorbs metering tolerance and normal load variation without nuisance alarms. (The transformer's own protections and the N-1 philosophy are covered in §2.)

Long-time band: set tr = 12 s at 6 × Ir on an I²t curve. Two checks:

Moderate overload 1.5 × Ir: t ≈ 12 × (6/1.5)² = 192 s ≈ 3.2 min Re-acceleration 2.4 × Ir (see below): t ≈ 12 × (6/2.4)² = 75 s

Sanity checks: (a) ~3 minutes at 150 % is comfortably inside a distribution transformer's short-time overload tolerance, so the breaker protects the transformer rather than the other way round. (b) Inrush: note carefully — the LV incomer never sees transformer inrush; that flows through the MV breaker when the transformer is energised and is the MV relay's problem (§10). What the LV incomer does see is motor re-acceleration: after a voltage dip or an ATS retransfer, every running motor draws starting-level current simultaneously. Estimate bus current ≈ 2.5 × FLC ≈ 5 800 A ≈ 2.4 × Ir for up to ~5 s. The L curve gives 75 s at that level — a 15× margin over the 5 s event. No nuisance trip. ✓

Step 2 — S (short-time) pickup Isd and delay tsd

The S element is the selectivity tool: high enough to ignore anything that is not a fault, delayed enough to let downstream devices clear their own faults first, yet fast enough to limit energy when it must act as backup.

Isd = 4 × Ir = 4 × 2 375 = 9 500 A;   tsd = 0.3 s, definite time (I²t OFF)

Pickup checks — must NOT operate for:

Worst legitimate surge = re-acceleration ≈ 5 800 A → margin 9 500 / 5 800 = 1.6 ✓ Full load + largest DOL start = 2 309 + (6 × 135) ≈ 3 100 A << 9 500 A ✓

Pickup check — MUST operate for: the smallest fault it is meant to back up is a fault at the MCC bus, ≈ 20 kA = 2.1 × pickup ✓ (solid detection even with ±10 % trip-unit tolerance).

Delay check: the downstream 400 A feeder MCCB clears its zone faults in ≤ 0.1 s (its own S delay) or < 0.05 s (its instantaneous). Grading margin:

Δt = 0.3 − 0.1 = 0.2 s ≥ breaker clearing (≈ 0.08 s) + tolerances (≈ 0.06 s) + safety ✓

Why not longer than 0.3 s? Two reasons. Energy: from Estimate 1, every extra 0.1 s of bus-fault arcing adds ≈ 0.9 MJ. Grading headroom: the MV transformer-feeder relay [52-T] must still sit a full margin above the LV incomer. With tsd = 0.3 s, [52-T] grades at ≈ 0.6 s and the MV incomer at ≈ 0.9 s. (§10's IDMT illustration lands at ≈ 0.38 s for the LV incomer at the same 38.5 kA bus fault — slower than this study's definite-time 0.30 s because an inverse curve must also ride through load-side surges; the schemes differ, not the philosophy. Both are indicative; the plant's formal coordination study reconciles the full chain.)

I²t OFF (definite time)? Yes here: the downstream device is an electronic-trip MCCB with its own definite-time S band, so flat-over-flat grading is clean and predictable. I²t ON would be chosen instead if grading over fuses or thermal-magnetic curves that bend — see settings.

Step 3 — I (instantaneous) pickup Ii

Ii = 10 × In = 10 × 2 500 = 25 000 A = 25 kA

The reasoning, in both directions:

Must see a genuine bus fault: 25 kA < 38.5 kA → bus-zone fault trips with no intentional delay ✓ Must NOT see feeder-zone faults: 25 kA > 20 kA (MCC bus fault, Step 0) → no overreach ✓

This is the elegant trick of an incomer instantaneous: cable impedance guarantees that only faults electrically close to the busbar itself can exceed 25 kA. Anything beyond the first metres of an outgoing cable is attenuated below pickup and is left to the feeder breaker (with the 0.3 s S element as backup). So genuine bus faults — the highest-energy, highest-arc-flash events — are cleared in ≈ 50–80 ms instead of 0.3 s, cutting their energy by a factor of four to six (§11), at zero cost to selectivity.

Tolerance sanity check: with −10 % pickup tolerance the element could operate from 22.5 kA; the MCC-bus fault is 20 kA, so margin remains (22.5 / 20 = 1.13) — adequate, but flag it: if the final study shortens the feeder cable or fattens its conductors, recheck this margin. If it erodes, raise Ii to 12 × or rely on ZSI instead (settings).

Motor contribution check: for an upstream (transformer-side) fault, the plant's running motors back-feed briefly through the incomer at roughly 4 × their combined FLC — a few kA, decaying within 1–2 cycles — well below 25 kA. No spurious operation. ✓

Step 4 — G (ground fault) pickup Ig and delay

Ig = 0.2 × In = 0.2 × 2 500 = 500 A;   tg = 0.2 s (I²t OFF), ZSI-enabled

The LV system is solidly earthed TN-S, so a bolted earth fault is large — but the faults that kill switchboards are arcing earth faults at a few hundred amps: too small for S or I, and small enough for L to tolerate for minutes while they burn. The G element (vector sum of the three phase CTs plus neutral CT) catches exactly this gap. 500 A is ≈ 22 % of FLC — far below any phase element, far above normal unbalance leakage. It grades over the feeder MCCB's own G element (set ≈ 120 A / 0.1 s, indicative) with the same 0.2 s logic as Step 2. (The MV-side earth-fault story — the 400 A NER — is separate and lives in §12.)

Settings summary — incomer ACB (indicative, study governs):

L — long-time pickup Ir0.95 × In = 2 375 A, band tr = 12 s @ 6 × Ir (I²t)
S — short-time pickup Isd4 × Ir = 9 500 A, tsd = 0.3 s, I²t OFF, ZSI
I — instantaneous Ii10 × In = 25 kA
G — ground fault Ig0.2 × In = 500 A, tg = 0.2 s, I²t OFF, ZSI

The coordination ladder — a TCC in words

A time-current coordination (TCC) chart plots every device's tripping band on log-log axes of current and time; selectivity means the bands never cross. The table below is that chart rendered as text: three fault scenarios, read across to see what each device does, who clears, and how long the fault lasts.

Scenario (current through the chain) MCC outgoing MCCB
(160 A TM; thermal 135 A, magnetic ≈ 1.6 kA)		 Feeder MCCB 400 A
(Ir 400 A; Isd 1.6 kA @ 0.1 s; Ii 8 kA)		 Incomer ACB, 3 200 A frame / 2 500 A sensor
(Ir 2 375 A; Isd 9.5 kA @ 0.3 s; Ii 25 kA)		 Who clears, and when
Sustained overload, 1.2 × motor FLC (≈ 162 A) — e.g. jammed conveyor Thermal element picks up (162 > 135 A); trips in minutes (thermal curve, ≈ 2–10 min at 1.2 ×) Sees 162 A within ≈ 300 A MCC load — below Ir = 400 A: no pickup, ever Bus total ≈ 2 340 A < 2 375 A: no pickup MCC outgoing only, after minutes. One motor lost; MCC, board and plant stay up.
5 kA fault at the 75 kW motor terminals (end of its cable run) Magnetic element (1.6 kA) operates: clears in < 0.03 s (TM MCCB, part current-limiting) S picks up (5 > 1.6 kA) and starts its 0.1 s timer — but the fault is gone at 0.03 s: timer resets. Backup only. 5 kA = 2.1 × Ir: only the L curve sees it (≈ 98 s notional). Deep backup. MCC outgoing, < 0.03 s. Two graded backups armed behind it. Only the motor circuit is lost.
38.5 kA bolted fault on the LV-MSB busbar itself No through-current (fault is upstream of it) — brief motor back-feed only No through-current — brief motor back-feed only 38.5 > 25 kA: instantaneous element fires; total clearing ≈ 0.05–0.08 s Incomer ACB, ≈ 0.06 s. Whole board lost (necessarily — it is the faulted zone), but energy held to ≈ 150 kJ-class, not MJ-class.

And the intermediate case worked in Step 0 — a 20 kA fault at the MCC-A bus — completes the picture: the feeder MCCB's instantaneous (8 kA) clears it in < 0.05 s; the incomer's S element (9.5 kA pickup, 0.3 s) is picked up and timing as backup but is beaten by 0.25 s; the incomer's I element (25 kA) correctly never sees it. Every rung of the ladder has exactly one primary device and at least one timed backup.

Read the ladder bottom-up and it says one thing: the closer the fault, the smaller the blackout and the faster the clearance — overloads cost one motor after minutes; cable faults cost one feeder after milliseconds; only a genuine busbar fault takes the board, and even then in under a tenth of a second. That property is not an accident of the hardware; it was constructed, number by number, in Steps 1–4.

What the Knobs Actually Do

Equipment settings

The study above used L, S, I and G as given. Here is what each element physically is, and what the secondary switches (time bands, I²t, ZSI, maintenance mode) change.

L — long time (ANSI 49, overload). A thermal model of the protected conductor, computed in software: the trip unit integrates I² over time, mimicking how the transformer winding or busbar actually heats, including cooling between overloads (thermal memory). It is the electronic descendant of the bimetal strip. Its two knobs are the pickup Ir (where heating starts to count) and the band tr (how much thermal inertia the model assumes). Slow by design — seconds to hours.

S — short time (ANSI 51, delayed short-circuit). A definite-time or inverse-time element whose entire purpose is selectivity: it recognises a fault immediately but deliberately waits (here 0.3 s) to give downstream devices first refusal. The breaker must carry the full fault current, fully closed, during this wait — which is exactly what the Icw short-time withstand rating certifies (see ratings decoded). An incomer without adequate Icw physically cannot offer a short-time delay.

I — instantaneous (ANSI 50). No intentional delay: pickup, fire. Reserved for currents so large they can only be a fault inside the breaker's own zone (Step 3's logic). It also serves as the breaker's self-preservation reflex — many MCCBs add a fixed, non-adjustable override above the settable range so the device always protects itself near its interrupting limit.

G — ground fault (ANSI 51N/50N). Operates on the vector sum of the phase (and neutral, in TN-S) currents, which is zero unless current is leaking to earth. Catches the low-current arcing earth faults invisible to L, S and I. Pickup is set as a fraction of In (here 0.2 ×), with its own short delay for grading.

Time bands and I²t in/out. Each delayed element offers a family of curves. With I²t OFF the S element is a flat shelf — same delay at any current above pickup — ideal for grading electronic device over electronic device, as in our study. With I²t ON the shelf tilts into a slope (trip time falls as current rises), shaped to nest above the bending curves of fuses and thermal-magnetic MCCBs without crossing them. Rule of thumb: flat over flat, slope over curve.

ZSI — zone-selective interlocking. The cleverest setting on the unit, and the answer to the painful trade in Step 2 (delay buys selectivity but costs energy). A pilot wire runs from each downstream trip unit to its upstream neighbour. When a downstream device picks up, it sends a restraint signal upstream: "this fault is in my zone — hold your full delay as backup." If the upstream device picks up and receives no restraint, the fault must be in its own zone — and it trips at a minimum delay (≈ 0.05–0.08 s) regardless of its set tsd. Result: busbar faults clear near-instantaneously at every level of the board, while feeder faults still enjoy fully graded backup. Fail-safe: a cut pilot wire means no restraint is ever received — devices simply revert to their set delays, losing speed but never selectivity. ZSI on the S and G elements of the incomer and feeders is cheap insurance and a major arc-flash lever (§11).

Anatomy of a trip — where the milliseconds go. "Clearing time" on a TCC is the sum of three distinct physical stages:

StageWhat happensTypical ACB (indicative)
1. Decision Trip unit samples the wave, confirms pickup, fires the trip actuator ≈ 10 ms (instantaneous element)
2. Opening Actuator strikes the latch; spring energy releases; contacts accelerate apart ≈ 25–40 ms
3. Arcing Arc drawn, driven into the chute, extinguished at a current zero (zeros every 8.3 ms at 60 Hz) ≈ 8–17 ms
Total clearing Fault inception to current zero ≈ 40–80 ms on instantaneous

Add any intentional delay (tsd, tg) before stage 2. This anatomy is why grading margins are ≈ 0.2–0.3 s, not 0.05 s: the upstream device must outwait the downstream device's total clearing, plus both devices' tolerances, plus a safety allowance (§10).

Maintenance mode / arc-flash reduction switch. Modern trip units provide a switchable alternate setting group — often a dedicated padlockable switch with a pilot light — that temporarily drops the instantaneous pickup (e.g. to ≈ 2 × In, no delay) while personnel work near the energised board. Selectivity is knowingly sacrificed for those hours; in exchange, any fault clears in stage-1+2+3 time only, collapsing incident energy by a factor of four or more exactly as computed in §11 — the clearing-time lever. The discipline is procedural: switch in before the work, switch out after — an assessment should check both that the feature exists and that there is a procedure governing it.

Field habit: photograph the trip-unit setting screen/dials of every ACB and outgoing MCCB during an assessment, and compare against the coordination study table. Settings drift — a night-shift "temporary" change to stop a nuisance trip becomes permanent — and a photo is the fastest honest record.

How the Hardware Kills an Arc

Equipment technology

Every breaker faces the same physics problem: once contacts part under load, the current does not stop — it leaps the gap as an arc, a self-sustaining plasma at 5 000–20 000 K that is, inconveniently, a rather good conductor. AC offers one gift: the current passes through zero twice per cycle (every 8.3 ms at 60 Hz). At each zero the arc momentarily dies; whether it stays dead is a race between the gap's recovering dielectric strength and the system's recovering voltage. Every interrupting technology is a different strategy for winning that race — or, at LV, for not even waiting for it.

Air — the LV workhorse (ACB arc chutes). An LV breaker can do something its MV cousins cannot: beat the arc with voltage. An arc in air drops roughly 20–30 V at each anode and cathode surface it touches. The ACB's arc chute is a stack of steel splitter plates; magnetic forces (from the arc's own current, shaped by the runner geometry) drive the arc up into the stack, where it is sliced into a dozen or more short arcs in series. A dozen arcs × ≈ 25 V each, plus the lengthened arc column, pushes the total arc voltage above the 400 V driving the fault. The source can no longer sustain the current: it is forced down toward zero ahead of the natural zero, and at the zero the cool, fragmented gap wins the recovery race easily. The same principle in miniature lives inside every MCCB.

Current limiting — refusing to carry the blow. A current-limiting MCCB goes further: its contact arms are shaped as opposed parallel loops, so fault current itself generates an electrodynamic repulsion that blows the contacts open within the first few milliseconds — before the trip unit has even finished deciding. The fast-rising arc voltage caps the current below its prospective peak (the "cut-off" current) and chops the I²t let-through to a fraction. Recall Estimate 2: let-through I²t is the damage currency for every cable and busbar downstream — current limiting is why a well-chosen MCCB can protect a cable smaller than the prospective fault would otherwise demand, and the basis of cascade/backup arrangements (IEC 60947-2 Annex A) where an upstream limiter lets a downstream breaker be applied above its stand-alone rating, per the manufacturer's tested tables only.

Vacuum — the MV standard. At 13.8 kV the arc voltage trick fails — no practical chute reaches kilovolts of arc drop — so MV breakers must win the current-zero race instead, and nothing recovers like a vacuum. The contacts sit inside a sealed ceramic bottle at ≈ 10⁻⁵ Pa; a contact gap of only 6–20 mm suffices for 17.5 kV class. With no gas to ionise, the arc burns in metal vapour boiled off the contacts themselves; at current zero that vapour condenses back onto the contact surfaces within microseconds, and dielectric strength rebounds at kilovolts per microsecond — faster than any transient recovery voltage the system can produce. Contact faces use spiral or axial-magnetic-field geometries to keep the arc diffuse and spinning so it never anchors and craters one spot. One genuine vice: current chopping. The vacuum arc is so eager to die that it can snap off at 2–5 A before the natural zero; interrupting an inductive circuit, the trapped L·(di/dt) reappears as an overvoltage spike. Modern CuCr contact alloys chop low, but switching motors and unloaded transformers still warrants surge arresters or RC snubbers at the load — a standard checklist item on the MV-SWGR transformer feeders (MV switchgear catalogue).

SF₆ — brilliant and banished. Sulphur hexafluoride is a near-perfect interrupting medium: ≈ 3 × the dielectric strength of air, electronegative (it hoovers up free electrons at current zero), self-healing, arc-quenching. Puffer designs compress the gas with a piston stroke and blast it axially through the arc at current zero; rotating-arc and self-blast designs use the arc's own energy to drive the flow. For decades it owned MV/HV switchgear. The problem is environmental: SF₆ is the most potent greenhouse gas formally assessed — global-warming potential on the order of 23 500 × CO₂ over 100 years, with an atmospheric lifetime measured in millennia — so a single leaked kilogram equals about 23 tonnes of CO₂. Regulation (notably the EU F-gas regime, echoed in procurement specifications worldwide, including increasingly in GCC utility practice) is phasing it out of new switchgear. Replacements at MV: vacuum interrupters housed in dry-air / "clean-air" insulated enclosures, and fluoronitrile (C4-FN) gas mixtures. For this plant's 13.8 kV switchgear the practical guidance is simple: specify vacuum interruption with air or SF₆-free gas insulation; treat any legacy SF₆ gear as a managed asset with leak monitoring and certified end-of-life gas handling.

  Air (LV ACB/MCCB) Vacuum (MV) SF₆ (MV, legacy)
Typical application ≤ 690 V; ACBs to 6 300 A, MCCBs to 1 600 A 3.6–40.5 kV switchgear — today's MV default MV/HV; being phased out of new MV gear
How it kills the arc Splitter plates raise arc voltage above system voltage; forces current down Metal-vapour arc; near-instant dielectric recovery at current zero Gas blast de-ionises arc path at current zero
Strengths Cheap, visible, current-limiting variants, easy inspection Sealed for life, ~30 000+ operations, no fire load, minimal maintenance Compact, excellent dielectric, high ratings per break
Weaknesses Arc chute erosion, larger size, open-air arc products Current chopping overvoltages (mitigate with arresters/RC); bottle is non-repairable GWP ≈ 23 500 × CO₂; leak monitoring and end-of-life gas handling obligations
Field checks Chute condition, contact wear indicators, mechanism lubrication Vacuum integrity (hi-pot test), contact erosion gauge, mechanism travel/time Gas density/pressure gauge and alarm, leak records

MCCB trip technologies — thermal-magnetic vs electronic. The thermal-magnetic (TM) MCCB is honest clockwork: a bimetal strip heated by load current bends and unlatches (the L function, fixed by physics), and a magnetic armature attracts at ≈ 5–12 × rating and unlatches instantly (the I function, sometimes adjustable).
