  • Confirm DG nameplate kVA ≥ essential load (confirm essential load list exists and is current).
  • Check the ATS is set to auto-mode and confirm the utility-loss trigger setpoint (UV and time delay).
  • Verify the DG auto-start sequence: is the start signal hardwired from the ATS or via the PMS?
  • Locate the load build-up sequence document — priority groups, inter-step time delays, and total reconnect time. Does the DG have adequate kVA headroom for each step?
  • Check fuel level indicator and fuel tank capacity (days of autonomy at full essential load).
  • Review DG test log: monthly no-load test and quarterly on-load test are minimum good practice.
  • Confirm the DG output breaker interlock prevents parallel operation with the utility (anti-paralleling relay or mechanical interlock on the ATS).

What a deficiency looks like.

  • DG present but ATS is in manual mode — operator must manually transfer during an emergency.
  • No documented essential load list or load build-up sequence; DG may be overloaded on reconnect.
  • DG kVA less than the verified essential load — DG will trip on overload when all essential motors reconnect.
  • No anti-paralleling interlock — DG can be connected in parallel with a live utility supply without a synchroniser, risking DG damage or backfeed.
  • DG is the only source for the bus-tie control power — loss of utility before DG starts blacks out the bus-tie relay itself.

P-4 — Load Shedding / Load Management Philosophy

Principle. When available generation is less than demand (utility loss, N-1 transformer loading, running on DG), a load-shedding scheme automatically disconnects non-essential loads in a defined priority order to protect the power supply to the essential process. Load shedding is triggered by under-frequency (UF), under-voltage (UV), or source overload detection, and acts before the supply collapses — typically within 1–5 cycles on a GOOSE-based system or 2–5 s on a traditional under-frequency relay scheme.

Priority tiers (indicative — confirm per plant):

Tier Examples Action on shed trigger
Essential (never shed) Safety systems, fire pump, emergency lighting, DCS, UPS, DCDB, ventilation for hazardous areas Never disconnected by automatic shedding
Priority 1 — shed last Critical process pumps, compressors, main product line drives Shed only on deepest overload (Stage 3+)
Priority 2 Utility services (cooling towers, HVAC compressors, secondary pumps) Shed at Stage 2 of the scheme
Priority 3 — shed first Non-essential loads, office buildings, workshops, site lighting (non-safety) Shed immediately at Stage 1 (first trigger)

When it applies. Any plant with more load than a single source can carry (N-1 condition) or with a standby DG that is smaller than the total load.

How to verify on a site visit.

  • Confirm a written load-shedding philosophy document exists and is current.
  • Verify priority assignments match the actual process criticality — ask the plant engineer.
  • Check relay/PMS settings: UF setpoints on the 60 Hz base (e.g. 59.3 / 59.0 / 58.7 Hz staged — indicative; confirm against the Saudi Grid Code under-frequency bands), UV setpoints and time delays.
  • Confirm load-shedding actuators (trip coils on MCC feeders or contactors) are wired and commissioned.
  • Review test records showing the scheme has been function-tested within the last year.
  • Check that critical VFD-driven loads are not shed before the drives have had time to attempt ride-through (coordinate with P-6).

What a deficiency looks like.

  • No load-shedding scheme — on N-1 transformer loading the surviving transformer simply overloads and trips, causing a total blackout.
  • Priority list is notional only (written on a diagram note) but not wired to any relay output.
  • Essential loads (fire pump, DCS) included in the auto-shed trip chain — risk of shutting down safety systems automatically.
  • Single-stage shedding only — all non-essential loads shed simultaneously, causing a large load step that overshoots and sheds essential loads too.
  • UF/UV setpoints not coordinated with the generator AVR/governor response time — scheme sheds before the generator has had time to respond.

P-5 — No-Break Supplies: UPS & DC Control Battery (DCDB)

Principle. The control system, protection relays, DCS/PLC I/O and critical instrumentation must remain energised through any supply disturbance — bus transfer, DG start-up, voltage dip, or total blackout. Two independent no-break systems serve this:

  • UPS — online double-conversion UPS providing clean 230/400 V AC with zero-interruption transfer to battery. Feeds DCS/SCADA servers, PMS, operator consoles, critical instrumentation, communications, and emergency lighting.
  • DCDB — 110 V DC (or 48/24 V DC) battery-backed charger panel feeding MV and LV protection relay trip/close coils, circuit-breaker charging motors, and switchgear indication. Remains live even when the AC bus is dead.

Both systems must have sufficient battery autonomy to ride through the longest credible outage before DG pick-up and reconnection (minimum 30 min autonomy for DCS; 2 h or more for DCDB is common good practice — confirm per plant requirement). On the Rev 3 reference plant the UPS is 100 kVA / 30 min and the DCDB is 110 V DC — indicative; battery sizing at 50 °C ambient needs particular care, because VRLA battery life roughly halves for every 8–10 °C above 25 °C. Specify an air-conditioned battery room (or Li-ion chemistry with a qualified BMS) and verify the room cooling is itself fed from the essential bus.

When it applies. All plants — without this layer, protection relays cannot operate during a blackout and the DG/ATS sequence cannot be supervised.

How to verify on a site visit.

  • Locate UPS and DCDB panels — confirm they are energised and alarms clear.
  • Check UPS battery age against manufacturer's recommended replacement interval (typically 5 years for VRLA).
  • Read the DCDB charger panel: float voltage, battery condition indicator, last discharge test date.
  • Confirm UPS and DCDB are fed from the essential bus (EDB) or the ATS output, not from the bus that goes dark on utility loss.
  • Review battery discharge test records — a full autonomy test (simulate mains loss, run on battery, confirm hold-up time) should be completed annually.
  • Verify that protection relay DC supplies are from DCDB, not from a 230 V AC adapter that dies with the bus.

What a deficiency looks like.

  • No DCDB — protection relay trip coils fed from AC 230 V adapters; on total blackout, relays cannot operate and breakers cannot be closed.
  • UPS present but fed from the non-essential bus — goes dark precisely when it is needed most.
  • Battery age > 7 years and no recent discharge test — capacity may be well below nameplate; hold-up time is unknown.
  • UPS in static-bypass mode (common after a fault) — inverter is not running; next mains dip causes an outage to the "protected" DCS loads.
  • DCDB charger alarm active (e.g. "battery fault" LED) but left unattended — plant is unaware the DC backup is compromised.

P-6 — Motor Re-Acceleration After Transfer or Voltage Dip

Principle. When supply is restored after a bus transfer or a voltage dip, multiple large motors will attempt to accelerate simultaneously. Their combined locked-rotor inrush (up to 6–7 × F.L.C. per motor) can collapse the restored bus voltage and prevent any motor from accelerating — a re-acceleration failure. The remedy is a sequenced re-acceleration scheme: motors restart in priority groups with time delays between groups, so each group reaches running current before the next group is connected.

Variable-frequency drives (VFD) offer additional protection: kinetic buffering (regenerating energy from the spinning load into the DC bus) and flying-restart (re-synchronising to the spinning motor) allow VFDs to ride through short dips (100–500 ms) and reconnect without a process stop.

When it applies. Any plant with multiple large motors (> ~30 kW individually, or significant aggregate DOL starting current) and a bus transfer or DG start-up sequence.

How to verify on a site visit.

  • Confirm a re-acceleration sequence document exists listing motor groups and inter-step time delays.
  • Check MCC starters: are auto-restart relay modules fitted (e.g. time-delay undervoltage restart relays)?
  • For VFD-driven loads, confirm flying-restart and kinetic-buffering functions are enabled in the drive parameter settings.
  • Verify that the DG's voltage and frequency stabilisation time is accounted for before the first motor group reconnects.
  • Review motor control philosophy: "auto-restart enabled / disabled" status for each motor — safety-critical loads (e.g. agitators in vessels) may require manual restart.

What a deficiency looks like.

  • No re-acceleration sequence — all motors attempt to restart simultaneously after a transfer, collapsing the restored bus and tripping the bus-tie or DG on overload.
  • Auto-restart disabled globally as a blanket safety measure — operators must manually restart every motor after any supply event, causing extended process downtime.
  • VFD kinetic-buffering disabled or trip-on-undervoltage set to < 50 ms — drives trip on every short utility dip, stopping the process when there was no real fault in the plant.
  • Re-acceleration time between groups too short — bus voltage collapses during step 2 and starves subsequent groups.

P-7 — Protection Coordination Philosophy (Selectivity & Clearing Time)

Principle. Protection must be selective: the device closest to the fault (the "discriminating" relay or fuse) clears before any upstream device operates. This isolates only the faulted zone and leaves the rest of the plant energised. Without selectivity, a fault on one MCC feeder can cascade to trip the whole LV bus or even the MV incomer. Equally, clearing time must be fast enough to limit fault damage and arc-flash incident energy (see Part 2 — Arc Flash) while remaining slow enough to discriminate from the zone below.

The protection coordination study must cover: MV fuses / IDMT relays (at MV-SWGR), LV ACBs (incomers, bus-tie and main feeders at LV-MSB), MCC MCCBs and fuses, and motor overload/short-circuit protection at every starter — full top-to-bottom cascade.

For a full treatment of protection, refer to Part 2 — Protection Coordination.

How to verify on a site visit.

  • Confirm a current protection coordination study exists and has been updated after any major plant modification.
  • Check relay settings against the study: IDMT curves, pick-up current, time-multiplier settings. Do they match?
  • Verify grading margins: upstream relay should have ≥ 0.25–0.4 s margin over the downstream relay at the maximum fault current (confirm with the study).
  • Check that bus-tie relay settings do not conflict with the incomer relay — bus-tie overcurrent must grade with both incomers.
  • Review incident energy calculations at key buses — if incident energy > 40 cal/cm² at any working position, this is a critical finding. See Part 2.
  • Confirm relay test records: IEC 60255 relay testing at commissioning and periodic functional tests (at least every 3 years for numerical relays).

What a deficiency looks like.

  • Protection settings have not been reviewed since commissioning; plant load has grown and the original study is no longer valid.
  • Upstream relay grading margin < 0.2 s — risk of sympathetic tripping of the whole bus on a downstream feeder fault.
  • All LV MCCBs rated the same as the incomer ACB — no discrimination; the incomer trips before the MCCB on a feeder fault.
  • MV incomer IDMT relay time-multiplier set to minimum (fast) — clears every disturbance including downstream faults before the LV protection has time to operate.
  • No record of relay test since commissioning — relay may be mal-set or defective.

P-8 — Earthing Philosophy (MV Resistance Earth via MV-NER; LV TN-S)

Principle. The earthing philosophy governs fault current magnitudes, touch voltages, EPR (earth potential rise) and protection sensitivity — it underpins all ground-fault detection. This plant uses two distinct earthing regimes:

  • MV system (13.8 kV) — resistance earthed via MV-NER: the neutral of each MV/LV transformer (TX-1, TX-2) star winding is connected to earth through a neutral earthing resistor. The NER limits the single-phase earth-fault current to a defined level (indicatively 400 A for 10 s on this plant: 7.97 kV phase-to-neutral / 400 A ≈ 19.9 Ω) — high enough for sensitive earth-fault relays to detect, low enough to limit damage and EPR. A running earth fault is detectable and the system can remain in service for a short period while the fault is located and isolated.
  • LV system (400/230 V) — TN-S (solid neutral earth): the LV transformer neutral is solidly earthed at the transformer; the protective earth (PE) and neutral (N) conductors are separate throughout the installation. Earth-fault current is high (limited only by loop impedance), enabling fast fuse/MCB/RCCB operation. See Part 2 — Earthing for calculation method.

When it applies. All plants — earthing regime affects protection relay settings, cable insulation requirements, touch-voltage compliance and arc-flash severity.

How to verify on a site visit.

  • Locate MV-NER: confirm resistance value (stamped on nameplate), voltage rating, continuous and timed current rating.
  • Check that the MV earth-fault relay (51G/64) pickup current is correctly set for the NER fault current (typically ≈ 10–20 % of NER rated current for sensitive detection).
  • At the LV transformer neutral, verify the neutral earthing point is accessible, bolted solid to the earth electrode, and clearly labelled. Check earth electrode condition if visible.
  • Confirm PE and N conductors are kept separate (TN-S): check at the main earth terminal in LV-MSB — PEN conductors (combined PE+N, TN-C) must not appear downstream of the MSB.
  • Check earth grid continuity: main earth bar, building structural earth, MV cable screen earths, and equipment frames all bonded to the same grid.
  • Review most recent earth electrode resistance test value (target typically < 1 Ω for a large plant; confirm per plant specification).

What a deficiency looks like.

  • MV-NER absent or bypassed — MV system is either unearthed (high transient overvoltages on earth fault) or solidly earthed (very high fault currents, equipment damage).
  • NER resistance value incorrect for the system voltage: too high → earth-fault protection insensitive; too low → defeats the purpose of resistance earthing.
  • LV neutral not earthed (floating neutral) — TN-S system undefined; touch voltages uncontrolled; MCBs and RCCBs may not trip on earth fault.
  • TN-C wiring present downstream of the MSB (PEN conductors in sub-distribution) — earth-fault protection sensitivity is reduced; shock risk is increased.
  • Earth electrode resistance > 10 Ω — substation EPR on fault will exceed 430 V touch-voltage limit; personnel hazard.
  • MV cable screen not bonded at both ends — circulating currents and touch voltage on screen during a cable fault.
Key takeaway — the eight templates form a stack: P-1 (redundancy) makes the topology robust; P-2 (auto-transfer) and P-3 (DG/ATS) restore supply on a source failure; P-4 (load shedding) prevents an overloaded source from collapsing; P-5 (UPS/DCDB) keeps the brain alive through any disturbance; P-6 (motor re-acceleration) reconnects the process without overloading the restored source; P-7 and P-8 underpin all of the above with selective protection and a safe earth reference. A plant that fully implements all eight templates has no credible single event that interrupts the essential process.

DCS / SCADA / PMS Integration & IEC 61850

Where you are in the workflow: having assessed the power-system hardware, now evaluate the intelligence layer that automates transfers, sheds loads and monitors the plant in real time.

The electrical system is not an island. It is supervised by a layered control architecture linking field devices through a Power Management System (PMS) to the plant DCS and SCADA. Understanding this architecture is essential because a SPOF in the control layer can neutralise expensive hardware redundancy — a bus-tie that cannot close automatically because the PMS has failed is no different from a bus-tie that was never installed.

The Four Layers

Layer Equipment / function Relevant tags
Layer 4 — DCS / SCADA Process control and operator HMI. Receives electrical status, alarms and metering from the PMS. Issues high-level commands ("start DG", "shed non-essential bus") — does not directly operate individual breakers in normal operation. Plant-wide; communicates via OPC-UA or Modbus TCP gateway from the PMS
Layer 3 — Power Management System (PMS) Dedicated electrical "brain": load shedding logic, automatic bus transfer, generator control and synchronisation, source priority management, energy metering aggregation, power-quality alarming. Acts on GOOSE messages from IEDs in sub-10 ms for fast functions. LV-MSB (BT control), DG, ATS, MCC-1..n shed outputs
Layer 2 — Bay / protection IEDs Numerical multifunction relays at each breaker — protection, measurement, control and event recording. Each IED is both a protection device and a source of real-time data (current, voltage, power, energy, fault records, event logs). IEC 61850 IEDs report directly onto the station bus. MV-SWGR IEDs, LV-MSB incomer / bus-tie relays, MCC feeder relays
Layer 1 — Field / process devices Breakers (device number 52), generator controller (AVR + governor + synchroniser), ATS logic, UPS and DCDB monitors, VFD controllers, power quality meters at intake. MV-MET, TX-1/2 winding monitors, DG, VFD, PFC controller

Communications Protocols

A redundant Ethernet / fibre station bus carries multiple protocols simultaneously:

  • IEC 61850 MMS (Manufacturing Message Specification): client-server reporting for monitoring, settings changes, commands and historian feeds. Used between IEDs and the PMS/SCADA. Bandwidth-efficient; latency typically 50–500 ms acceptable for this role.
  • IEC 61850 GOOSE (Generic Object Oriented Substation Events): fast multicast peer-to-peer messaging between IEDs — typical latency < 4 ms. Enables fast bus transfer, fast load shedding interlocks and protection interlocks without routing through the PMS server. This is the key enabler of sub-cycle electrical automation.
  • Modbus TCP / DNP3 / OPC-UA: gateways to the DCS and third-party devices (energy meters, power quality monitors). Standard for legacy integration.
  • PROFIBUS / PROFINET / EtherNet/IP: to VFD controllers (VFD) and motor starters in the MCCs.
Note — IEC 61850 GOOSE vs polled Modbus: Traditional Modbus polling cycles are typically 100–1000 ms. A load-shedding scheme that relies on polled Modbus may take hundreds of milliseconds to respond to a generation shortfall — fast enough for some schemes, too slow for fast bus-transfer or short-circuit interlocks. GOOSE operates on a publish/subscribe model with sub-4 ms latency and self-supervision (heartbeat), making it reliable for safety-critical interlocks.

Hardwired vs Networked: The Right Philosophy

The choice between hardwired and networked signals is a risk decision, not a technology preference. The rule of thumb is:

Signal type Preferred path Reason
Master trip, emergency stop, safety interlock (e.g. transformer Buchholz trip, fire suppression trip) Hardwired — dedicated copper to trip coil Deterministic, independent of IED / network health, fail-safe on wire break (if normally energised-to-hold)
Fast bus transfer authorisation, fast load-shed trip to feeder breaker GOOSE (IEC 61850) or hardwired Sub-4 ms; GOOSE is acceptable if the station bus is redundant and GOOSE retransmission is configured; hardwired provides ultimate independence
Control commands (open/close breaker) from PMS or DCS Networked (IEC 61850 MMS or Modbus) Operator-issued; latency of hundreds of ms acceptable; network path is auditable and logged
Status / measurement / alarms / metering Networked (MMS / Modbus / OPC-UA) Not time-critical; rich data, historian integration, no hardwiring cost
Warning — network SPOF: If the PMS server is the single path for the bus-tie auto-close command, a PMS server failure becomes a SPOF in the control layer. Ensure that IED-to-IED GOOSE or hardwired control wiring provides a backup path for the bus-tie transfer that does not depend on the PMS server being alive.

How to Verify the Control Architecture on Site

  • Confirm the PMS or IED-based auto-transfer function is enabled and tested (not just installed).
  • Ask for the GOOSE configuration or hardwired logic diagram for bus-tie auto-close — trace the signal path end-to-end.
  • Verify redundancy of the station bus: dual-ring or dual-star Ethernet, with managed switches and alarming on link failure.
  • Check that the DCS receives live electrical alarms (transformer temperature, UPS fault, DC battery fault) — not just a generic "electrical fault" point.
  • Confirm cybersecurity: control LAN is segmented from the corporate LAN (firewall / DMZ); IED firmware versions are current; remote access (if any) uses a VPN with multi-factor authentication.
  • Review the PMS UPS supply: the PMS server must itself be fed from UPS — a PMS that dies with the bus cannot execute the transfer scheme.
Key takeaway: the PMS and IEC 61850 GOOSE layer is the nervous system of the electrical system. A SPOF in this layer can prevent all the hardware redundancy from operating. Always verify that the auto-transfer signal path is independent of the supply it is meant to restore, and that hardwired back-up exists for all safety-critical trips.

Best Practice & Emerging Technology

Where you are in the workflow: use this section when writing the Recommendations Register — these are the technologies and approaches that should be cited when proposing enhancements beyond the baseline philosophy.

Digital Substation (IEC 61850 Process Bus)

In a conventional substation, copper wiring runs from every CT and VT secondary terminal to every relay panel. In a digital substation, merging units (MUs) sit at the primary equipment and digitise CT/VT samples, transmitting Sampled Values (SV) over fibre to the IED bay units. Benefits: dramatic reduction in copper wiring and termination points (a major source of wiring errors and maintenance burden), self-supervision of the measurement chain, simpler future expansion, and a unified data model (IEC 61850-9-2) that allows any IED from any vendor to consume the same SV stream. For an existing brownfield plant, a selective retrofit (new IEDs with process-bus capability at the point of planned relay replacement) is more practical than a full digital conversion.

Intelligent / Fast Load Shedding

Traditional under-frequency load shedding responds in 2–5 s. Modern systems using IEC 61850 GOOSE messaging and a PMS with a real-time power balance model can shed the correct load block in under 50 ms after a generation event. The scheme knows the exact MW of each feeder (from IED metering), calculates the deficit in real time, and sheds only what is necessary — no over-shedding, no under-shedding. This eliminates the risk of the DG tripping on overload during a utility loss event because non-essential loads were not shed quickly enough.

Tip — specify the response time: When writing a recommendation for a load-shedding upgrade, specify the required response time (e.g. "shed non-essential load within 100 ms of utility loss detection") — this drives the vendor to propose a GOOSE-based solution rather than a relay-timer scheme.

Arc-Flash Detection & Zone-Selective Interlocking (ZSI)

Arc-flash incident energy is proportional to arcing time. Two technologies reduce clearing time without sacrificing selectivity:

  • Arc-flash light + current detection: optical sensors in the switchgear busbar compartment detect the arc flash signature (intense light pulse + overcurrent) and trip the upstream breaker within 8–12 ms — an order of magnitude faster than IDMT relay operation. Dramatically reduces incident energy at the bus.
  • Zone-Selective Interlocking (ZSI): IEDs exchange ZSI signals: if both the downstream IED and the upstream IED see overcurrent simultaneously, it is a through-fault and the upstream IED stays slow (maintaining discrimination). If only the upstream IED sees current, it is a bus fault and the upstream IED trips instantaneously. ZSI achieves fast clearing for bus faults while maintaining full selectivity for feeder faults — without relaxing grading margins.

Reference: Part 2 — Arc-Flash Assessment for the incident energy calculation methodology.

Condition Monitoring & Predictive Maintenance

Unplanned failures of transformers, switchgear and motors are a primary cause of SPOF events — even well-designed redundant systems are defeated by simultaneous or sequential failures of components under stress. Condition monitoring moves maintenance from time-based (change everything at fixed intervals) to condition-based (change it when data says it is degrading). Key technologies:

  • Partial discharge (PD) monitoring on MV cables, switchgear and transformer bushings — detects insulation degradation before it becomes a fault.
  • Transformer winding temperature (WTI / OTI) and DGA (dissolved gas analysis for oil-filled transformers) — detects thermal stress and incipient faults.
  • Breaker health monitoring — contact resistance, spring energy, operating time — detects wear before a mechanical failure during a fault.
  • Motor winding temperature and vibration sensors integrated into the MCC IED — early detection of overload or mechanical bearing failure.
  • Digital twin / analytics platform — aggregates sensor data, applies ML models, and alerts the maintenance team to anomalies days or weeks before failure.

Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) & Grid-Forming Inverters

A BESS connected to the LV-MSB or MV bus via a bidirectional inverter can fulfil multiple roles simultaneously, making it one of the highest-value additions to a modern plant:

Function How BESS delivers it
Ride-through & bridging power Supplies full plant load for the DG start-up period (10–30 s) — eliminates the process dip between utility loss and DG pick-up; replaces the ATS dead time.
Synthetic inertia Grid-forming inverter emulates the inertia of a synchronous machine — slows ROCOF on loss of generation, giving protection schemes time to respond.
Voltage / frequency support Fast active and reactive power injection within milliseconds — stabilises voltage and frequency during disturbances before other controls respond.
Peak shaving Reduces peak demand charges by discharging during high-demand periods; charges during low-tariff periods.
Islanding capability With a grid-forming inverter and adequate energy capacity, BESS + DG can form a stable microgrid, disconnected from a disturbed utility, and reconnect seamlessly when the grid is stable.
Note — BESS sizing: BESS sizing for ride-through is driven by the DG start-up time and the essential load (e.g. 500 kW essential load × 30 s start-up time = ~4.2 kWh minimum, including inverter efficiency and state-of-charge margins). All figures are indicative; detailed sizing requires a dynamic simulation study.
Key takeaway: the six technology areas above (digital substation, intelligent load shedding, arc-flash detection/ZSI, condition monitoring, and BESS) represent the difference between a plant that handles its first SPOF event by luck and one that handles it by design. When writing the Recommendations Register, reference these as concept-level enhancements beyond the P-1 to P-8 baseline.

Grid Voltage Instability from Renewables

Where you are in the workflow: this section helps you identify whether the plant's exposure to grid-quality disturbances is a SPOF in its own right — and what mitigations to recommend.

As wind and solar generation displace synchronous plant on the transmission network, the grid becomes "weaker": less stored kinetic energy (inertia), a lower short-circuit level, and more volatile voltage and frequency. An industrial plant connected to such a grid will experience more frequent and more severe disturbances at its point of connection — dips, swells, fast rate-of-change-of-frequency (ROCOF) events, harmonics, and flicker — even when no fault occurs within the plant. On these 21 plants in the SEC Central and Eastern Operating Areas, utility grid mix and renewable penetration should be confirmed per plant, as exposure varies — Saudi Arabia's National Renewable Energy Program is adding multi-GW solar PV and wind blocks to the 60 Hz interconnected grid, so the disturbance environment will evolve over the life of the plant.

How Grid Instability Hurts the Plant

Grid disturbance Plant effect Example trigger
Voltage dip (short interruption < 1 s) Contactor drop-out (contactors typically release at 60–70 % Vn), VFD DC-bus undervoltage trip, motor stall Distant fault clearance on the transmission network; switching of a large inverter-connected source
Fast ROCOF event Loss-of-mains (LOM) relay at DG trips the generator offline; protection schemes operating faster than intended Sudden disconnection of large renewable block from the grid
Voltage swell (> 110 % Vn) Overvoltage trip on sensitive electronics, accelerated insulation ageing, capacitor bank overload Load rejection on a weak grid or tap-changer hunting
Harmonic distortion (THD rising) Overheating of capacitor banks (PFC resonance), neutral conductor overload, interference with protection relays Large inverter-based resources connected to the MV network near the plant
Voltage flicker Visible flicker on lighting, interference with sensitive process instrumentation Wind generation output variability, arc furnaces on the same feeder

Ride-Through: Don't Trip on a Transient

The first line of defence is ensuring the plant does not trip on disturbances it can survive. This requires coordinated settings across multiple device types:

  • Under-voltage ride-through (UVRT) — time-delayed UV trips: set the UV trip relay time delay so the plant rides through dips that conform to the applicable Saudi Grid Code / SEC Distribution Code LVRT curve (e.g. a 500 ms dip to 50 % Vn should not trip the plant — indicative; confirm the curve in force at the point of connection). Coordinate with the upstream utility protection so genuine faults in the plant still clear within the required time.
  • Contactor ride-through: DC-held coils or time-delay undervoltage relays on contactors hold them closed through a short dip (typically 0.5–1 s hold-up). Alternatively, specify contactors with a minimum hold-in voltage of 45–50 % Vn rather than the standard 70 %.
  • VFD kinetic buffering / flying-restart: enable these functions in drive parameters. Kinetic buffering allows the drive to regenerate motor inertia energy into the DC bus, extending ride-through. Flying restart re-synchronises to a spinning motor after a supply restoration without requiring a coast-to-stop cycle.
  • Loss-of-mains (LOM) relay sensitivity: if the DG is fitted with a ROCOF-based LOM relay, confirm the ROCOF setting is not over-sensitive. An overly sensitive LOM relay trips the DG on a grid event even when the DG is supplying essential loads correctly — converting a grid disturbance into a plant blackout.

Dynamic Voltage Support: Hold the Voltage Up

The second line of defence is active voltage support at the plant's point of connection. For plants where grid instability is identified as a material risk:

  • STATCOM / SVC: fast dynamic reactive power compensation (response in 1–4 cycles); stabilises voltage, reduces flicker, and supports the plant voltage through deep dips. Far faster than switched capacitor banks.
  • Dynamic Voltage Restorer (DVR): series compensation device that injects voltage in series with the supply, correcting dips and swells in real time for sensitive downstream loads. Typical response: < 1 ms.
  • Thyristor-switched or fast-switched detuned PFC banks: faster reactive support than contactor-switched PFC banks; ensure banks are detuned (7 % reactor) to avoid resonance with rising background harmonic levels.
  • OLTC / AVR coordination: confirm transformer on-load tap-changer (OLTC) deadband and response time are set to maintain LV bus voltage within ±5 % at all load conditions. Over-hunting of the tap-changer can worsen voltage instability.

Storage and Local Stability

For severe grid environments or plants with critical processes that cannot tolerate any interruption, storage-based solutions provide the highest level of protection:

  • BESS with grid-forming inverter: provides synthetic inertia, fast frequency response, and can island the essential plant automatically during a severe grid event. On grid restoration, the BESS microgrid controller checks synchronisation and reconnects the plant seamlessly. See Best Practice — BESS.
  • UPS and flywheel: keep control systems and critical instrument loads firm regardless of any external disturbance. Essential baseline (P-5).

See It Before It Trips You — Power Quality Monitoring

Continuous power quality (PQ) metering at the MV intake (MV-MET) and on critical LV buses provides the data to:

  • Characterise the actual grid disturbance environment (how many dips per month, minimum voltage, ROCOF histogram).
  • Set ride-through relay margins correctly based on measured data rather than assumptions.
  • Detect rising harmonic background and proactively adjust PFC tuning before resonance occurs.
  • Support claims against the utility if grid-quality events cause plant damage or downtime.
Tip — field check: During the site visit, ask whether the plant has experienced unexplained process trips that coincided with apparent voltage events. Check the DCS alarm historian for clusters of "undervoltage" or "frequency alarm" events — these are evidence of grid instability affecting the plant, and they justify a recommendation for ride-through improvements or PQ monitoring.
Key takeaway: a plant that trips on every grid disturbance has an effective SPOF in the utility connection, regardless of how much internal redundancy it has. Ride-through settings, contactor hold-in improvement, VFD flying-restart, and PQ monitoring are low-cost first steps; STATCOM, DVR or BESS are the higher-cost solutions for severe grid environments. Always check the PQ history before concluding the plant is reliable.

Typical Modifications Catalogue

Where you are in the workflow: use this catalogue when populating the Recommendations Register. Each row maps one modification to the SPOF it resolves, the affected tags, a concept description, and an indicative cost band. All costs are indicative H/M/L bands only — confirm scope and cost by market enquiry.

The catalogue below covers the most common electrical modifications identified on MV/LV industrial process plants. Every modification cross-references the SPOF type from the Part 3 — SPOF Library (variants A–E). Use these rows as the starting point for concept modification packages — each package must include a marked-up SLD, an equipment outline specification, a scheme description / sequence of operation, and a high-level bill of materials.

Note — concept level only: All modifications described here are at concept / feasibility level. Detailed design, equipment specification, procurement, and construction are out of scope for the SPOF assessment campaign. Indicative cost bands: H = high (typically > USD 200 k installed); M = medium (USD 30 k – 200 k); L = low (< USD 30 k). All figures are indicative and to be confirmed by market enquiry. Cost band currency shown is USD (catalogue order-of-magnitude basis); actual project costs must be confirmed by local market enquiry — the Jubail case study in Part 5 uses SAR as the client's currency. Indicative bands are currency-agnostic at H/M/L level.
Mod ID Modification SPOF type addressed (see Part 3 — Library) Affected tag(s) Concept description Typical scope to specify Indicative cost band
MOD-01 Add normally-open auto-close bus-tie to LV-MSB Type B — two transformers, no bus-tie LV-MSB, BT Install a 4-pole ACB bus-tie breaker (rated to carry full essential load) between Bus A and Bus B of the LV-MSB. Auto-close relay (UV-triggered on either incomer failure) with synchro-check and interlock to prevent closing onto a fault. Hardwired or GOOSE signal path independent of PMS server. ACB rating and frame size; protection relay specification (auto-transfer + overcurrent + synchro-check); wiring/cabling to existing bus; updated protection coordination study; FAT and commissioning test procedure. M
MOD-02 Second MV incomer and MV bus-section breaker Type C — single utility incomer / single MV bus MV-SWGR, MV-MET Provide a second independent utility feeder terminating on a second MV incomer panel with a normally-open bus-section breaker between the two MV bus sections. Dual independent MV metering. Auto-close bus-section on loss of either incomer (with protection coordination — bus-section relay must grade with both feeders). Second NER (MV-NER) for the second section. New MV switchgear extension (panels, busbars, cabling to new utility termination); updated MV IDMT relay settings; NER sizing; protection coordination study update; utility connection agreement amendment. H
MOD-03 Split duty/standby MCCs across Bus A and Bus B Type D — shared MCC / shared cable for duty and standby loads MCC-1, MCC-2, LV-MSB Reassign the standby motor starter(s) from the same MCC panel as the duty motor to a separate MCC panel fed from the alternate LV bus. Route the standby feeder cable on a physically separate path (different cable tray / conduit / trench, minimum 1 m separation). This ensures a single bus, panel, or cable fault cannot remove both duty and standby simultaneously. New MCC panel (or panel extension) on the second bus; new outgoing feeder from LV-MSB Bus B; cable routing survey and trench/tray design; updated LV protection coordination; updated SLD. M
MOD-04 Diesel generator set and ATS for essential loads Type A/C — no backup for utility loss DG, ATS, EDB Install a diesel generator set sized for the confirmed essential load list, with automatic transfer switch and essential distribution board. Automatic start on utility loss detection (UV relay), ATS transfer within the specified dead time, load build-up sequence, anti-paralleling interlock, and weekly / monthly test capability. Fuel tank for minimum 24 h autonomy at full essential load. DG sizing (kVA, voltage, frequency); ATS type and rating; EDB panel and distribution layout; essential load schedule and priority sequence; fuel system; acoustic enclosure; load test procedure; updated SLD and protection study. H
MOD-05 Upgrade manual ATS to automatic transfer with PMS interlock Type A/C — ATS in manual mode negates backup ATS, DG Replace existing manual or semi-automatic ATS with a fully automatic ATS unit interfaced to the PMS. PMS controls the transfer sequence (start DG, confirm voltage and frequency, transfer load, shed non-essential, confirm essential load is stable) and provides alarm and event records. Include hardwired UV trigger as a backup to PMS-commanded transfer. New ATS panel or module; PMS interface wiring / GOOSE; updated sequence-of-operation document; revised load build-up schedule; commissioning and test certificate. M
MOD-06 Add or resize detuned PFC capacitor bank Type — power factor penalty / harmonic resonance (not a SPOF but a reliability and quality risk) PFC, LV-MSB Install or replace a 7 % detuned capacitor bank (filter reactor + capacitor steps) at the LV-MSB to correct power factor to ≥ 0.95 lagging at full load. The 7 % detuning ratio prevents resonance with the 5th harmonic and above. Automatic power factor controller (APFC) switches stages in response to reactive demand. Size based on load-flow reactive demand — indicatively ≈ 580 kvar for this 2 MW plant (0.85 → 0.95 lagging), confirmed by a reactive power survey. Correction to ≥ 0.95 also clears the SEC power-factor clause (≥ 0.9 typical, with penalty/charge exposure below it — see Saudi Grid Code). Reactive power survey and load-flow study; capacitor bank rating and harmonic audit; APFC relay; installation space in MSB; updated protection and harmonic study. M
MOD-07 Separate cable routing of duty/standby feeders Type D — common cable route for duty and standby MCC-1, MCC-2, field loads Where duty and standby feeders currently share a cable tray, conduit or buried trench for any part of their route, physically separate them to different routes with minimum 1 m lateral separation (or structural fire barrier). This ensures a cable fire, mechanical damage, or flooded trench cannot take out both the duty and standby supply simultaneously. Survey and document all current dual-feed routes. Cable routing survey drawings (current and proposed); new cable tray / conduit / trench works; cable termination at both ends; fire barrier if separation is < 300 mm; updated cable schedule. L to M
MOD-08 Install UPS and DCDB for control system independence Type — control SPOF: protection and DCS lose supply during transfer/blackout UPS, DCDB Install an online double-conversion UPS (min. 30 min battery autonomy at full DCS / protection load) feeding all relay panels, DCS I/O, PMS servers, and communications. Install a separate 110 V DC DCDB (min. 2 h autonomy) for MV and LV protection relay trip/close coils, breaker charging motors, and emergency lighting. Both systems fed from the essential bus downstream of the ATS. UPS kVA sizing (DCS + communications + relay panels + emergency lighting); DCDB Ah sizing (protection load + breaker charging currents × hold-up time); sealed lead-acid or Li-ion battery selection; charger and rectifier specification; essential bus feeder capacity; commissioning and autonomy test. M
MOD-09 Arc-flash detection and zone-selective interlocking (ZSI) Type — arc-flash severity / slow clearing of bus faults LV-MSB, MCC-1..n, MV-SWGR Fit arc-flash light + current detection sensors in LV-MSB busbar compartments and selected MCC panels. Link sensors to the incomer ACB trip coil via a dedicated arc-flash protection relay (response < 10 ms). Where IEDs support it, configure ZSI between the incomer relay and feeder relays to achieve instantaneous clearing of bus faults while maintaining discrimination for feeder faults. Re-run arc-flash incident energy calculations after implementation to verify reduction. Arc-flash protection relay selection; sensor types and positions; wiring to trip coils; IED ZSI compatibility check; updated arc-flash study; arc-flash hazard label update; commissioning test. M
MOD-10 Earthing remediation — NER resize and earth-grid extension Type — earthing deficiency (MV unearthed or NER incorrect; LV earth grid resistance high) MV-NER, LV-MSB, plant earth grid If the MV-NER resistance value does not match the calculated earth-fault current requirement (see Part 2), replace with a correctly rated unit. If the LV earth grid resistance is above the target (typically < 1 Ω for a large plant), extend the electrode grid (driven rods, buried ring conductor, or copper mesh) to achieve the required resistance. Retest after installation. Update MV earth-fault relay settings to match the new NER current. MV earth-fault current calculation; NER resistance and rating specification; LV earth electrode resistance test and target value; electrode design (rod lengths, grid spacing, soil resistivity); updated MV 51G relay settings; commissioning test certificate; updated earthing study. L to M
MOD-11 Intelligent load-shedding PMS upgrade Type — slow or absent load shedding on N-1 transformer or DG supply LV-MSB, MCC-1..n, DG Replace or supplement existing under-frequency relay load shedding with a PMS-based fast load-shedding scheme. The PMS monitors real-time MW at each feeder (via IED metering), detects a generation deficit event (utility loss or transformer trip), and issues GOOSE trip commands to non-essential feeder breakers within 50–100 ms. Priority groups are configurable; scheme can shed the minimum necessary load rather than a fixed block. PMS software load-shedding module specification; IED GOOSE configuration for all shed feeders; priority load schedule and interlock list; PMS-to-DCS interface for shedding status alarms; factory acceptance test; commissioning test record; updated sequence of operation. M
MOD-12 VFD ride-through and motor re-acceleration scheme Type — motor stall / re-acceleration failure after supply restoration VFD, MCC-1..n Enable kinetic buffering and flying-restart in all VFD parameter sets. For direct-on-line (DOL) motors: install time-delay auto-restart relay modules in starters (essential motors only, per risk assessment). Prepare and commission a motor re-acceleration sequence (priority groups and inter-step time delays) and load it into the PMS. Set VFD undervoltage ride-through window to minimum 500 ms (or to the expected transfer time, whichever is greater). VFD parameter review and update for each drive; auto-restart relay module procurement for selected starters; re-acceleration sequence document; PMS sequence configuration; updated motor control philosophy; commissioning test (simulate supply dip and confirm successful re-acceleration). L
Tip — filling the Recommendations Register: Copy the Mod ID, Modification title, SPOF type and Affected tags directly into the Recommendations Register. The "Typical scope to specify" column becomes the basis for the concept modification package description. Priority (H/M/L) in the register should be driven by the FMECA criticality number, not by cost alone — a low-cost modification addressing a high-criticality SPOF should always be prioritised over an expensive modification addressing a low-criticality finding.
Key takeaway: the twelve modifications above cover the full set of SPOF types from Part 3. For each plant, scan the catalogue, identify which Mod IDs apply, and create the corresponding rows in the Recommendations Register — linked back to the FMECA finding that justifies them. All modifications are concept level only; each requires a detailed design phase before procurement or construction.

Part 4 — Self-Check

Where you are in the workflow: test your understanding of philosophies and modifications before moving to Part 5 field tasks.

Answer each question from memory, then expand the answer to check.

Q1 — Bus transfer speed. A plant has a fast bus transfer (FBT) relay set to close the bus-tie within 80 ms. A reviewer proposes replacing it with a residual-voltage (RV) transfer that waits 5 s. What is the process consequence of the change, and when would the RV approach ever be preferred?

Answer. With the FBT relay, the bus transfer completes before the residual motor voltages drift significantly out of phase — motors keep spinning and the process continues with minimal dip. With the RV transfer, all motors on the dead bus will coast for 5 s (or until their residual voltage decays to the threshold). This typically means a full process stop: pump pressures drop, reactors lose agitation, and a restart sequence is needed. For a continuous process, this is a production loss event that the FBT was designed to avoid.

RV transfer is preferred when: (a) the relay set does not support synchro-check or the motor inertia is very low (residual decays fast anyway); (b) there are large synchronous motors whose residual voltage takes a long time to decay and out-of-phase closing would cause damaging mechanical torque; or (c) the process can tolerate a 5 s stop (e.g. a utility building, not the core process). The key trade-off is process continuity vs mechanical torque on motors during closing.

Q2 — Load shedding trigger. A plant loses one of its two transformers. The surviving transformer is rated 1600 kVA. Before the fault, the total plant load was 2600 kVA (generic example — larger than this manual's 2353 kVA reference plant), split equally at 1300 kVA per transformer. What happens if there is no load-shedding scheme? What should the scheme shed first?

Answer. Without a load-shedding scheme, the surviving transformer is immediately loaded to 2600 kVA — 162 % of its rated capacity. The transformer will trip on thermal overload protection within seconds (if it has a Buchholz / winding temperature relay) or will sustain damage and trip within minutes. This produces a total plant blackout — a worse outcome than the original single transformer loss.

The load-shedding scheme must shed enough non-essential load to bring the surviving transformer below its rated capacity, with a margin. If the essential load is ~1100 kVA and the transformer is rated 1600 kVA, the scheme needs to shed at least 1000 kVA (2600 − 1600 = 1000 kVA minimum, preferably more for stability margin). It should shed Priority 3 loads first (site lighting, workshops, offices), then Priority 2 (HVAC, secondary utilities), keeping the core process alive. The essential loads (DCS, fire pump, critical process drives) must never be in the shed list.

Q3 — DCDB importance. A plant has a UPS for the DCS but no separate DCDB. The LV-MSB bus goes dead during a transformer fault. Can the bus-tie relay operate to close the bus-tie?

Answer. Only if the bus-tie relay trip coil is energised. If the relay coil is fed from the LV AC bus (e.g. via a 230 V AC adapter), it loses power when the bus goes dead — exactly when the auto-close command is needed. The relay cannot operate and the bus-tie stays open, leaving the dead bus dark even though the surviving transformer is healthy.

A DCDB supplies the relay trip and close coils from a battery-backed 110 V DC system that is independent of the AC bus state. With a DCDB, the bus-tie relay remains powered during the blackout and can execute the auto-close sequence. This is why the DCDB is a non-negotiable element of the control philosophy — the UPS protects the DCS, but the DCDB protects the protection system itself.

Q4 — IEC 61850 GOOSE vs Modbus. A PMS designer proposes using Modbus TCP polling (250 ms cycle time) for the fast load-shedding trip signal to MCC feeder breakers. What is the risk, and what should be specified instead?

Answer. The risk is that on a generation shortfall event, the load-shedding command takes up to 250 ms to reach the feeder breakers plus the breaker trip time. On a DG set, frequency may decay at 2–5 Hz/s on a severe overload: in 250 ms the frequency could drop 0.5–1.25 Hz below the 60 Hz base (i.e. through the indicative 59.3 / 59.0 Hz shed stages), potentially causing the DG governor to fail to recover or triggering spurious under-frequency trips on other loads before the shed command arrives. A 250 ms Modbus latency is too slow for a fast load-shedding scheme where the target response time is < 100 ms.

The specification should require IEC 61850 GOOSE messaging from the PMS (or directly IED-to-IED) to the feeder breaker trip coils. GOOSE latency on a local Ethernet network is < 4 ms, and the signal is self-supervised (heartbeat — the receiver knows if the publisher has failed). This achieves the required response time. Alternatively, hardwired trip outputs from a dedicated fast load-shedding relay provide a fully deterministic path if GOOSE is not available on the existing IEDs.

Q5 — Modifications catalogue. During a site visit you find: (a) duty and standby cooling water pumps are both fed from MCC-1, which is on Bus A; (b) the bus-tie is present but auto-transfer is disabled; (c) the UPS is in static bypass. Write three rows for the Recommendations Register, citing the correct Mod ID for each.

Answer (indicative — adapt to actual plant data).

Rec. RefFindingMod IDRecommended actionPriority
REC-01Duty and standby cooling pumps both fed from MCC-1 (Bus A) — common MCC and bus SPOF defeats standby redundancyMOD-03Relocate standby pump starter to a new or extended MCC panel on Bus B with separately routed feeder cable. Update SLD and protection study.High
REC-02Bus-tie auto-transfer disabled — manual operation only; loss of one transformer will not result in automatic bus restorationMOD-01Re-enable auto-transfer function in bus-tie relay / PMS; confirm UV trigger settings; add DCDB-backed GOOSE or hardwired trip path; issue test certificate.High
REC-03UPS in static bypass — inverter not running; next AC supply disturbance will directly interrupt DCS and protection relay suppliesMOD-08Restore UPS to online double-conversion mode immediately (check reason for bypass — typically a failed battery or faulty inverter). If UPS is unserviceable, procure replacement. Confirm battery age and conduct discharge test.High
Q6 — Grid instability. A plant engineer tells you the process trips unexpectedly two or three times a month, always on night shifts. The events appear to coincide with voltage "brownouts" visible on the DCS trend. No faults are found in the plant each time. What is the most likely cause, and what three actions would you recommend?

Answer. The pattern (recurring, night-shift timing, no internal fault, voltage sag visible) is characteristic of external grid voltage disturbances — likely voltage dips from the transmission or distribution network, possibly associated with renewable generation ramping or fault-clearance switching on the utility network. Night-shift timing often correlates with lower system demand (less inertia on the grid) and wind generation peaks.

Three recommended actions:

  1. Install or enable continuous power quality monitoring at the MV-MET metering point (IEC 61000-4-30 Class A meter). Capture dip depth, duration, and frequency. This provides the evidence needed to characterise the disturbances and correlate them precisely with the process trips.
  2. Review and increase undervoltage ride-through time delays on contactor coils and VFD undervoltage trip settings. If contactors are releasing at 70 % Vn after 20 ms, even a mild grid dip will cause mass dropout. Set the UV trip delay to ride through at least 500 ms of dips conforming to the applicable grid-code LVRT curve.
  3. Enable VFD kinetic buffering and flying-restart on all process-critical drives. Check motor control philosophy to confirm whether auto-restart is permitted for each motor (safety review required). This allows the plant to self-recover from a short dip without a manual restart sequence.

If the disturbances are confirmed to be severe (e.g. dips below 60 % Vn lasting > 1 s, several times per month), escalate to a recommendation for a STATCOM or BESS for dynamic voltage support.

You have completed Part 4. The eight philosophy templates give you a consistent framework for judging any MV/LV plant design. The modifications catalogue gives you the standard vocabulary for the Recommendations Register. Part 5 — Field Tasks now takes you through the complete per-plant workflow step by step: desk study, site visit, drawing verification, analysis, and final report assembly — starting with a worked walk-through of the first assignment.

Next: Part 5 — Field Tasks.

Saudi Grid Code & On-Site Generation

Where you are in the workflow: a reference section. Use it whenever a plant already has — or the Recommendations Register proposes — on-site generation: a diesel generator that might parallel the SEC supply, rooftop solar PV, or a battery energy storage system (BESS). It tells you what the Saudi codes require, what is allowed by default, and what needs SEC consent.

A 13.8 kV industrial customer in Saudi Arabia sits under two related rule books. The Saudi Grid Code governs the transmission system and large generators; the SEC Distribution Code translates those obligations down to distribution-connected customers like this plant. In practice the engineer needs both: the Distribution Code for connection conditions, metering, power factor and protection at the point of connection (POC), and the Grid Code for frequency/voltage operating bands and the behaviour expected of any generation that runs in parallel with the network. National installation requirements come from SBC 401 (the Saudi Building Code electrical chapter, based on the NEC model adapted to KSA) together with SASO-adopted IEC product standards. See Part 0 — Working in Saudi Arabia for the regulatory map.

Note — indicative figures: The numerical limits quoted in this section (frequency bands, voltage tolerances, power-factor thresholds, protection settings) are indicative training values, summarised at concept level. The codes are revised periodically; always confirm the clause in force with SEC / the Water & Electricity Regulatory Authority before settings are issued for a real connection.

What the Codes Ask of an Industrial Customer

Five clusters of requirements matter most to an MV/LV process plant of this size. Each is summarised below with the field-engineering consequence — i.e. what you should check or specify on site.

Requirement cluster Indicative content What it means for this plant
Frequency operating band Nominal 60 Hz. Continuous operation expected over a band of roughly 59.5–60.5 Hz, with time-limited operation in wider bands (down to ~58.0 Hz and up to ~61.5 Hz for seconds-to-minutes durations — indicative). Plant equipment (motors, VFDs, UPS input windows) must tolerate the band without nuisance tripping. Plant under-frequency load shedding (see P-4) must sit inside the band so it acts only on genuine islanded/DG events, e.g. staged at 59.3 / 59.0 / 58.7 Hz (indicative).
Voltage tolerance at 13.8 kV Steady-state supply voltage within about ±5 % of nominal under normal conditions, up to about ±10 % under disturbed/contingency conditions (indicative). Transformer off-circuit tap selection (typically ±2 × 2.5 % taps on TX-1/TX-2) must centre the 400 V bus within limits across the SEC voltage range and plant load range. Record actual 13.8 kV bus voltage during the site visit.
Power factor Customers are required to maintain power factor at or above a threshold — ≥ 0.9 lagging is the typical clause — with a reactive-energy penalty or charge mechanism below it. The PFC bank (≈ 580 kvar detuned, target 0.95) is therefore not just an economy measure but a compliance measure. An out-of-service PFC bank is a finding.
Fault ride-through (FRT) for parallel generation Generation operating in parallel with the network must not disconnect for voltage dips above the code's LVRT envelope, so that a transmission fault does not cascade into loss of distributed generation across the system. Relevant only while the DG (or future PV/BESS) is actually paralleled. Note the tension: FRT says "stay connected through a dip", anti-islanding says "disconnect when the grid is lost" — the interface protection settings must reconcile both, which is why SEC reviews them (see below).
Protection & intertrip at the POC Customer protection must grade with the SEC feeder protection; settings at the incomer are subject to SEC approval. Where embedded generation can feed the network, SEC may require an intertrip (direct transfer trip) from their upstream breaker to the customer's generation breaker, plus dedicated interface protection. The MV-SWGR incomer relay settings file should carry an SEC approval record. Any modification touching incomer protection (e.g. MOD-02) reopens that approval.

Worked example — does the plant meet the SEC power-factor clause?

Plant demand 2.0 MW at natural power factor 0.85 lagging (indicative):

Q_natural = P × tan(acos 0.85) = 2000 kW × 0.620 = 1240 kvar

To reach the PFC design target of 0.95 lagging:

Q_at_0.95 = 2000 kW × tan(acos 0.95) = 2000 × 0.329 = 658 kvar Q_PFC = 1240 − 658 ≈ 580 kvar (matches the installed PFC rating)

Check against the code threshold of 0.9: with the PFC bank in service at 0.95 the plant is comfortably compliant; with the bank out of service the plant sits at 0.85 — below the ≥ 0.9 clause — and accrues reactive-energy charges every hour the bank is down. Sanity check: 580 kvar ≈ 29 % of plant kW — a typical correction block for a motor-dominated industrial load.

Tip — read the bill. The fastest field check of power-factor compliance is the SEC invoice: a recurring reactive-energy or low-power-factor line item is hard evidence that the PFC bank is under-sized, mis-controlled, or out of service — even before you open the panel.

Frequency anchors on the 60 Hz system

Because every frequency-based setting in the plant hangs off the 60 Hz base, it pays to keep the anchor values in one place. All figures indicative:

Anchor Indicative value Owned by
Nominal system frequency 60.00 Hz SEC / Saudi Grid Code
Normal continuous operating band ≈ 59.5 – 60.5 Hz Saudi Grid Code
Plant UF load-shed stage 1 (Priority 3 loads) ≈ 59.3 Hz, 0.2–0.5 s delay Plant PMS / UF relay (P-4)
Plant UF load-shed stage 2 (Priority 2 loads) ≈ 59.0 Hz, 0.2–0.5 s delay Plant PMS / UF relay
Plant UF load-shed stage 3 (deepest — Priority 1) ≈ 58.7 Hz, 0.1–0.3 s delay Plant PMS / UF relay
DG governor steady-state band (islanded, isochronous) 60 ± 0.25 Hz Generator controller (Part 2 — Generator)
Interface 81U / 81O (only if paralleled with SEC) ≈ 59.2–59.5 Hz / 60.5–61.0 Hz, 0.5–2 s Interface protection, settings agreed with SEC
Note — when do the shed stages actually operate? On the interconnected SEC grid, frequency excursions below 59.5 Hz are rare system emergencies; the plant UF stages will realistically only operate while the plant is islanded on the DG (where a 1250 kVA machine's frequency moves fast on any overload) or during a major grid event. That is precisely why they are staged just below the normal band: deep enough to ignore ordinary grid wobble, shallow enough to save the DG before its own under-frequency trip (~57–58 Hz, machine-protective) ends the island entirely.

Paralleling the DG (or Future PV/BESS) with SEC

The single most important question for any on-site source is: does it ever run connected to the SEC network at the same time as the utility supply? The answer determines the entire protection and consent regime.

Operating mode What happens Consent / protection consequence
Open transition (break-before-make) — the default The ATS disconnects the utility before connecting the DG, and vice versa on retransfer. The DG and the SEC network are never electrically connected together — separation is guaranteed by the transfer-switch mechanism or a mechanical/electrical interlock. No parallel operation → normally no generation-connection consent needed beyond declaring the standby set. This is how the reference plant is configured (see Part 2 — ATS and Part 2 — Generator).
Closed transition (make-before-break) On retransfer the DG is synchronised to the restored utility and both are connected for a short overlap (typically < 100 ms) so the load never sees a break. Even a 100 ms overlap is parallel operation: it requires SEC consent, a synchroniser, and interface protection. Many utilities accept short closed transition with a fail-safe overlap timer that trips the DG breaker if the overlap exceeds the agreed window.
Extended parallel / export (peak lopping, PV, BESS) The on-site source runs continuously in parallel with SEC, sharing or exporting power. Full embedded-generation connection agreement: SEC studies, approved interface protection at the POC, possible intertrip, FRT compliance, and SEC-witnessed commissioning.

Interface protection — the watchdog at the point of connection

Whenever parallel operation is allowed, a dedicated interface protection relay watches the point of connection and disconnects the on-site source if the utility side misbehaves. The functions are simple and worth knowing by their ANSI numbers:

  • 27 / 59 — under/over-voltage: trips the interface if the SEC voltage leaves its permitted band (the source must not prop up, or be damaged by, an abnormal network).
  • 81U / 81O — under/over-frequency: trips if frequency leaves the operating band around 60 Hz (indicative settings cluster around 59.3 / 60.5 Hz with time delays — confirm against the current SEC requirements).
  • Vector shift and ROCOF (rate of change of frequency): loss-of-mains detection. If the SEC feeder opens upstream, the small on-site source is left "islanded" with the plant load; voltage angle and frequency lurch, and these elements detect the lurch and trip within a few cycles. An undetected island is dangerous — it back-energizes a network SEC believes is dead, and reclosing onto it out of phase can wreck the generator.

A plain-language picture of the two loss-of-mains elements: think of the on-site generator as a cyclist being towed by a truck (the SEC grid). While the rope is attached, the truck dictates the speed — the cyclist's pedalling barely matters. The instant the rope is cut, the cyclist's speed is suddenly set by their own legs against the hill: the cadence jumps (that step change in voltage phase angle is what the vector-shift element sees, typically a 6–12° jump) and then the speed starts drifting quickly up or down (that drift rate is what ROCOF measures, typically set at 0.5–1.0 Hz/s sustained over a few cycles). A big interconnected grid never changes speed that fast; a small island always does.

ANSI / element Watches Indicative setting (60 Hz / 13.8 kV base) Watch-out
27 under-voltage POC voltage too low ≈ 0.8 × Vn, 1–2 s Must sit outside the FRT/LVRT envelope, or the interface trips on dips the code requires it to ride through.
59 over-voltage POC voltage too high ≈ 1.10 × Vn, 0.5–1 s; ≈ 1.15 × Vn fast A lightly loaded island fed by the generator sees voltage rise within cycles.
81U / 81O under/over-frequency Frequency off the 60 Hz base ≈ 59.2–59.5 Hz / 60.5–61.0 Hz, 0.5–2 s Coordinate with the plant UF load-shed stages (59.3 / 59.0 / 58.7 Hz) so shedding gets a chance to save a legitimate island before the interface ends it.
Vector shift Step jump in voltage phase angle ≈ 6–12° Fast but can false-trip on severe external faults; many schemes pair it with ROCOF.
ROCOF (df/dt) Rate of change of frequency ≈ 0.5–1.0 Hz/s over 2–5 cycles Too sensitive → trips on ordinary grid events as renewable penetration grows (see ride-through); too slow → an island persists.
32 reverse power Power export toward SEC ≈ 2–5 % of source rating, 2–5 s Required where parallel operation is consented but export is not — enforces the "parallel, never export" agreement.
Intertrip (direct transfer trip) SEC upstream breaker position Hardwired / pilot / telecoms path, end-to-end tested The only certain loss-of-mains detection; SEC may mandate it where passive methods are judged insufficient. The communications path becomes plant-critical.
Anti-islanding is non-negotiable. If the plant ever adds parallel-capable generation — even "just for a 100 ms closed transition" — the loss-of-mains protection, its settings and its testing regime become part of the SEC connection agreement, and SEC may require a witnessed proof test before energization.

Solar PV and BESS at 60 Hz — what would change

Industrial rooftop and carport PV is increasingly attractive in the Kingdom: high irradiance, large flat roofs, and daytime-peaking process loads are a natural match. For this plant a future PV + battery (BESS) addition would be a Part 4 modification with the following electrical consequences (all indicative):

  • Interface at the LV-MSB. A PV array of a few hundred kWp would connect through string inverters to a dedicated outgoing way on each LV bus section. Inverters are certified to SASO-adopted standards (anti-islanding per IEC 62116) and configured with the 60 Hz Saudi grid profile — frequency band, voltage band, and frequency-watt response.
  • Fault contribution is small. Inverters limit fault current to roughly 1.0–1.2 × their rated current, so the plant's 38.5 kA LV fault level barely moves — the protection study is checked, not redone. Reverse-power flow through feeder protection, however, must be reviewed.
  • Dust soiling is the dominant derating. In Saudi conditions an uncleaned array can lose on the order of 15–25 % of yield per month to soiling (indicative — site-specific). A cleaning regime (and its water supply or dry-brush robots) belongs in the feasibility study, not as an afterthought.
  • BESS roles must be chosen, not assumed. Peak-shaving and PV time-shifting are energy applications tolerant of seconds-long response; replacing the UPS or riding through an SEC dip are power applications with strict response and certification demands. A BESS specified for one rarely does the other well — see the continuity templates at the start of this part.
  • Consent regime. Any PV/BESS that operates connected to the network is extended parallel operation: full SEC embedded-generation process, interface protection at the POC, and Saudi Grid Code compliance (including fault ride-through for larger plants).

Worked example — how much rooftop PV could this plant host?

Assume 5000 m² of usable, unshaded roof and a practical layout density of ~0.14 kWp/m² (walkways, plant rooms and tilt spacing included):

P_PV = 5000 m² × 0.14 kWp/m² ≈ 700 kWp (indicative)

Daily energy at a Saudi-typical 5.8 peak-sun-hours and a performance ratio of 0.80 (temperature losses dominate at 50 °C ambient — cell temperatures can exceed 75 °C):

E_day = 700 kWp × 5.8 h × 0.80 ≈ 3.2 MWh/day (clean array)

Compare with plant consumption at 2 MW demand and ~0.7 average load factor:

E_plant = 2000 kW × 0.7 × 24 h ≈ 33.6 MWh/day → PV ≈ 10 % of plant energy

Sanity check: 700 kWp on a 2 MW plant means PV can never exceed plant load during operation (no export hours), which simplifies the SEC agreement — a "parallel, never export" connection enforced by reverse-power protection is usually sufficient. All figures indicative; a feasibility study with measured irradiance and a structural roof survey governs.

Worked example — what dust soiling costs without cleaning

Take an indicative Saudi soiling rate of 20 % yield loss per month uncleaned (published site values span roughly 15–25 %/month and spike after shamal dust storms):

Loss after 1 month ≈ 0.20 × 3.2 MWh/day ≈ 0.64 MWh/day Average loss with fortnightly cleaning ≈ ½ × (0.20 × 0.5) ≈ 5 % ≈ 0.16 MWh/day

So a simple fortnightly cleaning cycle recovers roughly 0.5 MWh/day versus a month-uncleaned array — the cleaning contract (or dry-brush robot) typically pays for itself within the first year. Sanity check: this is why every credible KSA PV proposal carries a soiling-and-cleaning line item; treat its absence in a vendor offer as a red flag.

Indicative 60 Hz inverter grid-profile settings

Parameter Indicative setting Why
Nominal frequency / voltage 60 Hz / 400-230 V (LV interface) Saudi network base — never the factory-default 60 Hz export profile.
Continuous operating band ≈ 59.5 – 60.5 Hz Mirrors the Grid Code band; inverter stays connected through normal grid wobble.
Under/over-frequency trip ≈ 59.3 Hz and ≈ 60.5 Hz with ~2 s delay; fast trip ≈ 57.0 / 61.5 Hz Delayed stages coordinate with plant load shedding; fast stages protect equipment.
Frequency-watt response Linear power reduction above ≈ 60.2 Hz Inverter backs off output to help arrest a high-frequency event instead of tripping.
Voltage trip / ride-through ≈ 0.88 / 1.10 × Vn with LVRT envelope per code Rides through dips the code requires; disconnects from genuinely abnormal voltage.
Anti-islanding Active method, type-tested per SASO-adopted IEC 62116, trip ≤ 2 s Guarantees the PV cannot sustain an undetected island on a dead SEC feeder.
Reconnection Only after voltage and frequency stable in-band for ≈ 60–300 s, ramped return Prevents all inverters slamming back simultaneously after a disturbance.

BESS: peak-shaving battery vs UPS-grade battery — not the same machine

Attribute Peak-shaving / PV-shifting BESS UPS-grade / ride-through BESS
Job Cut the demand peak SEC bills for; absorb midday PV and release it later Hold the essential bus up through dips and the DG start gap (≤ 10 s)
Response time Seconds — scheduled or tariff-triggered Milliseconds — grid-forming or in-line, seamless to the load
Energy vs power Energy-heavy: 1–4 h at moderate power Power-heavy: minutes of storage at high power
Duty cycle Deep cycle daily — cycle life dominates sizing Rarely discharged — calendar life and availability dominate
Interface Grid-following inverter on LV-MSB; full parallel-operation consent Grid-forming / transfer-switched on the essential bus; coordinates with UPS and ATS
Relation to existing kit Supplements the tariff case for the PFC/metering review Supplements — never silently replaces — the 100 kVA / 30 min UPS and the DG

Worked example — does 700 kWp of PV change the fault level at LV-MSB?

Inverter rated current at 400 V:

I_rated = 700 kW / (√3 × 400 V) = 700 000 / 693 ≈ 1010 A

Inverter fault contribution ≈ 1.2 × rated (current-limited electronics, no rotating mass):

I_fault,PV ≈ 1.2 × 1010 A ≈ 1.2 kA — vs bus fault level 38.5 kA → ≈ 3 %

Sanity check: the switchgear short-circuit ratings are untouched; the real protection work is checking direction (reverse flow through incomer and feeder relays) and confirming the small PV infeed cannot hold up voltage enough to confuse UV-based transfer schemes. All figures indicative.

At 60 Hz nothing about PV physics changes — panels are DC. The frequency matters only to the inverters' grid profile and the anti-islanding settings, which must be ordered and commissioned for the Saudi 60 Hz network, not a generic 60 Hz export profile.

Embedded-generation modification checklist

  • Operating mode decided and documented: open transition / closed transition / extended parallel.
  • SEC consulted and consent obtained for any mode that ever parallels the network.
  • Interface protection specified (27/59, 81U/81O, vector shift or ROCOF) with settings agreed with SEC.
  • Fail-safe overlap timer specified for closed transition (trips DG breaker if overlap exceeds the agreed window).
  • Fault-level and protection-coordination study reviewed for the new source's contribution and reverse flows.
  • Inverters certified to SASO-adopted standards with the Saudi 60 Hz grid profile (anti-islanding per IEC 62116).
  • Soiling-cleaning regime and 50 °C derating included in the PV/BESS yield and sizing calculations.
  • SEC-witnessed commissioning and proof tests scheduled where required by the connection agreement.
Key takeaway: the moment any on-site source can be connected to the SEC network at the same time as the utility — even for 100 milliseconds — you have left "standby plant" and entered the Grid Code. Decide the operating mode first; the consent, protection and study obligations all follow from that single choice.

Desk Study Procedure

Where you are in the workflow: Part 5 is your field task guide. Work through it in order for every plant. You are here — beginning the pre-site desk study, before you travel to the plant.

The desk study is the preparatory phase that takes place in the office, typically 3–7 days before the site visit. Its purpose is to understand the plant's electrical architecture before you arrive, identify gaps in the document package, and frame the questions you need to answer on site. A thorough desk study shortens the site visit and substantially improves the quality of your FMECA.

Documents to request

Issue a formal document request to the plant owner / O&M team as soon as the plant is assigned. The table below lists the categories to cover and the specific detail to ask for.

Category Documents to request Why you need it
Single-line diagrams Overall SLD (all voltage levels); MV switchgear SLD; LV main switchboard SLD; MCC & distribution SLD; standby (DG/ATS) schematic. Request as-built revision with date stamp. Foundation of every analysis: SPOF identification, load-flow model, protection review, SLD verification on site.
Utility (SEC) connection data SEC connection agreement / service order; declared point-of-supply voltage (13.8 kV is typical for plants of this size); SEC-advised fault level at the intake (typically quoted in the 25–40 kA range — confirm the site-specific letter, indicative); metering arrangement; any Saudi Grid Code / SEC Distribution Code compliance correspondence. The SEC fault-level letter is the source datum for the short-circuit study; without it the MV switchgear withstand check cannot be closed. SEC Distribution Code conditions (power factor, harmonics) also frame the PFC review.
Equipment schedules & load list Electrical load schedule (kW, pf, demand factor per feeder); equipment schedule (make, model, year, rating for transformers, switchgear, generators, UPS, batteries, VFDs); cable schedule. Input for transformer sizing, load-flow, short-circuit, and genset sizing calculations (see Part 2 — Calculations).
Protection coordination study Most recent protection coordination / relay grading study with relay setting records (incomer, feeders, bus-section, transformer differential). Request the live controlled revision — not the original EPC study if the plant has been modified since. Grading selectivity is a common SPOF class. An outdated study (predating expansions or modifications) is itself a risk finding.
Earthing design & test records Earthing system design study (grid resistance, touch/step potential); soil resistivity report; as-installed grid resistance test results; bonding schedule. Date of last test. Earthing is a latent safety SPOF: a degraded or untested earth grid impairs earth-fault protection and creates touch/step hazards.
Arc-flash study IEEE 1584 / IEC 61482-1 arc-flash / incident energy study; PPE category labels in use; safe-working-distance schedule. Check that the study post-dates any switchgear replacement or protection re-grading. Obsolete arc-flash studies are a QHSE SPOF. An out-of-date study gives incorrect PPE boundaries — a direct safety risk.
Transformer condition records Factory acceptance test (FAT) and site acceptance test (SAT) reports; dissolved gas analysis (DGA) / oil-sample trend reports; temperature monitoring logs; thermography records. Transformer internal faults are high-severity events. DGA trend is the primary condition indicator — missing DGA data raises the Detection (D) rating in the FMECA.
Standby generator records Genset O&M manual; auto-start / load test log (date, duration, outcome); maintenance history; fuel-delivery records. Genset fails-to-start is a hidden (latent) failure mode — high Detection rating if test logs are absent or intermittent.
DC / battery charger records Station battery and charger O&M manual; battery capacity-discharge test records; DC-bus voltage trend; alarm-annunciator history. DC supply is the trip power source for MV protection. Charger ageing with no capacity test on record is a top-priority latent SPOF.
Switchgear maintenance records Circuit breaker service records (racking operations count, contact-wear status, timing tests, primary injection dates); switchgear inspection reports. Ageing switchgear with no primary injection records has uncertain protection reliability. Identifies candidates for the FMECA switchgear rows.
Asset register Plant electrical asset register (ISO 14224 format if available); tag list. Maps tags to equipment for FMECA rows and document traceability.

How to review the received documents

Follow this sequence when documents arrive:

  1. Open the Document Inventory form (SF-01) immediately. Log every document as it arrives: title, revision, date, format, quality (Good / Legible-only / Poor), and status (Received / Partial / Missing / Obsolete). Do not wait until after the visit.
  2. Study the master SLD first. Trace the power flow from the utility point of supply through MV-MET → MV-SWGR → TX-1/TX-2 → LV-MSB → MCC and loads.
