Lead time is a design constraint, not a procurement detail. Long-lead electrical equipment regularly sits on the project critical path. Indicative manufacturing lead times (ex-works, excluding shipping, customs and SASO/SABER clearance, and highly market-dependent):

EquipmentIndicative lead timeWhy it is long
MV/LV power transformers~6–12 monthsCore steel and winding capacity bookings; per-order design and routine testing
MV switchgear~6–12 monthsEngineered-to-order panels, protection integration, factory acceptance testing
LV switchboards / MCCs~4–8 monthsLocal panel-building queue plus breaker component lead times
Diesel generator sets~4–9 monthsEngine allocation, alternator matching, canopy build
UPS & battery systems~3–6 monthsBattery sourcing and certification documentation
A 9-month transformer lead time means the transformer specification must be frozen and ordered while the rest of the plant is still being designed. Late changes to "just" the impedance or the tapping range can restart the queue. Lock the long-lead datasheets first; design the rest of the plant around them.
When schedule is tight, ask vendors early about (a) stock or short-cycle variants of standard ratings, (b) production slot reservations against a letter of intent, and (c) which options force engineered-to-order treatment. Sometimes choosing the next standard rung up the ladder — already in stock — beats waiting for the "exact" unit.

How to Read a Datasheet

Indicative catalogue data. Product families and typical parameters are listed for training and familiarisation only, are approximate, and change with manufacturer revisions. No part numbers are given; always obtain current certified data sheets and SASO/SEC acceptance status from the manufacturer.

The anatomy of a datasheet — read it like an auditor, not a customer

A datasheet is written to be believed; you should read it to be verified. The single most important habit is this: every number on a datasheet is true only under stated reference conditions, and the reference conditions are usually printed in small type, in a footnote, or in a separate "service conditions" clause. IEC reference conditions typically assume a 40 °C maximum ambient, ≤ 1000 m altitude, clean dry air and a stated frequency. The plant in this manual sits at 50 °C design ambient, in dust and sand, on a 60 Hz system — so almost every headline number needs checking against a derating table before you may use it.

Read any datasheet in this order:

  • Standards line first. Which IEC standards, which edition, and — the key question — tested to them or merely "designed in accordance with" them (see the red-flag list below).
  • Service conditions next. Reference ambient, altitude, pollution/humidity class. This calibrates every other number on the page.
  • Then the ratings table, translating each row into the language of your Part 2 calculations.
  • Finally the footnotes — read every one. Footnotes are where ratings die.

Worked walk-through — a typical LV ACB datasheet table

Below is a fictitious but typical ratings table for an LV air circuit breaker of the class you would shortlist as the incomer of LV-MSB (which needs ≥ 2500 A continuous for the ~2309 A transformer LV full-load current, and a breaking capacity covering the tie-closed fault level of ~77 kA — see breaker selection and the short-circuit study; all indicative). No manufacturer or part number is implied.

Datasheet line (typical wording)Typical value (indicative)What it actually means for this plant
Rated current In (at 40 °C)2500 AContinuous capability at the reference ambient. Not yet usable — see the derating row.
Derated current at 50 °C / 55 °C~2400 A / ~2300 AThe number that matters in Saudi Arabia. At 50 °C switchroom-failure conditions this "2500 A" breaker carries ~2400 A — still above 2309 A FLC, but the margin has shrunk from ~8 % to ~4 %. Check the assembly derating too: the breaker inside a Form 4b cubicle runs hotter than the bare breaker the table describes.
Rated operational voltage Ue690 VMaximum system voltage at which performance is guaranteed. Our 400 V system is comfortably inside — but Icu is quoted per voltage: read the kA column for 400/230 V, not the headline.
Rated insulation voltage Ui1000 VDielectric design level of the insulation system; must be ≥ Ue.
Rated impulse withstand Uimp12 kVSurge/lightning impulse capability; relevant to insulation coordination of the installation.
Ultimate breaking capacity Icu at 400/230 V85 kAThe biggest fault the breaker can interrupt — once, possibly ending its service life. Must exceed the 77 kA tie-closed prospective fault (indicative).
Service breaking capacity Ics100 % of Icu (= 85 kA)The fault level it can interrupt and remain serviceable. For a main incomer, demand Ics = 100 % Icu; budget breakers quoting Ics = 50 % Icu are throwaway devices after a full fault.
Short-time withstand Icw85 kA / 1 sWhat it can carry without tripping while a downstream device clears — the property that makes time-graded selectivity possible. ACBs have high Icw; MCCBs typically do not. The time qualifier (1 s, 3 s) is part of the rating, never optional.
Rated frequency50 / 60 HzDual-rated — usable on the SEC 60 Hz system. Verify the trip unit and accessories share the dual rating (see below).
Degree of protection (front)IP30 (IP54 with door kit)The bare breaker is not dust-tight; the assembly around it must deliver the site's IP requirement.
Altitude≤ 2000 m without correctionAbove this, voltage and current corrections apply. Most Saudi industrial sites are low-altitude, but check — some inland plateau sites are not.
StandardsIEC 60947-1 / 60947-2, type-testedThe breaker product standard. The switchboard it lives in is verified separately to IEC 61439-1/-2 — two different certificates, both required.

Decoding the ratings that bite hardest in Saudi service

Rated current — at which ambient?

IEC LV gear is conventionally rated at 40 °C ambient (some assemblies at a 35 °C daily average). A Saudi outdoor or poorly cooled indoor location is designed at 50 °C, so the question to ask of every current rating is: "at what ambient, and what is the value at 50 °C?" A good datasheet has a derating table; a poor one is silent. Typical derating from 40 °C to 50 °C runs around 4–10 % depending on the device and enclosure (indicative). And remember the chain: breaker derating, busbar derating, cable derating (XLPE in 50 °C air ≈ ×0.82 — see cable sizing) all stack on the same circuit. The weakest derated link sets the circuit rating.

Icu vs Ics vs Icw — three different promises

These three kA numbers answer three different questions: can it break the worst fault at all (Icu), can it break a fault and stay in service (Ics), and can it sit there carrying the fault without tripping while something downstream clears (Icw, always with a time — e.g. 1 s or 3 s). Selection against the plant's fault levels (~38.5 kA one transformer, ~77 kA tie-closed, indicative) is worked through in Part 2 — breaker selection. On a datasheet, the trap is comparing one vendor's Icu against another's Ics, or accepting any kA figure quoted at a voltage other than yours.

Ue / Ui / Uimp

Three voltage ratings, three jobs: Ue is the operational voltage at which the performance tables are valid; Ui is the insulation design voltage (≥ Ue); Uimp is the impulse withstand for surges. Breaking capacity generally falls as Ue rises — always read the kA column at 400/230 V for this plant, not the flattering value at 240 V.

50/60 Hz — usually dual, never assumed

The SEC system is 60 Hz. Most modern switchgear, breakers, transformers in this class, and electronic equipment are genuinely dual-rated 50/60 Hz — but "most" is not "all", and the dual rating must appear on the datasheet and the nameplate, not in a salesman's email. Watch for: CTs/VTs and meters specified per-frequency; capacitor banks and detuned reactors whose tuning is frequency-specific by definition; and above all rotating machines. Motors and generator sets are not trivially dual-frequency: a 4-pole motor runs 1500 rpm at 60 Hz and 1800 rpm at 60 Hz, with different V/Hz, different fan loads and different driven-machine power demands. A genset alternator is wound and its engine governed for one frequency. Treat any 60 Hz-referenced data for rotating plant as inapplicable until the vendor issues 60 Hz data.

IP and IK — dust is the design case

IP ratings (IEC 60529) code ingress protection: first digit solids/dust, second digit water. This plant's outdoor minimum is IP54 — "dust-protected" and splash-proof; IP65 buys "dust-tight". IK ratings (IEC 62262) code impact resistance, relevant for gear in traffic areas. Two cautions: the IP rating belongs to the enclosure as installed (every unsealed gland hole voids it), and higher IP means less ventilation — re-check thermal derating whenever you raise IP.

Altitude and the standards line

Air insulates and cools less well at altitude; standard ratings hold to 1000 m (MV gear) or 2000 m (much LV gear), with correction factors above. Saudi sites are mostly low, but confirm — some inland sites exceed correction thresholds. Finally, the standards line: insist on the current edition of the relevant IEC standard (60947 for LV breakers, 62271 for MV gear, 61439 for assemblies, 60076 for transformers), cited as SASO-adopted IEC for Saudi procurement, with type-test evidence behind every claimed clause.

Red-flag checklist — when a datasheet is hiding something

Run this list over every datasheet that crosses your desk. Any single hit means "ask the vendor in writing"; two or more hits mean "treat the whole document as unverified marketing".

  • No type-test certificate offered, or "certificate available on request" with nothing attached — request it before technical acceptance, and check it names the same product family, frame and rating you are buying.
  • Ratings quoted at 20 °C or 25 °C ambient — laboratory-flattering conditions; demand the 40 °C standard values and the 50 °C derated values for Saudi service.
  • "Designed to", "in accordance with", "complies with the intent of" a standard — weasel wording. Only "type-tested to IEC xxxxx" (with the certificate) is evidence.
  • kA ratings quoted without a time qualifier or without the test voltage — a short-time withstand without its duration (1 s vs 3 s) or a breaking capacity without its voltage is not a rating, it is a slogan.
  • Ics not stated, or quietly set at 50 % of Icu on a main incomer application.
  • No 60 Hz column, or all performance data referenced to 60 Hz only — unacceptable for SEC-connected plant; rotating machines especially must have native 60 Hz data.
  • No derating tables at all (temperature, altitude, enclosure) — the vendor is leaving you to discover the limits in service.
  • Catalogue page submitted where a project datasheet was required — generic family data is not a commitment to your configuration.
  • IP rating quoted for the device but not for the assembly/enclosure as it will actually be installed on site.
  • Footnote symbols in the ratings table that lead to no visible footnote — request the unabridged document; the missing footnote is rarely good news.
Key takeaway: a datasheet is a claim, not a fact. The reference conditions (ambient, voltage, frequency, time) are part of every rating — a number quoted without its conditions is incomplete, and in a 50 °C, 60 Hz Saudi plant, incomplete usually means optimistic.

Saudi Certification & Vendor Approval

Indicative catalogue data. Product families and typical parameters are listed for training and familiarisation only, are approximate, and change with manufacturer revisions. No part numbers are given; always obtain current certified data sheets and SASO/SEC acceptance status from the manufacturer.

SASO conformity and the SABER platform — the concept

Technical compliance with an IEC standard and legal importability into Saudi Arabia are two different hurdles. The second is administered by SASO (the Saudi Standards, Metrology and Quality Organization), which adopts international standards — largely IEC for electrical products — as Saudi technical regulations, and requires regulated imported products to carry conformity certificates before they clear customs. The administrative vehicle is SABER, SASO's online conformity platform: at concept level, the importer registers the product, an accredited conformity assessment body verifies compliance against the applicable technical regulation, a product certificate of conformity is issued, and each consignment then receives a shipment certificate of conformity that customs checks at the border. (The exact scope of regulated categories, the certificate types and the process details evolve — treat this paragraph as orientation, and confirm current requirements per shipment.)

Why a field engineer should care about what looks like a customs formality: equipment that arrives without its SABER paperwork can sit in port while your outage window, your crane hire and your commissioning team do not. A transformer with a 9-month manufacturing lead time (see above) that then loses further weeks at customs for a missing certificate is a wholly avoidable project wound. Conformity certification belongs on the purchase order as a named deliverable, with the importer-of-record responsibility stated, not discovered at the port.

SASO conformity tells you the product class meets Saudi technical regulations. It does not tell you the product suits your application — that is what the datasheet, type-test certificate and your Part 2 calculations are for. Both hurdles must be cleared independently.

SEC vendor approval and the Saudi Building Code

For equipment on or facing the utility side — metering panels, the incoming MV switchgear arrangement, protection schemes at the intake — the Saudi Electricity Company operates approved-vendor and approved-material lists, and connection works are accepted against SEC specifications and the SEC Distribution Code. At concept level: it is not enough that your 13.8 kV switchgear is type-tested to IEC 62271-200; for the utility interface, the make and type should appear on (or be accepted onto) the relevant SEC list, and the intake design follows SEC standard arrangements. Getting a technically excellent but unlisted product accepted is possible but slow — for utility-facing packages, start from the approved list rather than from the open catalogue, and engage SEC early through the connection-application process. (List contents and procedures change; verify current SEC requirements per project.)

In parallel, the Saudi Building Code — specifically SBC 401 for electrical works — governs the installation design itself: wiring methods, protection, earthing, safety provisions for buildings and facilities. SBC 401 is the Saudi adaptation of international electrical-code practice, and authorities having jurisdiction review designs and installations against it. Practically: cite SASO-adopted IEC standards for products, SBC 401 for the installation, and the SEC Distribution Code / Saudi Grid Code for the utility connection — the three cover different territory and a compliant project needs all three (see the standards approach used throughout this manual).

The approved-vendor culture — Aramco/SABIC spillover

Beyond the regulator and the utility, Saudi industrial procurement has been shaped for decades by the practices of the largest end users — Saudi Aramco and SABIC — whose engineering standards and approved-manufacturer regimes (such as Aramco's materials-system specifications and vendor qualification processes) define, for their own projects, exactly which manufacturers and which factories may supply each commodity class. This culture spills over: many private industrial clients, EPC contractors and PMC consultants in the Kingdom adopt Aramco- or SABIC-style approved-vendor lists wholesale, or write "shall be from an approved manufacturer regularly supplying the Saudi market" clauses into otherwise ordinary specifications (indicative — this varies widely by client and contract).

For the field engineer the consequence is simple: the real catalogue for your project is the intersection of the vendor's catalogue and the client's approved-vendor list. Establish that intersection before falling in love with a product family. A technically superior drive or relay that is not on the client's list is, for your project, a product that does not exist — or one that costs you a months-long qualification exercise. Conversely, list-qualified vendors usually carry the Saudi-specific documentation (SASO certificates, 50 °C derating data, 60 Hz test reports, local service presence) as a matter of routine, which de-risks everything in the previous two sections.

The document set — who issues what, and when you need it

All of the above condenses into a finite set of documents. The table below is the field engineer's quick reference: for each document type, who issues it and at what project stage you must have it in hand (indicative — contract documentation requirements govern).

DocumentIssued byWhen you need it
Catalogue / family selection guideManufacturer (marketing/technical)Shortlisting and feasibility — before enquiry
Project datasheet (filled, signed)Manufacturer, against your enquiry datasheetTechnical bid evaluation; frozen at purchase order
Type-test certificates / reportsIndependent test laboratory or certified manufacturer labWith the technical bid; verified before PO, re-checked at FAT
Routine test report (your serial numbers)Manufacturer's factoryAt FAT / before despatch — witness where the contract allows
GA & foundation drawingsManufacturer (order engineering)For approval early in order execution — civil and cable design depend on them
SASO product certificate of conformityAccredited conformity assessment body via SABERBefore shipment is booked
SABER shipment certificate of conformityVia SABER platform, per consignmentAt customs clearance — every shipment
SEC vendor/material approval evidenceSEC (lists/acceptance correspondence)At design stage, before ordering utility-facing equipment
Client approved-vendor list confirmationClient / PMC procurementBefore shortlisting — it defines the playing field
Installation, O&M manuals and spare parts listManufacturerWith delivery; reviewed before energisation

What this means at the FAT and in the field

Pulling the threads together, here is the practical discipline. When you attend a factory acceptance test — or receive equipment on site — the hardware is only half the inspection. Ask for, and actually open, three documents: the SASO/SABER conformity certificate (does it cover this product category and this consignment?), the type-test report (does it name this family, frame and rating, this Icu/Icw, this standard edition?), and the routine test report (does the serial number match the nameplate in front of you, and did every routine test pass?). Then put the project datasheet beside the nameplate and read them line against line: voltage, frequency (60 Hz!), rated current and its reference ambient, fault ratings with their times, IP rating, tap range. Ten minutes of comparison at the factory saves ten weeks of dispute at site.

  • Confirm the vendor is on the client's (and where applicable SEC's) approved list before the technical evaluation starts.
  • Confirm SASO/SABER certification responsibility is named on the purchase order, with the importer of record identified.
  • At FAT: sight the type-test certificate and check it covers the exact family, frame, rating and standard edition supplied.
  • At FAT: obtain the routine test report and match serial numbers against nameplates, unit by unit.
  • Verify nameplate data against the frozen project datasheet — voltage, 60 Hz, current at stated ambient, kA ratings with time, IP rating.
  • Check 50 °C derating data is included in the final documentation, not just the 40 °C reference values.
  • File the complete dossier (datasheet, certificates, GA, O&M) before energisation — it is also your baseline for every future modification.
Key takeaway: in Saudi practice a piece of equipment is only "real" when four proofs line up — the client's vendor approval, the SASO/SABER conformity certificate, the type-test certificate for the design, and the routine test report for your serial number. The field engineer who asks for all four at FAT, every time, almost never gets surprised at site.

MV Switchgear Catalogue

Indicative catalogue data. Product families and typical parameters are listed for training and familiarisation only, are approximate, and change with manufacturer revisions. No part numbers are given; always obtain current certified data sheets and SASO/SEC acceptance status from the manufacturer.

Goal: learn how to read a medium-voltage switchgear catalogue the way a specifying engineer reads it — starting from what the plant actually needs (tag MV-SWGR, the 13.8 kV intake board), mapping each plant requirement to the catalogue parameter that answers it, and finishing with the specification card you would attach to a request for quotation. The vendor families named here (ABB UniGear, Siemens NXAIR, Schneider PIX and their relatives) are the ones you will most often meet on Saudi industrial sites, so the comparison doubles as field familiarisation. For the reasoning behind the ratings themselves, this section leans on the short-circuit study and the circuit-breaker tutorial; for how Part 6 catalogue sections are meant to be used, see the catalogue introduction.

What the Plant Needs — the Demand Side of the Catalogue

Before opening any catalogue, write down what you are shopping for. A catalogue is a menu of answers; if you do not arrive with the questions, the glossy photographs will choose for you. For this plant the questions are fixed by the Rev 3 plant basis and by the short-circuit study, and they define one piece of equipment: tag MV-SWGR, the medium-voltage switchboard that takes the SEC 13.8 kV supply and distributes it to the two transformer feeders (TX-1 and TX-2 — see the transformer catalogue section).

The shopping list, with the reason behind each line:

Requirement (indicative)Why this value
Rated voltage class 17.5 kV The system runs at 13.8 kV. IEC switchgear is built in standard voltage classes (7.2 / 12 / 17.5 / 24 / 36 kV); the class must be the next standard step at or above the highest system voltage, so a 13.8 kV system lands in the 17.5 kV class. The 12 kV class — the most common class worldwide, built for 11 kV systems — is not acceptable here; this is the single most common ordering error when a design is transplanted from an 11 kV country to a 13.8 kV SEC network.
Rated frequency 60 Hz The SEC grid is 60 Hz. Most catalogue type tests cover 50/60 Hz, but the rating must be declared at 60 Hz — it changes the peak withstand factor (2.6 instead of 2.5) and some instrument-transformer accuracy figures.
Busbar rating 630–1250 A The plant draws roughly 2 × 66.9 A at MV — trivially small. The bus rating is set by the smallest frames the product family offers (typically 630 A) and by any future-load margin, not by load current.
Short-time withstand 31.5 kA / 3 s The design utility fault level is 25 kA, but SEC states a network range of 25–40 kA. 31.5 kA is the next standard rating above the design duty; if SEC confirms a connection-point level above 31.5 kA in writing, the board class must rise to 40 kA. See the short-circuit section for the full reasoning.
Metal-clad, withdrawable vacuum breakers Earthed metal partitions between busbar, breaker and cable compartments limit fault spread and allow safe access; withdrawable trucks give visible isolation and fast breaker exchange. Vacuum is the dominant MV interruption technology (breaker tutorial).
Internal arc classification IAC AFLR 31.5 kA 1 s If an arc ever ignites inside the board, the enclosure must contain it for long enough for protection to clear, without burning or blowing panels into the operator aisle. Directly linked to the arc-flash study (§ arc-flash).
Service conditions 50 °C design ambient, dust Catalogue continuous ratings are referenced to 40 °C; Saudi rooms must either be cooled or the gear derated — covered in the Saudi-specific notes below.

Notice what is not on the list: load current barely features. A 2 MW plant on a 13.8 kV intake is a fault-level-driven purchase, not a load-driven one. The board you buy will spend its life carrying a few tens of amps and must nevertheless survive 31.5 kA for three seconds, because the withstand duty is set by the SEC network behind it, not by anything inside the fence. This inversion — tiny normal duty, enormous abnormal duty — is normal at the boundary between a utility and a small plant, and it is why the catalogue comparison below concentrates on withstand, arc classification and compartmentalization rather than ampacity.

Key takeaway: arrive at the catalogue with the demand side written down — 17.5 kV class, 60 Hz, 630–1250 A bus, 31.5 kA/3 s, metal-clad withdrawable vacuum, IAC AFLR — and treat every product page as a yes/no answer to that list. All values indicative; the formal studies and SEC's written fault-level confirmation govern.

The Vendor Families You Will Meet

Three air-insulated metal-clad families dominate the primary-distribution market in the Gulf, one from each of the big three manufacturers. They are functionally equivalent — all are withdrawable vacuum-breaker boards type-tested to IEC 62271-200 — and a specification written around the standard, not around a brand, will receive compliant offers from all three. Typical indicative class ranges:

FamilyVendorConstructionVoltage classes (typ.) Bus current (typ.)Short-time (typ.)Notes for this plant
UniGear ZS1ABB Air-insulated metal-clad, withdrawable VCB up to 17.5 kV (related variants to 24 kV) up to ~4000 Aup to ~50 kA Very widely installed in Saudi industry; 17.5 kV / 31.5 kA / 630–1250 A sits comfortably in the low-middle of the range.
NXAIRSiemens Air-insulated metal-clad, withdrawable VCB up to 17.5 kV (24 kV variant exists) up to ~4000 Aup to ~50 kA Common on SEC-adjacent projects; strong 60 Hz type-test coverage.
PIXSchneider Electric Air-insulated metal-clad, withdrawable VCB up to 17.5 kV (PIX-24 to 24 kV) up to ~4000 Aup to ~50 kA Often paired with Schneider relays and LV boards for single-vendor packages.

Read the table the right way round: the plant needs 31.5 kA and 630–1250 A, and every family comfortably exceeds that. The comparison between brands is therefore not won on headline ratings. It is won on delivery time, local service and spares presence in the Kingdom, SEC acceptance history, relay-integration preference (relay catalogue), and the 50 °C derating answer discussed in the Saudi notes.

Where ring main units fit — and why not here

Catalogues also offer a cheaper, smaller class of MV gear: the ring main unit (RMU) and its modular cousins — Schneider SM6 (modular, air- or gas-insulated functional units) and ABB SafeRing / SafePlus (sealed gas-insulated ring main blocks). These are secondary distribution switchgear: fixed-pattern (no withdrawable truck), mostly switch-fuse or small-breaker functions, designed to loop a distribution ring in and out and tee off one or two transformers. They are superb at that job — compact, sealed against dust, near-maintenance-free — and you will see thousands of them in Saudi utility kiosks.

They are the wrong product for MV-SWGR, for three reasons. First, withstand: typical RMU short-time ratings sit around 20–25 kA, below the 31.5 kA this intake demands. Second, protection: a utility-intake board needs full-function IED protection on withdrawable breakers with proper CTs and VTs (protection coordination), not fuse-switch combinations. Third, service continuity: a fixed-pattern sealed block cannot have a breaker racked out and exchanged in an hour. Know the RMU class so you can recognise when a vendor is quoting you one to win on price — and decline.

Gas-insulated primary gear and the SF6-free trend

For completeness, the same vendors offer gas-insulated primary switchgear — Siemens NXPLUS C (and the 8DJH family at secondary level), ABB ZX-series — where the busbars and breakers live in sealed, gas-filled stainless enclosures. The attraction is footprint (roughly half the width of air-insulated gear) and immunity to dust and humidity, which is genuinely tempting in the Saudi environment. The costs are price, less straightforward extension, and historically the gas itself: SF6 is the most potent greenhouse gas known and is being regulated out of new installations worldwide.

The industry's answer is the SF6-free generation: vacuum interrupters for breaking, with clean/dry air or proprietary low-GWP mixtures (ABB's AirPlus, Siemens' "Blue" portfolio, Schneider's air-insulated AirSeT take on SM6/RM) for insulation. Typical ratings already cover the 17.5 kV / 31.5 kA point this plant needs. For a new Saudi plant the practical guidance is: air-insulated metal-clad remains the default for a primary intake board in a conditioned room; if a gas-insulated board is chosen for footprint or environment, prefer the SF6-free variant and ask the vendor for its SASO/SEC acceptance status explicitly. Typical ranges only — no part numbers, no prices; tender data sheets govern.

Comparing offers — the classic traps

When three compliant-looking bids land on your desk, the differences hide in the fine print, and they recur so reliably they deserve a list:

  • The 12 kV substitution. A bid built on the vendor's high-volume 12 kV variant "suitable for 13.8 kV operation". Reject: operating a 12 kV-class board (28 kV/75 kV insulation ladder) at 13.8 kV erodes the insulation margin the type test promised. Demand the 17.5 kV type-test certificates by number.
  • IAC at a smaller current or shorter time. "Internal arc classified" with 25 kA/0.5 s in a footnote. The IAC figures must match the short-time rating and exceed the realistic clearing time.
  • IAC AFL, not AFLR. Cheaper, but commits the installation to a rear wall forever and complicates rear cable work.
  • "Metal-enclosed" sold as "metal-clad". Metal-enclosed is the umbrella term; only LSC2B-PM with earthed metal partitions is metal-clad in the sense this plant specifies. Check the LSC/partition codes, not the brochure adjective.
  • Ratings quoted at 40 °C only. Silent on 50 °C performance — see the Saudi notes; make the 50 °C statement a mandatory bid deliverable.
  • 50 Hz-only type tests. Rare from the big three, common from re-badgers. The 60 Hz declaration affects the peak factor and instrument transformer accuracy; ask for it explicitly.
A clean way to force comparability: send the specification card as a fill-in table and require each bidder to return it line by line with certificate references. A bidder who will not fill in a row is telling you something.

Anatomy of an MV Switchgear Catalogue Page

An MV switchgear catalogue is organised in two layers. The front pages describe the system — construction, compartments, arc classification, type tests. The back pages are the configurator — panel types, rating combinations, dimensions and weights per panel. Engineers who only read the back pages buy boards that meet the ratings but disappoint in service; the front pages are where the safety and maintainability are decided.

Panel types — the vocabulary of a line-up

A switchboard is ordered as a row of standard panels ("functional units"). The catalogue's panel-type table is the menu, and for this plant the line-up is short:

Panel typeFunctionThis plant (indicative)
IncomerReceives the utility cable; withdrawable breaker, full protection IEDs, metering CTs/VTs.1 × incomer from the SEC 13.8 kV connection, 630 A frame.
FeederWithdrawable breaker feeding a downstream load — here, a transformer.2 × transformer feeders (TX-1, TX-2), 630 A frames carrying ~66.9 A each.
Bus-tie / bus couplerSplits or joins bus sections. Not required on a single-incomer board; appears if a second SEC intake or a future MV generator is added.
Bus riserCarries the bus up/over to the adjacent section; partners a bus-tie or houses VTs.As above — only with a sectionalised bus.
Metering / VT panelBusbar voltage transformers, sometimes the utility tariff metering.1 × if SEC requires a dedicated metering panel at the intake (confirm with SEC early — it changes the line-up length).

Compartments and loss-of-service-continuity

"Metal-clad" means the panel interior is divided by earthed metal partitions into (at least) four compartments: busbar, circuit-breaker, cable (with CTs and the earthing switch), and the low-voltage box on top holding relays, meters and wiring. The point of the partitions is brutally practical: a fault, a tool, or a hand in one compartment is kept out of the others. IEC 62271-200 codifies this in two ratings you should read off every catalogue page:

Partition class — PM (partitions of metal, earthed) versus PI (insulating partitions). Specify PM for a primary board. Loss of service continuity class — what must be de-energised to open a compartment. LSC2B, the metal-clad benchmark, means you can open the breaker or cable compartment of one panel while the busbar and the other panels' cables stay live. On a single-incomer plant board this is what lets you work on a transformer feeder breaker while the other transformer keeps the plant alive. Lesser classes (LSC1, LSC2A) belong to secondary gear and RMUs.

Withdrawable versus fixed

In a withdrawable (draw-out) design the breaker sits on a truck; racking it from service to test to removed position physically separates the primary contacts behind automatic metal shutters. You gain visible-break isolation, a built-in test position for secondary injection, and breaker exchange measured in minutes. Fixed-pattern boards weld the breaker into the circuit and rely on a separate disconnector — cheaper and more compact, and the normal choice only at secondary level or in sealed gas-insulated designs where the interrupter is maintenance-free for life. For MV-SWGR, specify withdrawable.

Internal arc classification — decoding IAC AFLR 31.5 kA 1 s

The internal arc rating is a type test in which a real arc is deliberately struck inside each compartment and the enclosure must protect indicator cloths standing in for people. Decode the string: IAC = internal arc classified; A = authorised-personnel access only; F L R = the tested sides — Front, Lateral, Rear (an installation against a wall may only need AFL — but specify AFLR so the board can stand free); 31.5 kA 1 s = the arc current and duration survived. The arc current should equal the short-time rating; the duration must exceed the slowest realistic clearing time at the bus (check against the grading study and the arc-flash calculation). Note the asymmetry: short-time withstand is 3 s (a through-fault duty), internal arc is 1 s (an in-board arc, which protection must kill far faster).

IP ratings and the ratings ladder

Catalogue enclosure protection is typically IP3X or IP4X overall with IP2X between compartments — designed for a clean indoor room, not a dust storm. The implications for a Saudi switchroom are handled in the Saudi notes. Finally, every catalogue states the IEC 62271-1 insulation ladder for the voltage class; for 17.5 kV expect a 1-minute power-frequency withstand of 38 kV rms and a lightning impulse withstand of 95 kV peak (indicative standard values). If a data sheet shows the 12 kV-class ladder (28 kV / 75 kV), the wrong class is being offered — stop and query it.

Protection, Instrument Transformers and Interlocks

A switchgear panel is only as good as the measurement and intelligence built into it. The catalogue chapters on instrument transformers, low-voltage compartments and interlocking are where you verify that the board can host the protection scheme designed in the protection coordination section — and they are the chapters most often skipped at tender stage, which is why CT ratio changes and interlock disputes are the classic switchgear variation orders.

Relays and the LV compartment

Each panel's LV box hosts the protection IED (numerical relay), the trip/close control switches, indication lamps and terminal rows. Catalogues state which relay families are pre-engineered for the panel — typically the vendor's own (ABB Relion, Siemens SIPROTEC, Schneider Easergy/MiCOM; see the relay catalogue section) — but any IEC-compliant IED can be accommodated if you say so at tender. Specify, do not assume: relay family and model class, 110 V DC auxiliary supply from the plant DCDB, trip-circuit supervision, and at least one spare programmable contact per function. For the incomer, ask for the SEC-side requirements (intertrip, metering accuracy) to be confirmed in writing before the LV box drawings are approved.

CT and VT options

The cable compartment carries the current transformers; the catalogue offers block (resin) or ring-core types, single- or multi-ratio. Order the cores by duty: protection cores typically class 5P20, metering cores class 0.5 with an instrument security factor, ratios sized to the feeder duty (a 100/1 or 100/5 class on a 66.9 A transformer feeder is a typical indicative starting point — the protection study fixes the final ratio and burden). Voltage transformers come fixed or withdrawable; specify withdrawable, fused VTs so a failed VT or blown fuse can be exchanged without de-energising the bus. All accuracy figures must be declared at 60 Hz.

Voltage indication and earthing

Every panel should carry a capacitive voltage detecting system (VDS/VPIS to IEC 62271-206): bushing-mounted capacitive dividers feeding front-plate indicators that show, per phase, whether the cable side is live. This is the first thing a field engineer checks before racking or earthing, and the catalogue option costs almost nothing at order time and a panel rebuild afterwards. Pair it with the cable earthing switch — fault-making rated (it must survive being closed onto a live cable by mistake), operated from the front, with a clear mechanical position indicator.

Interlocks — the safety logic in steel

Catalogues list the standard interlock set; verify all of these are included and add project-specific keys:

  • Breaker can only be racked between service and test positions when open; the truck mechanically blocks closing mid-travel.
  • Earthing switch can only close when the breaker truck is in test/removed position; the truck cannot rack to service while the earthing switch is closed.
  • Automatic metal shutters cover the live spouts whenever the truck is withdrawn — padlockable per compartment.
  • LV plug interlock: the truck cannot rack to service with the secondary (control) plug disconnected.
  • Key interlocks with the upstream SEC point of connection and (if fitted) any bus-tie scheme, per the operating philosophy; key schedules are a deliverable to review at drawing approval, not at site.
At factory acceptance, physically attempt every forbidden operation — racking with the breaker closed, closing the earthing switch in service position. A correct interlock refuses smoothly; a misbuilt one lets you start the movement and jams. The FAT checklist in the Saudi notes includes this.

Selection for This Plant — Requirement to Catalogue Parameter

This is the worked mapping: each line of the plant's demand (Rev 3 Plant Data Card plus the short-circuit study) translated into the exact catalogue parameter to demand, and the typical family fit. Work down the table in order — voltage class first, withstand second, everything else after — because a failure on the first two lines disqualifies a product no matter how good the rest is.

Plant requirementCatalogue parameter to demand Value to write (indicative)Typical fit
SEC 13.8 kV system Rated voltage Ur; insulation levels Ud/Up (IEC 62271-1) 17.5 kV class; 38 kV rms 1 min; 95 kV peak BIL All three metal-clad families offer a 17.5 kV version — confirm it is the 17.5 kV type test, not a 12 kV unit "used at 13.8".
60 Hz grid Rated frequency fr, declared on the type-test certificates 60 Hz Standard for all families; check CT/VT accuracy declared at 60 Hz.
Utility fault duty 25 kA design (SEC range 25–40 kA) Short-time withstand Ik/tk; peak withstand Ip; breaker Isc (IEC 62271-100) 31.5 kA / 3 s; peak 2.6 × 31.5 ≈ 82 kA at 60 Hz; breaking 31.5 kA Comfortably inside every family's range; escalate the whole line to 40 kA if SEC confirms a higher connection-point level.
Plant load ~2 MW (2 × 66.9 A MV feeders) Rated continuous current Ir, bus and per panel Bus 630–1250 A; incomer and feeders 630 A frames Smallest frames in every family; choose 1250 A bus only if a future-load case justifies it.
Operator safety, arc event Internal arc classification (IEC 62271-200 annex) IAC AFLR 31.5 kA 1 s Standard or low-cost option on all families; confirm exhaust/plenum arrangement fits the room height.
Maintainability, service continuity LSC and partition classes; breaker mounting LSC2B-PM, withdrawable vacuum breakers, motor-charged spring mechanisms The defining feature of UniGear ZS1 / NXAIR / PIX — this line is what excludes RMUs.
Protection scheme (Part 2) IED family, CT/VT cores and classes, 110 V DC aux, VDS, interlocks Per previous sub-section All families integrate their own and third-party IEDs.
50 °C ambient, dusty site Service-conditions chapter; derated continuous ratings; enclosure IP Vendor-certified continuous ratings at 50 °C, or HVAC-conditioned room declared; IP4X minimum indoor See Saudi notes — this is the question vendors answer worst by default.

Worked check — margin between SEC duty and board rating

Confirm the 31.5 kA class actually covers the design duty with sensible margin. Design utility fault level (Plant Data Card): 25 kA at 13.8 kV.

Withstand margin = 31.5 kA / 25 kA = 1.26 → 26 % above design duty

Peak (first-loop asymmetric) duty at the bus, using the standard 60 Hz factor of 2.6 (see short-circuit section for where 2.6 comes from):

i_p,duty = 2.6 × 25 kA = 65 kA vs I_p,rated = 2.6 × 31.5 kA ≈ 82 kA ✓

Thermal withstand: the board is tested for 31.5 kA for 3 s, an I²t capability of:

(31.5 kA)² × 3 s ≈ 2977 kA²·s vs duty (25 kA)² × 1 s = 625 kA²·s ✓ (≈ 4.8× margin)

where 1 s is a conservative upper bound on backup clearing time from the grading study. Sanity check: all three margins are comfortable at the 25 kA design level — but if SEC's written confirmation comes back at, say, 35 kA (inside their stated 25–40 kA range), the withstand margin becomes 31.5/35 = 0.9 — under-rated, not allowed — and the whole board moves to the 40 kA class. This is why the fault-level letter is the first document on the critical path, before the RFQ goes out. All figures indicative; the formal study governs.

The specification card

Condensed into the data sheet you would attach to the RFQ for tag MV-SWGR — every value indicative, to be confirmed by the formal studies and SEC correspondence:

Tag / serviceMV-SWGR — main 13.8 kV intake and distribution board, SEC supply
StandardSASO-adopted IEC 62271-1 / -100 / -200; SEC Distribution Code; SBC 401
TypeMetal-clad, air-insulated, indoor, single busbar, LSC2B-PM
Rated voltage / insulation17.5 kV class (system 13.8 kV); 38 kV rms / 95 kV BIL
Rated frequency60 Hz
Busbar current630 A (option 1250 A), certified at 50 °C ambient or room HVAC stated
Short-time / peak withstand31.5 kA / 3 s; 82 kA peak (60 Hz)
Internal arcIAC AFLR 31.5 kA 1 s
Circuit breakersVacuum, withdrawable, 630 A, 31.5 kA, motor-spring, 110 V DC shunt trip/close, E2/M2 endurance class
Line-up (indicative)1 × incomer, 2 × transformer feeders (TX-1/TX-2), metering VT panel if required by SEC; 1 spare/space panel
Protection / meteringNumerical IEDs per protection study; CTs 5P20 + cl. 0.5 cores; withdrawable fused VTs; VDS on all panels
EarthingFault-making cable earthing switches; earth bar full length; NER interface per plant earthing design
Enclosure / environmentIP4X indoor; 50 °C design ambient; dust per site data; space heaters with thermostat
Documents with bidType-test certificates (incl. internal arc and 60 Hz), SASO/SABER status, SEC acceptance references, derating curves to 50 °C
Key takeaway: the specification is written in IEC 62271 parameter language, not brand language. Any of UniGear ZS1, NXAIR or PIX can satisfy this card; the card is what makes their offers comparable.

Saudi-Specific Practice — Heat, Dust, SEC and SASO

The 50 °C question

Every continuous current rating in an IEC switchgear catalogue is referenced to the IEC 62271-1 "normal service conditions": maximum ambient 40 °C (24-hour average 35 °C). The plant's design ambient is 50 °C. A board run at its catalogue current in a 50 °C room exceeds its tested temperature-rise limits at the contacts and joints — silently, year after year, until a spout or bus joint anneals and fails. The fix is administrative, not heroic: ask the vendor, in the RFQ, for certified continuous ratings at 50 °C. Because this board is thermally loafing (66.9 A on 630 A frames — about 11 % utilisation), the derated figure will still clear the duty by a wide margin; the point is to have it in writing rather than discovered in service. The rule of thumb vendors apply is a few percent of current per kelvin of excess ambient — a 630 A panel typically certifies somewhere around 540–580 A at 50 °C (indicative; the vendor's thermal calculation governs). The same question must be asked of the relay/IED operating range and the space-heater sizing.

In practice most Saudi switchrooms are HVAC-conditioned to hold ≤40 °C, which restores the catalogue ratings — but then the HVAC becomes electrical infrastructure: specify room over-temperature alarms to the control system, and check what the board is rated for during an HVAC failure on a 50 °C day, because that is exactly when the plant will refuse to shed load.

Dust and the room

Air-insulated switchgear breathes the room's air. Desert dust is conductive when combined with morning humidity, and a dust film across insulation is the classic precursor to the internal arc the IAC rating exists for. Practice: positively pressurised switchroom with filtered HVAC intake, door-closer discipline, IP4X enclosures as a backstop (not a substitute), space heaters running whenever panels are de-energised, and insulation inspection/cleaning in the maintenance plan. If the room cannot be kept clean — remote yard locations, temporary power — that is the legitimate use-case for sealed gas-insulated (preferably SF6-free) gear from the families section.

SEC acceptance and SASO/SABER paperwork

Two regulatory tracks run in parallel and neither is optional. First, SEC: the intake board sits at the utility boundary, so SEC reviews and accepts the design — fault level confirmation in writing, metering and protection requirements at the point of connection, intertrip and earthing interface, and in many cases a preference list of previously-accepted switchgear types. Engage SEC at specification stage, not at energisation. Second, SASO conformity: imported electrical equipment enters the Kingdom through the SABER platform with certificates of conformity against the SASO-adopted IEC standards. Make the vendor responsible in the purchase order for supplying type-test certificates (IEC 62271-1/-100/-200, including the internal-arc and 60 Hz evidence), SABER/SASO certification, and country-of-origin documents — missing paperwork strands switchgear in port for months far more often than technical faults do.

Factory acceptance test — scope checklist

The FAT is your last cheap chance to find problems. Witness it in person for an intake board. Indicative scope:

  • Verify ratings plates: 17.5 kV class, 60 Hz, 31.5 kA/3 s, IAC AFLR 31.5 kA 1 s, against approved data sheets.
  • Review type-test certificates and routine-test reports; confirm SASO/SABER and SEC acceptance documents are in the dossier.
  • Power-frequency withstand (routine) on main and auxiliary circuits witnessed and recorded.
  • Measure main-circuit resistance (ductor) across each breaker and bus joint; compare to vendor limits.
  • Rack every breaker through service–test–removed; check shutter operation and padlocking.
  • Attempt every forbidden interlock operation (rack with breaker closed, earth switch in service position, close with LV plug off) — all must be mechanically refused.
  • Operate each earthing switch; verify position indication and key-interlock scheme against the approved key schedule.
  • Secondary injection of every IED: pickups, curves and trip mapping per the approved settings file (protection study).
  • Primary injection of CT cores for ratio and polarity; VT ratio and fuse supervision; VDS indication per phase.
  • Function-test anti-pumping, trip-circuit supervision, and all alarms to terminal level at 110 V DC.
  • Check space heaters, thermostats and panel lighting; verify IP sealing, gland plates and cable-box sizing against approved cable schedules.
  • Confirm 50 °C derating statement, spares list, lifting/transport drawings and O&M manuals are delivered before shipment release.
Closing the loop. Everything in this catalogue section is indicative training data. The binding chain for the real purchase is: SEC written fault level → formal short-circuit study (Part 2) → approved data sheet (the spec card) → vendor certified drawings and type tests → FAT → SEC energisation approval. The catalogue's job is only to make you fluent for that conversation.

Distribution Transformers

Indicative catalogue data. Product families and typical parameters are listed for training and familiarisation only, are approximate, and change with manufacturer revisions. No part numbers are given; always obtain current certified data sheets and SASO/SEC acceptance status from the manufacturer.

Goal: learn to read a distribution-transformer catalogue the way a specifying engineer reads it — starting from what the plant actually needs (tags TX-1 and TX-2: 2 × 1600 kVA, 13.8/0.4 kV, Dyn11, Z = 6 %), translating each nameplate line into the plant consequence it controls, and finishing with the specification card you would attach to a request for quotation. The families named here (Siemens GEAFOL, Schneider Trihal, the Hitachi Energy dry-type and EcoDry ranges, and the hermetic oil-immersed distribution families) are the ones most often met on Saudi industrial sites. For the electrical reasoning behind the ratings — why 1600 kVA, why Dyn11, why 6 % — see the transformer calculation tutorial; for how Part 6 catalogue sections are meant to be used, see the catalogue introduction.

What the Plant Needs — the Two 1600 kVA Workhorses

Everything between the SEC 13.8 kV intake and the plant's 400/230 V world passes through two boxes of copper and steel: TX-1 and TX-2, each 1600 kVA, 13.8/0.4 kV, Dyn11, Z = 6 %, feeding the two halves of the split LV main switchboard with the bus-tie normally open. Each unit draws an indicative 66.9 A on its MV side at full rating and delivers 2309 A on its LV side. The plant's design demand is 2.0 MW at 0.85 power factor — 2353 kVA — so the pair runs at roughly 74 % in normal split operation and one unit carries about 87.5 % of its rating in the N−1 case with non-essential load shed. The impedance, 6 %, is the deliberate compromise that holds the LV short-circuit current to about 38.5 kA per transformer while keeping voltage regulation acceptable (the full reasoning lives in the transformer tutorial). All values indicative.

A transformer is the quietest purchase in the plant — no moving parts, no firmware, a forty-year design life — and that is exactly why catalogue mistakes hurt: an error ordered into a transformer is bolted into the plant for decades. The three decisions the catalogue forces on you are technology (cast resin or oil), losses class (you pay for losses every hour for forty years), and thermal basis (a 50 °C Saudi ambient is outside the IEC default assumptions and must be engineered, not hoped for).

Cast resin or oil-immersed — the first fork in the catalogue

Distribution transformers come in two basic technologies, and every vendor's catalogue splits at this fork on page one. In a cast-resin dry-type unit the MV windings are vacuum-cast in epoxy and the whole active part is cooled by the surrounding air (AN — air natural; optionally AF with fans). In an oil-immersed unit the core and windings sit in a tank of mineral or ester fluid that both insulates and carries heat to the tank wall and radiators (ONAN — oil natural, air natural; hermetically sealed in modern distribution sizes). Neither is "better"; they trade against each other:

CriterionCast resin (dry, AN/AF)Oil-immersed (hermetic ONAN)
Fire behaviour F1 fire class (self-extinguishing, low smoke); no flammable liquid. The default for indoor substations close to people and process. Tank of flammable fluid (mineral oil fire point ~170 °C); needs bunding, separation distances or fire walls per SBC; less-flammable ester fluids (K-class) soften but do not remove the issue.
Overload & cooling boost AN rating can typically be boosted ~40 % with add-on fans (AF) — attractive for the N−1 case. ONAN is the sealed rating; forced cooling (ONAF) is uncommon and clumsy at 1600 kVA distribution size.
Losses & cost Typically slightly higher load losses and higher purchase price at equal kVA and losses class. Usually the cheapest per kVA and the best loss figures at equal Ecodesign tier; the default for outdoor pad or yard installation.
Maintenance & failure mode No fluid to sample, leak or degrade; periodic cleaning of dust from windings is the whole maintenance plan — but that cleaning is mandatory in a dusty country. Hermetic units need almost nothing in service, but a tank breach is an environmental and fire event; oil sampling impossible on fully sealed designs.
50 °C ambient response Class F or H insulation gives thermal headroom; derate or specify higher insulation class — straightforward (see datasheet section). Oil top-temperature limits bite earlier; derating per IEC 60076 rules, and sun-exposed outdoor tanks need shading consideration.
Environment sensitivity Windings exposed to room air — dust and condensation are the enemies; specify an IP-rated enclosure and a clean room. Active part sealed away from the environment — inherently dust-proof.

For this plant the indicative baseline is cast-resin units indoors, in an air-conditioned or at least force-ventilated substation adjoining the LV switchroom: the units sit close to the LV-MSB (short 2309 A bus runs), the fire case is benign, and the AF fan option buys cheap N−1 margin. The oil-immersed alternative — two hermetic ONAN units on an outdoor pad — remains legitimate where the building cannot host them; the selection card in the selection section works for either.

Key takeaway: the plant's demand side is fixed before the catalogue opens — 2 × 1600 kVA, 13.8/0.4 kV, Dyn11, Z = 6 %, 60 Hz, 50 °C ambient. The catalogue only decides how those numbers are delivered: resin or oil, which losses tier, and what thermal margin. All values indicative; the detailed studies govern.

The Vendor Families You Will Meet

The cast-resin market is dominated by three family names you will meet on every Gulf tender list, plus a newer dry-type variant worth knowing; the oil-immersed market is broader but converges on the same hermetic sealed pattern from the same large vendors. All figures below are typical, indicative class ranges — never order from this table; order from the vendor's certified data sheet.

FamilyVendorTechnologyTypical range Insulation / fluidCoolingNotes for this plant
GEAFOLSiemens (Siemens Energy) Cast-resin dry-type ~100 kVA to ~40 MVA-class, up to 36 kV; 1600 kVA at 17.5 kV class is mid-range Class F (155 °C) typical; F1/E2/C2 environmental classes AN; AF option with cross-flow fans, typically +~40 % The archetypal cast-resin family; very widely installed in Saudi industry; well-documented 50 °C and 60 Hz variants.
TrihalSchneider Electric Cast-resin dry-type ~160 kVA to ~15 MVA-class, up to 36 kV Class F typical; F1/E2/C2 AN; AF option, typically +~40 % Often packaged with Schneider MV (SM6/PIX) and LV boards as a single-vendor substation; same conceptual offering as GEAFOL.
Dry-type / RESIBLOCHitachi Energy (ex ABB) Cast-resin / resin-impregnated dry-type Distribution sizes up to tens of MVA, up to 36 kV+ in special designs Class F or H available; F1/E2/C2 AN; AF option The RESIBLOC winding technique targets harsh-climate and high-mechanical- stress duties; class H option is directly useful at 50 °C.
EcoDryHitachi Energy Ultra-low-loss dry-type (amorphous-core variants) Distribution sizes ~up to a few MVA Class F/H AN Marketed on no-load-loss reduction — relevant here because both units stay energised 8760 h/year regardless of load (see losses discussion below).
Hermetic ONAN distribution Hitachi Energy / ABB heritage, and regional licensees Oil-immersed, hermetically sealed, corrugated tank ~50 kVA to ~3150 kVA, up to 36 kV Mineral oil (ester option); class A (105 °C) thermal system ONAN The global default outdoor distribution unit; strong local manufacture in the Kingdom (a SEC procurement advantage).
MineraSchneider Electric Oil-immersed, hermetic sealed ~25 kVA to ~3150 kVA, up to 36 kV Mineral oil (ester option) ONAN Schneider's hermetic ONAN family; same role as the Hitachi/ABB hermetic line.

Losses classes — the Ecodesign tiers as a common yardstick

Transformer efficiency is bought at order time, and the industry's clearest yardstick is the EU Ecodesign regulation (548/2014, "Tier 2" since 2021), which fixes maximum no-load (P0) and load (Pk) losses per size — paralleled by the IEC 60076 loss-class letters (e.g. A0Ak, the lowest-loss combination). Saudi Arabia does not apply the EU regulation as law — SASO efficiency requirements and SEC specifications govern — but quoting "Ecodesign Tier 2 compliant" or "A0Ak" in the RFQ is the cleanest way to force comparable loss figures from all bidders. Indicatively, a Tier-2-class 1600 kVA cast-resin unit shows a no-load loss of roughly 2.2–2.8 kW and a load loss of roughly 12–14 kW at 120 °C reference; the oil equivalent is lower on both counts. Remember the asymmetry: no-load loss is paid 8760 hours a year on both units the moment they are energised; load loss scales with the square of loading. At ~74 % split-bus loading both terms matter, which is why a cheap high-loss unit is the most expensive item in this catalogue over a 40-year life.

Altitude and ambient basis — read the small print

Every figure in the table above is referenced to the IEC 60076 "normal service conditions": altitude ≤ 1000 m and ambient ≤ 40 °C maximum (30 °C monthly / 20 °C yearly average). Altitude is rarely a problem on Saudi coastal and plateau sites below 1000 m — but check it for highland sites (Abha sits above 2000 m, where dielectric and cooling corrections both apply). Ambient, by contrast, is always a problem at a 50 °C design figure, and it is handled properly in the datasheet section below.

As with the switchgear families, the brand comparison is not won on headline ratings — all the families above deliver 1600 kVA at 13.8/0.4 kV without strain. It is won on certified losses, the written 50 °C / 60 Hz statements, local service and SASO/SEC paperwork, and delivery time.

How to Read a Transformer Datasheet

A transformer datasheet is a one-page contract: every line is a number the factory promises and a routine test will verify. Walk the nameplate top to bottom, and for each line know which plant problem it controls. The order below is the order the lines usually appear.

Rated power — and what "1600 kVA" actually promises

Rated power (IEC 60076-1) is the apparent power the unit can deliver continuously at rated voltage and frequency without exceeding the temperature-rise limits of its insulation class, at the standard ambient. It is a thermal promise, not an electrical limit — the copper does not know about kVA, only about heat. Two consequences: first, every kVA figure silently carries its ambient basis (40 °C max), which is why the Saudi correction below exists; second, short overloads are a matter of thermal time constants, not of the nameplate — cast resin tolerates cyclic overloads per IEC 60076-12 loading guides, oil per IEC 60076-7.

Vector group — Dyn11

Read Dyn11 as three facts: D — MV winding in delta; yn — LV winding in star with the neutral brought out (the plant TN-S system earths it solidly at the LV-MSB); 11 — the LV line voltage leads the MV by 330° (11 o'clock). The delta primary traps triplen harmonic currents and lets the star secondary serve unbalanced single-phase 230 V load; the clock number matters the day anyone parallels the two units through the bus-tie or adds a third source — paralleling units of different vector groups is a dead short. Both plant units must therefore be identical: Dyn11, same ratio, same impedance.

Impedance voltage — Z = 6 %

The impedance voltage is measured, not assumed: the routine short-circuit test applies whatever MV voltage drives rated current through a shorted LV winding, and expresses it as a percentage of rated voltage. Z fixes both ends of a see-saw: fault current down (LV Isc ≈ FLC / Z ≈ 2309 A / 0.06 ≈ 38.5 kA per unit — the number the entire LV switchboard withstand rating hangs on) and voltage regulation up. IEC tolerance on Z is ±10 % of the declared value: a unit delivered at 5.4 % pushes the LV fault duty toward ~42.8 kA, which still sits under a 50 kA board but eats margin — and units to be paralleled must match within the tolerance band or they will not share load. Check the test certificate value, not just the nameplate.

No-load and load losses

Two numbers, two physical origins, two bills. No-load loss P0 is the core (iron) loss — hysteresis and eddy currents in the laminations — present whenever the unit is energised, at any load, 8760 h/year. Load loss Pk is the winding (copper) I²R plus stray loss, quoted at rated current and a reference temperature (75 °C oil / 120 °C dry — check which, because comparing a 75 °C figure against a 120 °C figure flatters the wrong bidder). At the plant's ~74 % split-bus loading the load loss realised is Pk × 0.74² ≈ 0.55 Pk. Evaluate bids on capitalised losses (a typical owner formula prices each kW of P0 several times higher than each kW of Pk), not on purchase price.

Insulation class and temperature rise — the 50 °C correction

A dry-type insulation system is classed by the temperature it can endure for its design life: class F = 155 °C, class H = 180 °C. The datasheet pairs the class with a rated winding temperature rise — e.g. "class F, 100 K rise" — and the arithmetic only closes at the standard ambient: 40 °C ambient + 100 K rise + ~15 K hot-spot allowance ≈ 155 °C. Put the same unit in a 50 °C room and the hot-spot lands ~10 K above the class limit; insulation ageing roughly doubles for every 6–10 K of over-temperature, so a forty-year transformer quietly becomes a twenty-year one. IEC 60076-11 gives the corrections, and the buyer has two clean options:

  • Derate: accept a reduced continuous rating at 50 °C — indicatively around 7–10 % off (a "1600 kVA" unit certified at roughly 1440–1490 kVA), per the vendor's thermal calculation; or
  • Buy headroom (preferred here): order a class F system designed for a reduced rise (e.g. 80 K), or a class H system run at class-F rise, so the full 1600 kVA is certified at 50 °C ambient with the ageing budget intact. The cost premium is small against the asset life it protects.
The classic tender failure: the datasheet says "1600 kVA, class F" and nobody asks at what ambient. Make "rated power certified at 50 °C design ambient per IEC 60076-11" a mandatory line in the RFQ — and apply the same question to the AF fan rating and to oil units via IEC 60076-2.

Tappings — ±2 × 2.5 %

The MV winding carries an off-circuit tap changer with five positions: ±2 × 2.5 % around 13.8 kV. Its job is to correct a sustained deviation of the SEC supply voltage from nominal so the LV bus sits near 400 V — set once at commissioning after measuring the actual MV voltage, then revisited only if the network changes. "Off-circuit" means exactly that: the unit must be de-energised and isolated to move the selector; operating it on load destroys the contacts. Both plant units must always stand on the same tap, or the bus-tie paralleling case circulates current between them.

Inrush, sound and the remaining lines

Inrush: energising a 1600 kVA unit draws a magnetising inrush with a first peak typically 8–12 × rated current (indicatively 500–800 A on the 66.9 A MV feeder), decaying over tens of cycles. The MV feeder protection must ride through it — the relay's inrush (2nd-harmonic) restraint and the curve choice in the protection study depend on the vendor's declared inrush figure, so ask for it. Sound power level: per IEC 60076-10, a 1600 kVA dry unit typically declares around 65–75 dB(A) sound power — it matters for substations adjoining occupied rooms, and low-noise designs (reduced flux density) trade against no-load loss. Finally, check the insulation levels (for a 13.8 kV winding, the 17.5 kV-class ladder: 38 kV rms power-frequency / 95 kV peak lightning impulse, indicative) and the rated frequency line — which deserves its own discussion in the selection section.

Selection for This Plant — Requirement to Catalogue Parameter

The mapping from plant demand to catalogue parameter, worked with the Rev 3 Plant Data Card numbers. Work the table top to bottom — power and impedance first, because a failure there disqualifies a product regardless of the rest.

Worked check — rating, N−1 and fault duty from the Plant Data Card

Demand: 2.0 MW at 0.85 power factor.

S = P / pf = 2000 kW / 0.85 = 2353 kVA

Two units, split bus, normal operation:

Loading per unit = (2353 / 2) / 1600 = 1176 / 1600 ≈ 74 %

N−1 case — one transformer out, bus-tie closed, non-essential load shed so the survivor carries the essential demand:

Loading = 1400 kVA / 1600 kVA = 87.5 % → inside rating ✓

LV fault current per unit (infinite-bus approximation, see the transformer tutorial):

I_FLC,LV = 1600 kVA / (√3 × 0.4 kV) = 2309 A I_sc,LV ≈ I_FLC / Z = 2309 / 0.06 ≈ 38.5 kA (tie closed, two units: ≈ 77 kA)

Sanity check: 38.5 kA sits under the 50–65 kA LV-MSB rating with margin; the ~77 kA paralleled figure is exactly why the bus-tie is normally open and interlocked. If a vendor's unit arrives at the low end of the ±10 % impedance tolerance (5.4 %), the single-unit duty rises to ~42.8 kA — still inside the board rating, but the margin is consumed; this is why the test-certificate Z value is reviewed before energisation. All figures indicative; the formal short-circuit study governs.

The AN / AF decision — buying N−1 margin with fans

Cast-resin catalogues quote two ratings per frame: AN (natural air) and AF (forced air, add-on cross-flow fans), with AF typically +40 %. Two ways to use it on this plant, both indicative: order 1600 kVA AN units and treat the AF boost (~2240 kVA) as emergency headroom for the N−1 case — the survivor could then carry the full 2353 kVA demand without load shedding; or use AF to cover future load growth without changing frames. The discipline: the AF rating only exists while the fans, the fan supply and the fan controls work, so a design that depends on AF for the normal case has quietly created a new single point of failure — use AF as margin, not as the basis. Fans are triggered by winding temperature sensors (PT100) via the T-relay; test them at FAT.

The 60 Hz note — ordering for the SEC grid

The SEC grid is 60 Hz, and the unit must carry a 60 Hz nameplate. The physics is forgiving in this direction: core flux is proportional to V/f, so a transformer designed for 50 Hz and operated at the same voltage and 60 Hz runs at lower flux density — no saturation risk, slightly lower no-load loss. A 50 Hz-designed unit is therefore generally electromagnetically fine at 60 Hz (never the reverse — 60 Hz design at 50 Hz over-fluxes and saturates). But "generally fine" is not a procurement basis: cooling fans, motor drives in accessories, declared losses, sound level and the routine-test basis all shift with frequency, and SASO/SEC paperwork will ask for the 60 Hz declaration. The rule: always order a 60 Hz nameplate, with the routine tests performed and certified at 60 Hz; treat the 50-at-60 tolerance only as comfort that a misdeclared unit is not instantly dangerous, and verify fan and auxiliary ratings explicitly if any 50 Hz-basis component appears in the offer.

Plant requirementCatalogue parameter to demand Value to write (indicative)
2353 kVA demand, N−1 philosophy Rated power per IEC 60076-1, certified at site ambient 2 × 1600 kVA AN at 50 °C ambient (AF option ~+40 %)
SEC 13.8 kV system / 400 V TN-S Rated voltages, insulation levels, vector group 13 800 / 400–230 V; 17.5 kV-class ladder (38 kV rms / 95 kV BIL); Dyn11, both units identical
LV board withstand 50–65 kA Impedance voltage and tolerance, on test certificate Z = 6 % (±10 % IEC tolerance; matched pair for paralleling)
SEC 60 Hz grid Rated frequency on nameplate and routine tests 60 Hz declared and tested
Supply-voltage variation Off-circuit tappings on MV winding ±2 × 2.5 % (5 positions)
40-year energy bill P0 / Pk at stated reference temperature Ecodesign Tier 2 / low-loss class as reference; capitalised-loss evaluation
50 °C, dust, indoor Insulation class and rise; environmental/climatic/fire classes; enclosure Class F (reduced rise) or H; E2/C2/F1; IP31–IP44 enclosure (resin, indoor)
Protection integration Winding temperature devices; inrush data PT100 per limb + T-relay (alarm/trip/fan); declared inrush curve for the relay study
Key takeaway: every line of this card comes straight from the Plant Data Card or the studies — the catalogue's only freedoms are technology, losses tier and thermal margin. Specify in IEC 60076 parameter language and any of the families in the families table can bid comparably.

Saudi-Specific Practice — Heat, Dust, SASO and SEC

SASO certification and the SABER platform

Imported transformers clear Saudi customs through the SABER platform with certificates of conformity against the SASO-adopted IEC 60076 series — and SASO additionally maintains minimum energy-performance requirements for distribution transformers, so the losses figures are a regulatory matter, not just an economic one. Make the vendor contractually responsible for: SABER product and shipment certificates, type-test and routine-test reports (IEC 60076-1/-11 for dry, -1/-2 for oil, including the 60 Hz basis), and country-of-origin documents. Missing conformity paperwork strands transformers in port far more often than technical defects do. Note also that several of the families in the families table are manufactured inside the Kingdom under licence — local content (and the IKTVA programme) can be a tender criterion and usually shortens delivery.

SEC approved-vendor expectations

Where the transformer sits at or near the SEC boundary, expect SEC to apply its distribution-transformer specifications (the SEC standard transformer specs, e.g. the 51-SDMS series for distribution equipment) and, in practice, an approved-vendor expectation: makes and factories with an SEC acceptance history clear review faster. Even for plant-side units behind the metering point, aligning the data sheet with SEC's spec families (loss levels, fittings, rating-plate content) costs little and removes review friction. Engage SEC at specification stage and get the requirements — including the fault-level letter that drives the LV board rating — in writing before the RFQ goes out.

Heat, dust and the substation room

The 50 °C thermal answer was engineered in the datasheet section; the room has to deliver it. Cast-resin windings breathe the room air, and desert dust is the technology's one real weakness: a dust film on the resin surface, wetted by morning humidity, tracks — and a tracking flashover inside an unenclosed transformer is a plant-down event. Practice for this plant, all indicative: cast-resin units in IP31–IP44 ventilated enclosures (the enclosure derates the unit slightly — make the vendor certify the enclosed rating), in a force-ventilated or HVAC-conditioned room with filtered intake and over-temperature alarms to the control system; PT100 winding sensors wired to the T-relay with staged fan-start / alarm / trip; and scheduled de-energised cleaning of the windings written into the maintenance plan. Oil units outdoors dodge the dust problem (sealed tank) but inherit solar gain, sand abrasion of radiators and bunding requirements per SBC 401 and the fire code.

Seismic and transport

Saudi sites are mostly low-seismicity, but SBC requirements and (for industrial-city sites) the local authority may still ask for seismic qualification of anchorage — cast-resin units are top-heavy, and the anchor-bolt calculation is the vendor's to certify against the declared seismic class. Transport is the larger practical risk: a 1600 kVA unit is a ~3–5 t fragile load, and resin windings dislike shock. Specify impact recorders on each unit for the road journey, inspect on arrival before off-loading signature, and check the catalogue's transport dimensions and enclosure split early — door and corridor sizes have redesigned more substations than electrical calculations have.

At the factory acceptance test, witness the routine tests (ratio, vector group, impedance, losses, applied/induced voltage, partial discharge for resin units — PD is the cast-resin quality fingerprint, indicatively ≤10 pC), and read the measured impedance and losses against the capitalisation in the bid. The FAT certificate values, not the catalogue, become the plant's protection-study and energy-model inputs.

Procurement review checklist

  • Ratings line verified: 2 × 1600 kVA, 13.8/0.4 kV, Dyn11, Z = 6 %, 60 Hz nameplate — both units identical, impedances matched within tolerance.
  • Rated power certified in writing at 50 °C design ambient (IEC 60076-11 / -2), including the AF and enclosed ratings where applicable.
  • Losses (P0/Pk) declared at a stated reference temperature, evaluated on capitalised cost, and compliant with SASO efficiency requirements.
  • Insulation class/rise, fire-behaviour class (F1) and climatic/environmental classes (C2/E2) stated; IP enclosure rating and room ventilation design agreed.
  • Tappings ±2 × 2.5 % off-circuit confirmed; commissioning tap-setting procedure and same-tap rule for both units in the O&M documents.
  • Protection interfaces complete: PT100/T-relay schedule, declared inrush data to the relay study, marshalling to DCS/PMS defined.
  • Paperwork chain complete: SABER/SASO certificates, type- and routine-test reports at 60 Hz, SEC spec alignment and acceptance references, transport/impact-recorder and seismic anchorage documents.
Closing the loop. Everything in this section is indicative training data. The binding chain for the real purchase is: SEC written requirements → formal load and short-circuit studies (Part 2) → approved data sheet → vendor certified drawings and test reports → FAT → site acceptance and energisation. The catalogue's job is only to make you fluent for that conversation.

LV Circuit Breakers

Indicative catalogue data. Product families and typical parameters are listed for training and familiarisation only, are approximate, and change with manufacturer revisions. No part numbers are given; always obtain current certified data sheets and SASO/SEC acceptance status from the manufacturer.

Goal: learn to read a low-voltage circuit-breaker catalogue the way a specifying engineer reads it — starting from what the plant's 400/230 V TN-S system actually demands (incomers carrying 2309 A, a bus that can see 38.5 kA from one transformer or roughly 77 kA with the tie closed), mapping each demand to the IEC 60947-2 parameter that answers it, and finishing with the breaker schedule for tags LV-MSB and MCC-1..n. The vendor families named here (ABB Emax 2 and Tmax XT, Schneider Masterpact MTZ and ComPacT NSX, Siemens 3WA and 3VA, Eaton NZM) are the ones you will meet on Saudi industrial sites. For the physics of interruption and the LSIG protection study behind the settings, see the circuit-breaker tutorial and the short-circuit study; for how Part 6 sections are meant to be used, see the catalogue introduction.

Three Tiers of LV Breaker — Where Each One Lives in This Plant

Low-voltage breakers come in three constructional tiers, and the easiest way to keep them straight is to walk the plant's own LV system from the transformer down to the last socket. All three tiers are certified to the same standard family — IEC 60947-2 for industrial breakers (ACBs and MCCBs) and IEC 60898-1 for the household-grade miniature breakers in final distribution boards — but they differ enormously in frame size, breaking capacity, adjustability and price.

TierTypical framesWhere in this plantDefining traits
ACB — air circuit breaker 800–6300 A LV-MSB incomers from TX-1/TX-2 (FLC 2309 A each), the normally-open bus-tie, and the largest outgoing feeders. Open-construction interrupter in air; usually withdrawable; high short-time withstand (Icw) so it can hold on through a downstream fault while a smaller breaker clears it — the anchor of selectivity. Always an electronic trip unit.
MCCB — moulded-case circuit breaker 16–1600 A LV-MSB outgoing feeders to MCC-1..n, the PFC bank, DB/EDB sub-boards; MCC incomers and large motor feeders; the ATS/DG LV connections. Sealed moulded housing; mostly fixed-mounted (plug-in/withdrawable options exist); current-limiting designs with very high Icu; thermal-magnetic or electronic trip; little or no intentional short-time delay capability at the small end.
MCB — miniature circuit breaker 0.5–125 A Final circuits in DB/EDB and the UPS distribution: lighting, small power, sockets, instrument supplies at 230 V. DIN-rail, fixed characteristics (B/C/D curves), breaking capacity typically 6–10 kA (IEC 60898-1) — acceptable only because cable impedance has collapsed the fault level by the time it reaches the final board.

The plant's numbers explain why the tiers exist. At the LV-MSB bus the prospective short-circuit current is about 38.5 kA with one transformer, and roughly 77 kA in the abnormal case where the bus-tie is closed with both transformers in parallel — a duty only an ACB or a heavy MCCB class can face. Two floors of cable later, at a 230 V lighting board, the same network can only deliver a few kA, and a 6 kA MCB is honest equipment. Matching breaker tier to the local fault level, not the worst one in the plant, is what keeps the switchboard affordable — and getting that local level right is precisely the job of the short-circuit study.

The LV system basis for everything in this section: 400/230 V, 60 Hz, TN-S earthing, fed by 2 × 1600 kVA transformers (LV FLC 2309 A each, Z = 6 %) onto a split bus with a normally-open tie. All values indicative per the Rev 3 plant basis; the formal studies govern.
Key takeaway: ACBs guard the bus and survive faults long enough to let others trip first; MCCBs clear feeder and motor faults hard and fast; MCBs mop up final circuits where the network can no longer deliver a big fault. One plant, three tiers, one selectivity story.

Air Circuit Breakers — the Families for LV-MSB

Three ACB families dominate the Gulf market, one per major vendor, and they are close functional equivalents: all are IEC 60947-2 category B breakers with electronic trip units, available fixed or withdrawable, in three or four physical frame sizes that between them span roughly 800 to 6300 A.
