A specification written in IEC parameter language will draw compliant offers from all three. Typical indicative ranges at 400 V:

FamilyVendorFrame range (typ.) Icu classes at 400 V (typ.) Icw 1 s (typ.)Notes for this plant
Emax 2ABB ~800–6300 A over four frames (E1.2–E6.2 class sizes) ~42 / 66 / 100 / up to ~150 kA depending on frame and performance level ~42–100 kA by frame/level Ekip trip-unit family with built-in metering and an embedded power-controller option; wide Saudi installed base. A 2500–3200 A frame at the 85–100 kA performance level covers the incomer duty here.
Masterpact MTZSchneider Electric ~800–6300 A over three frames (MTZ1 / MTZ2 / MTZ3 class sizes) ~42 / 50 / 66 / 100 / up to ~150 kA by performance level (N/H grades) ~42–100 kA by frame/level Micrologic X control unit, strong diagnostics/comms ecosystem; natural pairing with ComPacT NSX downstream for cascading/selectivity tables.
3WASiemens ~630–6300 A over three frames ~55 / 66 / 85 / up to ~130 kA by performance level ~50–100 kA by frame/level ETU trip units with optional IEC 61850/Modbus comms modules; successor to the long-serving 3WL family you will still meet in existing Saudi boards.

Withdrawable versus fixed

ACBs are offered in fixed and withdrawable (cassette) versions. The withdrawable version racks between connected, test and disconnected positions exactly like its MV cousin: you gain visible isolation, secondary-injection testing in the test position, and breaker exchange in minutes — which on a two-incomer board like LV-MSB means one transformer's incomer can be swapped while the other half of the bus keeps the plant alive. The price is a deeper panel and a few percent on cost. For the three big breakers in this plant (two incomers and the bus-tie), specify withdrawable; for large outgoing ACB feeders, fixed is acceptable if the maintenance philosophy tolerates a dead-board exchange.

What actually differentiates the families

  • Trip-unit ecosystem — metering accuracy, protection options (LSIG and beyond), communications and diagnostics; this is where the brands genuinely diverge (see the trip-unit section).
  • Icw at the frame you need — the short-time withstand that underwrites time-graded selectivity; compare at your frame and performance level, not the brochure maximum.
  • Service in the Kingdom — spare trip units, racking accessories, local retrofit programmes; an ACB lives 25–30 years, far longer than its electronics.
  • Selectivity and cascading tables — vendor-published, type-tested pairings with their own downstream MCCBs; mixing brands sacrifices these tables and pushes you back onto first-principles coordination.
Catalogue frame names rarely equal current ratings: a "2500 frame" houses rating plugs or sensor sizes from roughly 1000 to 2500 A. Specify the sensor / rating-plug current In and the frame Iu separately, and leave sensor headroom for the 50 °C derating discussed in the datasheet section.

Moulded-Case Circuit Breakers — Feeder and Motor Workhorses

If the ACB is the board's anchor, the MCCB is its workhorse: every feeder from LV-MSB to an MCC, every MCC incomer, every large motor and the PFC bank connection is an MCCB decision. Four families cover most of the Saudi market. Each is built as a short ladder of physical frames, and each frame is sold in several breaking-capacity tiers — the same box with progressively stronger interrupting internals, typically stepping ~25 / 36 / 50 / 70 / 100 kA (some to 150 kA+) at 400 V. Typical indicative ranges:

FamilyVendorFrame range (typ.) Icu tiers at 400 V (typ.)Trip technology Notes for this plant
Tmax XTABB ~16–1600 A across several frame sizes ~25–70 kA standard tiers; high tiers to ~120–150 kA Thermal-magnetic (TMD/TMA) small frames; Ekip electronic from mid frames up Shares the Ekip trip-unit language with Emax 2 — one engineering tool and one spares philosophy across the whole board.
ComPacT NSXSchneider Electric ~16–630 A (NSX); larger duties to ~1600 A in the companion PowerPacT/ComPacT NS class ~25 / 36 / 50 / 70 / 100 kA tiers (F/N/H/S/L-style grades) Thermal-magnetic or Micrologic electronic Extensive published cascading/selectivity tables under Masterpact MTZ — the classic single-vendor LV package.
3VASiemens ~16–1000 A (3VA1/3VA2 classes); larger in companion families ~25–55 kA standard; ~70–110 kA high tiers Thermal-magnetic or ETU electronic ETU options mirror the 3WA ACB units; good 60 Hz documentation.
NZMEaton ~16–1600 A over four frames (NZM1–NZM4) ~25 / 36 / 50 / 100 / 150 kA tiers (B/C/N/H grades) Thermal-magnetic or electronic Strong motor-protection variants; common in packaged MCCs and OEM skids arriving on site.

Thermal-magnetic versus electronic

A thermal-magnetic MCCB trips on heat (a bimetal bends — long-time overload) and on magnetics (a solenoid snaps — instantaneous short-circuit). It is cheap, immune to electronics failure, and adjustable only coarsely (thermal pickup, sometimes the magnetic threshold). An electronic MCCB measures current with built-in sensors and a microprocessor: full LSIG adjustability, settable short-time delay for selectivity, metering and communications. The practical split for this plant: electronic trip units on the LV-MSB outgoing feeders and MCC incomers (where selectivity against the ACBs must be engineered — see the LSIG study), and thermal-magnetic is acceptable for small, deep-downstream feeders where an instantaneous trip is fine. Note one honest limitation: a thermal element calibrated at 40 °C reads the hot switchboard air as load — another reason the 50 °C question in the Saudi notes matters.

Current limitation — the MCCB's party trick

Many MCCBs are current-limiting: their contacts blow apart electrodynamically within the first quarter-cycle and the arc voltage chokes the fault before it ever reaches its prospective peak. A current-limiting MCCB facing a prospective 38.5 kA may let through the energy of only a fraction of that — which is what makes vendor cascading (back-up) tables possible: a downstream breaker of modest Icu certified, by type test, to be protected by the current-limiting breaker above it. Cascading saves real money in MCCs, but only within one vendor's published tables — it is a type-tested combination, never a calculation you may improvise.

Key takeaway: pick the MCCB frame for the load current, the breaking-capacity tier for the local fault level, and the trip technology for the selectivity job the breaker has to do. Frame, tier and trip unit are three separate decisions hiding in one catalogue number.

Electronic Trip Units — the Intelligence in the Breaker

On an MV board the breaker is dumb muscle and the protection lives in a separate relay. On an LV board the two are fused into one product: the electronic trip unit bolted to the breaker face is simultaneously the protection relay, the meter and the communications gateway. When you compare ACB or MCCB offers, you are mostly comparing trip units. Each vendor has one trip-unit language spanning its range:

Trip-unit familyVendor / breakersProtection (typ.) Metering (typ.)Comms (typ.)
Ekip (Touch/Hi-Touch class) ABB — Emax 2 ACBs, Tmax XT MCCBs LSIG standard; options for directional, residual/earth-leakage, dual settings (e.g. a DG operating mode), embedded load-shedding/power-control logic Class-1-grade power and energy metering on the better units; waveform capture on top grades Modbus RTU/TCP modules; IEC 61850 via native module or gateway; ZSI terminals standard on ACB units
Micrologic X (and Micrologic 5/6/7 on NSX) Schneider — Masterpact MTZ ACBs, ComPacT NSX MCCBs LSIG per unit class (suffix G = earth fault, 7-class = earth leakage); dual settings option; per-pole display, advanced diagnostics modules Energy/power metering built in; accuracy grade rises with unit class (~Class 1 typical on the better units) Modbus; IEC 61850 through a communications gateway; ZSI on MTZ; Bluetooth/NFC field apps for settings read-out
ETU series Siemens — 3WA ACBs, 3VA MCCBs LSI / LSIG by ETU grade; neutral protection settable; dual parameter sets on higher grades Current metering on basic units, full power/energy on higher ETUs Modbus RTU/TCP modules, PROFINET/PROFIBUS heritage, IEC 61850 via comms module; ZSI supported on ACB grades

LSIG — the four letters that define the unit

Every trip unit is described by which of the four classic protection elements it carries — L (long-time, the overload element), S (short-time, the delayed short-circuit element that buys selectivity), I (instantaneous) and G (earth fault). An "LSI" unit has no earth-fault element; an "LI" unit cannot be time-graded. The full theory — what each pickup and delay means, how the curves are set for this plant's breakers, and the worked grading between MCC, feeder MCCB, incomer ACB and the transformer's MV protection — lives in the circuit-breaker section of Part 2; the catalogue task is simply to buy units that have the elements the study needs. For this plant: incomers, bus-tie and LV-MSB outgoing feeders need full LSIG; on a 400/230 V TN-S system the G element is your low-level earth-fault detection, and it must be ordered — it cannot be retrofitted into a unit that lacks the sensing.

Zone-selective interlocking (ZSI)

Time grading has a cruel price: the breaker nearest the source — facing the biggest fault — waits longest, and every added tenth of a second multiplies the arc energy at the main board. ZSI is the cure: a hard-wired pilot pair between trip units. When a downstream unit sees the fault, it sends a restraint signal upstream ("I have it — hold to your set delay"); when the upstream unit sees a fault and no restraint arrives, the fault must be in its own zone and it trips at minimum time instead of its grading delay. You keep full selectivity and lose the waiting. All three ACB trip-unit families support ZSI on their S and G elements; specify the interlocking wiring between the two incomers, the bus-tie and the major outgoing feeders, and demand the scheme be tested at FAT.

Metering and communications

Because every major breaker already measures its own current and voltage, the trip units double as the LV system's metering layer: per-feeder kW, kvar, energy and basic power quality, reported over Modbus (native on all families) or IEC 61850 (native module or external gateway, by family) to the plant DCS/PMS. Practical specification points: state the metering accuracy class you need (energy metering to ~Class 1 is a typical ask for incomers), order the comms module with the breaker rather than retrofitting, and remember the trip unit's protection function is self-powered from its current sensors but its display, metering and comms need the external 24 V DC auxiliary supply — feed it from a supervised source, and alarm its failure.

Standardise one vendor's trip-unit language per switchboard. A board whose incomers speak Ekip, feeders speak Micrologic and motor breakers speak ETU needs three engineering tools, three spares stocks and three training courses — a real cost no catalogue page will show you.

How to Read a Breaker Datasheet

An LV breaker datasheet is a dense grid of IEC 60947-2 symbols, and three of them — Icu, Ics, Icw — look interchangeable and are not. Misreading them is the most common LV specification error after the frame/rating confusion. Decode the page line by line:

SymbolNameWhat it really meansWhat to check for this plant
IcuUltimate breaking capacity The biggest fault the breaker can interrupt once and survive in a legally defined but possibly degraded state (test sequence O–CO). It is a never-exceed number, not a service rating. ≥ the worst prospective fault at the breaker's terminals — 38.5 kA at the bus normally, ~77 kA if tie-closed operation is credible (selection below).
IcsService breaking capacity The fault level the breaker can interrupt and remain serviceable (sequence O–CO–CO). Quoted as a percentage of Icu: 50, 75 or 100 %. A breaker with Ics = 100 % Icu goes back to work after the fault; at 50 % it survives the test but should be inspected/replaced. Prefer Ics = 100 % Icu for incomers and critical feeders; never compare one vendor's Icu against another's Ics.
IcwShort-time withstand current The fault current the breaker can carry without tripping or welding for a stated time — 1 s or 3 s. This is what lets an ACB hold on through its short-time delay while a downstream MCCB clears. Only category B breakers have a meaningful Icw. Icw must exceed the bus fault current for longer than the slowest set S-delay; this plant targets Icw ≥ 65 kA/1 s on the main ACBs.
Category A / BUtilisation category B = rated for short-time withstand (designed to be time-graded; ACBs and large MCCBs). A = no rated Icw; trips without intentional delay (most MCCBs). Incomers and bus-tie must be category B; downstream MCCBs are normally category A and grade by current and energy instead of time.
In vs IuRating vs frame Iu is the frame's maximum continuous current; In is the installed sensor or rating plug. One physical frame covers many In values; trip settings are multiples of In. Quote both: e.g. "3200 A frame, In = 2500 A sensors" for the incomer leaves a clean upgrade path.
Ue / UimpRated operational / impulse withstand voltage Ue is the voltage the ratings are declared at — and breaking capacity falls as Ue rises, so a breaker is "65 kA at 400 V" but perhaps "35 kA at 690 V". Uimp (typically 8–12 kV) sets surge robustness and clearances. Read every kA figure in the 400 V column — quoting the 690 V column undersells the breaker, quoting a 240 V column oversells it.

The reference-temperature trap — derating at 50 °C

Continuous ratings are declared at a reference ambient — and "ambient" here means the air around the breaker inside the switchboard, which runs 10–15 K above the room. Catalogues publish derating tables per frame: as a typical indicative guide, expect a usable 0.8–0.9 factor on In by the time a 50 °C Saudi room plus internal switchboard heating is accounted for — but this varies with frame, connection style and enclosure, so always verify against the vendor's own derating table; never apply a generic factor to a real purchase. Thermal-magnetic MCCBs derate inherently (the bimetal feels the heat); electronic units are compensated but the copper and insulation behind them are not, so the tables still apply. The room HVAC question in the Saudi notes is the other half of this answer.

60 Hz applicability

IEC 60947-2 breakers from the major vendors are dual-rated 50/60 Hz and the 60 Hz point matters less than it does for MV gear — but it is not nothing: a few ratings shift slightly at 60 Hz (iron losses in sensors, marginal derating on some large frames), and the peak-withstand arithmetic upstream of the breaker uses the 60 Hz asymmetry factor. The discipline is the same as everywhere in this manual: require the datasheet and routine tests to state 60 Hz explicitly rather than assuming the 50 Hz figures carry over.

The classic datasheet con: a headline "100 kA breaker" that turns out to be 100 kA Icu at 240 V, with 50 % Ics, in the smallest frame only. Always read the kA number with its three qualifiers: at which voltage, Icu or Ics, and for which frame.

Selection for This Plant — From Plant Data Card to Breaker Schedule

Now apply the datasheet vocabulary to the Rev 3 plant numbers. The driving figures: each 1600 kVA transformer delivers an LV full-load current of 2309 A; the prospective short circuit at LV-MSB is about 38.5 kA from one transformer and roughly 77 kA if the bus-tie were closed with both transformers in parallel (see the short-circuit study for the derivation). All results indicative; the formal study and vendor derating tables govern.

Worked selection — LV-MSB incomer ACB

Step 1 — frame for the load. Transformer LV full-load current:

I_FLC = S / (√3 × U) = 1,600,000 VA / (1.732 × 400 V) ≈ 2309 A

The next standard ACB sensor rating above 2309 A is 2500 A — a comfortable 8 % margin at catalogue conditions.

Step 2 — check the 50 °C derating. Apply a typical indicative in-board derating factor of 0.85 (vendor table governs):

I_usable = 0.85 × 2500 A ≈ 2125 A < 2309 A ✗ — fails hot

A 2500 A frame that is adequate at 40 °C can be inadequate in a hot Saudi switchboard. Move up one frame and re-check:

I_usable = 0.85 × 3200 A ≈ 2720 A > 2309 A ✓ (≈ 18 % margin)

So the incomer is specified as a 3200 A-frame ACB with In = 2500 A sensors (or the vendor's certified 50 °C solution on a 2500 A frame, if their table proves it — an HVAC-held room changes this arithmetic; get the vendor statement in writing either way).

Step 3 — breaking capacity. Normal duty is one transformer per bus section: 38.5 kA. But if operations can ever close the bus-tie with both transformers live — even briefly, for a no-break changeover — the bus sees:

I_sc,tie-closed ≈ 2 × 38.5 kA ≈ 77 kA

A 50 kA breaker is then under-rated the moment the tie closes. Select the 85–100 kA performance level (all three ACB families offer it), with Ics = 100 % Icu:

I_cu ≥ 85 kA > 77 kA ✓

Step 4 — short-time withstand for selectivity. The incomer must hold on through its S-delay while a downstream MCCB clears. Target:

I_cw ≥ 65 kA / 1 s > 38.5 kA single-TX duty ✓ (≈ 1.7× margin)

which also keeps honest margin for grading studies at the tie-closed level when ZSI is active. Note the constraint this creates: at tie-closed duty the prospective fault (≈ 77 kA) exceeds Icw 65 kA/1 s, so at that duty the breakers must clear via ZSI/instantaneous rather than ride out a full short-time delay — only the single-transformer 38.5 kA duty may be time-graded within Icw. Sanity check: 3200 A-frame ACBs at the high performance level typically carry Icw of 65–85 kA/1 s — the requirement sits inside every family's range, so this is a selection, not an exotic special.

The same logic, condensed into the indicative breaker schedule:

PositionTypeFrame / In (indicative) Icu at 400 VIcwTrip unitNotes
LV-MSB incomers (×2, from TX-1/TX-2)ACB, withdrawable, cat. B 3200 A frame, In 2500 A (50 °C check above) ≥ 85 kA (covers ~77 kA tie-closed) ≥ 65 kA / 1 s LSIG electronic, ZSI, Modbus/IEC 61850 Ics = 100 % Icu; castell/electrical interlock with bus-tie (2-of-3 logic).
LV-MSB bus-tie (normally open)ACB, withdrawable, cat. B 3200 A frame, In 2500 A (full transfer capability) ≥ 85 kA≥ 65 kA / 1 s LSIG electronic, ZSI Same rating as the incomers — it can stand in for either; interlocked to prevent uncontrolled paralleling.
LV-MSB outgoing feeders to MCC-1..nMCCB, electronic ~250–630 A frames per feeder schedule ≥ 50 kA tier (margin over 38.5 kA at the bus) Category A (current/energy grading) LSI(G) electronic for grading under the ACBs Earth-fault element per the TN-S protection plan.
PFC bank feederMCCB, electronic Sized ~1.35–1.5 × bank current (≈ 580 kvar detuned bank → ~840 A bank current → ~1250 A frame class) ≥ 50 kA tier—LSI electronic Overrating absorbs harmonic and tolerance current in the detuned bank; confirm with the PFC vendor.
MCC incomers / large motor feedersMCCB Per MCC load list (~160–630 A typical) Per the local calculated fault level — typically 36–50 kA tiers after cable impedance —Electronic on incomers; thermal-magnetic or motor-class on starters Cascading tables may permit lower tiers under a current-limiting upstream MCCB — same vendor only.
The cheaper alternative — and its price. If operating procedures guarantee the tie is only ever closed dead (break-before-make via the interlock), the duty never exceeds 38.5 kA and a 50–65 kA breaker class suffices — consistent with the plant basis of an LV-MSB rated 50–65 kA with the tie normally open. The 85–100 kA selection above buys the freedom to make no-break transfers and forgives an interlock failure. That is an operations-philosophy decision to make consciously and record — not a default to inherit silently from either the cost sheet or the catalogue.

Saudi-Specific Practice — Heat, Dust, Paperwork and Spares

SASO/SABER conformity

LV breakers are regulated imports: they enter the Kingdom through the SABER platform with certificates of conformity against the SASO-adopted IEC standards (IEC 60947-2 for ACBs/MCCBs, IEC 60898-1 for MCBs). Make the supplier contractually responsible for SABER product and shipment certificates, type-test reports from a recognised laboratory, and country-of-origin documents — and check that the certificate covers the exact frame, performance level and trip-unit combination being shipped, because "family" certificates with the high-kA variants excluded are a known trap. As elsewhere in this manual: missing paperwork strands switchboards in port far more often than technical faults do.

SEC acceptance and SBC 401

The LV breakers themselves are inside the plant fence, so SEC's interest is lighter than at the MV intake — but not zero. SEC and the Saudi Building Code (SBC 401, the Kingdom's adoption of the electrical code) shape the LV installation rules: TN-S earthing practice, discrimination requirements for safety-services supplies, and the acceptance of the metering arrangement where SEC tariff metering touches the LV system. Where the DG/ATS standby scheme interfaces the SEC supply, the changeover breakers and their interlocks are part of what the inspector walks down — keep the approved single-line and interlock schedule in the switchroom dossier.

Heat: the switchroom is part of the breaker

Everything in the derating discussion lands here. The workable Saudi pattern is an HVAC-conditioned LV switchroom held at or below 40 °C, which restores catalogue ratings — with the derated 50 °C figures held in reserve as the documented answer to "what happens when the HVAC fails on an August afternoon". Specify: room over-temperature alarm to the DCS, the vendor's written derating statement for every frame at 50 °C, and temperature-rise type-test evidence for the assembled switchboard (IEC 61439-2), not just the loose breakers — the board manufacturer's form factor and busbar design move heat around in ways the breaker catalogue cannot see.

Dust and IP

Desert dust plus morning humidity is mildly conductive, and it settles on arc-chute louvres, trip-unit electronics and racking mechanisms. Indoors, specify switchboard enclosures at IP4X minimum (IP54 where the room quality is doubtful, noting the thermal price of sealing); any breaker outdoors or in unconditioned space lives in an IP54-minimum enclosure per the plant basis. Keep the switchroom positively pressurised with filtered intake, and put insulation-surface and arc-chute inspection on the maintenance plan — a dust film inside an ACB arc chute is the LV cousin of the MV internal-arc precursor.

Spares and vendor presence in the Kingdom

An ACB mechanism lives 25–30 years; its electronics will not. Plan accordingly:

  • Spare trip units — at least one of each LSIG type used, pre-loaded with the approved settings file; a failed trip unit otherwise immobilises an incomer for months of lead time.
  • One complete spare ACB of the common frame (or at minimum a spare breaker element for the withdrawable cassettes) — the classic justification for making incomers and bus-tie identical, as the schedule above does.
  • MCCB spares by tier — a small stock of each frame/tier/trip combination actually installed; resist "equivalent" substitutions that silently break the cascading and selectivity tables.
  • Local service — ABB, Schneider, Siemens and Eaton all maintain Saudi service organisations; weight the bid evaluation toward proven in-Kingdom retrofit and spares history, and ask for the named local service centre, not the regional one.
  • Settings custody — trip-unit setting files are protection settings; store them with the relay settings under the same change control, and verify them by secondary injection at FAT and at commissioning.
Key takeaway: in Saudi service the breaker's environment — room temperature, dust, the SABER dossier and the spares shelf — decides its real reliability at least as much as the kA numbers on the datasheet. Specify the environment and the paperwork with the same rigour as the ratings.

Breaker Procurement Review — Checklist

  • Confirm every breaking-capacity figure as Icu and Ics at 400 V, 60 Hz, for the exact frame offered — incomers/tie sized for the tie-closed duty (~77 kA → 85–100 kA class) or a documented dead-tie operating restriction in place.
  • Verify incomers and bus-tie are utilisation category B with Icw ≥ 65 kA/1 s, matching the grading delays in the approved LSIG study (Part 2).
  • Check frame and sensor sizing against the vendor's 50 °C / in-board derating table (typical 0.8–0.9 factor) — 2309 A incomer duty cleared with margin, in writing.
  • Confirm trip-unit scope: full LSIG where the study requires it, ZSI wiring between incomers/tie/major feeders, metering class and Modbus/IEC 61850 comms modules ordered with the breakers.
  • Verify mixed-brand coordination is avoided, or every cascading/selectivity claim is backed by the vendor's published type-tested tables for the exact pairings offered.
  • Check SABER/SASO certificates cover the exact frame, performance level and trip-unit variants shipped; type-test reports and 60 Hz declarations in the dossier.
  • Confirm the spares package: spare LSIG trip units, common spare ACB element, MCCB stock by frame/tier, named in-Kingdom service centre, and settings files under change control.
Closing the loop. Everything in this section is indicative training data. The binding chain for the real purchase is: formal short-circuit study (Part 2) and protection coordination study (LSIG settings) → approved breaker schedule and switchboard data sheets → vendor certified data sheets, derating tables and type tests → IEC 61439-2 assembly verification and FAT with secondary injection → site settings verification. The catalogue's job — here as throughout Part 6 — is only to make you fluent for that conversation.

Motor Control Centres

Indicative catalogue data. Product families and typical parameters are listed for training and familiarisation only, are approximate, and change with manufacturer revisions. No part numbers are given; always obtain current certified data sheets and SASO/SEC acceptance status from the manufacturer.

Goal: learn to read a motor control centre catalogue the way a specifying engineer reads it — starting from what the plant's motor population actually needs (tags MCC-1..n, fed from LV-MSB at 400 V, 60 Hz), translating each need into the IEC 61439-2 parameter that answers it, and finishing with a worked sizing for this plant. The vendor families compared here (Rockwell CENTERLINE 2500, ABB MNS, Siemens SIVACON S8, Schneider Okken/BlokSeT) are the ones you will most often meet on Saudi industrial sites. For the motor theory behind the starters, lean on the motors section; for how Part 6 catalogue sections are meant to be used, see the catalogue introduction.

What the MCC Does in This Plant

A motor control centre is a switchboard that has specialised in one job: starting, protecting and stopping motors, dozens of them, packed into standardised compartments ("buckets") that a technician can exchange in minutes. Where the LV-MSB is a distribution board — a few large breakers feeding sub-boards — an MCC is a density product: a 2 m-wide column may hold ten or fifteen motor starters, each with its own short-circuit protection, contactor, overload device, control terminals and door-mounted controls, all plugged onto a common vertical bus.

In this plant the MCCs (tags MCC-1..n) hang off the two sections of the LV-MSB at 400/230 V, 60 Hz, TN-S. They carry the process motor population — pumps, fans, compressors, agitators — with three starter technologies side by side in the same line-up (the trade-offs between them are developed in the motors section):

Starter typeTypical use here (indicative)What sits in the bucket
DOL (direct-on-line) Small motors, roughly up to 15–22 kW, where the ~6× starting inrush is tolerable on the bus. MCCB or fuse-switch + contactor + overload relay (thermal or electronic).
Star-delta Mid-size motors on low-inertia loads where a reduced-voltage start is enough and speed control is not needed. Protection device + three contactors + timer; six motor leads instead of three.
VFD-fed Pumps and fans where flow control saves energy, and the largest machines (up to the plant's largest motor, ~160 kW typical) where a soft, current-limited start protects the bus and the process. Either the VFD itself in an oversized bucket, or — common for larger drives — a feeder bucket in the MCC supplying a wall- or floor-mounted drive (see the VFD catalogue section).

Intelligent versus conventional MCCs

A conventional MCC wires each starter's control circuit in copper: start/stop commands, run/trip status, maybe an analogue ammeter signal, all marshalled to terminal strips and multicore cables back to the control system. It is simple, field-repairable with a screwdriver, and every signal you did not cable at design time is a site modification later.

An intelligent MCC (iMCC) replaces the simple overload relay with a communicating motor-protection unit per bucket (Rockwell E300, ABB UMC100, Siemens SIMOCODE, Schneider TeSys T are the typical families) and replaces the multicore with a network drop — PROFINET, EtherNet/IP or Modbus TCP. Each bucket then reports current, thermal capacity, earth-fault leakage, starts-per-hour and trip diagnostics to the DCS/PMS, and accepts commands over the same link. The price is roughly 10–20 % on the MCC and a network engineering effort; the return is condition data that turns motor maintenance from calendar-based to evidence-based, and commissioning loops that take minutes instead of hours. For a plant of this size the practical recommendation is an iMCC for process-critical motors and conventional starters for trivial ones — but choose one philosophy per MCC; mixed boards confuse both technicians and the control system.

Hardwired trip rule. Even in a fully intelligent MCC, safety functions — emergency stops, process interlocks that protect people or machines — stay hardwired to the contactor coil circuit. The network carries information and convenience commands; it must never be the only path for a protective stop.
Key takeaway: an MCC is bought as a system (bus, form of separation, bucket standardisation, communications philosophy), not as a pile of starters. Decide intelligent vs conventional, starter technology bands, and spare-bucket policy before opening any vendor catalogue.

The Vendor Families You Will Meet

Four LV switchboard/MCC families dominate the Gulf industrial market. All are type-tested assemblies to IEC 61439-2, all offer withdrawable motor-starter buckets and Form 4 separation, and all can be built conventional or intelligent. As with MV switchgear, a specification written in standard parameters — not brand names — will draw compliant offers from every one of them. Typical indicative class ranges:

FamilyVendorMain bus (typ.)Icw (typ.) SeparationBucketsArc-fault containmentComms
CENTERLINE 2500 (IEC version)Rockwell Automation up to ~3200 Aup to ~65 kA / 1 s Form 4 (typ. 4b) Withdrawable units, sizes standardised per starter rating Arc-resistant variants and arc-containment options available EtherNet/IP native (IntelliCENTER), E300 electronic overloads
MNSABB up to ~6300 Aup to ~100 kA / 1 s Form 4 (typ. 4b) Withdrawable modules from fractional sizes; very high packing density Arc-tested designs per IEC TR 61641; MNS Digital condition monitoring PROFINET / Modbus TCP / IEC 61850 options, UMC100 motor controllers
SIVACON S8Siemens up to ~7000 Aup to ~100 kA / 1 s Form 4 (typ. 4b) Withdrawable, plug-in or fixed units in the same line-up Internal-arc tested per IEC TR 61641, optional arc-quenching device PROFINET native, SIMOCODE pro per starter
Okken (process) / BlokSeTSchneider Electric up to ~7300 A (Okken)up to ~100 kA / 1 s Form 4 (typ. 4b) Withdrawable drawers; BlokSeT as the lighter general-purpose line Arc-tested per IEC TR 61641 options Modbus TCP / Ethernet, TeSys T / TeSys island motor management

Read the table the right way round, exactly as for the MV board: this plant's MCC duty (bus around 800–1600 A per MCC, fault duty around 30 kA at the MCC bus — derived in the selection sub-section) sits in the lower half of every family's range. The decision between brands is therefore won on: integration with the chosen control system (a Rockwell DCS pulls CENTERLINE, a Siemens DCS pulls SIVACON), local panel-builder and service presence in the Kingdom, bucket standardisation and spares commonality with the rest of the site, delivery time, and the quality of the vendor's 50 °C derating answer.

What "withdrawable bucket" buys you

A withdrawable starter unit plugs onto the vertical bus through shuttered contacts and carries its load and control connections on plugs. Racking it out isolates the unit with a visible, padlockable break — and a failed starter is exchanged for the shelf spare in minutes, live bus, no outage to the neighbours. Fixed and plug-in construction is cheaper and acceptable for feeders that rarely change, but for process motor starters the withdrawable pattern is what keeps a Saudi plant running between maintenance windows. Insist that bucket sizes are standardised so that one spare of each size covers the board, and order 10–20 % equipped spare buckets plus empty space for future ones.

When comparing offers, ask each bidder for the same three drawings: a typical DOL bucket schematic, the bucket-size table for your motor list, and the vertical-section arrangement showing forms of separation. Brochure adjectives differ; these three drawings make the products directly comparable.

How to Read MCC Catalogue Data — IEC 61439-2 Parameters

An MCC data sheet speaks IEC 61439-2. Six parameters carry almost all of the engineering content; learn to find them on the first page of any offer.

Rated current of the assembly (InA) and of each circuit (Inc)

The main horizontal bus rating (InA) and the rating of each vertical bus and outgoing unit (Inc) are verified values — proven by temperature-rise test on the assembled board, not by adding up component nameplates. Crucially, they are declared at a reference ambient (35 °C daily average, 40 °C peak, per the standard's normal service conditions). Every ampere figure in the catalogue silently assumes that room; see the derating discussion below.

Short-time withstand current (Icw) and peak (Ipk)

Icw is what the bus structure survives, braced and bolted, for the rated time (usually 1 s) while upstream protection clears a fault. It must exceed the prospective fault current at the MCC bus — calculated after the incoming cable impedance, which is why the MCC can legitimately carry a lower withstand class than the 38.5 kA (one transformer) duty at the LV-MSB. The peak rating Ipk covers the first asymmetric loop; the catalogue applies the standard factor (n = 2.1 for Icw > 50 kA, less below). Some buckets are alternatively protected by current-limiting devices and declared with a conditional rating (Icc) — acceptable, but then the protective device is part of the type test and cannot be substituted at will.

Form of separation — why this plant says Form 4b

IEC 61439-2 grades how thoroughly the assembly's interior is partitioned, from Form 1 (no internal separation) to Form 4 (busbars, each functional unit, and each unit's terminals all separated from one another). The sub-letter matters: Form 4b puts each unit's outgoing terminals in their own compartment, not in a shared cable alley. The practical meaning for a field technician: with Form 4b you can open one starter's terminal compartment to re-terminate a motor cable while every other circuit on the board stays energised behind earthed metal. On a process plant where the MCC can never be fully shut down, Form 4b is the standard specification; Form 3b is the cost-down compromise you will be offered, and the difference appears precisely at 02:00 when a motor cable must be changed live-board.

Internal arc — IEC TR 61641

Arc containment in LV assemblies is a technical report option, not a core type test like the MV IAC rating — so it must be explicitly requested. Ask for arc-fault containment tested to IEC TR 61641 at the board's fault level and protection clearing time, and state whether persons-protection (criteria 1–5) or also equipment-protection (criteria 6–7) is required. Active arc-quenching devices and arc-detection relays tripping the incomer are catalogue options that buy clearing times in tens of milliseconds.

IP rating for a dusty site

For an indoor, HVAC-conditioned Saudi switchroom specify IP42 as a practical minimum (protected against dripping water and >1 mm objects); IP54 if the room cannot be guaranteed clean — but beware: sealing a board to IP54 traps its own heat, and the catalogue will show a current derating of typically 5–15 % for the higher IP class. Dust is the enemy twice over — as a tracking film on insulation and as a thermal blanket on components — so filtered, positively pressurised rooms beat hermetic boards.

The 50 °C question, bucket by bucket

The components inside a bucket — contactors, overload relays, MCCBs, and above all VFDs — are themselves rated at 40 °C (many contactors at 55–60 °C, MCCBs at 40–50 °C with derating tables). The air inside a loaded MCC column runs 10–15 K above the room. At a 50 °C design ambient the in-board air can reach 60–65 °C, and component catalogues answer with derating curves: an MCCB that carries 100 A at 40 °C may be good for only ~85–90 A at 60 °C. The clean solutions, in order of preference: condition the room to ≤40 °C and treat the HVAC as electrical infrastructure; size starters one frame generously; and require the vendor to certify the whole assembly's ratings at 50 °C room ambient in writing. Never accept "components per manufacturer data" as the answer — the assembly temperature-rise verification is exactly the vendor's job under IEC 61439.

The classic Gulf MCC failure is thermal, not electrical: a fully-loaded DOL bucket in an unconditioned room trips on overload every afternoon in August and is "fixed" by winding the overload setting up — defeating the motor's protection. If afternoon nuisance trips appear, measure the in-board temperature before touching any setting (see motor protection).

Selection for This Plant — Worked Sizing

Take a representative board, MCC-1, fed by cable from one section of the LV-MSB. All figures indicative and to be confirmed by the load list and the formal short-circuit study.

Worked calculation — MCC bus rating with diversity

Assume MCC-1 carries a connected motor group totalling ~700 kW of mixed pumps and fans, including the plant's largest motor (~160 kW). Connected full-load current at 400 V, taking an aggregate pf × efficiency of ~0.80:

I_connected = P / (√3 × U × pf × η) = 700 000 / (1.732 × 400 × 0.80) ≈ 1263 A

Not all motors run at once, nor at full load. With a demand/diversity factor of 0.7 from the load list:

I_demand = 0.7 × 1263 A ≈ 884 A

Add a 20 % growth margin and allow for the 50 °C room (target ≤80 % utilisation of the verified rating):

I_bus ≥ 884 × 1.2 / 0.8 ≈ 1326 A → select 1600 A main bus

Sanity check: 1600 A at 400 V is ~1.1 MVA of bus capacity for a board demanding ~0.6 MVA — generous but normal for an MCC, where bucket count and future space, not amperes, usually fill the board first. The incomer from LV-MSB is then a 1600 A frame ACB or switch.

Worked calculation — fault level at the MCC bus after the cable

The Plant Data Card gives Isc ≈ 38.5 kA at the LV-MSB with one transformer (bus-tie normally open). Equivalent source impedance, treated as mostly reactive:

Z_source = U / (√3 × I_sc) = 400 / (1.732 × 38 500) ≈ 6.0 mΩ

Feeder to MCC-1: 2 parallel runs of 3×1c 240 mm² Cu XLPE, 40 m. Per run R ≈ 3.2 mΩ, X ≈ 3.2 mΩ; two in parallel:

Z_cable ≈ (1.6 + j1.6) mΩ Z_total ≈ |1.6 + j(6.0 + 1.6)| = |1.6 + j7.6| ≈ 7.8 mΩ I_sc,MCC = 400 / (1.732 × 0.0078) ≈ 29.6 kA → say ≈ 30 kA

Sanity check: 40 m of LV cable knocks the fault level from 38.5 kA to ~30 kA — cable impedance dominates fast at 400 V, which is exactly why the MCC may be specified one withstand class below the MSB. Specify Icw = 50 kA / 1 s anyway: it is a standard class in every family, gives ~65 % margin, and stays valid if the MCC is ever re-fed by a shorter cable. All values indicative; the formal study governs.

The specification card

Tag / serviceMCC-1 — process motor control centre, fed from LV-MSB section 1
StandardSASO-adopted IEC 61439-1/-2 (motor starters IEC 60947-4-1); SBC 401
System400/230 V, 60 Hz, TN-S, 3P+N+PE
Main bus / incomer1600 A verified at 50 °C room ambient (or HVAC-conditioned room declared)
WithstandIcw 50 kA / 1 s; peak per IEC 61439-1
Separation / constructionForm 4b, withdrawable motor starter units, standardised bucket sizes
Arc faultContainment tested to IEC TR 61641 at 50 kA, persons-protection criteria minimum
StartersDOL ≤ ~22 kW; star-delta or VFD above; largest motor ~160 kW (typical) VFD-fed
Motor managementIntelligent MCC: communicating motor protection per bucket, Ethernet-based fieldbus to DCS/PMS; E-stops hardwired
Enclosure / environmentIP42 (IP54 if room unconditioned, with derating declared); 50 °C design ambient; space heaters
Spares≥15 % equipped spare buckets, one shelf spare per bucket size, 20 % empty space
Documents with bidIEC 61439-2 design-verification evidence, 50 °C rating statement, bucket-size table, SASO/SABER status
Key takeaway: the MCC is specified by four numbers and one letter — 1600 A bus, 50 kA/1 s, IP42, 50 °C certified, Form 4b — plus the intelligent-MCC decision. Everything else is bucket engineering against the motor list.

Variable Frequency Drives

Indicative catalogue data. Product families and typical parameters are listed for training and familiarisation only, are approximate, and change with manufacturer revisions. No part numbers are given; always obtain current certified data sheets and SASO/SEC acceptance status from the manufacturer.

Goal: learn to read a variable frequency drive catalogue — the most footnote-dense document in the LV equipment world — and come out with a correct frame size for a real machine at a real Saudi ambient. The families compared (ABB ACS580/ACS880, Danfoss VLT FC302, Siemens SINAMICS G120/G150, Rockwell PowerFlex 755) cover most of what you will meet on site. The motor-side physics lives in the motors section; the harmonics interaction with the capacitor bank in the power factor correction section; the method of Part 6 in the catalogue introduction.

Why VFDs Here — Energy, Soft Starting and the Harmonics Bill

A VFD rectifies the 400 V, 60 Hz supply to DC and re-synthesises a variable-voltage, variable-frequency output, so the motor runs at exactly the speed the process needs. For the centrifugal pumps and fans that dominate this plant's load, the affinity laws make this lucrative: shaft power scales with the cube of speed, so running a pump at 80 % speed takes roughly half the power (0.8³ ≈ 0.51) that throttling the same flow with a valve would leave you paying. Across a motor population running thousands of hours a year, the drive typically repays itself in one to three years of energy alone.

The second benefit is electrical: a VFD starts a motor at a programmed current limit, typically 100–150 % of rated current, instead of the ~6× inrush of a DOL start. For the plant's largest motor (~160 kW, ~280 A full load), that is the difference between a ~1700 A inrush sagging the 400 V bus and a start the neighbours never notice. Mechanically, the soft ramp spares couplings, belts and the pump's check valves.

The price: harmonics — 6-pulse versus AFE

The standard, cheapest VFD front end is a 6-pulse diode rectifier. It draws current from the supply in two pulses per half-cycle, rich in 5th, 7th, 11th and 13th harmonics — at 60 Hz, that is 300, 420, 660 and 780 Hz currents circulating in the plant. A 6-pulse drive without any choke can show a total harmonic current distortion (THDi) of 80–100 %; with the DC-link or AC line chokes most reputable drives now build in, 30–45 % is typical. The cures, in rising order of cost: line/DC chokes (built in or added), passive harmonic filters, 12-pulse arrangements (phase-shifting transformer), and the active front end (AFE) — an IGBT rectifier that draws near-sinusoidal current (THDi ≈ 3–5 %), at unity or even controllable power factor, and can regenerate braking energy to the supply. AFE costs roughly 1.5–2× the 6-pulse drive and a few percent of efficiency; on this plant it is justified case-by-case, not by default.

Harmonics are also why the plant's PFC bank is detuned at 7 % (tuning frequency ≈ 227 Hz at 60 Hz — safely below the 5th harmonic at 300 Hz): plain capacitors on a VFD-loaded bus would resonate near the 5th/7th harmonics and cook themselves. The full story, including why 227 Hz, is in the PFC section — read it before buying either the drives or the capacitors, because the two purchases interact.

Where the VFD lives. Smaller drives mount in MCC buckets (one to two frame sizes larger than the equivalent DOL bucket, for heat); large drives such as the 160 kW machine's are floor- or wall-mounted in the switchroom and fed from an MCC feeder bucket — see the MCC selection sub-section.

The Vendor Families You Will Meet

Every major vendor splits its range into a general-purpose line (pumps, fans, V/f or open-loop vector, wall/bucket mount) and a high-performance line (full vector control, options for AFE, regenerative and cabinet builds). The table shows the pairs you will most often meet, with indicative 400 V-class data:

FamilyVendorClassPower range at 400 V (typ.) Control modesBuilt-in harmonic mitigationEnclosure (typ.)
ACS580ABBGeneral purpose ~0.75–500 kW Scalar (V/f) and sensorless vector DC/swinging chokes as standard; integral EMC filter IP21 standard, IP55 option (wall mount)
ACS880ABBHigh performance / industrial ~0.55 kW to multi-MW (module and cabinet builds) Direct torque control (DTC), with or without encoder Chokes standard; 12-pulse, low-harmonic AFE and regenerative variants IP21/IP55 wall; IP42+ cabinet
VLT AutomationDrive FC302DanfossGeneral/industrial ~0.37–1100 kW V/f, VVC+ voltage vector, flux vector with/without encoder Dual DC-link chokes standard (THDi ~40 % typical); integral RFI filter IP20/IP21 to IP54/IP66 depending on frame; strong high-ambient back-channel cooling options
SINAMICS G120 (modular) / G150 (cabinet)SiemensGeneral purpose G120 ~0.37–250 kW; G150 ~75 kW–multi-MW V/f and sensorless vector; servo-grade control in the S-series relatives Line chokes/filters optional or built in by frame; clean-power AFE in related lines G120 IP20 (cubicle-mount), IP55 variants; G150 cabinet IP20–IP54
PowerFlex 755 / 755TL/TRRockwell AutomationHigh performance ~7.5 kW–multi-MW (755T cabinet range) V/f, sensorless and flux vector with encoder options 755TL low-harmonic (active front end), 755TR regenerative; EtherNet/IP native IP20 (MCC/cabinet), Flange/IP54 options

As with the MCC, brand selection is mostly an integration question: a Rockwell iMCC argues for PowerFlex in the same line-up; a Siemens DCS for SINAMICS; ABB and Danfoss compete strongly on pump/fan duty and high-ambient data. What matters more than brand is that the plant standardises on one general-purpose family: one set of spares, one programming tool, one set of commissioning habits across twenty drives beats five optimal brands every time.

Catalogue power ratings (kW) are marketing shorthand for "fits a typical 4-pole motor of this rating". Always select on current, not kilowatts — the motor's nameplate amperes against the drive's rated output amperes at the actual duty class, switching frequency and ambient. The worked selection below shows why.

How to Read a VFD Data Sheet — the Footnotes That Size the Drive

A VFD data sheet quotes one headline kilowatt number and then takes most of it back in footnotes. Five footnote families decide whether the drive actually fits the application; check all five, every time.

Normal duty versus heavy duty — the same drive, two ratings

Every industrial drive carries (at least) two current ratings. Normal duty (variable torque) assumes a pump/fan-type load and allows an overload of typically 110 % for 60 s every 10 minutes. Heavy duty (constant torque) covers conveyors, crushers, positive-displacement pumps, and allows typically 150 % for 60 s — at a continuous current some 15–25 % lower from the same hardware. A drive sold as "200 kW" is a 200 kW normal-duty drive and roughly a 160 kW heavy-duty one. Selecting a centrifugal pump drive on the heavy-duty column wastes money; selecting a crusher drive on the normal-duty column burns IGBTs. Decide the duty class first, then read only that column.

Switching frequency versus derating

The output IGBTs chop the DC link at a switching frequency of typically 2–4 kHz (default) up to 8–16 kHz (quiet). Every doubling of switching frequency roughly doubles the inverter's own switching losses, and the data sheet answers with a derating table — commonly 10–20 % current reduction at the higher settings. Motor acoustic noise drops as switching frequency rises, so commissioning engineers love to wind it up; the derating table is why the drive then trips on heatsink temperature in July. Size at the switching frequency you intend to run, and write that frequency on the data sheet.

Ambient temperature derating — the Saudi line

Drive ratings are referenced to 40 °C (some compact frames to 45 °C, with a 50 °C option). Above the reference, catalogues derate output current by typically 1–1.5 % per °C, usually up to an absolute ceiling of 50–55 °C. At this plant's 50 °C design ambient an unconditioned location costs 10–15 % of the drive's current — very often exactly the margin that pushes the selection into the next frame size, as the worked example below shows. Altitude derating (above 1000 m) stacks on top; most Saudi coastal and central sites are unaffected, but check.

Output side: dv/dt, motor cable length and bearing currents

The inverter output is not a sine wave but a train of steep-edged pulses. Each edge (high dv/dt) launches a reflection down the motor cable that can nearly double the voltage at the motor terminals when the cable is long. Data sheets answer with a maximum motor cable length — typically 100–150 m screened without remedy, extendable to ~300 m with output dv/dt or sine-wave filters — and with requirements for symmetrical screened motor cable, 360° gland earthing, and insulated non-drive-end bearings or shaft earthing rings on larger motors (typically ≥100 kW) to stop bearing-current pitting. Check the motor itself is declared per IEC 60034-25 for converter duty; on this plant the 160 kW pump motor must be (motors section).

EMC category and the residual checklist

IEC 61800-3 sorts built-in RFI filters into categories C1–C4; for an industrial plant on its own transformer, category C3 (second environment) is the normal specification — but only with the screened-cable and earthing discipline above. Finally, scan for: input voltage tolerance (±10 % of 400 V) and 60 Hz declaration; built-in chokes (or order them); STO (safe torque off) if the safety design uses it; and the protection ratings the drive gives its motor — electronic overload, stall, underload (a dry-running pump shows as underload), and motor thermistor input.

Key takeaway: a VFD is selected on rated output current at four declared conditions — duty class, switching frequency, ambient temperature, installation altitude — plus a motor-cable and EMC check. The headline kilowatts on the cover are the start of the conversation, not the answer.

Worked Selection — a 160 kW Pump Drive at 50 °C

Apply the data-sheet method to the plant's largest machine: a 160 kW centrifugal pump, 4-pole motor, base speed 1800 rpm at 60 Hz (synchronous; ~1780 rpm at full load with slip), installed in a switchroom with a 50 °C design ambient. All values indicative.

Worked calculation — frame selection with ambient derating

Step 1 — motor current. Nameplate full-load current of a typical 160 kW, 400 V, 4-pole motor (η ≈ 0.96, pf ≈ 0.86):

I_FLC = P / (√3 × U × pf × η) = 160 000 / (1.732 × 400 × 0.86 × 0.96) ≈ 280 A

Step 2 — duty class. Centrifugal pump → variable torque → normal duty column, 110 % / 60 s overload. Required continuous drive current: ≥ 280 A.

Step 3 — first guess. A "160 kW" 400 V drive frame typically offers ~300–310 A normal duty at 40 °C and default switching frequency. On paper: 300 A > 280 A ✓.

Step 4 — apply the 50 °C derating (typical 1.25 %/°C above 40 °C, i.e. 12.5 % for 10 K):

I_50°C = 300 A × (1 − 0.125) ≈ 262 A < 280 A ✗ — frame too small

Step 5 — next frame up. The "200 kW" frame typically offers ~370 A normal duty at 40 °C:

I_50°C = 370 A × 0.875 ≈ 324 A ≥ 280 A ✓ (margin ≈ 16 %)

Step 6 — overload check. Pump starting and transients within 110 % × 324 A ≈ 356 A for 60 s — ample for a current-limited pump ramp. Sanity check: one frame size (~25 %) bought back the 12.5 % ambient loss plus margin; if the room is instead HVAC-held at ≤40 °C, the 160 kW frame would suffice — but then the drive's availability hangs on the air-conditioning. For the plant's largest motor, buy the frame, not the HVAC promise. Indicative only; the vendor's selection tool at the declared conditions governs.

Input choke versus detuned PFC — make the two purchases talk

Specify the drive with its DC-link or AC input chokes (3–5 % impedance, built in on most of the families above). The chokes roughly halve the drive's harmonic current injection, which directly offloads the detuned PFC bank: the 7 % reactors (tuning ≈ 227 Hz at 60 Hz) keep the ~580 kvar bank out of resonance with the 5th harmonic at 300 Hz, but every ampere of 5th harmonic the chokes absorb at the drive is an ampere the reactors do not have to swallow as heat. The two specifications must state the same assumptions: if the harmonic study that sized the PFC assumed choked drives, an unchoked substitution at purchase quietly invalidates it. Conversely, never let a vendor "value-engineer" plain capacitors into this plant — on a VFD-loaded bus, detuned is mandatory.

Base speed bookkeeping at 60 Hz

A 4-pole motor here runs 1800 rpm synchronous at 60 Hz — not the 1500 rpm of 50 Hz countries. Pump curves, V/f settings and any "percent speed" scaling in the control system must all be built on the 60 Hz base: set the drive's motor data from the 60 Hz nameplate, field-weakening point at 60 Hz, and maximum frequency per the pump's mechanical limit. A 50 Hz parameter file imported from a sister plant abroad will run the pump 17 % slow and the "energy saving" will be an underperforming process.

Saudi-Specific Practice — SASO, Dust, Service and Harmonics Limits

SASO/SABER conformity and local service

Drives and MCCs imported into the Kingdom clear customs through the SABER platform with certificates of conformity against the SASO-adopted IEC standards (IEC 61800 series for drives, IEC 61439 for assemblies). Make the supplier contractually responsible for SABER certification, type-test and routine-test documentation, and country-of-origin papers — paperwork strands equipment in port far more often than technical defects do. Then look past the catalogue to the vendor's in-Kingdom organisation: certified service centre locations, spare-parts stock (fans and DC-link capacitors are the drive consumables), guaranteed response time, and Arabic/English commissioning support. A drive family with a service hub in Dammam or Jubail beats a marginally better one supported from Dubai.

Dust and heat at the installation

Drive heatsinks are forced-air cooled and desert dust blankets them within months. Practice for this plant: drives indoors in filtered, positively pressurised, HVAC-conditioned rooms wherever possible; replaceable intake dust filter mats on cabinet drives, on the maintenance plan with a dated change record; through-panel ("flange" or back-channel) mounting for bucket drives so the heatsink rejects heat outside the enclosure; and heatsink cleaning plus cooling-fan replacement intervals (typically 3–5 years at high ambient) in the CMMS from day one. If a drive must live outside conditioned space, specify IP54/IP55 variants and apply the full 50 °C derating of the worked selection — never both seal a drive and skip the derating.

Harmonics limits at the SEC connection

SEC connection requirements limit the distortion a customer may inject into the 13.8 kV network, assessed at the point of common coupling; IEEE 519 is the common reference framework (THDv typically ≤5 % at the PCC, with individual and total current-distortion limits scaled to the ratio of available fault current to load current). The compliance chain for this plant: drive count and front ends from the load list → harmonic study at the PCC → chokes/filters/AFE chosen to meet the limits → detuned PFC per the PFC section → measurement campaign at commissioning to close the loop. Keep the study with the SEC connection file; if SEC queries distortion later, the dated commissioning measurements are the evidence. All limits indicative — the SEC-stated values for the actual connection agreement govern.

Procurement checklist — MCC and VFD package

  • MCC verified to IEC 61439-2: 1600 A bus and bucket ratings certified at 50 °C room ambient (or conditioned room declared), Icw 50 kA/1 s, Form 4b, withdrawable buckets, arc containment per IEC TR 61641.
  • Intelligent-MCC philosophy fixed and consistent: communicating motor protection per bucket, one fieldbus to the DCS/PMS, safety stops hardwired.
  • Spares policy in the purchase order: ≥15 % equipped spare buckets, shelf spare per bucket size, drive spare fans/filters, and 20 % empty space per MCC.
  • Every VFD selected on current at declared conditions — duty class (normal vs heavy), switching frequency, 50 °C ambient derating (1–1.5 %/°C above 40) — with the 160 kW pump drive sized one frame up per the worked selection.
  • Drive input chokes (3–5 %) included and stated in the harmonic study; study results compatible with the 580 kvar detuned 7 % PFC bank (227 Hz at 60 Hz) and IEEE 519-based SEC limits at the PCC.
  • Output-side engineering closed: screened symmetrical motor cables within length limits, 360° glands, dv/dt or sine filters where needed, insulated bearings/shaft earthing on motors ≥ ~100 kW, motors converter-rated per IEC 60034-25.
  • SABER/SASO certificates, 60 Hz declarations, 50 °C derating statements and in-Kingdom service/spares commitments delivered with the bid — not after award.
Closing the loop. Everything here is indicative training data. The binding chain for the real purchase is: load list and motor data sheets → harmonic and short-circuit studies → approved MCC and VFD data sheets → vendor certified drawings and IEC 61439 / IEC 61800 verification evidence → FAT → site measurements at commissioning. The catalogue's job is to make you fluent for that conversation.

UPS Systems

Indicative catalogue data. Product families and typical parameters are listed for training and familiarisation only, are approximate, and change with manufacturer revisions. No part numbers are given; always obtain current certified data sheets and SASO/SEC acceptance status from the manufacturer.

Goal: learn to read a UPS catalogue the way a specifying engineer reads it — starting from what this plant actually needs (tag UPS: 100 kVA, 30 minutes autonomy, feeding the control system, operator stations and other loads that must not see even a flicker), mapping each requirement onto the catalogue parameter that answers it, and finishing with a worked selection. The vendor families named here are the ones most often met on Saudi industrial sites. For the engineering behind the UPS itself — topology, battery sizing, transfer behaviour — this section leans on the UPS and DC systems tutorial; for how Part 6 catalogue sections are meant to be used, see the catalogue introduction.

What the Plant Needs — Double-Conversion Online as the Standard

IEC 62040-3 recognises three UPS topologies, and a catalogue will happily sell you any of them. Offline (VFD class) passes raw mains to the load and switches to the inverter only when the mains fails — a transfer gap of several milliseconds that office PCs tolerate and process controllers may not. Line-interactive (VI class) adds voltage regulation but still exposes the load to mains frequency and most disturbances. Double-conversion online (VFI class) rectifies the incoming AC to DC and re-creates a new, clean AC waveform continuously: the load is always on the inverter, the battery sits on the DC link ready to take over with literally zero transfer time, and no mains disturbance short of total rectifier failure reaches the output.

For an industrial process plant the choice is not really a choice: double-conversion online (VFI) is the plant standard, and everything in this catalogue section assumes it. The plant's 100 kVA UPS carries the DCS/PMS controllers, marshalling and server cabinets, operator workstations, critical instrumentation and telecoms — loads whose value lies not in their kW but in what happens to the other 2 MW when they go dark. The 30-minute autonomy is not sized for riding out a long outage; it is sized to bridge the gap to the 1250 kVA diesel generator (ATS open transition ≤10 s — see the standby power section) with a wide margin, and, if the DG fails to start, to give operators time to drive the process to a safe, orderly stop instead of a crash stop.

Where the UPS sits. The UPS is an LV load like any other — fed at 400/230 V, 60 Hz from the essential side of the plant LV system (downstream of the DG/ATS arrangement), with its output distributed on a dedicated UPS distribution board. It is a separate system from the 110 V DC switchgear tripping supply (DCDB) covered in the DC systems catalogue section — do not let a vendor merge the two to save a battery bank.
Key takeaway: specify VFI (double-conversion online) to IEC 62040-3, 100 kVA, 30 min autonomy, 400/230 V 60 Hz — and treat every catalogue page as a yes/no answer to that line. All values indicative; the load schedule and formal studies govern.

The Vendor Families You Will Meet

Four manufacturers dominate the three-phase industrial UPS market in the Gulf, and each fields a family that comfortably covers the 100 kVA point. All are VFI double-conversion machines with transformerless IGBT power stages; the meaningful differences are modularity, footprint, efficiency strategy and local service depth, not topology. Typical indicative ranges:

FamilyVendorTypical rangeArchitecture Efficiency (typ., VFI / eco)Parallel / redundant Notes for this plant
93PM / 9395Eaton 93PM ~30–500 kVA; 9395 to ~1.2 MVA 93PM modular (hot-swap power modules in one frame); 9395 large monolithic/core ~96–97 % VFI; ~98–99 % in high-efficiency mode Internal N+1 via modules; external parallel of frames 93PM at 100 kVA gives N+1 inside a single frame — attractive where floor space is tight.
Galaxy VS / VXSchneider Electric VS ~10–150 kVA; VX ~500 kVA–1.5 MVA VS modular power-module design; VX large frame ~96–97 % VFI; ~99 % in "eCS" eco-style mode VS parallels several units; internal module redundancy option Galaxy VS sits squarely on the 100 kVA point; often bundled with Schneider LV gear for single-vendor packages.
Liebert ITA2 / EXL S1Vertiv ITA2 ~5–80 kVA (parallel to higher); EXL S1 ~100 kVA–1.2 MVA ITA2 compact monolithic; EXL S1 large monolithic, optional core redundancy ~95–96.5 % VFI; ~98–99 % eco/dynamic-online Both parallel up to 4–8 units At 100 kVA you are at the top of ITA2 paralleling or the bottom of EXL S1 — ask the vendor which platform they actually offer here.
PowerValue / MegaFlexABB PowerValue ~10–120 kVA; MegaFlex ~250 kVA–1.6 MVA (modular DPA relatives below) PowerValue monolithic; DPA families decentralised modular (each module a complete UPS) ~95–96.5 % VFI; ~98–99 % eco DPA modular lines offer true module-level N+1 with hot-swap ABB's decentralised-parallel-architecture pitch is strongest where online module swap matters.

Modular versus monolithic — what you are really buying

A monolithic UPS is one power path in one box: simple, cheap per kVA, but a power-stage failure means the whole machine drops to bypass until a service visit. A modular UPS builds the rating from hot-swappable power modules (e.g. 5 × 25 kVA for a 100 kVA N+1 system): a failed module is ejected and replaced in minutes with the load still on inverter, and N+1 redundancy costs one extra module rather than a whole second machine. The prices cross over surprisingly low; at 100 kVA a modular frame with N+1 is routinely competitive with two paralleled monolithic units, and far more competitive on floor area. The catch to check in the catalogue: a modular frame has shared elements (static switch, backplane, controls) — ask the vendor which of these are redundant, because a "fully redundant" brochure claim sometimes ends at the power modules.

Efficiency classes — VFI versus eco-mode

Every catalogue now headlines two efficiency numbers. The honest one is double-conversion (VFI) efficiency, typically 95–97 % at the 100 kVA class: the load is always on the inverter and the missing 3–5 % becomes heat in the UPS room (at 100 kVA that is 3–5 kW of continuous HVAC load — budget it). The headline 98–99 % figure is eco-mode (and its "advanced"/"dynamic" variants): the load runs on raw mains through the static bypass and the inverter wakes only when a disturbance is detected — which reintroduces exactly the transfer gap VFI was bought to eliminate. For an office or data hall with tolerant IT loads, eco-mode is a defensible energy play; for a process plant control system, run in full VFI and say so in the specification, and treat eco-mode as a commissioning setting to be locked out. Compare vendors on VFI efficiency at your load point, not on the brochure headline.

As with the MV board, the brand decision at 100 kVA is rarely won on electrical performance — all four families pass. It is won on service: local Saudi service centre and spares, guaranteed response time, battery supply chain, and the vendor's willingness to certify performance at elevated ambient (next sub-section).

How to Read a UPS Datasheet

A UPS datasheet is two pages of numbers, perhaps eight of which matter. Read them in this order.

1. The IEC 62040-3 classification string — VFI-SS-111

The single most information-dense line on the sheet. Decode VFI-SS-111:

  • VFI — output is independent of input Voltage and Frequency variations (true double conversion). The alternatives are VI (voltage independent) and VFD (voltage and frequency dependent) — reject both for this duty.
  • SS — output waveform is Sinusoidal both in normal operation and in battery operation. (Letters X or Y here flag non-sinusoidal behaviour under some conditions — fine for a cheap office unit, not here.)
  • 111 — dynamic output performance classes for three test conditions (mode change, linear step load, non-linear step load). Class 1 is the tightest tolerance band; 111 means the output stays inside it for all three. A "311" machine lets the voltage dip well outside limits on mode change — exactly the moment your controls care about most.

Specify VFI-SS-111 verbatim and ask for the type-test evidence. A vendor who quotes "online double conversion" without the classification string is asking you to take the dynamic performance on trust.

2. Efficiency at part load — where your UPS will actually live

Catalogue efficiency is usually quoted at 100 % load. Your UPS will never be there: a 100 kVA machine carrying a 50–60 kVA control-system load — and half of that in an N+1 or 2N arrangement — operates at 25–60 % load for its whole life. Modern transformerless machines hold efficiency well down to ~25 % load (typically within 1–2 points of peak), but older or cheaper designs fall off a cliff below 50 %. Demand the efficiency curve (25 / 50 / 75 / 100 % load points) rather than the single headline figure, and evaluate lifetime losses at your realistic operating point. Modular machines have a structural advantage here: some can put surplus modules to sleep and keep the active ones at their efficiency sweet spot.

3. Overload and the static bypass

The overload table tells you how the UPS behaves when downstream loads gang-start or a fault begins to develop. A typical inverter overload claim reads: 110 % for 60 min, 125 % for 10 min, 150 % for 1 min (indicative — every family differs). Beyond that, the static bypass takes over: it can typically carry 1000 % for a cycle or two — but only if the mains is present and healthy. On battery, with no mains behind the bypass, the overload capability is whatever the inverter alone can do. Read the overload table twice: once for "mains available" and once for "on battery", because the second column is the one that applies during the DG start window.

4. Inverter short-circuit capability — the coordination trap

This is the number engineers most often fail to look up, and it bites hardest. A 400 V utility supply feeds a downstream fault with tens of kiloamps, and miniature circuit breakers trip magnetically in 10 ms. An inverter is a current-limited electronic source: on battery, it can deliver only about 2–3 × In into a fault, and only for around 100 ms to a few hundred ms before it shuts down to protect its IGBTs. For the plant's 100 kVA UPS, In ≈ 144 A at 400 V, so the fault current available on battery is roughly 300–430 A — total, for the whole board.

Now follow the consequence: a B-curve MCB needs 3–5 × its own rating to trip magnetically, a C-curve 5–10 ×. A 32 A C-curve MCB on the UPS distribution board needs up to 320 A — most of the inverter's entire fault capability — to clear instantaneously. A fault on one branch circuit while on battery can therefore drag the whole UPS bus down (every healthy load sees the voltage collapse) until either the MCB trips thermally (seconds — far too slow) or the inverter trips on overload, killing every control system the UPS exists to protect. The fixes are design decisions, not settings: keep UPS final circuits small (10–20 A), use B-curve or fast electronic protection, consider fuses (which clear far faster at low fault multiples), and check every branch device against the vendor's declared fault-clearing figure. When the mains is present, the static bypass masks all this by feeding the fault from the mains — which is why the problem only ever reveals itself during a real outage.

Classic failure scenario. Power fails; the UPS rides through perfectly; ninety seconds later a borderline cable fault on one operator-station circuit flashes over. On any normal board it would be a one-MCB event. On the inverter, the 350 A available cannot fire the 32 A C-curve magnetically; the bus voltage collapses; every controller on the UPS reboots simultaneously — during a plant-wide blackout. Selectivity on a UPS board must be engineered against 2–3 × In, not against the mains fault level.

5. Input THDi and the generator handshake

The UPS rectifier is a non-linear load on whatever feeds it. Legacy 6-pulse thyristor rectifiers drew 25–30 % THDi; modern IGBT/PFC front ends draw <3–5 % THDi at near-unity input power factor. This matters twice in this plant: it pollutes the LV bus less (helping the PFC and other loads), and — critically — it is kind to the 1250 kVA DG. A high-THDi rectifier load on a standby generator distorts the alternator voltage and can confuse its AVR exactly when the UPS is recharging a depleted battery at maximum rectifier demand. Specify input THDi ≤5 % at full load, input pf ≥0.99, and a configurable battery-recharge current limit / walk-in so the UPS ramps its demand gently when the ATS lands it on the generator (standby power section).

6. Temperature rating — the 40 °C default versus the 50 °C site

Almost every UPS datasheet quotes full rating at 40 °C (some at 35 °C), with operation to 50 °C only at derated power — typically in the region of 10–20 % derate, vendor-specific. The plant design ambient is 50 °C. The standard answer is the same as for the switchgear: the UPS lives in an HVAC-conditioned room held at 20–25 °C — which the batteries demand anyway, far more urgently than the electronics (see the battery-room notes). But the specification must still ask two questions in writing: the certified output rating at 50 °C (the HVAC-failure case), and the electronics' maximum survival/alarm temperature. An over-temperature alarm to the control system is mandatory; a UPS that quietly derates or trips on a 50 °C afternoon during an HVAC fault is a single point of failure hiding behind a comfort statistic.

Selection for This Plant — 100 kVA / 30 min, Worked

The plant requirement (Rev 3 Plant Data Card): UPS 100 kVA, 30 minutes autonomy, 400/230 V, 60 Hz, feeding the control and safety-critical auxiliary loads. Translate that into catalogue language line by line:

Plant requirementCatalogue parameter to demand Value to write (indicative)
Clean, no-break supply to controls IEC 62040-3 classification VFI-SS-111, true online double conversion; eco-mode lockable out
Rating Output power at pf 0.9–1.0 (modern machines are kVA = kW rated at unity) 100 kVA / ≥90 kW, three-phase 400/230 V, 60 Hz
Autonomy Battery runtime at design load and end-of-life capacity 30 min at design load, batteries aged (80 % EOL factor) and at declared room temperature
Availability Redundancy architecture N+1 (modular frame or two paralleled units) — see discussion below
Fault clearing downstream Inverter fault current and duration, on battery Declared A and ms figures; UPS board selectivity study based on them
Generator compatibility Input THDi, input pf, walk-in / recharge current limit THDi ≤5 %, pf ≥0.99, programmable recharge limit (DG 1250 kVA upstream)
Maintenance without outage Bypass arrangement Internal static bypass + external wrap-around maintenance bypass with interlocked make-before-break switching
50 °C site Ratings at 40 / 50 °C; alarm/derate behaviour Conditioned room declared; certified 50 °C behaviour in writing

N or N+1?

A single 100 kVA machine (N) protects against supply failures but is itself a single point of failure: an inverter fault drops the controls onto raw bypass mains — which, during the very outage the UPS was bought for, is dead. The step up is N+1: either a modular frame with one redundant power module (e.g. 5 × 25 kVA for a 100 kVA duty) or two paralleled 100 kVA units. For a control system whose loss stops a 2 MW plant, the modular-N+1 route is the usual sweet spot: near-2N availability at far less than 2N cost and footprint. Whichever is chosen, the battery and the static bypass must be examined separately — an N+1 set of inverters hanging off one battery string and one bypass is N+1 in name only. Two battery strings (or a centre-tapped dual-string bank) and a per-unit bypass close those holes.

Worked selection — rating, battery handshake and bypass

Step 1 — confirm the rating. Indicative audited control-load schedule: DCS/PMS cabinets 28 kW, operator stations and servers 12 kW, critical instruments and analysers 8 kW, telecoms/network 5 kW, UPS distribution losses and margin 7 kW → ~60 kW. At a design power factor of 0.9:

S = P / pf = 60 kW / 0.9 ≈ 67 kVA

Apply a growth/diversity allowance of ~25 % and round to the standard frame:

67 kVA × 1.25 ≈ 84 kVA → next standard frame = 100 kVA ✓

The Plant Data Card's 100 kVA is confirmed, loaded at ~60–70 % — a healthy point for efficiency and overload headroom.

Step 2 — battery energy for 30 min. The energy the battery must deliver, taking inverter efficiency ~0.95:

E = P × t / η = 60 kW × 0.5 h / 0.95 ≈ 31.6 kWh

With design factors for aging (÷0.8, so a battery at its 80 % end-of-life capacity still delivers the duty) and temperature/design margin (÷0.9):

E_design = 31.6 / (0.8 × 0.9) ≈ 44 kWh

On a typical ~432 V DC link (36 × 12 V blocks):

Capacity ≈ 44 000 Wh / 432 V ≈ 102 Ah → specify ~100–120 Ah blocks (30-min rate)

Sanity check: 44 kWh is roughly the energy in four domestic EV-charger-hours — physically about two battery cabinets of 100 Ah VRLA blocks; plausible. The vendor sizes the real string from the cell maker's constant-power tables at the 30-minute rate, which differ from the 10-hour Ah rating — the full method, including why short-rate discharge punishes Ah capacity, is worked in the UPS battery sizing calculation. Note that 30 min ≫ the 10 s ATS transfer (standby section): the autonomy is sized for the DG failing, not for the DG starting.

Step 3 — bypass arrangement. Specify: internal static bypass (automatic, on inverter overload/failure), plus an external wrap-around maintenance bypass panel so the entire UPS — static switch included — can be isolated and replaced with the load fed from mains. Interlock it make-before-break, and feed the bypass line from the same essential (DG-backed) source as the rectifier so a transfer to bypass during an outage still lands on a live supply. All figures indicative; the load schedule and the vendor's certified battery calculation govern.

Key takeaway: the order is rating → topology (VFI-SS-111) → redundancy (N+1, including battery and bypass) → battery (30 min, aged, at temperature) → bypass (static + external maintenance) → generator handshake (THDi, walk-in). A UPS bought on kVA alone answers one line of eight.

DC Systems & Batteries

Indicative catalogue data. Product families and typical parameters are listed for training and familiarisation only, are approximate, and change with manufacturer revisions. No part numbers are given; always obtain current certified data sheets and SASO/SEC acceptance status from the manufacturer.

Goal: learn to read battery and charger catalogues for the plant's 110 V DC system (tag DCDB) — the supply that trips and closes the switchgear. The structure mirrors the UPS section: what the plant needs, the technology families on offer, the charger/rectifier catalogue, a worked sizing sketch, and the Saudi-specific practice. Engineering background: the UPS and DC systems tutorial; usage of Part 6: the catalogue introduction.

What the Plant Needs — Why DC Security Outranks the UPS

Every circuit breaker in the MV switchgear, and the main LV breakers, trip and close from a 110 V DC battery system distributed through the DC distribution board (DCDB). The same system typically carries protection relay (IED) power supplies, trip-circuit supervision, switchgear indication, and often emergency lighting and SCADA/RTU loads. It is a battery + charger(s) + DCDB arrangement, deliberately independent of every AC supply in the plant — because its defining duty arrives precisely when the AC is in trouble.

Here is the ranking that surprises newcomers: the DC system matters more than the UPS. If the UPS dies, the control system goes dark and the plant trips — expensive, but the protection still works; the plant fails safe. If the DC system dies, the protection relays may still measure a fault perfectly and close their output contacts into a dead trip coil — and nothing trips. A fault then burns until some upstream device (perhaps SEC's own protection, seconds away) clears it, by which time a transformer or a busbar is destroyed and people have been exposed to an arc that should have lived for 100 ms. A dead battery does not announce itself the way a dead UPS does: the chargers keep the bus voltage up, every indication lamp glows, and the system looks healthy right up to the moment it is asked to deliver a 10–20 A trip-coil pulse from a battery with no capacity left.
