This is why DC systems get dual chargers, continuous battery monitoring, trip-circuit supervision and disciplined discharge testing — and why this catalogue section exists separately from the UPS one.

The silent failure mode. A failed battery on float looks identical to a healthy one — full voltage, no alarms on a basic charger. The failure is only discoverable by measurement: impedance/conductance trending, periodic capacity discharge tests, and a battery-monitoring option on the charger. Buy the monitoring with the system; retrofitting it after the first failure-to-trip investigation is the expensive way to learn.
110 V DC versus the UPS — division of labour. Switchgear tripping/closing, IED supplies and trip-circuit supervision live on the 110 V DC system; the DCS/PMS, operator stations and instrumentation live on the 230 V AC UPS (UPS catalogue section). Keep them separate: different voltages, different duty cycles (continuous IT load versus rare brutal pulses), different battery technologies, different failure consequences. Vendors occasionally propose a combined "one big battery" scheme — decline it.

Battery Technologies — VRLA, Vented NiCd, Li-ion

Three battery technologies compete for stationary duty, and the catalogue comparison is dominated by one variable this plant has in abundance: temperature. Typical indicative characteristics:

Parameter (typ., indicative)VRLA (AGM) Vented NiCdLi-ion (LFP)
Design life at 20 °C 5–10 y (long-life classes to ~12 y) 20–25+ y 10–15 y (calendar)
Effect of heat Life halves per 8–10 °C above 20 °C — a "10-year" block at a 35 °C average lasts ~2–3 y Tolerant — capacity and life degrade only mildly at high ambient; survives excursions that destroy VRLA Better than VRLA, worse than NiCd; BMS derates or blocks charge at high cell temperature
Energy density / footprint Moderate — compact cabinets Low — biggest footprint and weight per kWh; open stands High — smallest and lightest, ~50–70 % less floor area than VRLA
Maintenance Low ("maintenance-free" is marketing — impedance trending and torque checks still needed) Periodic electrolyte topping-up, cleaning; honest, inspectable condition Minimal manual work; BMS does the monitoring (and becomes a component to maintain)
Failure behaviour Sudden open-circuit or dry-out possible; hard to see coming without monitoring Gradual, graceful capacity loss; very rugged against deep discharge and ripple BMS-supervised; cell faults isolated, but BMS itself is a single point of attention
Typical replacement cycle in GCC service ~3–5 y in imperfectly cooled rooms ~15–20+ y ~10–12 y projected (field history still short)
Relative first cost per kWhLowest 2–3× VRLA2–3× VRLA and falling
Natural home in this plant UPS battery (conditioned room, cost-driven) Switchgear 110 V DC tripping battery Emerging alternative for the UPS battery

Reading the table for a Saudi plant

VRLA AGM (valve-regulated lead-acid, absorbed glass mat) is the default catalogue offer because it is cheapest and compact. Its Achilles heel is the Arrhenius arithmetic in row two: every 8–10 °C above 20 °C halves its life. At Saudi ambients an unconditioned battery room sits at 35–45 °C for months — a nominal 10-year VRLA block becomes a 2-year consumable, and it fails towards open-circuit, the failure mode that kills a trip supply silently. VRLA is acceptable only with a reliably cooled room, and even then it earns its keep on the UPS, not on the trip battery.

Vented (flooded) NiCd is the heavy, expensive, ugly option that refuses to die. Pocket-plate NiCd tolerates heat, deep discharge, high ripple and abuse; its capacity loss is gradual and measurable; design lives of 20+ years are routine and achieved in practice. Its costs are real: topping-up maintenance, 2–3× the price, the largest footprint, and a lower cell voltage (1.2 V/cell, so ~92 cells for 110 V versus ~54 lead-acid cells) — more connections to torque and inspect. For switchgear tripping duty in the Gulf, vented NiCd is the preferred technology, and most regional utility and oil-and-gas specifications say so explicitly (Saudi notes).

Li-ion (LFP — lithium iron phosphate) has crossed from EVs into the stationary catalogues, mainly as a UPS battery: a quarter of the weight, half the footprint, 5–10× the cycle life of VRLA, integrated battery management (BMS) with cell-level monitoring as standard. For UPS autonomy duty it is genuinely attractive where floor space or cooling capacity is tight. For the 110 V switchgear battery it remains the cautious choice for now: certification trails lead-acid/NiCd (IEEE/IEC sizing practice is newer), the BMS adds an electronic dependency into the one system that must not have any, and fire-safety review (thermal runaway, room suppression) is extra engineering. Watch this space; specify it for the UPS if footprint demands, not yet for the trip battery.

Key takeaway: chemistry selection is a temperature decision before it is a cost decision. In this climate: NiCd for the 110 V DC trip battery; VRLA (or increasingly LFP) for the UPS battery in a conditioned room. Whole-life cost, not purchase price, is the comparison that matters — a NiCd bank outlives 4–6 generations of badly-housed VRLA. All values indicative.

Chargers and Rectifiers — the Other Half of the Catalogue

The battery makes the headlines; the charger does the work. In normal operation the charger (a controlled rectifier, thyristor or switch-mode) carries the entire standing DC load and float-charges the battery; the battery only ever discharges when the AC input is lost or a load pulse exceeds the charger's limit. Indicative vendor families you will meet on Gulf projects: AEG SVS (industrial thyristor chargers, a de-facto standard in oil and gas), Benning (Thyrotronic/Tebetron lines), Chloride/Exide (industrial chargers with a long regional install base), and GE/ABB industrial rectifier lines. All offer the same functional menu; the specification decides which options are ticked.

Float and boost charge

Two charging regimes, selected automatically or by key-switch. Float is the permanent state: constant voltage chosen so the battery stays full without gassing — for a 110 V nominal system, typically around 1.40–1.42 V/cell for NiCd or 2.23–2.27 V/cell for lead-acid (≈ 120–125 V at the bus for a ~54-cell lead-acid string; the 92-cell NiCd string in this plant floats at ≈ 130 V — hence the dropper diodes discussed below; indicative, per cell maker). Boost (equalise) is a temporary elevated voltage — ~1.45–1.55 V/cell NiCd — used to recharge quickly after a discharge or to recondition cells. Boost voltage can push the bus towards or beyond the upper limit of connected equipment (110 % of Un = 121 V, see the voltage window below), so the catalogue option to demand is either automatic boost with a bus-voltage limiter, or dropper diodes/a dual-bus arrangement that keeps the load bus inside its window while the battery sees the boost voltage. Verify this in the catalogue rather than discovering it when the IEDs alarm on overvoltage during commissioning.

Dual-charger redundancy

The standard high-integrity arrangement is two 100 % chargers on the one battery and DCDB — either both floating in load-share, or one duty and one auto-standby. The point: a charger failure then leaves the system fully functional with no time pressure, instead of starting a silent countdown defined by battery autonomy. For a plant this size a single charger plus a rigorous alarm scheme is the economy option; given that the whole DC system costs a fraction of one MV panel, dual chargers fed from different LV sources (one each side of the LV-MSB bus-tie, or one DG-backed) are cheap availability. Each charger needs: AC fail, charger fail, DC undervoltage/overvoltage alarms — all wired to the control system, not just to a lamp on the front of a panel in an unmanned room.

Battery monitoring and supervision

The catalogue options worth their price: battery monitoring (string current, voltage, temperature; per-block or per-cell modules on better systems; impedance/conductance trending on premium ones), battery circuit continuity supervision (detects an open fuse, corroded link or open cell — the silent killers), end-of-discharge protection (low-voltage disconnect, set to protect the battery without stranding the trip duty), and a discharge-test facility (a test load connection or charger current-walk-down mode so the periodic capacity test does not require dismantling the system). Temperature-compensated float charging is near-mandatory for VRLA and useful for NiCd in this climate.

DC earth-fault monitoring — the unearthed IT bus

Tripping DC systems are run unearthed (IT): neither pole is bonded to earth. The reason is availability — a single earth fault on an IT bus causes no current to flow and nothing trips; the system carries on working. But a second earth fault, on the other pole, short-circuits part of the system — and the classic catastrophic version is a positive-pole earth fault plus an earth fault on a trip-coil wire, which can trip breakers spuriously or, worse, hold trip circuits inoperative. The first fault is therefore an urgent maintenance alarm even though nothing has failed yet. Every charger catalogue offers an earth-fault monitoring relay (balanced-bridge or pulse-injection type) that continuously measures the insulation resistance of each pole to earth and alarms below a threshold (indicatively tens of kΩ). Specify it, alarm it to the control system, and make "find the first earth fault this week" a maintenance rule — the second one chooses its own time.

Charger sizing rule of thumb from the catalogues: rated DC current ≥ continuous standing load + battery recharge current (commonly recharge to 90 % in ≤10 h, i.e. roughly C10/7 to C10/5 for NiCd per the maker's tables). For the plant's system that lands at an indicative 25–40 A charger — small hardware, outsized consequences.

Selection for This Plant — a Worked 110 V DC Sizing Sketch

Battery sizing for a DC system is a duty-cycle exercise: list every load, when it occurs and for how long across the design backup period, then size the cell so the bus voltage never leaves its window even at the end of the period, aged, at the room temperature. The full formal method (IEEE 485 for lead-acid, IEEE 1115 for NiCd, period-by-period with the cell maker's published rate tables) belongs to the vendor and the detailed design; what follows is the engineer's sketch that checks the vendor's answer for sanity. Backup period: 8 hours — long enough to cover an extended grid outage with a failed DG, holding protection, emergency lighting and SCADA alive while the plant is made safe.

The voltage window first

The window is what actually sizes the battery. Connected equipment must work across the whole excursion of an unregulated battery bus: breaker shunt trip coils are typically rated 70–110 % of Un (77–121 V for 110 V), closing coils typically 85–110 %, IEDs commonly 80–120 %. The design convention is to end the discharge at ≥80 % Un = 88 V at the battery terminals, leaving the margin between 88 V and the 77 V trip-coil floor to cover cable voltage drop to the furthest trip coil at full pulse current — check that drop explicitly on long DCDB runs. The top of the window (121 V) is what constrains boost charging, as flagged in the charger sub-section.

Worked sizing sketch — 110 V DC battery, 8 h duty

Step 1 — duty cycle (indicative load list).

LoadCurrentWhen
Continuous: IED/relay supplies, indication, trip-circuit supervision, SCADA/RTU~8 A0–8 h
Emergency lighting (DC luminaires, switchrooms) ~10 A0–3 h
Momentary: tripping burst — several breakers trip on loss of supply (~5 coils × ~10 A)~50 A first minute
Momentary: close/spring-charge duty at restoration (~2 motors × ~10 A + closing coils)~30 A last minute

Step 2 — ampere-hours, crude integration.

Ah = 8 A × 8 h + 10 A × 3 h + (50 A + 30 A) × (1/60) h ≈ 64 + 30 + 1.3 ≈ 95 Ah

Note where the energy lives: the dramatic 50 A trip burst contributes ~1 Ah; the boring 8 A standing load contributes 64 Ah. Capacity is bought for the long tail; the burst instead stresses voltage dip, which is where NiCd's flat, stiff discharge behaviour earns its price.

Step 3 — design factors. Aging factor 1.25 (system still delivers at 80 % end-of-life capacity), temperature/derating factor ~1.1 (room design temperature versus the 20–25 °C rating basis; cell-maker table value governs), design margin 1.1 (future breakers, load growth):

C ≈ 95 Ah × 1.25 × 1.1 × 1.1 ≈ 144 Ah → specify ~150–160 Ah (C10) cells

Step 4 — string. Vented NiCd at 1.2 V/cell nominal: ~92 cells gives 110 V nominal; float at ~1.41 V/cell ≈ 130 V battery voltage — confirm against the load bus upper limit and use dropper diodes or tap arrangement per the charger catalogue. End-of-discharge at 1.00–1.05 V/cell ≈ 92–97 V ≥ the 88 V floor ✓.

Sanity check: ~150 Ah × 110 V ≈ 16.5 kWh — about a third of the UPS battery's energy, in a bank that costs more; that is the price of 20-year heat-tolerant certainty. The real sizing is period-by-period to IEEE 1115 with the manufacturer's discharge tables, done and stamped by the vendor; this sketch exists so you can tell a sensible quotation from a wrong one. All figures indicative.

Spec card in one line each: 110 V DC unearthed (IT); vented pocket-plate NiCd, ~92 cells, ~150 Ah C10 (vendor-sized to IEEE 1115 / SASO-adopted IEC 62259); dual 100 % chargers, float/boost, temperature compensation; earth-fault, battery-continuity and voltage alarms to the control system; DCDB with fused/MCB outgoing ways, segregated trip and close supplies per board; end-of-discharge ≥88 V; boost limited to keep the load bus ≤121 V.

Saudi-Specific Practice — Heat, Hydrogen, SASO and the NiCd Habit

The battery room at 50 °C — HVAC as protection infrastructure

Batteries are the most temperature-sensitive equipment in the plant, sited in the country least inclined to indulge them. Cell-maker ratings are referenced to 20–25 °C; the site design ambient is 50 °C. For VRLA the Arrhenius rule (life halves per 8–10 °C above 20 °C) makes an uncooled room a battery shredder; even NiCd, though it survives, loses some capacity and life at sustained high temperature. The design conclusions: condition the battery/UPS rooms to a stable 20–25 °C; treat that HVAC as protection infrastructure, not comfort — alarm its failure to the control system and state in the design what happens during an HVAC outage on a 50 °C day (this is exactly the case where NiCd's tolerance buys you days of grace and VRLA's intolerance buys you an incident report). Record room temperature continuously; the battery's warranty and its real capacity both follow that log.

Ventilation and hydrogen

Charging batteries electrolyse water and evolve hydrogen — vented NiCd routinely (especially on boost), VRLA only slightly in normal float but significantly in fault/overcharge. Hydrogen is explosive from 4 % in air, and it pools at the ceiling. Practice per SASO-adopted IEC 62485-2: calculate the ventilation air flow from cell count and charge current; for a vented NiCd room provide dedicated mechanical extract (high-level), interlock or alarm the fans with charging, keep ignition sources and unsealed fittings out of the hydrogen zone, and fit eyewash facilities for the electrolyte. The HVAC therefore does two safety jobs at once — cooling and dilution — and its failure alarm matters twice.

SASO/SABER and SEC expectations

The same two regulatory tracks as the switchgear apply. Imported batteries, chargers and UPS equipment clear customs through the SABER platform with SASO certificates of conformity against the SASO-adopted IEC standards (IEC 62040 series for UPS, IEC 62259/60623 for NiCd cells, IEC 62485-2 for installation safety) — make the vendor contractually responsible for the certificates, because a battery bank stuck in port ages in the container. SEC acceptance touches the DC system indirectly but firmly: the intake protection that SEC reviews assumes a healthy 110 V DC supply, and SEC (and SBC 401) expectations for substation auxiliary supplies follow the same dual-charger, monitored-battery pattern described here. Align the spec card with the SEC interface requirements before the RFQ goes out.

Why the Gulf specifies NiCd — the rationale in one paragraph

Regional utility, ARAMCO-style and large-industrial specifications converge on vented NiCd for switchgear DC duties, and the logic is exactly this section's arithmetic: the trip battery is the one component whose failure converts a cleared fault into a destroyed substation; lead-acid life collapses exponentially with the temperature this region supplies for free; NiCd's failure mode is gradual and measurable rather than sudden and silent; and over a 25-year plant life one NiCd bank is cheaper than the 4–6 VRLA banks (plus one failure-to-trip investigation) it replaces. Buy VRLA where the room is reliably cold and the consequence of failure is a reboot; buy NiCd where the consequence is a fire. Indicative guidance — the project specification and reliability study govern.

Acceptance and routine-test checklist

  • Verify ratings plates and certificates: UPS VFI-SS-111 to IEC 62040-3 at 60 Hz; NiCd cells to SASO-adopted IEC 62259; SABER/SASO documents complete before shipment.
  • Witness UPS FAT: full-load heat run, transfer tests (mains fail, return, overload to bypass and back), efficiency at 25/50/75/100 % load, and the declared inverter fault-current test.
  • Witness an 8 h (DC) / 30 min (UPS) capacity discharge test on the actual battery strings, at declared temperature, to the end-of-discharge voltage — and file the curves as the baseline.
  • Confirm dual chargers load-share and that AC-fail, charger-fail, DC over/undervoltage, battery-continuity and earth-fault alarms each reach the control system individually.
  • Inject a deliberate single earth fault on each DC pole and verify the IT-bus earth-fault monitor alarms at the specified insulation threshold.
  • Check the voltage window end to end: boost charge with load bus ≤110 % Un; measured voltage at the furthest trip coil ≥70 % Un during a simulated multi-breaker trip burst.
  • Inspect battery room provisions against IEC 62485-2: ventilation flow calculation, high-level extract, HVAC failure alarm, temperature recording, eyewash and signage.
Closing the loop. Everything here is indicative training data. The binding chain for the real purchase is: audited load schedules (UPS kW and DC duty cycle) → the worked methods in the UPS/DC tutorial → vendor IEEE/IEC battery sizing and certified data sheets → FAT with witnessed discharge tests → site commissioning inside the voltage window. The catalogue's job is to make you fluent for that conversation — and to make sure the quietest system in the plant is never the one that fails loudest.

Diesel Generator Sets

Indicative catalogue data. Product families and typical parameters are listed for training and familiarisation only, are approximate, and change with manufacturer revisions. No part numbers are given; always obtain current certified data sheets and SASO/SEC acceptance status from the manufacturer.

Goal: learn to read a generator-set catalogue the way a specifying engineer reads it — starting from what the plant actually needs (tag DG: a 1250 kVA ESP standby set at 400/230 V, 60 Hz, 1800 rpm, in a 50 °C desert ambient), translating each plant requirement into the catalogue parameter that answers it, and finishing with a worked frame selection. The engineering behind the sizing lives in the standby generator tutorial; this section is the shopping companion to it, used as explained in the catalogue introduction.

What the Plant Needs — the Standby Role Defined

Begin, as always in Part 6, on the demand side. The plant draws about 2.0 MW at 0.85 power factor — roughly 2353 kVA — from the SEC 13.8 kV intake. The standby diesel generator does not back up that whole plant. It backs the essential bus only: the loads whose loss creates a safety, asset-damage or restart problem — emergency lighting, instrument and UPS supplies, fire and gas systems, critical cooling and lube pumps, the control building HVAC that keeps the switchgear and servers alive, and whatever process loads the shutdown philosophy declares "must ride through a grid outage". Everything else stays dark until SEC returns.

That single decision — essential bus, not whole plant — sets the size class. The essential load list (built in the Part 2 tutorial) sums to an indicative ~900 kVA peak, and after motor-starting and 50 °C ambient checks the selected machine is a 1250 kVA standby-rated (ESP) set at 400/230 V, 60 Hz. On the 60 Hz SEC grid a 4-pole machine runs at 1800 rpm — a number worth internalising, because a 50 Hz catalogue page for the "same" engine quotes 1500 rpm and different powers; mixing the two columns is a classic transplant error, exactly like ordering 12 kV-class switchgear for a 13.8 kV system.

Why not back the whole plant? A set able to carry 2353 kVA continuously through a long outage would be a ~2500 kVA-class machine (or two paralleled units), with several times the capital cost, fuel storage, cooling airflow and exhaust treatment — bought to cover the rare hours per year when SEC is down, and running at part load (bad for diesels) whenever the outage load is modest. Standby engineering is the art of buying exactly the kVA the shutdown philosophy demands, and not one frame more. All figures indicative; the load schedule and detailed studies govern.

The set connects to the essential bus through the automatic transfer switch (tag ATS, open transition, ≤ 10 s total break — see the ATS catalogue section below and the ATS logic tutorial). Within that 10 s budget the engine must crank, fire, reach 1800 rpm and 400 V, and close onto a bus that then hits it with motor re-acceleration. Every catalogue parameter examined in this section — rating class, load-acceptance steps, transient voltage dip, fuel rate — exists to answer whether a given frame can survive exactly that sequence, year after year, at 50 °C.

The ISO 8528 Rating Ladder — ESP, PRP, COP

The single most consequential line on a genset data sheet is not the kVA number — it is the rating class printed next to it. The same physical engine-alternator pair carries two or three different kVA figures depending on how often and how hard you intend to run it, codified in ISO 8528-1. Reading "1250 kVA" without reading the class beside it is like reading a speed limit without noticing whether it is in km/h or mph.

ClassNameIntended duty (indicative)Load profileTypical use
ESPEmergency Standby Power Utility-outage backup only; typically ≤ 200 h/year, with a limited fraction (vendors often state ~25 h/year) at full nameplate. No overload capability — ESP is already the ceiling. Variable load; average over 24 h commonly limited to ~70 % of rating This plant's DG — grid is the normal source, the set runs only on SEC failure and on test.
PRPPrime Power Unlimited hours per year at variable load (24-h average typically ≤ 70 %), with 10 % overload available 1 h in 12. Variable Remote sites and rental fleets where the genset is the utility.
COPContinuous Power Unlimited hours at constant 100 % load, no overload. Constant base load Baseload/cogeneration duty; the lowest kVA figure of the three.

The three classes are rungs of one ladder for one machine. As a rule of thumb, ESP ≈ 110 % of PRP, and COP sits another 10–15 % below PRP — so a frame sold as "1250 kVA ESP" is typically about 1136 kVA PRP and roughly 1000 kVA COP (indicative; each vendor publishes its own exact ladder). The physics is thermal and tribological: the extra 10 % standby power is borrowed from the engine's wear and temperature margins on the argument that it will only ever be used a few dozen hours a year.

Why mis-applying ESP as prime destroys engines. Run a standby-rated set as the everyday power source and you are operating continuously inside the margin the manufacturer reserved for emergencies: higher exhaust and coolant temperatures, higher cylinder pressures, faster lube-oil degradation, and service intervals computed for 200 h/year arriving every few weeks instead. The failure pattern is depressingly repeatable — burned exhaust valves, cracked heads, turbocharger failures and bearing wear inside the first year or two — and the manufacturer's warranty is void from the first month, because the controller's hour counter tells the story. If a site genuinely needs daily generation (a construction phase before SEC connection is the common Saudi case), buy or rent a PRP-rated set for that phase; do not burn the plant's emergency machine.
Key takeaway: this plant's DG is specified as 1250 kVA ESP at 50 °C site conditions — an emergency-standby figure valid because the SEC grid, not the engine, is the normal source. Every comparison between catalogue pages must be made at the same rating class, the same site conditions and the same frequency, or it is not a comparison at all.

The Vendor Families You Will Meet

Four genset families dominate the Gulf industrial market in the 1–2 MVA class this plant shops in. All build packaged sets — engine, alternator, radiator, base frame, controller — type-tested as a unit to ISO 8528; a specification written around the standard and the site conditions will draw compliant offers from all of them. Typical indicative ranges at 60 Hz, 1800 rpm:

FamilyVendorEngine series (typ.) kVA range at 60 Hz / 1800 rpm (typ., standby) Native controllerNotes for this plant
Cat electric powerCaterpillar C-series (C9–C32) mid-range; 3500 series above ~1.5 MVA ~250 kVA to ~3000+ kVA; the 1250 kVA point sits at the top of the C32 class / bottom of the 3500 class Cat EMCP series Very strong dealer network in the Kingdom; rental fleet compatibility is a plus for temporary paralleling.
Cummins Power GenerationCummins QSK series (electronic) and the long-lived KTA series ~250 kVA to ~3500 kVA; 1250 kVA falls in the QSK40/KTA50 class PowerCommand Engine, alternator and ATS from one factory simplifies the DG–ATS signal interface (ATS families).
Kohler–SDMOKohler Energy KD series engines plus packaged sets on Kohler, Mitsubishi and other blocks ~20 kVA to ~4000 kVA across the packaged range Kohler APM / DEC series European (ex-SDMO) packaging heritage; wide enclosure catalogue.
MTU (Rolls-Royce Power Systems)MTU Series 1600 / 2000 / 4000 ~700 kVA to ~3500+ kVA; 1250 kVA sits in the Series 2000 class MTU controller line Premium positioning; strong in data-centre and utility standby.

Controllers — native versus aftermarket

The controller is the genset's brain: it cranks, monitors, protects, synchronises and talks to the plant. The majors fit their own (Cat EMCP, Cummins PowerCommand, Kohler DEC/APM, MTU's line); the large packager market — firms that marry a bought-in engine and alternator on their own base frame, very common in the Gulf — fits aftermarket controllers, dominated by Deep Sea Electronics (DSE) and ComAp. These are perfectly capable devices; the specification points that matter are Modbus/Ethernet integration to the plant control system (ATS/PMS interface), remote start/stop and status contacts, protection functions per the study, and local support for spares and firmware. What to be wary of is not the controller brand but an undocumented packager: demand the ISO 8528 type-test evidence for the packaged set, not just the engine brochure.

Enclosures and silencers

At 1250 kVA the catalogue offers the set open-skid (for an equipment room), in a weatherproof enclosure, or in a containerised package (ISO 20/40 ft) with integral tank, attenuation and switchgear. For a Saudi outdoor installation the enclosure must be specified for dust (filtration on combustion and cooling air, minimum IP54 thinking on the electrical compartments per the plant basis) and for 50 °C cooling airflow — a sound-attenuated canopy restricts airflow, and the attenuation and the derating interact. Silencer grades run industrial (~−15 dB) / residential (~−25 dB) / critical (~−35 dB); typical attenuated canopies achieve around 75–85 dB(A) at 1 m (indicative). Specify the noise limit at the plant boundary and let the vendor pick the hardware.

Ask every bidder one question early: "state the set's standby rating at 50 °C ambient, with your dust filters and your canopy fitted, at 60 Hz / 1800 rpm". The brochure kVA almost never survives that sentence intact — see the data-sheet anatomy next.

Anatomy of a Genset Data Sheet

A generator-set data sheet is four documents stapled together: ratings (already covered), site derating, dynamic performance (load acceptance and transient response), and consumption and emissions. The brochure headline is written for a cool, low-altitude, unfiltered, open-skid machine; the Saudi answer lives in the footnotes.

Site derating — altitude, temperature, dust

Engine ratings are referenced to standard conditions (ISO 3046 / ISO 8528: 25 °C air, 100 kPa, 30 % humidity, with most vendors guaranteeing full power up to 40 °C and a stated altitude). Beyond the reference point the diesel loses breath: thinner or hotter air means less oxygen per stroke. Indicative rules of thumb — the vendor's curves govern:

  • Temperature: roughly 0.5–1 % of power per °C above 40 °C for a typical air-to-air aftercooled engine. At the plant's 50 °C design ambient that is an indicative 5–10 % off the brochure figure.
  • Altitude: typically ~1 % per 100 m above the rated altitude (often 1000 m for turbocharged engines). Most Saudi coastal and central sites are low enough for this to be minor — but check Riyadh-region sites (~600–700 m).
  • Dust filtration: heavy-duty combustion-air filters and canopy inlet attenuation/filtration add intake restriction — a further 1–3 % indicative, plus a maintenance obligation: a choked filter in a shamal re-derates the set in real time.
  • Alternator: the alternator derates too (class H insulation, typically rated at 40 °C); the packaged-set rating at 50 °C is the lower of the engine and alternator answers.

Load acceptance — ISO 8528-5 performance classes

A standby set is judged by what happens in the first seconds after the ATS closes, not by its steady-state nameplate. A diesel cannot swallow its full rating in one gulp: the turbocharger needs time to spool, so a naturally limited "first step" of load is all it can take without stalling or a deep frequency excursion. ISO 8528-5 packages this as performance classes — G1 (lighting-grade), G2 (general purpose: typical limits around 20 % transient voltage dip and recovery within a few seconds), G3 (tighter: ~15 % dip, faster recovery, for telecoms/UPS-heavy loads), and G4 (by agreement). The data sheet expresses the same physics as a load acceptance table: e.g. "first step 60 % of standby rating" with stated dip and recovery. For this plant — UPS rectifiers, contactor coils that drop out below ~70–80 % volts, and re-accelerating motors — specify class G2 as minimum, G3 preferred, and check the largest single load step against the table, not against the nameplate (worked in the selection below).

Transient dip and recovery

Two curves matter: voltage dip (alternator transient reactance X′d versus the kVA of the step — bigger alternator frame = smaller dip; vendors offer an "upsized alternator" option exactly for motor-heavy standby buses) and frequency dip (engine inertia and governor response). The data sheet states both for defined steps. Check them against what the essential bus tolerates: UPS input windows, contactor hold-in, VFD undervoltage trips. If the dip fails the check, the fix is usually one alternator frame size, far cheaper than one engine size.

Fuel consumption and sound

Fuel rate is tabulated at 50 / 75 / 100 % load. For a 1250 kVA (≈1000 kWe) class set, indicative figures are roughly 130 L/h at 50 %, 185 L/h at 75 %, 250 L/h at 100 % — i.e. a specific consumption around 0.20–0.27 L/kWh. These numbers size the day tank and the autonomy claim (worked below). Sound data is given as dB(A) at defined distances per enclosure option; specify the limit at the receptor (plant boundary, control-room wall), not at the canopy. Finally, check the small print on fuel specification — Saudi diesel grades and sulphur content versus the engine's emissions package — and on coolant for sustained 50 °C operation.

Key takeaway: read a genset data sheet in this order — rating class, then the 50 °C/dust derated figure, then the load-acceptance and dip tables, then fuel. A set that passes the first two lines but fails the dynamic check will black out the essential bus it was bought to save.

Selection for This Plant — from Load List to 1250 kVA ESP

This is the worked path from the plant's needs to the catalogue frame, on the Rev 3 Plant Data Card basis. The full engineering treatment is in the Part 2 standby generator tutorial; here it is compressed into the sequence a specifier actually follows on the catalogue page. All numbers indicative.

Worked selection — essential bus standby set

Step 1 — essential load. The essential load schedule (lighting/small power, UPS input, fire & safety, critical pumps and fans, control-building HVAC) sums to an indicative running peak of:

S_essential ≈ 900 kVA (≈ 765 kW at pf 0.85)

Step 2 — motor-start check. The largest single motor step on the essential bus is an indicative 75 kW pump (≈ 90 kVA running). Started direct-on-line at ~6 × FLC:

S_start ≈ 6 × 90 kVA ≈ 540 kVA (at ~0.2 pf, mostly reactive)

Against a 1250 kVA frame this step is ~43 % of rating — inside a typical G2/G3 load-acceptance first step, with a predicted transient dip well under 20 % on the standard alternator (vendor dip curve governs). The PMS sequences loads in steps (ATS/load-sequencing tutorial) so the set never sees the whole 900 kVA at once.

Step 3 — growth and pf margin. Add ~10 % schedule growth:

S_required ≈ 900 × 1.10 ≈ 990 kVA

Step 4 — site derating at 50 °C. Work backwards: a frame whose reference standby rating is ~1400 kVA, derated an indicative 1 %/°C for the 10 °C above 40 °C plus filter/canopy losses:

S_site ≈ 1400 kVA × (1 − 0.10) ≈ 1260 kVA → select 1250 kVA ESP at site conditions

Step 5 — utilisation sanity check.

990 kVA / 1250 kVA ≈ 0.79 → ~79 % loaded at peak ✓

Comfortably inside the ESP envelope, high enough to avoid chronic light-running (wet stacking) on test runs. Result: 1250 kVA ESP, 400/230 V, 60 Hz, 1800 rpm, class G3 preferred — matching tag DG on the Plant Data Card. Detailed load schedule and vendor transient data govern the final frame.

Worked sketch — fuel autonomy and day tank

Specify 8 h autonomy at an average outage load of ~75 % (the essential bus rarely sits at peak for a whole outage). From the data-sheet fuel table, ~185 L/h at 75 % for this class:

V_fuel = 185 L/h × 8 h = 1480 L → specify ≥ 1500 L usable; round to a 2000 L day tank

Sanity check: 2000 L gives ~10.8 h at 75 % load, or a full 8 h even at 100 % (250 L/h × 8 h = 2000 L). Add low-level alarm at ~4 h remaining, fill connection sized for a road tanker, and bunding per SBC/civil requirements. Longer autonomy belongs in a separate bulk tank with transfer pumps, not a bigger day tank at the engine.

The remaining specification lines come straight from the earlier sub-sections: ISO 8528 ESP rating declared at site conditions (ratings ladder), G2/G3 load acceptance with the 540 kVA step demonstrated (data-sheet anatomy), 60 Hz / 1800 rpm type evidence, dust-rated enclosure and silencer to the boundary noise limit, controller with remote start and Modbus to the plant control system, and the ATS interface contacts defined jointly with the ATS selection.

Automatic Transfer Switches

Indicative catalogue data. Product families and typical parameters are listed for training and familiarisation only, are approximate, and change with manufacturer revisions. No part numbers are given; always obtain current certified data sheets and SASO/SEC acceptance status from the manufacturer.

Goal: learn to read an automatic transfer switch catalogue — transition types first (because they define the product class), then ampere and withstand ratings, then the controller features — and finish with the worked pick for this plant's essential bus (tag ATS: open transition, ≤ 10 s, fed by SEC normal / DG standby). The transfer logic and timing engineering is in the ATS tutorial; the generator it pairs with is in the genset catalogue section above.

Transfer Schemes — Open, Delayed, Closed, Bypass

An ATS is conceptually simple — two sources in, one load out, an interlock that forbids both at once — but catalogues split into four scheme families, and choosing the scheme is the real engineering decision; the ampere rating is just shopping.

SchemeHow it transfersDead time (typ.)Use it when…
Open transition (break-before-make) Opens the connected source, then closes the other. The two sources are never paralleled. Tens of ms to a few s of switch dead time; total outage on grid failure is governed by engine start — ≤ 10 s end-to-end is this plant's standard. Default for standby duty. Loads ride on the UPS (100 kVA / 30 min) or simply tolerate the gap. No utility consent needed because the DG never touches the SEC network.
Delayed transition (open with centre-off pause) Opens, pauses deliberately in a centre-off position (programmable, often 1–5 s), then closes the other source. Seconds, on purpose. Motor and transformer-rich loads: the pause lets motor residual voltage decay so re-energisation does not slam an out-of-phase back-EMF — torque transients that snap shafts and trip breakers. The in-phase-monitor option is the faster alternative for the same problem.
Closed transition (make-before-break) Synchronises and parallels the two live sources for ≤ 100 ms, then opens the outgoing one. Used for return transfer and load tests; a dead grid still means an open transfer. Zero (when both sources are live). Seamless monthly testing and return-to-utility. Requires utility consent — paralleling with the SEC network engages the Saudi Grid Code / SEC Distribution Code interconnection rules and usually mandated protection (see Saudi notes).
Bypass-isolation Any of the above, built with a second, manually-operated switch path so the automatic mechanism can be isolated, drawn out and serviced while the load stays fed. n/a (a construction option, not a transition type) When the ATS itself must not be a maintenance single point of failure — hospitals, data centres, and a defensible option for this plant's essential bus.
Where the 10 seconds goes. For this plant's open transition the budget is roughly: undervoltage sensing and timers confirm the SEC loss (~1–3 s, to ride through dips without nuisance starts), engine crank and field build-up to 1800 rpm / 400 V (~5–8 s for a warm 1250 kVA-class set), then the transfer operation itself (< 1 s). The ATS controller owns the first and last items; the engine owns the middle. The full timing diagram is worked in the Part 2 ATS tutorial.

The Vendor Families You Will Meet

The ATS market splits into purpose-built transfer-switch specialists and genset-vendor lines designed to pair with their own controllers. Typical indicative ranges:

FamilyVendorAmpere range (typ.) Transition types offeredController features (typ.) Notes for this plant
ASCO 7000 SERIES ASCO Power Technologies (Schneider Electric) ~30 to 4000 A Open, delayed, closed transition; bypass-isolation versions with draw-out automatic switch Group-5/7-class controllers: in-phase monitor, programmable timers, engine exerciser, load-shed contacts, comms (Modbus/Ethernet) The industry benchmark for critical-power ATS; the bypass-isolation option fits an essential bus that cannot tolerate ATS maintenance outages.
Cat ATC contactor-type ATSCaterpillar ~40 to 3000 A class Open and delayed transition; closed-transition variants in the larger frames ATC controller family, engineered to talk natively to Cat EMCP genset controllers; exerciser, timers, status comms Natural pick when the DG is a Cat set — single-vendor responsibility for the start-signal chain.
Cummins OTPC / BTPCCummins Power Generation ~40 to 4000 A OTPC: open/delayed transition power-contactor type; BTPC adds bypass-isolation; closed-transition variants exist PowerCommand ATS controllers, native integration with PowerCommand genset controls; in-phase monitor, load shed, network comms Same single-vendor logic as Cat, for a Cummins DG.
TransferPacT / ASCO 300-class tiers Schneider Electric / ASCO ~40 to 1600 A (TransferPacT); ASCO 300 SERIES to ~3000 A Open and delayed transition; simpler feature set Commercial-tier controllers: basic timers, exerciser, fewer programmable I/O and comms options The value tier — adequate for non-critical sub-distribution transfers, usually not the pick for a plant's main essential-bus ATS.

Construction classes hiding behind the brands

Underneath the branding, catalogues offer three mechanism constructions, and the difference matters more than the logo. Dedicated transfer mechanisms (ASCO 7000, OTPC class) are purpose-built double-throw devices, mechanically interlocked over-centre mechanisms type-tested specifically for transfer duty — the benchmark for endurance and withstand. Contactor-based switches are two mechanically and electrically interlocked contactors — economical at smaller sizes. Breaker-pair arrangements use two motor-operated circuit breakers with interlocks; they add integral overcurrent protection but transfer more slowly and their endurance is breaker-class, not contactor-class. For IEC procurement, all should be type-tested to IEC 60947-6-1 (transfer switching equipment); that certificate, at 400 V / 60 Hz, is the line to demand regardless of family. Utilisation category matters too: AC-33A/AC-33B (mixed/motor loads) rather than AC-31 (non-inductive) for this bus.

Decide early who supplies the ATS: with the genset (one responsibility for the start chain and timing) or with the LV switchboard package (one responsibility for busbar, withstand and arc containment). Both work; an ATS bought separately from both is how interface gaps are born. Whichever route is chosen, the DG controller, ATS controller and plant control system signal map is a drawing to approve before manufacture — see the selection.

Anatomy of an ATS Data Sheet

Rated current versus withstand — two different questions

The ampere number in the model name answers the easy question: how much load current the switch can carry continuously. The hard question is printed further down as the withstand and closing rating (WCR): how much fault current the switch can carry — and, worse, close onto — without welding or exploding, for long enough for an upstream protective device to clear. An ATS is not a breaker; it does not interrupt faults. It must survive them while something else does the interrupting. The WCR is therefore stated in combination with a protecting device: either "any breaker" for a stated time (e.g. X kA for 3 cycles / specific-breaker tables) or a short-time rating (e.g. X kA for 0.05–0.5 s) that lets it coordinate with an upstream breaker's short-time delay. The rule is absolute: WCR ≥ prospective fault current at the point of installation, verified against the actual upstream breaker or fuse type and its clearing time — not against a generic assumption. On this plant the essential-bus fault level is dominated by the LV network (one transformer ≈ 38.5 kA at the MSB; the level at the ATS depends on intervening cable and is fixed by the short-circuit study).

3-pole versus 4-pole — the neutral decision

On this plant's TN-S system the question "switch the neutral or not?" must be answered deliberately. A 3-pole ATS leaves the neutral solidly shared between SEC and DG sources; a 4-pole ATS transfers the neutral with the phases (overlapping or early-make/late-break neutral contacts in better designs, so the load neutral is never floated). The driver is earth-fault protection integrity: with both source neutrals permanently interconnected, parallel neutral return paths form, and residual/earth-fault relays on either source can be desensitised or made to nuisance-trip by circulating neutral current. Where the DG has its own neutral-earth bond and earth-fault protection — the normal arrangement for a packaged set, and the assumption here — specify 4-pole and switch the neutral. (The 3-pole alternative requires a single, carefully engineered neutral-earth bond and a protection review; it is a design decision, not a default.) The earthing logic is developed in the Part 2 earthing material.

Endurance, duty and environment

  • Endurance cycles: catalogues state mechanical and electrical operation counts (IEC 60947-6-1 classes; thousands of cycles for dedicated mechanisms). A standby ATS transfers rarely but is exercised monthly — 30+ years × 12 tests is only ~400 cycles, so endurance is rarely limiting; contact inspection intervals are.
  • Utilisation category: AC-33A/B for the mixed motor/UPS load of an essential bus, declared at 400 V, 60 Hz.
  • 50 °C derating: as with every catalogue in Part 6, the ampere rating is referenced to 40 °C (and often to free-air or a specific enclosure). Ask for the certified continuous rating at 50 °C, in the offered enclosure — an indicative 5–10 % reduction — plus IP54-thinking dust protection if outside the conditioned room.
  • Controller data: pickup/dropout voltage and frequency windows (set for 400 V / 60 Hz nominal), programmable timers (engine start delay, transfer, retransfer, cool-down), in-phase monitor or programmed-pause options, load-shed/elevator pre-signal contacts, engine exerciser, and comms protocol to the plant control system.
Key takeaway: an ATS data sheet is read in this order — transition type, then WCR against the real upstream device, then poles (4-pole for this TN-S plant), then the 50 °C ampere rating, and only then the controller features. A switch that merely matches the load current but not the fault duty is a bomb with a brand name.

Selection for This Plant — the Essential-Bus ATS

Worked selection — ATS for tag ATS

Step 1 — frame current. The ATS must carry whatever the DG can deliver, because the genset breaker — not the ATS — is the limiting protection on the standby side. Full-load current of the 1250 kVA set at 400 V:

I = S / (√3 × U) = 1,250,000 VA / (1.732 × 400 V) ≈ 1804 A

Next standard frame up:

1804 A → 2000 A frame class

Sanity check: the essential load itself (~900 kVA ≈ 1300 A) fits with margin; sizing to the source rather than the load is the conservative convention and costs one frame size.

Step 2 — withstand and closing rating. Demand WCR ≥ the prospective short-circuit current at the ATS location from the short-circuit study (upper bound: one-transformer infeed ≈ 38.5 kA at the LV-MSB; the value at the essential switchboard is lower after cable impedance — the study fixes it). Verify the WCR against the actual upstream breaker type and clearing time, or specify a short-time-rated switch coordinated with the upstream breaker's short-time delay.

Step 3 — poles and transition. 4-pole (switched neutral, overlapping contacts) for the TN-S system with a neutral-earthed DG; open transition with programmable delayed transition / in-phase monitor for the motor loads; total transfer budget ≤ 10 s per the plant standard. Bypass-isolation construction is the recommended option so the essential bus never goes dark for ATS maintenance.

Result (indicative): 2000 A, 4-pole, open/delayed-transition bypass-isolation ATS, IEC 60947-6-1 class AC-33, 400/230 V 60 Hz, WCR per the short-circuit study, rated at 50 °C. The formal studies and vendor certified data govern.

Signal interfaces — the lines on the drawing that make it work

The ATS is a node in a control chain, and the interface list is part of the specification, not an afterthought:

  • To the DG controller: the hardwired, fail-safe engine-start contact (closed = run is the safe convention), plus generator-available status back to the ATS; remote stop and common alarm.
  • To the plant control / building management system: position indication (normal / emergency / centre-off), source-availability status, transfer-in-progress, fail-to-transfer alarm, exerciser status — via volt-free contacts and/or Modbus.
  • Load shed / staged reconnection: programmable contacts that drop or delay non-priority essential loads so the DG sees the staged load steps assumed in the genset selection.
  • UPS coordination: the 100 kVA UPS rides through the ≤10 s break; confirm its input window accepts the DG's voltage/frequency excursions on retransfer (timing tutorial).

Saudi-Specific Practice — SASO, SEC and the Desert

SASO / SABER conformity

Imported transfer switches and generator sets clear Saudi customs through the SABER platform with certificates of conformity against the SASO-adopted standards (IEC 60947-6-1 for the ATS; ISO 8528 and the LV directives' IEC equivalents for the packaged genset). Make the vendor contractually responsible for the SABER/SASO certificates, type-test reports at 60 Hz, and country-of-origin documents at order stage — paperwork strands equipment in port far more often than technical faults do, exactly as noted for the MV switchgear.

SEC notification and the closed-transition question

An open-transition ATS never connects the DG to the SEC network, so no interconnection approval is engaged — this is a quiet but real advantage of the plant's chosen scheme. The moment closed transition (or any paralleling, however brief) is contemplated, the installation becomes an embedded generator paralleling with the distribution network: SEC must be notified and must consent, the Saudi Grid Code / SEC Distribution Code interconnection requirements apply, and protection (sync-check, reverse power, over/under voltage and frequency, and the mandated means of disconnection) must be agreed with SEC in writing. The ~100 ms overlap does not exempt it. If seamless retransfer is ever requested by operations, treat it as a project with an SEC workstream, not an ATS option tick-box.

Siting — heat and dust

Best practice is the ATS inside the conditioned electrical room with the essential switchboard, restoring the 40 °C catalogue basis. If it must live outside (e.g. in a containerised genset package), specify the enclosure for dust (minimum IP54 per the plant environmental basis), shaded installation, the certified 50 °C ampere rating, and anti-condensation heaters for coastal humidity swings. Verify the controller electronics' declared operating temperature — control boards, not copper, are usually the first thermal casualty.

Exercising under permit

A standby system that is never tested is a hope, not a system. Set the ATS exerciser for a monthly on-load test (engine start, transfer, 30–60 min on load to reach full operating temperature and avoid wet stacking, retransfer) — but on an operating plant this is a planned operation under the permit-to-work system, scheduled with operations, with the essential loads' tolerance of the two open-transition breaks confirmed in advance, and logged: start time, time-to-ready (verify ≤ 10 s), load reached, fuel state, alarms. Quarterly, add a genuine "pull-the-plug" black test of the whole chain — sensing, start, transfer, sequencing — because the exerciser's polite pre-arranged test exercises everything except the part that fails in real life: the loss-of-source detection.

  • Confirm DG rating is declared ESP per ISO 8528 at site conditions — 1250 kVA at 50 °C, with dust filters and enclosure fitted, at 400/230 V, 60 Hz, 1800 rpm (indicative; studies govern).
  • Check the largest essential motor-start step against the vendor's load-acceptance table and transient-dip curve (ISO 8528-5 class G2 minimum, G3 preferred).
  • Verify day-tank autonomy calculation against the data-sheet fuel rates (indicative 8 h → ~2000 L) with low-level alarm and bunding.
  • Verify ATS frame (2000 A class for 1804 A DG full-load current) and WCR ≥ prospective fault current with the actual upstream device per the short-circuit study.
  • Confirm 4-pole (switched neutral) construction for the TN-S system, with overlapping neutral contacts, and the open/delayed-transition scheme within the ≤ 10 s budget.
  • Approve the DG-controller / ATS / plant-control signal map (engine start, status, load shed, alarms) before manufacture; test every line at commissioning.
  • Collect SASO/SABER certificates and 60 Hz type-test reports with the bid; if closed transition is ever proposed, obtain SEC written consent first.
Closing the loop. Catalogue fluency is the means, not the end. The binding chain for the real purchase runs: essential-load schedule and shutdown philosophy → genset sizing study and ATS timing study → approved data sheets → vendor certified drawings and type tests → FAT and the witnessed black test → the monthly exercising log. Every number in this section is indicative training data; the formal studies and SEC/SASO correspondence govern.

Protection Relays & IEDs

Indicative catalogue data. Product families and typical parameters are listed for training and familiarisation only, are approximate, and change with manufacturer revisions. No part numbers are given; always obtain current certified data sheets and SASO/SEC acceptance status from the manufacturer.

Goal: learn to read a protection-relay catalogue the way a protection engineer reads it — starting from the plant's protection scheme (designed in the protection coordination section), mapping each protected object to the class of intelligent electronic device (IED) that protects it, and learning which catalogue parameters actually decide whether a relay fits: the ANSI function list, the CT requirements, the I/O count, the station-bus capability and the auxiliary supply. The four vendor families compared here — ABB Relion, Siemens SIPROTEC 5, SEL and GE Multilin — are the ones a field engineer in the Kingdom will meet most often. For how Part 6 catalogue sections are meant to be used, see the catalogue introduction.

What the Plant Needs — IEDs in This Protection Chain

A numerical IED is a small industrial computer that samples currents and voltages a few thousand times per second, runs protection algorithms in firmware, and closes a trip contact when an algorithm says so. One hardware box replaces a whole panel of the electromechanical relays it descends from — overcurrent, earth fault, thermal, under/overvoltage — and adds what no electromechanical relay could offer: event and disturbance records, self-supervision, communications, and settings you change from a laptop instead of a screwdriver. The protection philosophy for this plant is fixed in the protection coordination section; this section is about choosing the hardware that executes it.

Walk the single line from the SEC intake down and list every place a decision-making device sits. That list is the IED shopping list:

Location (tag)Protected objectIED class needed Core functions (indicative)
MV-SWGR incomer SEC 13.8 kV intake, board busbar (by extension) Feeder protection IED 50/51 phase overcurrent, 50N/51N earth fault graded with the NER let-through (400 A), 27/59 voltage, 81 frequency, breaker-failure, SEC intertrip interface.
MV-SWGR feeders Transformer feeders TX-1 / TX-2 (66.9 A MV FLC each) Feeder or transformer protection IED 50/51, 50N/51N, thermal image 49; optionally 87T transformer differential — the 1600 kVA trade-off is discussed in the selection sub-section.
LV-MSB incomers / bus-tie 400 V main switchboard, ~2309 A per transformer ACB electronic trip units (not separate IEDs) LSIG protection in the breaker itself; zone-selective interlocking between incomers, tie and outgoing ACBs — see the circuit-breaker tutorial.
MCC-1..n large motors LV motors above roughly 75 kW Motor protection IED 49 thermal model, 48/51LR locked rotor and prolonged start, 46 unbalance, 66 starts-per-hour, undercurrent for pumps.
MCC-1..n small motors Motors below ~75 kW Thermal overload relay or electronic motor manager Class 10/20 overload, phase loss; protection by switchgear components rather than full IEDs.
Where the boundary sits. In a plant this size the numerical-IED population is small — typically three to five devices on the MV board plus a handful of motor IEDs — because below the LV incomers the protection intelligence lives inside breaker trip units and motor starters. Do not let a vendor sell an IED for every LV feeder; the money is better spent on the few MV devices, where a wrong or slow trip takes out the entire plant.

One supporting actor appears throughout: the plant 110 V DC battery system (tag DCDB). Every IED and every breaker trip coil runs from it, precisely so that protection still works during the voltage collapse of a close-up fault. When reading relay catalogues, the auxiliary-supply range is therefore not a footnote — it is the second thing to check after the function list.

The Vendor Families You Will Meet

Four IED families dominate industrial MV protection in the Gulf. All are numerical, all are type-tested to the IEC 60255 series, all speak IEC 61850, and a specification written in ANSI-function and standards language will receive compliant offers from each. The differences that matter are engineering-tool ecosystems, local support, and which family the switchgear vendor pre-engineers into the LV compartment. Typical indicative positioning:

FamilyVendorMembers you will meet (indicative) Application coverageIEC 61850Arc-flash option Typical CT/VT inputs
Relion 615/620ABB REF615 / REF620 (feeder), RET620 (transformer), REM620 (motor) Full MV distribution set: feeder O/C + E/F, transformer differential, motor protection; standardised configurations make ordering simple. Native — GOOSE and MMS, edition 2; widely used for GOOSE-based blocking and interlocking schemes. Integrated arc-fault detection option (lens/fibre sensors into the same IED) — popular because no separate arc relay is needed. Typically 3–4 phase CTs + sensitive earth CT, 1–5 A selectable; VT inputs on -620 versions; sensor (Rogowski/divider) variants exist.
SIPROTEC 5 / CompactSiemens 7SJ8x (feeder O/C), 7UT8x (transformer differential), 7SK8x (motor), 7SX800 (compact class) Modular hardware — order the CPU and add measurement/I-O modules; one family scales from a simple feeder to a multi-winding differential. Native — GOOSE and MMS, strong process-bus (9-2) support in the larger frames. Arc protection via fast binary inputs and point sensors or a dedicated companion device, configured in the same DIGSI tool. 4–8 CT inputs depending on modules, 1/5 A software-selectable; VT modules optional; large binary I/O expansion.
SEL 700 seriesSEL (Schweitzer) SEL-751 (feeder), SEL-787 (transformer), SEL-710 (motor) North-American heritage — very strong at 60 Hz, ubiquitous in oil & gas and Aramco-orbit plants; renowned event reporting (SER and oscillography). Supported (GOOSE and MMS) alongside SEL's own MIRRORED BITS peer-to-peer protocol. Dedicated arc-flash option: point/loop fibre light sensors plus fast overcurrent supervision in the 751/710. Typically 3–4 phase + neutral CT inputs, 1/5 A per order code; optional VT card; conformal-coated boards standard — relevant in dusty climates.
Multilin 8 seriesGE Vernova 850 (feeder), 845 (transformer), 869 (motor) — successors to the 750/745/469 installed base Full distribution and machines set; the legacy Multilin 469/745 population in older Saudi plants makes like-for-like upgrades a common reason to meet this family. Native — GOOSE and MMS, edition 2. Arc-flash detection option with light sensors on the 8-series platform. 4–8 CT inputs, 1/5 A, optional VT bank; RTD modules (valuable for motor and transformer winding temperature).

Read the table as four answers to the same question. Any family can protect this plant; the deciding factors are: (1) which family the MV-SWGR vendor pre-wires by default (ABB boards arrive with Relion, Siemens with SIPROTEC — fighting that costs engineering hours); (2) which engineering tool your maintenance team already knows — PCM600, DIGSI 5, AcSELerator or EnerVista are each a training investment; (3) spares and support presence in the Kingdom; and (4) whether you want arc-flash detection integrated in the same box, which all four offer but price differently. Mixing families across one small board is legal but unwise — two engineering tools, two spare-relay types, two training courses, for three panels.

Standardise on one family per plant and keep one tested spare of each hardware variant on the shelf. A numerical IED fails safe (its watchdog contact alarms) but a feeder without protection cannot stay in service; with a spare and an exported settings file, exchange-and-restore is under an hour.

How to Read an IED Data Sheet

An IED data sheet is long because the device does many jobs, but only six blocks of it decide selection. Read them in this order.

1 — The ANSI function list

The first page is a grid of ANSI/IEEE device numbers — the shorthand the whole protection world shares. For this plant the functions that must appear are:

ANSIFunctionWhy this plant needs it
50 / 51Instantaneous / time overcurrent (phase) The workhorse: graded phase-fault protection on the incomer and both transformer feeders (grading study).
50N / 51NInstantaneous / time earth fault On a resistance-earthed 13.8 kV system the earth-fault current is capped by the NER at 400 A — phase elements never see it, so dedicated, sensitive earth elements are mandatory and must clear well inside the NER's 10 s rating (earthing section).
87TTransformer differential Optional at 1600 kVA — the trade-off is worked through in the selection sub-section.
27 / 59Under / overvoltage Detects SEC supply collapse or abnormal voltage; feeds the load-shed and DG-start logic.
81 U/OUnder / overfrequency 60 Hz grid disturbance detection; SEC connection requirements may dictate settings at the intake.
49, 48/51LR, 46, 66Thermal image, locked rotor, unbalance, starts-per-hour The motor-IED set for large motors on the MCCs.
50BFBreaker failure If a feeder breaker fails to clear, re-trip and trip the incomer — cheap insurance on a single-bus board.

2 — CT requirements

The relay is only as honest as its CTs. For plain overcurrent IEDs the catalogue asks for a protection-class core, typically 5P20 — at most 5 % composite error at 20 × rated current — with the rated burden matched to the wiring run. Differential protection (87T) is stricter: the data sheet specifies a minimum knee-point voltage (class PX/X or an equivalent 5P requirement) per winding, because the two CT sets must not saturate unequally during a heavy through-fault — unequal saturation manufactures a phantom differential current and trips a healthy transformer. If you choose 87T, the CT sizing calculation in the relay manual becomes a mandatory design deliverable, ordered with the switchgear: CT cores are the hardest thing to change after manufacture. Check also that the IED's rated input (1 A or 5 A, usually software-selectable) matches the CT secondaries, and that accuracy is declared at 60 Hz.

3 — Binary I/O count

Count what the scheme actually needs before reading the options table: trip and close outputs per breaker, breaker position (52a/52b) inputs, earth-switch position, trip-circuit supervision, gas/temperature inputs from the transformer (Buchholz-equivalent, winding temperature on the 1600 kVA units), SEC intertrip receive, watchdog out, and spare channels for the unforeseen. A base-model feeder IED ships with roughly 8–14 binary inputs and 5–10 output contacts; transformer and motor variants offer expansion modules. The classic error is ordering the base I/O and discovering at commissioning that trip-circuit supervision and the intertrip have consumed every spare — specify ~30 % spare I/O in the RFQ.

4 — Station bus: IEC 61850 and GOOSE

All four families support IEC 61850 — the substation communication standard — over an Ethernet station bus. Two services matter here. MMS reporting carries measurements, events and disturbance records up to the plant control system (DCS/PMS). GOOSE is the fast peer-to-peer service: a relay publishes a status change (typically in a few milliseconds) that other relays subscribe to. In this plant GOOSE (or hard wiring on a board this small) implements the blocking / zone-selective-interlocking scheme: a feeder IED that sees the fault tells the incomer IED "this one is mine — hold back", letting the incomer carry a fast setting for genuine bus faults without losing grading. The same mechanism carries arc-flash detection trips. Check the data sheet for: 61850 edition, GOOSE performance class, redundancy protocol on dual ports (PRP/HSR or RSTP), and whether the 61850 licence is included or a paid option — it is the most common hidden cost on the order.

5 — Auxiliary supply

Specify the plant standard 110 V DC from DCDB. Catalogue power-supply variants are wide-range (e.g. 48–250 V DC); make sure the variant ordered covers 110 V DC including the battery end-of-discharge voltage (around 88 V for a 110 V system), and that binary input thresholds are ordered for 110 V DC so a half-earthed trip wire cannot spuriously assert an input.

6 — Environment

Most IED data sheets state a continuous operating range of about −10 °C to +55 °C (storage wider). In this plant every IED lives in the conditioned MV/LV switchrooms, so the 50 °C site ambient is not a problem while the HVAC runs — but check the figure anyway, ask what is certified during an HVAC failure, and prefer conformally coated electronics (standard on some families, an option on others) as cheap insurance against dust and humidity ingress.

Key takeaway: select IEDs on six blocks — ANSI functions, CT requirements (5P20, knee-point for 87T), binary I/O with ~30 % spare, IEC 61850 GOOSE capability, 110 V DC auxiliary, and −10…+55 °C environment. Everything else in the data sheet is configuration, not selection.

Selection for This Plant — Mapped Picks

Translating the Plant Data Card and the protection scheme into device classes — family-level picks only, every line indicative and subject to the formal protection study:

DutyDevice class to specifyFamily examples Key catalogue checks
SEC incomer, MV-SWGR Feeder protection IED with voltage inputs REF615/REF620, 7SJ8x, SEL-751, Multilin 850 50/51 + 51N graded against the NER 400 A / 10 s let-through; 27/59/81 for SEC interface; 50BF; GOOSE for the blocking scheme; SEC protection requirements at the point of connection confirmed in writing.
Transformer feeders TX-1 / TX-2 Feeder IED; optionally transformer IED with 87T REF615 or RET620; 7SJ8x or 7UT8x; SEL-751 or SEL-787; 850 or 845 50/51 set above 66.9 A FLC with inrush margin; 51N; 49 thermal; winding-temperature inputs; see the 87T trade-off below.
LV incomers, bus-tie, large feeders on LV-MSB ACB electronic trip units (LSIG), ZSI-capable Per the LV switchboard family — see the breaker tutorial Coordination: the MV feeder IED curves must sit above the LV incomer trip-unit curves with clear daylight; ask both vendors for curves on a common 400 V base before settings are frozen.
Motors > ~75 kW on MCC-1..n Motor protection IED REM620, 7SK8x, SEL-710, Multilin 869 Thermal model tuned for 1800 rpm/60 Hz motors and 50 °C ambient via RTD inputs; starts-per-hour; undercurrent for pumps.
Smaller motors Electronic overload / motor manager in the starter Per MCC vendor Class 10/20 overload, phase-loss; communications to DCS optional.

The 87T question at 1600 kVA

Transformer differential protection (87T) compares current into the 13.8 kV winding with current out of the 0.4 kV winding; any imbalance beyond the bias characteristic means an internal fault, cleared in a few cycles with no grading delay. On large transformers it is non-negotiable. At 1600 kVA it is a judgement call, and the honest catalogue answer is that most plants this size do not fit it:

  • For 87T: fastest possible clearing of an internal fault limits tank damage and fire risk; with only two transformers, losing one for months of repair halves plant capacity (the bus-tie scenario in the SPOF study).
  • Against 87T: a dedicated transformer IED, a matched knee-point-rated CT set on both sides (the 2309 A LV side CT accommodation in the LV incomer is the awkward part), and engineering for inrush stabilisation — meaningful cost against a transformer this small. The conventional alternative — MV 50/51 with inrush-proof settings, 51N, plus the transformer's own devices (winding temperature, pressure relief, gas detection on sealed units) wired to trip — covers most internal failure modes a few hundred milliseconds slower.

Indicative recommendation for this plant: feeder IEDs with full transformer-device tripping, no 87T, unless the owner's insurer or reliability target says otherwise — and if 87T is chosen, order the CT cores with the switchgear and the LV board, not afterwards. Either way the decision must be written into the protection study, not left to the panel builder's default.

One scheme, one author. The incomer IED, feeder IEDs and LV trip units form a single grading ladder. If the MV relays are set by the switchgear vendor and the LV trip units by the switchboard vendor, nobody owns the gap between them — the classic result is an LV bus fault tripping the MV incomer first. One protection engineer must own the whole curve set from the SEC fuse-line down to the largest MCC starter (protection coordination).
Key takeaway: three to five well-chosen MV IEDs from one family, ZSI- capable LV trip units coordinated with them, motor IEDs only above ~75 kW, and a deliberate, documented yes/no on 87T — that is the entire relay purchase for a 2 MW plant. All picks indicative; the protection study governs.

Auxiliary Equipment (NER, SPD, PFC)

Indicative catalogue data. Product families and typical parameters are listed for training and familiarisation only, are approximate, and change with manufacturer revisions. No part numbers are given; always obtain current certified data sheets and SASO/SEC acceptance status from the manufacturer.

Goal: learn to read the catalogues of the three "quiet" equipment classes that make the main equipment safe and efficient: the neutral earthing resistor (tag NER) that tames earth-fault current, the surge protective devices that tame lightning and switching transients, and the power-factor-correction bank (tag PFC) that tames the reactive power bill. None of them does anything on a good day — which is exactly why their data sheets are misread more often than switchgear data sheets. For the engineering behind each, see earthing, lightning and surge protection and power factor correction.

Neutral Earthing Resistors — Reading an NER Data Sheet

The NER is conceptually the simplest item in the plant — a resistor between the 13.8 kV system neutral and earth — and its data sheet is correspondingly short. That brevity is a trap: every line on it is load-bearing. The plant's NER duty, from the Plant Data Card and the earthing section: 7.97 kV, 400 A, 10 s → 19.9 Ω.

Vendor families

FamilyVendorConstruction (typ.)Notes
Post GloverPost Glover Resistors Stainless-steel grid/edge-wound elements, ventilated steel enclosures; NGR-monitoring relay offered alongside Long-established NER specialist, common on North-American-influenced (60 Hz, oil & gas) projects in the region.
CressallCressall (EV resistor families) Stainless grid and wound elements; modular outdoor enclosures UK specialist, strong IEC project track record; desert-spec enclosures available.
Microelettrica / Metal Deployé Microelettrica Scientifica; Metal Deployé Resistances Punched stainless grids in galvanised or stainless enclosures Widely used by European switchgear OEMs; often arrive packaged with the MV board contract.

All serious NERs are stainless-steel grid resistors: punched plates or edge-wound strip in open frames, chosen because stainless alloy holds its resistance value and mechanical strength while being driven hundreds of kelvin upward in seconds. Cast iron and wire-wound alternatives exist at small ratings; for a 13.8 kV plant neutral, specify stainless grid and move on.

The five lines that matter on the data sheet

Data-sheet lineThis plant (indicative)How to read it
Rated voltage (line-to-neutral)7.97 kV System line-to-line voltage ÷ √3 = 13.8 kV / 1.732. This is the voltage the resistor sees during a full earth fault; insulation class and creepage follow from it.
Rated let-through current400 A The deliberate cap on earth-fault current — high enough for relays to see (51N grading), low enough to limit burning damage at the fault point. The whole earthing design hangs on this number.
Resistance19.9 Ω Simply 7967 V / 400 A. Verify the tolerance (typically ±10 %) and the stated reference temperature — resistance rises as the grids heat, which slightly reduces fault current; acceptable, but the relay study should use the cold value.
Time rating10 s The resistor carries 400 A for 10 s without exceeding its temperature-rise class, then needs to cool. Protection must clear earth faults well inside this — typically ≤1 s with backup inside 10 s. A continuous-rated NER (for systems that run faulted, per IEEE 32 extended ratings) is several times the size and price; do not pay for it unless the operating philosophy genuinely keeps a faulted system energised.
Temperature-rise classe.g. 760 K rise (short-time, per IEEE 32 practice) How hot the elements may run at the end of the rated time. Check the stated rise is on top of the 50 °C site ambient, not the standard 40 °C — a desert NER ends its 10 s hotter than the type-test assumption unless the vendor accounts for it.

Worked check — the data-sheet numbers agree with each other

Always close the triangle on an NER data sheet:

V_LN = 13.8 kV / √3 = 7.97 kV R = V_LN / I = 7967 V / 400 A = 19.9 Ω Energy per event ≈ I² × R × t = 400² × 19.9 × 10 s ≈ 31.8 MJ

Sanity check: 31.8 MJ is roughly the energy of 9 kWh — a domestic kettle running for four hours — dumped into a steel grid in ten seconds. That is why the enclosure is ventilated, why the time rating exists, and why the resistor must be allowed to cool before a second event. All figures indicative.

Enclosure, siting and monitoring

The NER lives outdoors next to the transformer or switchroom. Specify the enclosure for desert siting: hot-dip galvanised or stainless housing, IP23 minimum for a ventilated resistor (the elements must breathe; a sealed IP54 box defeats the cooling — instead use mesh-screened louvres, vermin- and sand-screened) and check the vendor's stance on wind-blown sand accumulation on the grids. Include an elevated plinth, padlockable access (the terminals sit at 7.97 kV during a fault) and a warning label stating the time rating.

Finally, the line most often missed: an NER can fail open-circuit — corroded joint, broken grid — and nothing visible happens until the next earth fault, when the system behaves as unearthed and healthy-phase voltages run wild. Specify an NGR monitoring relay (offered by the NER vendors themselves and as a function in some feeder IEDs): it injects a small sensing signal through the resistor continuously and alarms on open or shorted elements. Wire it to alarm at the DCS and, per the operating philosophy, to trip — see the earthing section for the system behaviour it protects against.

Surge Protection — MV Arresters and LV SPDs

Surge protection comes in two distinct catalogue worlds that must be read with two distinct vocabularies: metal-oxide surge arresters on the MV system (IEC 60099-4 language: Uc, Ur, energy class) and surge protective devices on the LV system (IEC 61643-11 language: Type 1/2/3, Iimp/In, Up). The coordination philosophy — why protection cascades from the service entrance inwards — is in the lightning and surge section; this is the catalogue view.

MV arresters at 13.8 kV

FamilyVendorTypical scope
POLIMABB Silicone-housed MO arresters, distribution through station class; the common choice packaged with ABB switchgear and transformers.
3EL / 3EKSiemens Silicone-housed MO arresters, distribution and medium/station duty.
Hubbell (Ohio Brass)Hubbell Power Systems Distribution and station arresters with a large 60 Hz utility installed base; frequent on SEC-side and overhead-line scopes.

Three catalogue decisions, in order:

(1) Class. Distribution class arresters (lower energy, lighter duty) protect overhead distribution equipment; station class (higher energy capability, better protective level) protect substations and major plant. For the plant intake and the 1600 kVA transformers, common industrial practice on a cable-fed intake is a heavy-duty distribution-class or small station-class arrester at the overhead-to-cable transition and/or transformer terminals — driven by whether the SEC feed arrives overhead (higher lightning exposure: go station class) or fully in cable (lower exposure). Confirm the supply arrangement with SEC before specifying.

(2) Uc / MCOV — the line everyone gets wrong. The continuous operating voltage the arrester can hold forever must exceed the maximum phase-to-earth voltage it will ever see continuously — and on this resistance-earthed system, during an earth fault the two healthy phases rise toward the full line-to-line voltage, 13.8 kV, for up to the NER's 10 s rating. The arrester must ride through that temporary overvoltage. The data sheet's TOV curve answers it: indicatively, a 13.8 kV resistance-earthed system lands on a rated voltage Ur around 15–18 kV with Uc/MCOV around 12.7–15.3 kV — noticeably higher than the ≈8 kV phase-earth voltage would suggest, precisely because of the earthing method. An arrester chosen as if for a solidly-earthed system will cook during the first sustained earth fault.

(3) Energy class and protective level. Check the nominal discharge current (10 kA class typical here), the energy/charge classification, and the residual voltage Ures at nominal discharge — which must sit comfortably below the equipment lightning withstand (95 kV BIL for the 17.5 kV-class switchgear, with margin per IEC 60071 insulation coordination). Housing: silicone, with creepage specified for the desert pollution class; mount with disconnectors and counters where visible indication is wanted.

LV SPDs at 400/230 V

FamilyVendorTypical scope
DEHN (DEHNventil / DEHNguard families)DEHN Full Type 1 / 2 / 3 portfolio including combined Type 1+2 spark-gap/MOV units; the reference brand in lightning-protection scopes.
OBO Bettermann V-seriesOBO Bettermann Type 1/2/3 portfolio, common in Gulf building and industrial contracts.
Phoenix Contact (FLASHTRAB / VALVETRAB) Phoenix Contact Type 1/2/3 plus the signal/instrumentation SPD ranges — useful for one-stop DCS loop protection.
PRD / PRF1 familiesSchneider Electric Type 1/2/3 integrated with the Schneider LV switchboard system — arrives pre-engineered when the LV boards are Schneider.

The LV catalogue logic is the Type 1 / 2 / 3 cascade (IEC 61643-11 test classes): Type 1 at the service entrance / LV-MSB, tested with the 10/350 µs impulse (Iimp, typically 12.5–25 kA per pole), absorbs partial lightning currents where the lightning protection system bonds to the power system; Type 2 at distribution boards (DB/EDB, MCC incomers), tested with 8/20 µs (In typically 20 kA), clips what passes the first stage; Type 3 at sensitive terminal equipment (UPS input, DCS cabinets), fine-clipping to a low protective voltage.
