Read off each data sheet: Uc ≥ 255 V for a 230 V phase-to-neutral TN-S system, the protective level Up (≤1.5 kV at terminal equipment is the usual target), the short-circuit withstand with the specified backup fuse, and the status contact for DCS alarming of a failed cartridge. Keep cable stubs short — the cascade only coordinates if the catalogue's separation-distance or energy-coordination rules between stages are followed (lightning section).

In TN-S, specify the connection scheme deliberately (4-pole, or "3+1" with a summary gap to PE) and order SPDs with remote-signalling contacts wired to the DCS — a surge arrester that died silently last thunderstorm protects nothing during the next one.

Power Factor Correction — Reading a Capacitor-Bank Catalogue

The plant target, from the Plant Data Card and the power-factor-correction section: raise 2.0 MW at pf 0.85 to pf 0.95 with ≈580 kvar of automatically switched, 7 % detuned capacitance on the 400 V bus (tag PFC). The catalogue task is to buy that as an engineered cubicle, not as a bag of capacitors.

Vendor families

FamilyVendorWhat it is (typ.)
VarSet (with PowerLogic controllers) Schneider Electric Engineered LV capacitor-bank cubicles, contactor- or thyristor-switched, detuned options, var controller included; integrates with Schneider LV boards.
QCap capacitors / DynacompABB Dry gas-filled capacitor elements (QCap) built into banks; Dynacomp is the thyristor-switched active/dynamic range for fast-changing loads.
FrakoFrako German PFC specialist — capacitors, detuned reactors, controllers and complete cubicles; common via panel builders.
EPCOS / TDKTDK Electronics Capacitor elements, reactors and controllers sold to panel builders — what is often inside a locally assembled Saudi bank; judge the panel builder's engineering, not just the component brand.

The detuned reactor — why 7 % and what it does to every other number

A plant full of VFDs (tag VFD) injects harmonic currents. A plain capacitor bank can resonate with the supply-transformer inductance near one of those harmonics, amplifying it until capacitors fail and fuses scatter. The fix is a series detuning reactor in each step, sized so that reactor + capacitor are series-resonant below the lowest significant harmonic — the branch then looks inductive to the 5th harmonic and above and cannot resonate with them. The detuning factor p = 7 % means the reactor's reactance is 7 % of the capacitor's at fundamental.

Worked check — tuning frequency and capacitor voltage at p = 7 %

Series tuning frequency on a 60 Hz system:

f_res = f_n / √p = 60 Hz / √0.07 = 60 / 0.2646 ≈ 227 Hz

Sanity check: 227 Hz sits below the 5th harmonic (300 Hz at 60 Hz) and clear of the 3rd (180 Hz) — the branch is inductive at every harmonic a 6-pulse VFD produces. The reactor also raises the fundamental voltage across the capacitor terminals:

U_cap = U_network / (1 − p) = 400 V / 0.93 ≈ 430 V

so the capacitors inside a 7 % detuned 400 V bank must be rated typically 440–480 V, not 400 V. A catalogue that offers 400 V-rated capacitors behind a 7 % reactor is offering early failures. All figures indicative.

kvar at rated voltage versus kvar at network voltage

The most common PFC ordering error follows directly: capacitor output scales with voltage squared,

Q_at_network = Q_rated × (U_network / U_rated)²

so a "100 kvar, 480 V" capacitor delivers only 100 × (400/480)² ≈ 69 kvar on a 400 V network before the reactor effect is added back. Reputable catalogues quote the effective kvar of the complete detuned step at network voltage — always confirm that the ≈580 kvar plant target is specified on that basis, in writing, and let the vendor pick element ratings to deliver it.

The rest of the data sheet

ParameterWhat to demand (indicative)
Bank size and stepping ≈580 kvar effective at 400 V / 60 Hz, in steps (e.g. 12 × 50 or a 25/50/100 mix) so the controller can track load without hunting; target pf 0.95 set on the var controller.
Detuning p = 7 % (tuned ≈227 Hz at 60 Hz); reactors with linear current characteristic and thermal cutouts wired to trip the step.
Harmonic current capability Declared permissible harmonic loading of capacitors and reactors against the plant's measured/estimated VFD spectrum; overcurrent margin typically 1.3–1.5 × In for the capacitor elements.
Switching technology Contactor-switched (with pre-charge damping contacts) is standard and fine for slowly varying process load; thyristor-switched / active (e.g. Dynacomp class) responds in cycles instead of seconds — worth it only for fast-cycling loads (welders, crushers, hoists). Choose on load dynamics, not brochure appeal.
Safety features Self-healing dry or gas-filled elements with overpressure disconnectors, discharge resistors to ≤75 V in 3 min (or fast-discharge for thyristor banks), HRC fusing or breaker per step.
Thermal design at 50 °C The killer line in the desert: capacitor life roughly halves per ~7 K over-temperature, and detuning reactors are genuine heaters inside the cubicle. Demand the cubicle's certified rating at 50 °C ambient — forced ventilation sized by the vendor, temperature supervision tripping steps, reactors in a separated/ventilated compartment — or install in the conditioned switchroom and say so in the RFQ.
Controller Var controller with pf target, step-wear rotation, harmonic (THD-V) alarm, and comms to the DCS/PMS so a dead bank raises a flag before the SEC bill does.
Key takeaway: specify the PFC bank by effective kvar at 400 V / 60 Hz with 7 % detuning and a 50 °C certified cubicle — ≈580 kvar for this plant — and let the vendor engineer element voltages and step sizes to deliver it. Banks bought as raw capacitor kvar at rated voltage chronically under-deliver by 20–30 %.

Saudi-Specific Practice — SASO/SABER, Spares and Support

Conformity paperwork

Everything in this fragment — IEDs, NER, arresters, SPDs, capacitor banks — is imported electrical equipment and therefore passes through the SASO conformity regime via the SABER platform: certificates of conformity against the SASO-adopted IEC standards (IEC 60255 series for relays, IEEE 32 / IEC practice for the NER, IEC 60099-4 for arresters, IEC 61643-11 for SPDs, IEC 60831/60931 for LV capacitors), plus SEC acceptance where the device sits at or interacts with the utility interface — notably the incomer IED functions and settings, which SEC reviews against the Distribution Code. Make the vendor responsible in the purchase order for SABER registration and certificate delivery; the auxiliary equipment is exactly the category that gets forgotten until it is sitting in customs while the switchgear it serves is already on its plinth. SBC 401 applies to the LV installation side (SPD provision and earthing practice in particular).

Spares philosophy

These are low-population, long-lead items; the spares logic is different from bulk consumables:

  • IEDs: one spare unit per hardware variant (not per installed relay), kept with current firmware and the plant settings files exported and version- controlled. A relay model is typically manufactured for a decade and supported for another; plan a mid-life fleet review rather than ad-hoc replacements.
  • NER: spare grid elements and joint hardware rather than a whole spare resistor; the enclosure and frame outlive the plant. Keep the NGR-monitor spare with the IED stock.
  • Arresters / SPDs: arresters are not repairable — hold one spare set per class; for LV SPDs hold plug-in cartridges per type, and expect to consume them: a cartridge that sacrificed itself in a storm did its job.
  • PFC: capacitor elements are wear parts at 50 °C — hold one or two step's worth of elements, step fuses and a spare switching contactor (contactor contacts wear fastest of all); record step capacitance annually and trend it, replacing elements that drift more than ~5 % down.

Vendor support in the Kingdom

All families named here are represented in Saudi Arabia, but with different depth: the switchgear-OEM relay families (ABB, Siemens, GE, Schneider) come with the OEM's Saudi service organisation; SEL runs regional protection support strong in the oil & gas corridor; NER, arrester and SPD specialists typically work through local agents — so name the local agent and the firmware/settings support arrangement in the bid evaluation, not after award. For IEDs, ask explicitly: who in-Kingdom can reconfigure the relays and run secondary injection, and within how many days? A protection scheme nobody local can maintain decays into "do not touch" folklore within five years.

  • One IED family selected for the MV board; settings files exported, version-controlled and one tested spare per hardware variant on the shelf.
  • Incomer IED 51N grading checked against the NER 400 A / 10 s let-through; clearing well inside 10 s (protection study).
  • 87T decision (fit / omit at 1600 kVA) documented in the protection study; if fitted, knee-point CT cores ordered with the switchgear and LV board.
  • NER data sheet triangle verified: 7.97 kV / 400 A / 19.9 Ω / 10 s; ventilated desert-spec enclosure; NGR monitoring relay wired to DCS.
  • MV arrester Uc/TOV checked against the 10 s healthy-phase rise of the resistance-earthed 13.8 kV system, not against phase-earth voltage alone.
  • LV SPD Type 1/2/3 cascade located per the zone plan with status contacts to DCS (lightning section).
  • PFC bank specified as ≈580 kvar effective at 400 V / 60 Hz, 7 % detuned (≈227 Hz), cubicle certified at 50 °C; SABER/SASO certificates for all items in the dossier before shipment.
Closing the loop. Everything in this catalogue section is indicative training data. The binding chain for the real purchases runs through the formal studies — protection coordination, earthing, insulation coordination and the power-quality/PFC study — then approved data sheets, vendor certified documents, FAT and SEC acceptance. The catalogue's job is only to make you fluent for that conversation.
Note: These appendices are quick-reference and printable tools — concise distillations of the manual's technical content intended for use in the field and at the desk. All figures are indicative; confirm against plant-specific data and the governing standards before acting on them.

A — Glossary of Terms and Abbreviations

Alphabetical table of abbreviations and terms used across this manual. Expand from the course overview glossary and includes terms introduced in Parts 4 and 5. All voltage and frequency references follow the Saudi (SEC) Rev 3 basis of this manual (13.8 kV MV, 400/230 V LV, 60 Hz, 50 °C design ambient), with standards cited as SASO-adopted IEC plus the SEC Distribution Code / Saudi Grid Code and SBC 401.

Term / Abbreviation Meaning and context in this manual
ACBAir Circuit Breaker. Large LV breaker used for switchboard incomers and the bus-tie on LV-MSB. Ratings typically 1600–6300 A; short-time withstand up to 65–100 kA (1 s).
AFBArc-Flash Boundary. The working distance at which incident energy falls to 1.2 cal/cm² (the onset-of-a-second-degree-burn threshold). Determined per IEEE 1584-2018.
ANSIAmerican National Standards Institute. Device numbering scheme (e.g. 50/51, 87T) used on SLD relay/breaker labels as industry convention alongside IEC.
ATSAutomatic Transfer Switch. Monitors the utility supply and, on failure, automatically transfers the essential bus to the standby DG. A failed-to-transfer ATS is one of the highest-RPN items in a typical FMECA.
Availability (A)A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR). The fraction of calendar time a component or system is in its functioning state. Expressed as a decimal (e.g. 0.9992) or as annual downtime hours.
AVRAutomatic Voltage Regulator. Controls the excitation of the diesel generator alternator to maintain output voltage within limits during load changes.
BESSBattery Energy Storage System. Grid-connected or plant-connected battery system providing ride-through energy, inertia emulation, fast frequency / voltage support, or islanding capability.
BT (or B/T)Bus-Tie. The normally-open ACB (or contactor) connecting Bus A and Bus B of the LV-MSB split busbar. Closes automatically on loss of one incomer to allow the healthy transformer to back-feed the dead bus. A critical SPOF-mitigation element.
Cable ampacityThe continuous current-carrying capacity of a cable under defined installation conditions (temperature, grouping, burial). Determined from IEC 60364 / manufacturer tables after applying derating factors.
CT / VTCurrent Transformer / Voltage Transformer. Instrument transformers that step down MV/LV currents and voltages to safe measurement levels (e.g. 5 A / 110 V secondary). Feed protection relays, metering and SCADA.
DBDistribution Board. Final-circuit LV panel fed from the LV-MSB or an MCC; supplies lighting, small power and HVAC loads via MCBs/RCBOs.
DCDBDC Distribution Board (battery and charger system). Provides 110 V DC (typical) for MV switchgear trip/close coils and protection relay auxiliary supplies. A DCDB failure silences protection — very high severity in the FMECA.
DCSDistributed Control System. Plant-wide process control system with operator HMI. Receives electrical status (breaker position, voltages, currents) and issues high-level electrical commands.
Detuned filterA series reactor added to each PFC capacitor step so the branch is tuned below the lowest characteristic harmonic (7 % reactor → tuning frequency f/√0.07 ≈ 227 Hz on a 60 Hz system, safely below the 5th harmonic at 300 Hz). Prevents harmonic resonance between the capacitor bank and the supply impedance in VFD-rich plants. The plant PFC bank is 7 % detuned.
DGDiesel Generator set. Standby power source sized for the essential-load bus (1250 kVA indicative, ESP rating at 50 °C ambient). Includes AVR, governor, fuel day-tank, battery starting and acoustic enclosure.
DOLDirect-On-Line. Simplest motor starting method — motor connected straight to supply. Starting current typically 5–7 × FLC; suitable for motors up to ~7.5 kW or where the bus can absorb the inrush.
DVRDynamic Voltage Restorer. Series power-electronics device injecting a compensating voltage to protect plant loads against upstream voltage sags and swells.
EDBEssential Distribution Board. Fed via the ATS from the DG (or utility essential bus). Supplies safety-critical loads that must remain energised during a utility outage.
ESP / PRP / COPISO 8528-1 genset duty ratings. ESP — Emergency Standby Power (variable load, limited hours/yr, no sustained overload); PRP — Prime Rated Power (variable load, unlimited hours, 10 % overload 1 h in 12); COP — Continuous Operating Power (constant base load). The plant DG is rated 1250 kVA ESP at 50 °C (indicative). Always read the rating with its ambient — Saudi summer temperatures derate engine output, so a nameplate quoted at 25 °C is optimistic here.
FMECAFailure Mode, Effects and Criticality Analysis. Structured IEC 60812 method: list failure modes for each component, assess effects on the system, score Severity (S), Occurrence (O), Detectability (D), compute RPN = S × O × D, and rank for action. The primary SPOF quantification tool in this campaign.
FLCFull-Load Current. The rated continuous current at full load: I = S / (√3 · V) for 3-phase. Basis for breaker sizing, cable selection and protection relay pickup settings.
GOOSEGeneric Object-Oriented Substation Event. IEC 61850 fast peer-to-peer message over the station bus used for inter-IED tripping, bus-transfer logic and load-shedding. Latency typically <4 ms.
IACInternal Arc Classification per IEC 62271-200 (e.g. IAC AFLR 25 kA 1 s). States which sides of an MV switchgear panel (Front, Lateral, Rear) protect personnel against an internal arc of stated current and duration. Check the IAC rating plate on MV-SWGR during the site visit.
IDMTInverse Definite Minimum Time. Overcurrent relay characteristic (IEC 60255-151 curves: SI, VI, EI) where operating time decreases as fault current increases. The workhorse 51 element used for time-current grading between MV and LV devices in this plant.
IEDIntelligent Electronic Device. Numerical multifunction protection relay performing overcurrent (50/51), earth-fault (50N/51N), differential (87T), voltage (27/59) and other protection functions, plus measurement and control.
Incident energyThe thermal energy per unit area (cal/cm²) deposited on a worker at the working distance during an arc-flash event, computed per IEEE 1584-2018 from bolted fault current, clearing time, gap and enclosure geometry. Sets the arc-rated PPE category and the label on LV-MSB / MCC doors. 1.2 cal/cm² is the onset-of-second-degree-burn threshold.
IscShort-Circuit Current (symmetrical). The prospective fault current at a bus under bolted three-phase conditions. Used to specify the breaking capacity (Icu) of switchgear. Indicative this plant: ~38.5 kA per 1600 kVA, Z = 6 % transformer at 400 V; ~77 kA with the bus-tie closed (both TX paralleled — a condition the interlock must prevent); 25 kA design at 13.8 kV (SEC range 25–40 kA).
LPLLightning Protection Level. Classes I–IV per IEC 62305, determined by risk assessment. Sets air-termination geometry, down-conductor spacing and earth-electrode requirements.
LPSLightning Protection System. The complete system of air terminations, down conductors and earth electrodes protecting the plant structure and equipment against direct lightning strikes.
LVLow Voltage. Utilisation voltage; in this manual 400/230 V, 3-phase, 60 Hz per the SEC LV standard (IEC: ≤ 1 kV AC). Confirm per plant.
LVRT / UVRTLow / Under Voltage Ride-Through. The capability or protection settings that allow plant equipment (particularly VFD-driven loads) to remain connected through a defined voltage dip rather than tripping and exacerbating the disturbance.
MCCMotor Control Centre. LV assembly of motor starter bays (DOL, star-delta, soft-starter, VFD) feeding process motors. Tags MCC-1 … MCC-n per the plant scheme.
MCCBMoulded-Case Circuit Breaker. Compact LV protective device for outgoing feeders and motor circuits. Breaking capacity ratings from ~10 kA to 85 kA; motor-protection versions incorporate thermal/magnetic or electronic trip units.
MMSManufacturing Message Specification. IEC 61850 protocol for client–server monitoring, control and data exchange between IEDs and SCADA/EMS. Complements GOOSE for non-time-critical data.
MTBFMean Time Between Failures. MTBF = 1/λ (h). Average operating time between successive failures. Higher MTBF = lower failure rate = more reliable. See Appendix G for indicative values.
MTTRMean Time To Repair / Restore. Average time from failure occurrence to return to service (detection + logistics + repair + recommissioning). MTTR is the key lever that automation (auto bus-tie, ATS) acts on — reducing it from hours (manual) to seconds (auto-transfer) dramatically raises availability.
MVMedium Voltage. Higher distribution voltage; in this manual 13.8 kV — the standard SEC industrial distribution voltage (IEC: 1 kV < MV ≤ 36 kV; equipment is 17.5 kV class). Actual utility voltage must be confirmed per plant.
MV-METMV Utility Revenue Metering Cubicle. Contains utility-grade CTs/VTs and the tariff meter at the point of supply. Not a protection device — verify its presence and metering-point location during desk study.
MV-NERNeutral Earthing Resistor. Connected between the MV transformer neutral and earth to limit earth-fault current (400 A in this plant) while keeping faults detectable. R = 7.97 kV (13.8 kV / √3) ÷ 400 A ≈ 19.9 Ω, rated 10 s (indicative).
MV-SWGRMV Metal-Clad (or metal-enclosed) Switchgear. 13.8 kV bus (17.5 kV class) with vacuum circuit breakers; incomer, bus-section and transformer-feeder panels. Rated above the prospective fault level (indicative: 31.5 kA / 3 s withstand against a 25 kA design fault; SEC quotes 25–40 kA at the point of supply).
N+1 / N-1N+1: one more capacity unit than the minimum required — so one can be lost without losing the function. N-1: the condition after one unit has failed; the system must still serve the essential load. The 2 × 1600 kVA + bus-tie arrangement is N-1 secure for essential load (~55 % of total).
NERNeutral Earthing Resistor — see MV-NER. (US literature: NGR, Neutral Grounding Resistor.)
NER / NGR monitorA monitoring relay that continuously verifies the neutral earthing resistor path is intact (neither open-circuited nor shorted) by injecting a small sensing signal. An open NER is a latent failure: the system silently becomes unearthed and the first earth fault is then neither limited nor reliably detected. Common practice on resistance-earthed MV systems; a low-cost, high-value FMECA mitigation for MV-NER.
PCCPower Control Centre. The main LV switchboard that distributes power to MCCs and outgoing feeders. In this manual, LV-MSB is the PCC. Distinct from an MCC (which only controls motors).
PFCPower Factor Correction. Automatic capacitor bank (with 7 % detuned reactors to handle VFD harmonics) that raises plant power factor from ~0.85 to ≥ 0.95, reducing reactive demand and transformer / cable loading.
PMSPower Management System. Dedicated electrical controller responsible for: load shedding on generation failure, automatic bus transfer (bus-tie / ATS), generator synchronisation, and energy management. Interfaces with the DCS.
PPEPersonal Protective Equipment. Arc-rated PPE is required for work on or near energised electrical equipment; the required category is set by the arc-flash study (incident energy in cal/cm²). Non-intrusive site surveys in this campaign do not require arc-rated PPE if live-panel access is not needed — but confirm with the host's QHSE procedure.
QHSEQuality, Health, Safety and Environment. Management system framework (ISO 9001 / 14001 / 45001) applied to all site activities. All surveys in this campaign are non-intrusive; QHSE and permit-to-work rules of the host facility always take precedence.
RAMReliability, Availability, Maintainability. The quantitative study of system uptime: how often failures occur (R), what fraction of time the system works (A), and how quickly it is restored (M). See Part 3 — FMECA / RAM.
RBDReliability Block Diagram. Series / parallel model used to compute system availability from component data. Series elements degrade availability; parallel (redundant) paths multiply it up. See Part 3.
Ride-throughThe ability of equipment (VFDs, contactors, UPS-fed controls) to stay operational through a short supply disturbance — a voltage dip, phase jump or brief interruption — instead of tripping. Achieved by kinetic buffering in drives, DC-bus hold-up, delayed undervoltage settings, and UPS support. The Saudi Grid Code imposes fault ride-through duties on generating plant; inside the factory fence, ride-through settings determine whether a 200 ms utility dip becomes a full process trip.
ROCOFRate of Change of Frequency. Measure of frequency volatility on the grid (Hz/s). Increases as synchronous generation (with inertia) is displaced by inverter-based renewables. High ROCOF can trip plant protection (df/dt relays) prematurely.
RPNRisk Priority Number. FMECA criticality score = Severity (S) × Occurrence (O) × Detection (D), each scored 1–5 (higher = worse outcome / more frequent / harder to detect). RPN max = 125. Used to rank FMECA findings for action priority. Priority bands: P1 = SPOF with S ≥ 4 or RPN ≥ 60; P2 = RPN 36–59; P3 = RPN 16–35; P4 < 16. See Appendix C.
SABERSaudi Arabia's electronic conformity-assessment platform, operated under SASO. Regulated products (including much electrical equipment) require SABER product and shipment Certificates of Conformity before customs clearance. For the field engineer: missing SABER status is a procurement red flag, not a site finding. See Appendix H.
SASOSaudi Standards, Metrology and Quality Organization. The Kingdom's national standards body; adopts IEC standards (usually verbatim or with national deviations) as SASO IEC standards and operates the SABER conformity scheme. In this manual "SASO-adopted IEC" means: apply the IEC technical content, cited through its Saudi adoption. See Appendix H.
Saudi Grid CodeThe technical code governing connection to and operation of the Saudi transmission system (with the Distribution Code covering distribution-connected users such as this plant). Sets frequency/voltage operating ranges, fault ride-through, reactive and power-factor obligations, protection and metering requirements at the point of common coupling.
SBC 401Saudi Building Code — Electrical (SBC 401). The Kingdom's statutory electrical installation code (technically based on NFPA 70/NEC with Saudi amendments), enforced through municipal permitting. Coexists with SASO-adopted IEC design practice on industrial plants; where requirements differ, the contractually specified code governs — flag conflicts, do not silently pick one.
SCADASupervisory Control and Data Acquisition. Supervisory monitoring and control layer for the power system; aggregates IED data, trends energy, and provides alarm management to operators.
SECSaudi Electricity Company. The Kingdom's principal electricity utility and the supply authority assumed in this manual: 13.8 kV, 60 Hz point of supply, fault level 25–40 kA, connection per the SEC Distribution Code and SEC company standards (metering, protection interface, earthing at the intake). The utility intake arrangement (MV-MET, incomer protection) must be agreed with SEC.
SLDSingle-Line Diagram. One-line schematic showing the power system's main components and their electrical connections, using IEC 60617 symbols. The primary document for SLD verification on site and for SPOF hunting.
SPD (Type 1/2/3)Surge Protective Device per IEC 61643-11. Type 1 (tested with 10/350 µs impulse) at the service entrance / LV-MSB where lightning current can enter; Type 2 (8/20 µs) at distribution boards; Type 3 close to sensitive terminal equipment. Installed as a coordinated cascade with status contacts wired to the DCS. Essential in the Saudi coastal lightning environment.
SPOFSingle Point of Failure. A component or path whose failure alone disables a function or supply that must remain available. The central concept of this assessment campaign. See Part 3 and Appendix F.
STATCOM / SVCStatic Synchronous Compensator / Static VAr Compensator. Fast dynamic reactive-power devices used for voltage stabilisation and flicker / voltage-sag mitigation on weak or renewable-heavy grids.
TN-STN-S earthing system. IEC 60364 LV earthing arrangement where the neutral (N) and protective earth (PE) conductors are kept separate throughout. Used at 400 V LV in this plant.
TX-1 / TX-2MV/LV power transformers 1 and 2. Each indicatively 1600 kVA, 13.8 / 0.4 kV, Dyn11, Z ≈ 6 % (MV FLC 66.9 A, LV FLC 2309 A), feeding the split LV-MSB busbar. Both together provide N-1 redundancy via the bus-tie.
UPSUninterruptible Power Supply. Battery-backed, no-break clean AC supply for the DCS/PLC/SCADA, field instrumentation and critical communications. This plant: 100 kVA / 30 min autonomy, online double-conversion (VFI), indicative — confirm per plant.
VFD / VSDVariable Frequency Drive / Variable Speed Drive. Power-electronic converter providing continuously variable speed control of AC motors (pumps, fans, compressors). Reduces starting current to near FLC; generates harmonics requiring detuned PFC.
VFIVoltage and Frequency Independent — the IEC 62040-3 classification (VFI-SS-111) of a true online double-conversion UPS: output voltage and frequency are independent of input disturbances because power is always rectified and re-inverted. The plant UPS (100 kVA / 30 min indicative) is specified VFI. Contrast VI (line-interactive) and VFD-class (off-line) topologies, which pass some disturbances through.
WCRWithstand Current Rating (also rated short-time withstand, Icw). The RMS current a bus, switchboard or transfer switch can carry for a stated time (e.g. 65 kA / 1 s) without damage, while protection clears the fault. For the ATS, the WCR must be verified against the available fault current and the upstream device's clearing time — an ATS with a 3-cycle WCR behind a 0.3 s graded breaker is mis-applied.
ZSIZone-Selective Interlocking. Communication scheme between LV ACBs and MCCBs that allows the faulted zone's device to trip instantaneously while upstream devices remain on their normal graded delay — achieving both selectivity and fast arc-flash clearance.
λ (lambda)Failure rate (failures per hour). λ = 1/MTBF. The primary input to reliability calculations. See Appendix G for indicative values per equipment class.
Key takeaway: 61 terms defined, including the Rev 3 Saudi regulatory vocabulary (SEC, SASO, SABER, SBC 401, Saudi Grid Code) and genset/UPS rating language (ESP/PRP/COP, VFI, WCR). Use this table when reading the manual in the field or when completing FMECA and report forms. Cross-references to the relevant manual sections are embedded in key definitions.

B — IEC / IEEE Standards Reference

The table below lists the principal standards cited across this manual and the reference training material. The "Where it applies" column states the specific use in an MV/LV process-plant SPOF assessment context. This manual is standalone — the descriptions below are sufficient context; you do not need to hold copies of these standards in the field, but you should be aware of their scope.

Standard Title / Scope Where it applies in this manual
IEC 60034 (series) Rotating electrical machines — general requirements and ratings for AC motors. Motor nameplate data verification; LV-vs-MV feed threshold (~150–200 kW); MCC starter selection.
IEC 60076 (series) Power transformers — ratings, losses, impedance, tap changers, test methods. Transformer rating and Z% verification (TX-1/TX-2); N-1 loading check; fault-level calculation input.
IEC 60364 (series) Low-voltage electrical installations — design, protection, cable selection, earthing (TN-S, TT, IT), derating rules. Cable sizing and derating (§6, Module 2); LV TN-S earthing; distribution board design. Applied to LV-MSB, MCC, DB/EDB.
IEC 60812 Analysis techniques for system reliability — FMEA / FMECA procedure, including criticality analysis and RPN scoring. The FMECA form (Appendix E, Form 2) follows IEC 60812 column structure. All FMECA worksheets in the campaign use this method. See Part 3 — FMECA.
IEC 60831 Shunt power capacitors for AC power systems — capacitor bank ratings, protection. PFC bank specification and protection (PFC tag); detuned reactor sizing for VFD harmonic environment.
IEC 60909 Short-circuit currents in three-phase AC systems — calculation method for prospective fault currents. Short-circuit study (§4, Module 2). Used with power-system software (ETAP / DIgSILENT) to size breaking capacity of LV-MSB, MCC and all ACB/MCCB.
IEC 60947-2 Low-voltage switchgear and controlgear — circuit breakers (ACB and MCCB ratings, breaking capacity Icu/Ics, trip-unit types). ACB / MCCB specification for LV-MSB incomers, bus-tie and outgoing feeders; breaking-capacity verification against fault level from IEC 60909 study.
IEC 61078 Reliability block diagrams — construction rules, series/parallel formulae, system availability computation. RBD analysis in Module 5 and Part 3; quantifying availability benefit of N+1 transformer + bus-tie redundancy.
IEC 61439-1/-2 Low-voltage switchgear assemblies — type-tested assemblies: Form segregation, busbar bracing, temperature rise, short-circuit withstand. Specification and verification of LV-MSB (Form 4b) and MCC assemblies. Check form segregation during site visit.
IEC 61800 (series) Adjustable speed electrical power drive systems — ratings, EMC, protection, safety. VFD specification; harmonic emission from VFD drives (input to PFC detuning requirement).
IEC 61850 Communication networks and systems for power utility automation — station bus, GOOSE (fast tripping/transfer), MMS (monitoring). Defines data models for IED interoperability. PMS integration; GOOSE-based fast bus-transfer and load shedding (Module 4). Present on digital substations; check whether IEC 61850 is deployed during desk study.
IEC 62271-100 High-voltage AC circuit breakers — ratings, making/breaking capacity, tests. MV circuit breaker specification (vacuum CBs in MV-SWGR).
IEC 62271-200 AC metal-enclosed switchgear and controlgear for rated voltages above 1 kV and up to and including 52 kV — construction, type testing, internal arc classification (IAC). Specification and condition assessment of MV-SWGR during site visit; IAC rating relevant to arc-flash risk at MV level.
IEC 62305 (series) Protection against lightning — risk assessment (Part 2), physical damage (Part 3), electrical and electronic systems (Part 4). Lightning protection assessment (§12, Module 2); LPS class selection; bonding to earth grid. Applies to all plant buildings and outdoor structures.
IEEE 80 Guide for safety in AC substation grounding — touch voltage, step voltage, earth grid design. Earth-grid design and touch/step voltage compliance (§12, Module 2); MV substation earthing verification.
IEEE 493 Recommended practice for the design of reliable industrial and commercial power systems ("Gold Book") — reliability data, design methods, redundancy principles, RBD analysis. Primary source of indicative equipment failure-rate and MTBF data (see Appendix G). Referenced in Module 5 (FMECA & RAM) and the FMECA form.
IEEE 1584 Guide for performing arc-flash hazard calculations — empirical model for incident energy and arc-flash boundary at AC equipment. Arc-flash / incident-energy study (§11, Module 2). Preferable (not required) in this campaign. Determines PPE category and arc-flash boundary at LV-MSB and MCC.
ISO 14224 Petroleum, petrochemical and natural gas industries — collection and exchange of reliability and maintenance data for equipment. Defines equipment taxonomy, failure modes and data formats. Equipment taxonomy for populating the FMECA and Document Inventory (SF-01). Cross-referenced for failure-rate data alongside IEEE 493.
ISO 20815 Petroleum, petrochemical and natural gas industries — production assurance and reliability technology. RAM / RBD availability analysis methodology (Module 5); preferable study deliverable in this campaign.
NFPA 70E Standard for Electrical Safety in the Workplace (USA/ANSI). Arc-flash PPE categories, boundary definitions, energised work permits. Arc-flash PPE selection and labelling (§11, Module 2); host QHSE requirements may reference NFPA 70E even in IEC-governed plants. Non-intrusive surveys should not trigger NFPA 70E energised-work requirements.
Note — Saudi (Rev 3) application: Standard numbers and editions are indicative; always confirm the governing edition with the project specification and the local regulatory authority. In Saudi Arabia the IEC standards above are applied through their SASO adoptions, alongside the SEC Distribution Code / Saudi Grid Code (connection, metering, protection interface at the 13.8 kV point of supply) and SBC 401 (statutory installation code). See Appendix H — SASO / SEC Standards Map for the topic-by-topic mapping.
Key takeaway: 20 standards referenced. IEC 60812 (FMECA), IEC 60909 (fault level), IEC 62271-200 (MV switchgear) and IEEE 493 (reliability data) are the four you will use most in this campaign.

C — Formula Reference Card

Consolidated printable reference of every key electrical calculation formula used in this manual. For worked examples and derivations see Part 2 — Calculations. All quantities are SI; constants: √3 = 1.732, 1 pu = 100 %. All worked numbers are on the Rev 3 Saudi basis: 13.8 kV / 400-230 V, 60 Hz (plant 2.0 MW at pf 0.85 → 2353 kVA; 2 × 1600 kVA, Z = 6 %). Frequency-dependent quantities (reactance, motor speed, capacitor output) are evaluated at f = 60 Hz — do not reuse 60 Hz table values without correction. All values are indicative.

Quantity Formula Symbols Indicative example (this plant)
Apparent power S = P / pf S: apparent power (kVA/MVA); P: active power (kW/MW); pf: power factor. 2.0 MW / 0.85 = 2.35 MVA
3-phase current (from S) I = S / (√3 × V) I: line current (A); S: apparent power (VA); V: line-to-line voltage (V). 1600 kVA @ 400 V → 2309 A (TX LV FLC); 1600 kVA @ 13.8 kV → 66.9 A (TX MV FLC); 2353 kVA @ 13.8 kV → 98.4 A (plant total MV)
Motor full-load current I_fl = P / (√3 × V × pf × η) P: shaft power (W); V: line voltage (V); pf: motor pf; η: efficiency. 110 kW, 400 V, pf 0.86, η 0.95 → 194 A
DOL starting current I_start ≈ 6–7 × I_fl Use 6.5 × as indicative; confirm with motor manufacturer's data. 6.5 × 194 A = 1260 A
Motor synchronous speed N_s = 120 × f / p N_s: synchronous speed (rpm); f: frequency (Hz); p: number of poles. Actual speed = N_s − slip. At 60 Hz all speeds are 20 % higher than the 60 Hz equivalents. 4-pole @ 60 Hz → 1800 rpm (2-pole 3600, 6-pole 1200); same motor at 60 Hz would run 1500 rpm
Inductive reactance X = 2π × f × L X: reactance (Ω); f: frequency (Hz); L: inductance (H). At 60 Hz, reactances are 60/50 = 1.2 × the 60 Hz values — affects voltage drop, fault X/R and cable tables. L = 0.35 mH → X = 2π × 60 × 0.00035 = 0.132 Ω (vs 0.110 Ω at 60 Hz)
Transformer loading (pu) Loading = S_load / S_rated Values > 1.0 pu indicate overload; N-1 loading must be ≤ 1.0 pu (continuous). 1.175 / 1.600 = 0.73 pu (normal); 1.29 / 1.60 = 0.81 pu (N-1)
Short-circuit current I_sc = I_fl / Z(pu) I_fl: transformer LV FLC (A); Z(pu): transformer impedance as decimal (e.g. 0.06). Assumes infinite upstream source — conservative. 2309 / 0.06 ≈ 38.5 kA per TX; both TX in parallel (bus-tie closed) ≈ 77 kA — why the tie is interlocked open. MV design fault 25 kA (SEC range 25–40 kA)
Cable derating — required rating I_z(req) = I_B / (k_g × k_t) I_B: design (load) current (A); k_g: grouping factor; k_t: temperature factor. Select cable with tabulated I_z ≥ I_z(req). 194 / (0.80 × 0.94) = 258 A → 95 mm² Cu XLPE
Voltage drop (3-phase feeder) ΔV = √3 × I × (R·cosφ + X·sinφ) × L I: current (A); R, X: conductor resistance / reactance (Ω/km); L: length (km); cosφ/sinφ from load pf. Use 60 Hz reactance values (X is 1.2 × the 60 Hz table figure). 194 A, 120 m, 95 mm² Cu @ 60 Hz → ≈ 9.8 V ≈ 2.4 % (limit ≤ 5 %)
PFC reactive power required Q_c = P × (tanφ1 − tanφ2) P: active power (kW); φ1: present pf angle; φ2: target pf angle. 2000 × (0.620 − 0.329) = 582 kvar → ≈ 580 kvar bank, 7 % detuned (pf 0.85 → 0.95)
Capacitor output vs frequency Q = 2π × f × C × V² Q: reactive output (var); C: capacitance (F); V: voltage (V). Output is proportional to frequency: a capacitor delivers 60/50 = 1.2 × its 60 Hz kvar at 60 Hz. Always order capacitor steps and detuned reactors specified for 60 Hz; a 7 % reactor then tunes the branch to 60/√0.07 ≈ 227 Hz, below the 5th harmonic (300 Hz). A "50 kvar @ 400 V, 60 Hz" can → 60 kvar at 60 Hz (and its reactor detuning shifts) — never mix 60 Hz and 60 Hz PFC components
Generator sizing (essential load) S_DG ≥ (P_ess / pf) × k_step P_ess: essential active power (kW); pf: generator operating pf (0.8); k_step: step-load factor (~1.25). Quote the rating ESP at 50 °C ambient — high Saudi ambient derates the engine. (0.65 MW / 0.8) × 1.25 = 1.02 MVA → 1250 kVA DG (next standard size, with margin for 50 °C derating)
Transformer voltage regulation ΔV% ≈ load_pu × (R%·cosφ + X%·sinφ) load_pu: per-unit loading; R%, X%: transformer resistance and reactance in percent. 0.73 × (1.2×0.9 + 5.9×0.436) = 2.7 %
Protection grading margin t_up = t_down + Δt Δt: grading margin (typically 0.3 s) accounting for CB opening time, relay overshoot, CT error and safety margin. LV incomer 0.20 s → MV feeder relay 0.50 s
Arc-flash boundary AFB ≈ D × √(E / 1.2) D: working distance (m); E: incident energy at D (cal/cm²); 1.2 cal/cm² = onset-of-burn threshold. Simplified inverse-square scaling (full IEEE 1584 model required for labelling). 0.6 m × √(10/1.2) = 1.7 m
NER resistance R_NER = (V_LL / √3) / I_ef V_LL: MV line-to-line voltage (V); I_ef: target earth-fault current (A). Rate the NER for the fault duration (e.g. 10 s). (13,800 / 1.732) / 400 = 7970 / 400 ≈ 19.9 Ω (NER 7.97 kV, 400 A / 10 s)
Earth conductor (adiabatic) A = I × √t / k A: conductor cross-section (mm²); I: fault current (A); t: clearing time (s); k: material constant (143 for Cu/PVC). 38,500 × √0.2 / 143 = 120 mm² Cu
Availability A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR) MTBF: mean time between failures (h); MTTR: mean time to repair (h). Annual downtime = (1 − A) × 8760 h. TX: 200,000 / (200,000 + 168) = 0.99916 → ~7.4 h/yr
Series availability (RBD) A_sys = A1 × A2 × … × An All elements must function; system availability is always lower than the worst individual element. 0.9992 × 0.9994 = 0.9986 (e.g. incomer + TX in series)
Parallel availability 1-of-2 (RBD) A_par = 1 − (1 − A1)(1 − A2) Function survives if at least one element works. Much higher than series for the same components. 1 − (1−0.99916)² = 0.99999929 → ~22 s/yr down
FMECA Risk Priority Number RPN = S × O × D S: Severity (1–5); O: Occurrence (1–5); D: Detection difficulty (1–5; 5 = hardest to detect). RPN max = 125. Higher RPN = higher risk. Priority bands: P1 = SPOF with S ≥ 4 or RPN ≥ 60; P2 = RPN 36–59; P3 = RPN 16–35; P4 < 16. Rank and act top-down. ATS: S=5, O=3, D=4 → RPN = 60 (P1 in example FMECA)
Reliability (survival function) R(t) = e(−λt) λ: failure rate (failures/h) = 1/MTBF; t: mission time (h). R(t) = probability no failure in time t. λ = 5×10-6/h, t = 8760 h → R = e−0.044 = 0.957
Tip: For arc-flash and short-circuit studies, always use recognised power-system software (ETAP, DIgSILENT, SKM or equivalent) with the full IEC 60909 / IEEE 1584 models. The simplified formulas above are for quick manual checks and teaching purposes only.

60 Hz ↔ 60 Hz conversion quick table

Much published reference data (cable tables, motor catalogues, capacitor cans) is tabulated at 60 Hz. Before reusing any such figure on this 60 Hz plant, apply the corrections below. Resistance, kVA, FLC and thermal ratings do not change with frequency; everything built on reactance or speed does.

Quantity 50 → 60 Hz behaviour Practical consequence on this plant
Inductive reactance XL× 1.2 (proportional to f)Cable and transformer X up 20 %: slightly higher reactive voltage drop, slightly lower X/R-driven peak factor differences — use 60 Hz impedance data in studies.
Capacitive reactance XC÷ 1.2 (inversely proportional)Capacitors draw 20 % more current and deliver 20 % more kvar at the same voltage — a 60 Hz-rated PFC can is overstressed at 60 Hz.
Motor synchronous speed× 1.2 (1500 → 1800 rpm, 4-pole)Centrifugal pump/fan power rises roughly with speed cubed — a 60 Hz motor-pump set moved to 60 Hz overloads unless re-rated or VFD-limited.
Transformer core flux (same V)÷ 1.2 (V/f lower)A 60 Hz-designed transformer runs less saturated at 60 Hz and the same voltage — benign; the reverse direction (60 Hz unit on 60 Hz) is the dangerous one.
Detuned-filter tuning frequency× 1.2 (7 % reactor: 189 → 227 Hz)Reactor-capacitor steps must be engineered as a 60 Hz set; mixing 60 Hz reactors with 60 Hz cans detunes the detuning.
Relay / trip-unit settings in secondsUnchangedTime grading margins are frequency-agnostic; settings in cycles, however, are 16.7 ms per cycle at 60 Hz, not 20 ms.
Key takeaway: 23 formulas on one page, all worked on the 13.8 kV / 400 V / 60 Hz Rev 3 basis. The three you will use most in the field are the FLC formula, the short-circuit formula (I_sc = I_fl / Z), and the availability formula (A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)). Remember the three 60 Hz traps: reactance is 1.2 × the 60 Hz value, motor speeds are 1.2 × (1800 rpm for 4-pole), and capacitor kvar is 1.2 × nameplate-at-60 Hz. For full calculations, use the Part 2 worked examples.

D — Tag Scheme and SLD Conventions

Full tag table and IEC 60617 symbol key used across this manual, the training modules, and the master single-line diagram. Use only these tags when completing assessment forms. Source: the reference design document and Part 1 — Equipment Function Table.

Full Equipment Tag Table

Tag Equipment Voltage level Function in this plant
MV-MET Utility revenue metering cubicle 13.8 kV (MV) Utility billing / revenue metering point. Contains utility-grade CTs and VTs and the tariff meter. Not a protection or switching device.
MV-SWGR MV metal-clad switchgear 13.8 kV (MV) The main 13.8 kV distribution assembly (17.5 kV class). Houses the utility incomer CB (52-I), MV bus-section CB, transformer-feeder CBs (52-T1, 52-T2) with protection IEDs (50/51, 50N/51N, 87T). Withstand 31.5 kA / 3 s against a 25 kA design fault (SEC range 25–40 kA). Fault level basis for all downstream short-circuit ratings.
MV-PROT MV protection and control IEDs 13.8 kV (MV) Numerical IEDs providing overcurrent (50/51), earth-fault (50N/51N), transformer differential (87T), and under/over-voltage (27/59) protection. Relays are supplied from the DCDB so they remain operative even on AC failure. A latent FMECA row — loss of DC supply to MV-PROT disables protection.
MV-NER Neutral Earthing Resistor 13.8 kV (MV) Resistance-earthing of the MV system neutral. Limits earth-fault current to 400 A (indicative), controlling arc damage while keeping faults detectable. R = 7.97 kV / 400 A ≈ 19.9 Ω (indicative at 13.8 kV). Rated for fault duration (10 s). Consider an NER monitoring relay — an open NER is a latent failure.
TX-1 MV/LV power transformer 1 13.8 kV / 400 V boundary Steps down 13.8 kV to 400 V for LV distribution. Indicative: 1600 kVA, Dyn11, Z ≈ 6 %, ONAN or cast-resin (MV FLC 66.9 A, LV FLC 2309 A). Feeds Bus A of LV-MSB. Normal loading ~73 % (indicative).
TX-2 MV/LV power transformer 2 13.8 kV / 400 V boundary As TX-1. Feeds Bus B of LV-MSB. Together with TX-1, the two transformers provide N-1 redundancy via the bus-tie (BT).
LV-MSB Main LV switchboard / PCC 400 V (LV) The Power Control Centre. Split busbar (Bus A / Bus B) with ACB incomers from TX-1 and TX-2, a motorised normally-open bus-tie (BT), and MCCB outgoing feeders to MCCs, VFDs, PFC, UPS and DBs. Form 4b segregation (indicative). Bus rated ~3200–4000 A (total plant LV current ≈ 3396 A), 50–65 kA withstand against 38.5 kA per-TX fault current; the bus-tie interlock prevents the 77 kA paralleled condition.
BT Bus-tie circuit breaker 400 V (LV) The normally-open ACB (or contactor) connecting Bus A and Bus B of LV-MSB. Closes automatically (via PMS/ATS logic) on loss of one LV incomer to allow the surviving transformer to back-feed the dead bus. The bus-tie is a critical SPOF-mitigation element; verify its auto-transfer scheme is commissioned and periodically tested.
MCC-1 … MCC-n Motor Control Centres 400 V (LV) LV assemblies of motor starter bays (DOL, star-delta, soft-starter, VFD). Compartmentalised to IEC 61439; withdrawable or fixed. Each MCC feeds a group of process motors. For SPOF assessment: check whether duty and standby motors share the same MCC (common element — see Appendix F).
VFD Variable Frequency Drive(s) 400 V (LV) Power-electronic speed controllers for pumps, fans and compressors. Injects harmonics into the supply — requiring detuned PFC capacitors. Also provides LVRT capability and kinetic buffering through voltage sags.
PFC Power factor correction bank 400 V (LV) Automatic capacitor bank with 7 % detuned reactors, components rated for 60 Hz. Corrects plant pf from ~0.85 to ~0.95+. Sized ≈ 580 kvar (indicative). Detuning prevents resonance with VFD harmonic currents. Loss of PFC causes reactive demand increase and exposure under the SEC power-factor regime.
DG Diesel generator set 400 V (LV) Standby power source for the essential bus. Indicative rating 1250 kVA (ESP at 50 °C ambient), 1800 rpm 4-pole at 60 Hz; includes AVR, governor, day-tank, battery starting. Connected via the ATS (open transition, ≤ 10 s). Verify weekly auto-test-on-load schedule, fuel level and battery condition during site visit.
ATS Automatic Transfer Switch 400 V (LV) Detects loss of utility supply and automatically starts and connects the DG to the essential bus. A P1-priority item (RPN 60) in the example FMECA. A failed-to-transfer ATS on a utility outage = total loss of essential load. Verify commissioning records and test schedule.
UPS Uninterruptible Power Supply 400 V AC / battery No-break, clean AC supply for DCS/PLC/SCADA, field instrumentation and critical communications. Indicative: 100 kVA online double-conversion (VFI), 30 min battery autonomy (to be confirmed per plant). Loss of UPS during a supply disturbance can cause a DCS/PLC crash — verify battery condition (and temperature control of the battery room at 50 °C ambient) and bypass availability.
DCDB DC distribution board (battery and charger) 110 V DC Provides DC supply for MV switchgear trip / close coils and protection relay auxiliary circuits. A DCDB failure can silence all MV protection — extremely high severity. Verify battery condition (float voltage, impedance test) and charger operation.
DB Distribution board (normal) 400 V (LV) Final-circuit panel for lighting, small power, HVAC, and sockets. Fed from LV-MSB or MCC outgoing feeders. MCB/RCBO protection.
EDB Essential distribution board 400 V (LV) As DB, but supplied via the ATS from the essential bus (DG-backed). Feeds safety-critical loads that must remain live during a utility outage (e.g. emergency lighting, fire-alarm panel, safety system auxiliaries).

IEC 60617 Symbol Key

The master SLD and all SPOF example diagrams use IEC 60617 symbols rendered by the slddraw.py library. The table below summarises the symbols you will encounter when reading or red-lining the drawings on site.

Drawing basis note. The single-line diagrams on which these symbols appear were drawn for the original 13.8 kV / 400 V / 60 Hz reference design — read 13.8 kV as 13.8 kV, 400 V as 400 V and 60 Hz as 60 Hz.
Symbol description IEC 60617 representation (text description for field use) Used in this plant for
Utility / grid source Circle with AC sine-wave mark inside 13.8 kV SEC utility feeder supply connection
Circuit breaker (closed) Solid filled square on conductor All normally-closed breakers: MV CBs, LV ACBs (incomers), MCCB feeders
Circuit breaker (open / N.O.) Hollow (outline) square on conductor Bus-tie (BT) — normally open; auto-closes on N-1
Isolator / disconnector Open knife-contact symbol on conductor Maintenance isolation points upstream of CBs
Fuse Rectangle on conductor MV HRC fuses (where used); LV MCB/fuse in final circuits
Two-winding transformer Two interlinked circles (Dyn11 — delta primary, star secondary with neutral) TX-1 and TX-2
Neutral earthing resistor Resistor symbol in series to earth on the transformer neutral conductor MV-NER (resistance earthing of the 13.8 kV system neutral)
Earth / ground Three horizontal bars of diminishing length All earth connections; transformer neutral solidly earthed at 400 V (TN-S)
Capacitor bank (PFC) Two parallel plates (capacitor symbol) PFC detuned capacitor bank on LV-MSB Bus A or B
Motor Circle with letter M Process motors (pumps, fans, compressors) fed from MCCs
Generator Circle with letter G DG alternator symbol
Labelled equipment block Rectangle with tag name and sub-label MCC, VFD, UPS, ATS, DB, EDB, DCDB
Busbar Bold horizontal line MV bus (firebrick colour); LV Bus A / Bus B (navy colour) in the SLD
SPOF indicator (red-lined SLD) Red dashed ring around the failure-point element with red caption Used on red-line mark-ups to flag identified SPOFs. Not a standard IEC symbol.
Tip — reading the SLD on site: When verifying the drawing against the as-found plant, check: (1) bus-tie switch — is it normally open and does it have an auto-transfer scheme? (2) transformer taps — what position are they set at? (3) ATS — is the transfer scheme commissioned? (4) DG — is the auto-start enabled? These four items account for most SPOF findings on routine site visits.
Key takeaway: 17 equipment tags (including MV-PROT), 14 IEC 60617 symbols. The tag scheme is fixed — do not invent new tags; update the reference document if a new item is found on a plant that does not fit any existing tag.

E — Blank Assessment Forms

Printable blank versions of the four per-plant deliverable forms. Open all four as soon as a plant is assigned; fill the header during desk study so the form is ready on site. See Part 0 — The Four Deliverable Forms for descriptions of what each form contains and when it is completed. The filled-in example in Part 5 shows how a completed set looks.

SF-01 — Document Inventory

Record all electrical documents requested, received and reviewed. Add rows as required. Documents not received should be chased via the site contact before or during the site visit. Form IDs SF-01…SF-04 match the worked example in Part 5 — Jubail Case Study.

Plant reference / ID
Assessment reference

SF-02 — FMECA Worksheet

Failure Mode, Effects and Criticality Analysis per IEC 60812. One row per item / failure mode. Score S, O, D on a 1–5 scale (5 = worst); RPN = S × O × D (max 125). Priority bands: P1 = SPOF with S ≥ 4 or RPN ≥ 60; P2 = RPN 36–59; P3 = RPN 16–35; P4 < 16. See Part 3 — FMECA method for scale definitions. Scores are indicative; calibrate scales using IEEE 493 / ISO 14224 data for real studies.

Plant name
Assessment reference
Engineer
FMECA revision
Date
Item ref. Tag Function Failure mode Local effect System effect (worst case) Existing controls / detection S (1–5) O (1–5) D (1–5) RPN Recommended action Rec. ref.

SF-03 — Recommendations Register

Ranked list of recommended modifications and improvements derived from the FMECA. One row per recommendation. Link each row back to the FMECA reference (Rec. ref. = FM-xx). Risk ratings: H = High, M = Medium, L = Low. Cost: H / M / L indicative only.

Warning — NON-INTRUSIVE surveys only. Do not open energised panels, operate switches or breakers, or conduct any live electrical test. Visual inspection, nameplate reading and photography (where permitted) are the only permitted activities. Observe host permit-to-work, LOTO and QHSE rules at all times.
Form ID mapping — blank forms to worked example (Jubail, Part 5): SF-01 = Document Inventory (rows: DOC-xx); SF-02 = FMECA Worksheet (rows: FM-xx); SF-03 = Recommendations Register (rows: REC-xx); SF-04 = Site-Visit Report (observations: OBS-xx, photos: PH-xx, actions: ACT-xx). These IDs align with the Part 5 Jubail case study.
Key takeaway: Four blank forms — SF-01 Document Inventory, SF-02 FMECA Worksheet, SF-03 Recommendations Register, SF-04 Site-Visit Report. Open them at plant assignment, fill the header during desk study, and update iteratively through site visit, analysis and recommendations stages.

As-Found Verification Sheet

Use this sheet during post-site analysis to verify that nameplate data captured on site matches the calculated parameters and to record a Pass/Fail verdict for each key item. This directly supports the "assessment = verification" thesis: every FMECA row for a sizing or rating finding must trace to a completed row here. All calculations are indicative; confirm with project-specific studies.

Plant name
Assessment reference
Engineer
Date completed
Tag Nameplate: kVA (or kW) Nameplate: Z% (TX) or FLC (motor) Nameplate: V (primary / secondary) Nameplate: rated A (LV FLC) Nameplate: Icu (kA) — switchgear / cable Calculated FLC (A) [I = S / (√3·V)] Calculated Isc (kA) [FLC / Z%] Pass / Fail / Flag Notes / FM-ID if finding
