DCDB charger—110/48/24 V DC—(trip coil load A)—☐ Pass  ☐ Fail
(add rows)

Pass criteria (indicative): TX loading ≤ 80 % nameplate kVA in normal; ≤ 100 % in N-1. ACB / MCCB rated current ≥ calculated FLC × 1.25 (service factor). Switchgear Icu ≥ calculated Isc. Cable ampacity ≥ FLC after derating (XLPE in air at 50 °C ambient: factor ≈ 0.82). DG kVA ≥ essential load kVA × 1.1 at the ESP/50 °C rating. UPS autonomy ≥ 30 min. DCDB autonomy ≥ 2 h. Nameplate frequency must read 60 Hz and TX ratio 13.8/0.4 kV. All criteria indicative — confirm against governing specification.

F — SPOF-Hardening Principles (One-Page Summary)

Distilled from Part 3 — SPOF Analysis and Module 3. Use this page as a rapid scan-reference when reviewing an SLD or writing recommendations. Every principle has a trade-off in cost, complexity and footprint — apply redundancy where the consequence of failure justifies it (critical / essential loads first). All values and configurations are indicative.

Principle What it means in practice Typical implementation (tags) SPOF it eliminates Cost / complexity
N+1 redundancy Provide at least one more capacity unit than the minimum. The surviving unit(s) must carry the essential load on loss of one unit. 2 × TX-1/TX-2 (each capable of carrying essential load at ~81 % loading on N-1); N+1 MCC feeders for critical motors. Single transformer / single source H capital; H benefit for critical loads
Bus segregation Split the LV busbar into independent sections (Bus A / Bus B) so a bus fault or maintenance on one section does not take the whole plant. LV-MSB split bus; Form 4b compartmentalisation of MCC assemblies. Single un-split LV bus (Diagram A) M capital; H benefit (contained fault zone)
Bus-tie with auto-transfer The normally-open bus-tie closes automatically within seconds on loss of one LV incomer, connecting the healthy transformer to the dead bus. N-1 loading must be verified (≤ 1.0 pu continuous). BT ACB with auto-transfer scheme via PMS or hard-wired interlock; loss-of-voltage / breaker-position logic. Periodic on-load test required. Two buses with no tie (Diagram B) M capital; very H benefit (MTTR reduced from hours to seconds)
Dual utility incomers Two independent utility feeds to the MV bus from different utility substations or circuits, with a closeable MV bus-section between them. Two incomer panels in MV-SWGR (52-I1, 52-I2); MV bus-section CB (52-BS). Single utility feeder / single MV incomer (Diagram C) H capital (utility connection); H benefit for essential-load continuity
Separate routing — duty vs standby Duty and standby motors / loads must be fed from different sources, MCCs and cable routes. A single cable, MCC bus or MCC incomer serving both duty and standby is a common-element SPOF. Duty pump on MCC-1 (Bus A), standby pump on MCC-2 (Bus B); cables in separate trays on opposite sides of plant. Shared cable / single MCC for duty + standby (Diagram D) M capital; H benefit (eliminates common-mode failure)
Standby generation (DG + ATS) A standby diesel generator, automatically started and connected via the ATS on utility loss, backs up the essential bus for safety-critical and process-critical loads. DG set (1250 kVA ESP at 50 °C, indicative) + ATS + EDB. ATS open-transition transfer typically ≤ 10 s (depends on DG run-up time). Auto-test on load weekly; fuel and battery monitoring continuous. Total loss of supply to essential loads on utility outage H capital per kVA; H benefit for safety loads
UPS for control / protection No-break, clean AC supply for the DCS/PLC/SCADA and instrumentation. Prevents a supply disturbance from crashing the control system and causing a process trip or safety incident. UPS (100 kVA, 30 min autonomy indicative) + DCDB (110 V DC for MV switchgear trip / close coils). Loss of control / protection on any supply disturbance M capital; H benefit for control continuity and protection dependability
Condition monitoring and testing Scheduled on-load testing (DG / ATS weekly; bus-tie transfer periodically) and continuous condition monitoring (transformer oil / temperature, battery impedance, UPS health) reduces Detection score (D) in the FMECA without changing failure rate. Monitored via PMS / DCS; oil/temperature sensors on TX-1/TX-2; battery monitoring on UPS and DCDB. Latent failures in standby equipment (DG fails to start; bus-tie fails to close) L–M capital; H benefit (lowers RPN through better detection)
Fault containment and selectivity Graded protection (time-current coordination) ensures the device closest to a fault trips first, isolating the smallest possible zone. Zone-selective interlocking (ZSI) maintains both selectivity and fast arc-flash clearance. Relay / trip-unit settings in MV-SWGR IEDs and LV-MSB ACBs; ZSI between ACB and outgoing MCCBs. Upstream breaker trips on a downstream fault (wider zone lost than necessary) L capital (settings only, unless extra relays); H benefit for zone containment
Earthing integrity A continuous, low-impedance earth system (grid + bonding + NER) ensures earth faults are reliably detected, current limited, and touch/step voltages kept safe. MV-NER (R ≈ 19.9 Ω, 400 A / 10 s indicative, with NER monitoring relay); LV TN-S; earth grid (≤ 1 Ω indicative, IEEE 80 touch/step check); 120 mm² Cu earth conductors (indicative); bonding to LV-MSB, MCC, cable armour. Undetected earth faults; personnel electrocution; equipment damage from high fault energy M capital; safety-critical
Environmental hardening (Rev 3 / Saudi) Design every rating and enclosure for the actual environment: 50 °C ambient, dust/sand, coastal salt. Heat is a slow common-mode failure mechanism — it ages every battery, capacitor and power module on site simultaneously. Min IP54 outdoor enclosures; switchroom/battery-room HVAC (itself on the essential bus); cable derating ≈ 0.82 for air at 50 °C; DG rated ESP at 50 °C; filter maintenance on VFD and UPS cooling paths. Common-mode thermal degradation of all electronics and batteries; HVAC loss silently shortening UPS/DCDB autonomy M capital; H benefit — and the HVAC itself must appear in the FMECA

SPOF-Hunt Checklist — Quick Scan of an SLD

  • Is there a single utility incomer / single MV bus? (Diagram C SPOF — check for dual incomers or DG/ATS coverage).
  • Is there only one transformer? (Diagram A SPOF — check for N+1 or bus-tie capability).
  • Is the LV bus split into sections A and B? If not, the whole LV plant is a single zone.
  • Is there a bus-tie (BT) between Bus A and Bus B? Is it normally open with auto-transfer?
  • Are duty and standby motors fed from different MCCs on different buses, via separately routed cables? (Diagram D SPOF).
  • Is the DG connected via an ATS? Is the ATS auto-transfer commissioned and tested regularly?
  • Is there a UPS for DCS/PLC/SCADA? Is there a DCDB for MV switchgear trip/close supplies?
  • Are transformer differential protection (87T) and earth-fault protection (50N/51N) commissioned on both TX-1 and TX-2?
  • Is the NER present and rated correctly? (Check neutral CT and earth-fault relay are connected).
  • Is protection coordination current — does the study reflect the as-found breaker / relay configuration?
  • Is the switchroom / battery room HVAC healthy and alarmed? (At 50 °C ambient, HVAC loss is a common-mode threat to UPS, DCDB and VFD electronics.)
  • Is the NER supervised by a monitoring relay? An unmonitored open NER silently unearths the 13.8 kV system.
Key takeaway: Eleven hardening principles; twelve SPOF-hunt checklist items. The five most common findings are: no bus-tie auto-transfer, duty and standby on the same MCC, single utility incomer with no DG coverage, ATS not commissioned, and outdated protection coordination study. Address these first.

G — Indicative Failure-Rate and MTBF Data

Indicative λ (failure rate), MTBF and MTTR figures per equipment class, drawn from the reliability analysis in Module 5 and typical industrial power-system data. Use these values for preliminary FMECA scoring and RBD availability estimates only.

Warning — indicative values only. The figures in this table are indicative and tentative. They are representative of industrial power-system equipment in good condition under reasonable maintenance regimes. Actual failure rates vary significantly with age, maintenance history, environment (tropical, desert, coastal), load factor, manufacturer and installation quality. For any real reliability or availability study, these figures must be replaced with values confirmed against IEEE 493 (Gold Book), ISO 14224, or plant-specific historical maintenance data. Do not cite this table as a primary source in a formal study.
Equipment class Tag(s) Indicative λ (failures/yr) Indicative MTBF (h) Indicative MTTR (h) Indicative Availability A Notes
MV metal-clad switchgear (panel) MV-SWGR ~0.006 / yr ~150,000 h 168 h ~0.99889 Per panel (bus section, incomer or TX feeder). Includes vacuum CB and protection relay. MTTR includes spares logistics.
MV/LV power transformer TX-1, TX-2 ~0.004 / yr ~200,000 h 168–720 h ~0.99916 (at 168 h MTTR) Cast-resin units have lower fire risk but similar electrical failure rate to oil-filled. MTTR can extend to 720+ h if rewinding required. Oil/temp monitoring reduces latent failure.
LV switchboard / PCC (complete assembly) LV-MSB ~0.010 / yr ~90,000 h 8–72 h ~0.99920 (at 72 h MTTR) Busbar fault rare but high severity; most failures are in individual breakers or auxiliaries. Form 4b segregation limits fault zone and reduces effective MTTR.
Air Circuit Breaker (ACB) LV-MSB incomer / BT ~0.015 / yr ~60,000 h 4–24 h ~0.99960 (at 24 h MTTR) Includes failure-to-trip (most critical mode) and failure-to-close (bus-tie BT failure on auto-transfer). Regular trip testing improves Detection (D) score.
Moulded-Case Circuit Breaker (MCCB) Feeders in LV-MSB, MCC ~0.020 / yr ~45,000 h 2–8 h ~0.99982 (at 8 h MTTR) Higher failure rate reflects larger population; most failures affect only the individual feeder. Selectivity (coordination) prevents upstream trip.
Motor Control Centre (MCC, complete) MCC-1 … MCC-n ~0.030 / yr ~30,000 h 4–24 h ~0.99920 (at 24 h MTTR) Aggregate figure for the assembly including contactors, overloads and starters. A single starter failure affects only one motor; a bus fault or incomer failure affects all motors on that MCC.
Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) VFD ~0.050 / yr ~18,000 h 4–48 h ~0.99733 (at 48 h MTTR) Higher failure rate than simple contactors; power electronics are sensitive to temperature, harmonics and humidity. MTTR can be long if a power module must be ordered. Consider bypass contactor for critical drives.
Power factor correction bank (PFC) PFC ~0.020 / yr ~45,000 h 8–48 h ~0.99893 (at 48 h MTTR) Capacitor element failure is the dominant mode; detuned reactors are generally more reliable. Failure increases reactive demand but does not typically cause a supply outage — Medium severity.
Diesel generator set (complete) DG ~0.060 / yr (start-to-run) ~15,000 h (run) 4–48 h Conditional: P(start success) ~0.95 per demand Distinguish between failure-to-start (~5 % demand rate indicative) and failure-while-running (< 1 %/h). Weekly tested-on-load reduces P(fail to start). Fuel, battery and coolant condition are the main latent risks.
Automatic Transfer Switch (ATS) ATS ~0.030 / yr ~30,000 h 2–24 h ~0.99920 (at 24 h MTTR) Failure-to-transfer on demand is the critical mode (highest RPN in typical FMECA). Redundant control logic, position monitoring and monthly on-load tests materially reduce the Occurrence and Detection scores.
UPS (online, double-conversion) UPS ~0.030 / yr ~30,000 h 2–24 h ~0.99920 (at 24 h MTTR) Battery ageing is the dominant long-term failure mode; inverter electronics also fail. Battery impedance testing (annually) identifies degraded cells before failure. A bypass path and redundant UPS greatly improve system availability.
DC battery and charger (DCDB) DCDB ~0.020 / yr ~45,000 h 4–48 h ~0.99893 (at 48 h MTTR) Battery failure can silence all MV protection — extremely high severity. Float-voltage monitoring and annual discharge testing are essential. Keep a spare battery module on site for critical plants.
Neutral Earthing Resistor (NER) MV-NER ~0.002 / yr ~500,000 h 8–24 h ~0.99998 Passive element; low failure rate. Open-circuit or short-circuit failure both change earth-fault protection behaviour. Verify resistance and neutral CT integrity during assessment.
MV/LV feeder cable (per km) Feeders between tagged equipment ~0.003 / yr / km (LV XLPE) ~300,000 h / km 8–72 h ~0.99976 (at 72 h MTTR) Buried LV cable; failure rate increases with age, installation damage and proximity to other services. A single cable shared by duty and standby loads is a common-element SPOF (Diagram D): use separately routed cables.

RBD Availability Examples

Configuration Formula Indicative result Annual downtime
Single transformer (series) A = 0.99916 0.99916 ~7.4 h/yr
Two transformers 1-of-2 (parallel) A = 1 − (1−0.99916)2 0.99999929 ~22 s/yr
Transformer + ACB in series A = 0.99916 × 0.99960 0.99876 ~10.9 h/yr
ATS (manual MTTR ~8 h vs auto ~0.003 h) Manual: A = 30000/(30000+8) = 0.99973
Auto: A = 30000/(30000+0.003) = 0.99999990		 Manual: 0.99973
Auto: ~1.000		 Manual: ~2.3 h/yr
Auto: ~3 s/yr (each transfer event additionally imposes ~10 s of dead time per demand, which the UPS rides through)
DG + ATS (both must work) A = A_DG × A_ATS = 0.9980 × 0.99973
A_DG derivation: DG failure-to-start probability ≈ 5% per demand (1 demand/yr); MTBF running ≈ 15,000 h; MTTR ≈ 24 h. Effective A_DG = 1 − P(fail-to-start) ≈ 0.9980 (indicative — dominated by start-reliability; full RAM model needed for real studies).		 ~0.99773 ~19.6 h/yr (manual ATS)
Warning — Saudi environment adjustment. The indicative figures above are temperate-climate base values. At a 50 °C design ambient with dust and (on coastal sites) salt, expect the electronics-rich classes — VFD, UPS, battery systems, PFC capacitors — to sit at the worse end of their ranges or beyond: electrolytic capacitor and battery life roughly halves per 8–10 °C of sustained over-temperature. Treat switchroom HVAC as a reliability-critical system in its own right (it appears in the hardening table of Appendix F), and bias the FMECA Occurrence score upward for thermally stressed equipment rather than quoting the table values unmodified.
Note — MTTR as the key lever: The ATS example illustrates why auto-transfer (MTTR reduced from 8 h to ~10 s) delivers a far larger availability improvement than any realistic reduction in failure rate. Auto-close bus-tie and auto-transfer ATS are the highest-value reliability upgrades in a typical MV/LV plant. This principle is central to the recommendations generated by this campaign.
Key takeaway: 14 equipment classes tabulated. Use these indicative figures for initial FMECA scoring only. Replace with IEEE 493 / ISO 14224 / plant maintenance records for formal studies. The single most impactful design choice visible in the MTBF data is the auto-transfer MTTR reduction: from ~8 h (manual) to seconds (auto), turning ~2.3 h/yr loss into a few seconds per year (plus ~10 s of dead time per transfer demand, ridden through by the UPS).

H — SASO / SEC Standards Map

This appendix explains how standards work in Saudi Arabia for an electrical engineer used to plain-IEC projects, and then maps every major topic in this manual to its IEC/IEEE technical basis and its Saudi regulatory reference. Nothing in the engineering changes — Ohm's law is the same in Jubail as in Geneva — but the citation chain that makes a design contractually and legally acceptable in the Kingdom is different, and getting it wrong delays procurement, customs clearance and energisation approval. All references in this appendix are indicative; the project specification and the authority having jurisdiction define the governing editions.

How SASO adopts IEC standards

SASO (Saudi Standards, Metrology and Quality Organization) is the Kingdom's national standards body. Rather than writing electrical standards from scratch, SASO adopts international standards — overwhelmingly IEC for electrical equipment — and publishes them as Saudi standards, usually with the structure SASO IEC xxxxx. An adoption may be identical (the IEC text verbatim) or modified (with national deviations — the classic example being the LV supply voltage and the 60 Hz frequency). The practical consequences for you:

  • The technical content you learned from the IEC standard almost always applies. When this manual cites IEC 60909 for fault calculations or IEC 61439 for switchboard verification, the SASO adoption carries the same clauses.
  • Check the national deviations first. They cluster around voltage (400/230 V LV), frequency (60 Hz), ambient temperature (calibration of ratings to Saudi climate) and marking/language requirements.
  • Cite the Saudi adoption in deliverables. A datasheet that references "IEC 60076" is technically fine; a datasheet that references "SASO IEC 60076 (IEC 60076)" clears review faster. This manual uses the shorthand SASO-adopted IEC.
Note — the dual-code reality. Saudi industrial practice runs IEC-based equipment standards alongside SBC 401, the Saudi Building Code electrical chapter, which is technically derived from NFPA 70 (the US NEC). On a process plant you will routinely see IEC switchgear installed under an NEC-flavoured installation code, with IEEE 80 earthing and IEEE 1584 arc-flash studies on top. This mix is normal and workable — but it means you must always ask which code governs a given scope and flag conflicts (e.g. cable sizing tables, earthing terminology) rather than silently assuming one family.

SABER — conformity before the box ships

SABER is SASO's electronic conformity-assessment platform. For regulated products — which include a large share of LV electrical equipment — the importer must obtain a Product Certificate of Conformity (valid typically one year) and a per-shipment Shipment Certificate through SABER before customs clearance. Equipment families also fall under Saudi Technical Regulations (for example for LV electrical equipment and appliances), which define which standards the certificate must attest against.

  • For the desk study: SABER status is a procurement/QA document, not a site observation. Ask the supply chain for the certificates with the test reports.
  • For recommendations: when an FMECA action implies new equipment (e.g. adding an NER monitor or SPDs), note that delivery time must include SABER certification if the vendor has no current Saudi certificate.
  • Red flag: a vendor who cannot state their SABER/SASO position for the offered family. The major manufacturers in Appendix I maintain Saudi certification as a matter of course.

SEC company standards and the grid codes

The Saudi Electricity Company (SEC) is both the utility you connect to and a de-facto standards authority: its Distribution Materials Specifications and company standards govern everything at and upstream of the point of supply — the 13.8 kV connection, the revenue metering arrangement (MV-MET), the intake protection interface, and approved-vendor requirements for utility-facing equipment. Two codes sit above this:

  • Saudi Grid Code — connection to the transmission system and duties of large users/generators: operating frequency band around 60 Hz, voltage ranges, fault ride-through, reactive capability.
  • SEC Distribution Code — the distribution-level counterpart that applies to a 13.8 kV-connected plant like this one: supply standards, power-factor expectations (the ≈ 580 kvar PFC bank exists largely to live comfortably under this regime), harmonic emission limits, metering and protection coordination at the intake.
Tip — three questions to settle with SEC early. (1) The confirmed fault level at the point of supply (design value here: 25 kA, SEC range 25–40 kA — it sizes MV-SWGR). (2) The required intake protection and metering single-line (it fixes the MV-MET panel arrangement). (3) The earthing interface — whether the SEC network earthing and the plant MV-NER philosophy are compatible. All three are desk-study items; none can be verified by site inspection alone.

SBC 401 — the statutory installation code

The Saudi Building Code (SBC) series is the Kingdom's statutory construction code, enforced through municipal permitting; SBC 401 is its electrical volume, technically based on NFPA 70 (NEC) with Saudi amendments. On industrial plants its practical reach is mostly buildings, life safety and final-circuit installations (the DB/EDB world), while the process electrical system is specified to SASO-adopted IEC. Where both could apply, the contract's order of precedence governs — and where you find a genuine conflict (conductor sizing method, RCD vs GFCI language, earthing terminology), record it as a finding rather than improvising a hybrid.

Topic-by-topic standards map (indicative)

The table maps each technical topic of this manual to its international basis and the Saudi reference you should cite alongside it. All rows indicative — adoption status, edition years and regulation numbers change; verify on SASO's catalogue and in the project specification before citing in a formal deliverable.

Topic (manual section) International basis Saudi reference (indicative) Field note
LV installation design (Part 2 — cables)IEC 60364 seriesSASO IEC 60364 adoptions; SBC 401 for building scopesWatch the IEC-vs-NEC seam on mixed projects; confirm which governs final circuits.
MV switchgear MV-SWGRIEC 62271-200 (assembly), 62271-100 (CBs), 62271-1 (common)SASO IEC 62271 adoptions; SEC material specs at the intake17.5 kV class, 31.5 kA / 3 s, IAC classification; SEC approval needed for the intake panels.
LV assemblies LV-MSB / MCCIEC 61439-1/-2SASO IEC 61439 adoptionsDesign verification at 50 °C ambient — temperature-rise verification is the Saudi pain point.
Power transformers TX-1/TX-2IEC 60076 series (60076-11 dry-type)SASO IEC 60076 adoptions; SASO energy-efficiency (MEPS) regulation for distribution transformersSaudi MEPS sets minimum efficiency tiers — a cheap high-loss unit may be non-importable.
MV/LV power cablesIEC 60502-1/-2 (XLPE 1–36 kV)SASO IEC 60502 adoptions; SEC specs for utility-side cablesDerate for 50 °C air / hot soil; 90 °C XLPE is the default conductor system here.
Protection relays / IEDs (protection)IEC 60255 series; IEC 61850 (communications)SASO IEC adoptions; SEC protection interface requirements at the incomerSEC will state required intake protection functions and grading limits toward the network.
Short-circuit calculation (Part 2)IEC 60909Cited via SASO adoption / project specUse the SEC-confirmed source fault level (25–40 kA at 13.8 kV) as the boundary condition.
Earthing / grounding (Part 2)IEC 60364-5-54; IEEE 80 (substation grids — common practice)SBC 401 earthing rules for buildings; IEEE 80 accepted practice for substationsIEEE 80 touch/step design is the regional norm even on IEC plants; soil is dry and resistive — test, don't assume.
Lightning protection (Part 2)IEC 62305 seriesSASO IEC 62305 adoptions; SBC requirements for buildingsRisk assessment per 62305-2; coastal east-coast sites see real storm days — do not skip the study.
Surge protection (SPDs)IEC 61643-11SASO IEC 61643 adoptionsType 1/2/3 cascade per the zone concept; status contacts to DCS.
Arc flash (Part 2)IEEE 1584-2018; NFPA 70E (PPE programme) — common practiceHost QHSE systems typically invoke NFPA 70E directlyUS-style arc-flash labels are the regional expectation, including on IEC switchgear.
UPS UPSIEC 62040 series (62040-3 performance classes)SASO IEC 62040 adoptions; SABER certification for the product familySpecify VFI-SS-111; battery room HVAC matters more than the inverter at 50 °C.
DC systems DCDBIEC 62485 (battery safety); IEEE 485 sizing (common practice)Cited via project spec110 V DC tripping supply; duty-cycle sizing per IEEE 485 is the regional norm.
Diesel generator DGISO 8528 series (ratings, governing, acceptance)SASO ISO 8528 adoptions; environmental/noise rules for enclosuresState the rating as ESP/PRP at 50 °C; 1800 rpm 4-pole at 60 Hz.
ATS ATSIEC 60947-6-1 (transfer switching equipment); UL 1008 family equally commonSASO IEC adoptions / SABERCheck WCR vs available fault current and upstream clearing time.
LV circuit breakers (ACB/MCCB)IEC 60947-2SASO IEC 60947 adoptions; SABER for the familiesIcu/Ics at 50 °C and altitude per site; 38.5 kA duty on LV-MSB feeders.
Motors (Part 2)IEC 60034 seriesSASO IEC 60034 adoptions; SASO motor MEPS (IE-class efficiency tiers)60 Hz nameplates: 1800/3600 rpm; verify service factor at 50 °C ambient.
VFDs VFDIEC 61800 series (61800-5-1 safety, -3 EMC)SASO IEC adoptions / SABERDerate or oversize for 50 °C switchroom; harmonics interact with the SEC emission limits.
PFC capacitors PFCIEC 60831-1/-2 (LV shunt capacitors)SASO IEC 60831 adoptions60 Hz-rated cans and reactors only; 7 % detuning → ≈ 227 Hz tuning at 60 Hz.
FMECA / reliability (Part 3)IEC 60812; IEC 61078; IEEE 493; ISO 14224Methodology standards — cited as-isNo Saudi deviation; data sources unchanged.
Energy metering / tariffIEC 62052/62053 meter standardsSEC metering specifications at MV-METUtility owns the metering point; you verify location and CT/VT classes only.
Grid connection & power qualityIEC 61000 series (EMC/harmonics)Saudi Grid Code / SEC Distribution Code; ECRA/regulator rulesHarmonic and pf obligations at the point of common coupling; the detuned PFC and VFD filters serve these.

Field notes — what to ask for, per document

During the desk study, the Saudi regulatory layer translates into a short list of extra documents to request alongside the usual drawing set (SF-01). The table below lists them with the question each one answers.

Document to request Issued by / held by What it tells you
SEC connection agreement / service confirmationSEC ↔ plant ownerConfirmed supply voltage (13.8 kV), capacity, metering point, and the contractual fault level — the boundary condition for the whole short-circuit study.
SEC-approved intake protection single-line and settingsSEC / commissioning contractorThe grading limit you must coordinate under at the incomer; changing intake settings needs SEC concurrence.
SABER product / shipment certificates for main equipmentImporter / procurementConformity status of MV-SWGR, TX, LV-MSB, UPS etc.; absence is a procurement finding, not a safety finding.
SBC 401 permit / municipal electrical approvalMunicipality / ownerWhich scopes were permitted under the building code — usually buildings and life safety; mismatches with as-built are findings.
Transformer MEPS / efficiency declarationManufacturerCompliance with the Saudi minimum-efficiency regulation for distribution transformers; relevant when recommending replacements.
Power-factor / harmonic compliance statementsOwner / consultantStanding of the plant under the SEC Distribution Code reactive and harmonic limits; explains the sizing of PFC and any filters.
Warning — do not self-certify. Nothing in this appendix authorises an assessor to declare equipment compliant with SASO regulations, the SEC codes or SBC 401. Your deliverable records which documents exist, which are missing, and where the as-found plant contradicts them. Compliance declarations belong to the manufacturer (SABER), the installer (permits) and the owner (SEC agreement).
Key takeaway: in Saudi Arabia the engineering is IEC, the citation is SASO, the connection is SEC, the building is SBC 401, and the customs gate is SABER. Twenty-one topic rows above give the indicative mapping; the project specification and the authority having jurisdiction always define the governing editions.

I — Vendor Family Index

A one-stop index of every vendor product family discussed in Part 6 — Equipment Catalogues, grouped by equipment class and linked to the catalogue section where each family is described in context (typical parameters, selection logic, Saudi support position). Use this appendix when you are standing in front of a panel trying to place a nameplate brand, or when drafting a recommendation and you want to point the reader at the right catalogue chapter.

Indicative catalogue data. Product families and typical parameters are listed for training and familiarisation only, are approximate, and change with manufacturer revisions. No part numbers are given; always obtain current certified data sheets and SASO/SEC acceptance status from the manufacturer.

How to use this index

  • Identify: read the brand and family from the nameplate (e.g. "UniGear ZS1", "Masterpact MTZ2", "SIVACON S8") and find the row below — the link takes you to the catalogue section with the family's typical ratings.
  • Specify: when an FMECA recommendation implies new equipment, cite the equipment class and two or three reference families from the table — never a part number — and let procurement obtain certified, SABER-cleared offers.
  • Beware look-alikes: families evolve (ComPacT NSX vs the older Compact NSX, Emax 2 vs Emax) and licence-built local variants exist. The vintage matters for spares — record the exact family string and year from the nameplate.

Index by equipment class

Equipment class (plant tag) Vendor Product family / families Catalogue section
MV switchgear, 13.8 kV (MV-SWGR) ABBUniGear ZS1 (air-insulated, withdrawable VCB); SafeRing / SafePlus (gas-insulated RMU); ZX series (GIS); eco-gas "blue-line" variantsPart 6 — MV switchgear
SiemensNXAIR (air-insulated); NXPLUS (GIS); "Blue" vacuum / clean-air portfolioPart 6 — MV switchgear
Schneider ElectricPIX (air-insulated); SM6 (modular RMU); AirSeT (SF6-free evolution)Part 6 — MV switchgear
(panel IEDs)See protection relay rows below — ABB Relion, Siemens SIPROTEC, Schneider Easergy/MiCOM, SEL, GE MultilinPart 6 — protection relays

Schneider ElectricTrihal cast-resin; Minera oil-immersed (hermetic ONAN)Part 6 — transformers
Hitachi Energy (ABB heritage)Dry-type ranges incl. EcoDry; hermetic oil-immersed distribution unitsPart 6 — transformers

Siemens3WA (ACB); 3VA (MCCB)Part 6 — LV breakers
EatonNZM (MCCB) and associated ACB line-upPart 6 — LV breakers

ABBMNS / MNS Digital; UMC100 motor controllersPart 6 — MCC

Schneider ElectricOkken and BlokSeT (with TeSys starters)Part 6 — MCC

DanfossVLT FC302 familyPart 6 — VFD

Rockwell AutomationPowerFlex 755Part 6 — VFD
UPS, online double-conversion (UPS) Eaton93PM and 9395 familiesPart 6 — UPS
Schneider ElectricGalaxy familyPart 6 — UPS
VertivLiebert EXL familyPart 6 — UPS
ABBIndustrial UPS with decentralised parallel architecturePart 6 — UPS
DC systems — chargers and batteries (DCDB) AEG Power SolutionsSVS-type industrial thyristor chargersPart 6 — DC systems
BenningThyrotronic industrial charger familyPart 6 — DC systems
GE / ABB heritageIndustrial battery charger linesPart 6 — DC systems
Diesel generator sets (DG) CaterpillarDiesel genset ranges with EMCP control panelsPart 6 — gensets
Cummins Power GenerationGenset ranges with PowerCommand controlsPart 6 — gensets
Kohler Energy (SDMO heritage)Genset ranges with DEC / APM controllers (DSE controllers common on packagers' sets)Part 6 — gensets
Automatic transfer switches (ATS) ASCO Power Technologies (Schneider Electric)7000 Series (heavy duty); 300 Series (standard tier)Part 6 — ATS
CumminsOTPC / BTPC transfer switches (PowerCommand-integrated)Part 6 — ATS
CaterpillarATS lines integrated with EMCP genset controlsPart 6 — ATS
Protection relays / IEDs (MV-PROT) ABBRelion 615 / 620 series (REF/RET/REM)Part 6 — relays
SiemensSIPROTEC 5 (and Compact) seriesPart 6 — relays
Schneider ElectricEasergy P3 / MiCOM heritage seriesPart 6 — relays
SEL (Schweitzer)700 series — SEL-751 (feeder), SEL-787 (transformer), SEL-710 (motor); MIRRORED BITS communicationsPart 6 — relays
GE VernovaMultilin 8 series (and the installed-base 469/745, Multilin 850/869)Part 6 — relays
Auxiliaries — NER, SPD, PFC (MV-NER, PFC) Post Glover; CressallNeutral earthing resistor assemblies (NER/NGR)Part 6 — auxiliaries
DEHN; OBO Bettermann; Phoenix ContactDEHNventil / DEHNguard; V-series; FLASHTRAB / VALVETRAB — SPD Type 1/2/3 cascadesPart 6 — auxiliaries
Schneider ElectricVarSet PFC banks with PowerLogic controllers; switchboard-integrated SPDsPart 6 — auxiliaries
ABB; TDK (EPCOS)QCap capacitors / Dynacomp dynamic compensation; capacitor elementsPart 6 — auxiliaries

Saudi support and spares considerations

Family choice in the Kingdom is rarely decided on electrical performance — the major families above are all competent. The differentiators an assessor should note are:

  • Local presence: the global majors (ABB, Siemens, Schneider, Eaton, Rockwell, Caterpillar, Cummins, Kohler/SDMO, SEL) maintain Saudi sales, service and in several cases assembly/panel-building operations; response time for a failed VFD power module or ACB pole differs by weeks between a locally supported family and an orphan import. Record the nameplate family and the local service route in SF-01.
  • SASO/SABER position: mainstream families ship with current Saudi conformity certificates; an exotic family may be technically excellent but slow to re-import — an MTTR input to the FMECA, not just a procurement note.
  • 50 °C data availability: insist on vendor derating curves at site ambient. Families marketed primarily for European 40 °C practice may need one frame size up; the catalogue sections in Part 6 flag this per class.
  • Installed-base continuity: matching the plant's existing family (e.g. adding an MTZ to a Masterpact board) simplifies spares, tools and training — but verify the new generation is form-fit compatible; family names persist across incompatible generations.
Note — single-vendor pull. Part 6 repeatedly observes that vendors bundle: Schneider MV (PIX/SM6) pulls Schneider relays and LV boards; a Rockwell DCS pulls CENTERLINE MCCs; a Siemens DCS pulls SIVACON and SINAMICS; ASCO pairs naturally with the Schneider ecosystem, PowerCommand ATS with Cummins sets. Bundling simplifies interfaces and spares but concentrates your supply-chain and obsolescence risk in one vendor — note it in the FMECA when a plant is 100 % single-brand.
  • Nameplate family string and generation recorded exactly (photo PH-xx referenced).
  • Catalogue section in Part 6 reviewed for the family's typical ratings at 50 °C.
  • Local Saudi service/spares route identified for the installed family.
  • SABER/SASO conformity status confirmed for any newly recommended equipment class.
  • Recommendation cites equipment class + two or three reference families — no part numbers.
Key takeaway: eleven equipment classes, every family name used in Part 6 in one table. Identify the family from the nameplate, follow the link for typical parameters and selection logic, and remember: families and Saudi acceptance status change — certified manufacturer data governs, and no part numbers are ever cited in this manual.

J — Concepts Companion Index

Alongside this manual there is a separate, self-contained companion file, concepts-rev3.html, built with the concept-explainer method: one chapter per core concept, each developed from first principles — the problem the concept solves, the physical intuition, the governing mathematics, and a conceptual map tying it to its neighbours. The companion is deliberately plant-agnostic: it teaches the physics and the engineering logic, while this manual applies them to the specific 13.8 kV / 400 V / 60 Hz plant. This appendix lists the fourteen concepts, what each chapter covers in one line, and which section of this manual applies it — so you can move between "why is it so" (companion) and "what do I do about it here" (manual) without hunting.

What the companion is (and is not)

  • It is a tutorial: generous explanations, analogies before algebra, worked micro-examples on round numbers, and a concept map per chapter.
  • It is self-contained and offline, like this manual — no external links, no scripts.
  • It is not a substitute for the calculations in Part 2 of this manual: the companion's numbers are pedagogical; the manual's worked examples are on the binding Rev 3 plant basis (2 × 1600 kVA, Z = 6 %, Isc 38.5 kA, NER 19.9 Ω, and so on).
  • It is not hyperlinked from here — the manual is a single file and links below stay within it; open the companion file separately when you want the deep dive.

The fourteen concepts

# Concept chapter One-line summary Applied in this manual
1Per-unit systemWhy engineers divide everything by a base value — so transformers "disappear" and a 13.8 kV fault study and a 400 V fault study speak the same dimensionless language.Part 2 — per-unit
2Short-circuit currentsWhere fault current comes from, why it is tens of times load current, and how source and transformer impedance set the 38.5 kA / 77 kA numbers that size every breaker.Part 2 — short circuit
3TransformersHow two windings and a core trade voltage for current, what Dyn11, Z % and losses really mean, and why impedance is both a fuse and a voltage-drop tax.Part 2 — transformer
4Circuit breakersHow you stop a 38 kA arc in a box — interruption physics (vacuum at MV, air at LV), the meaning of Icu/Ics/Icw, and trip-unit anatomy.Part 2 — breakers
5Protection & coordinationRelays as the system's reflexes: pickup, time grading, IDMT curves and zones — tripping the smallest possible slice of plant, fast.Part 2 — protection
6Arc flashThe fireball, not the shock: incident energy, working distance and clearing time per IEEE 1584, and why faster tripping is the cheapest PPE.Part 2 — arc flash
7EarthingWhy current must have a deliberate way home: TN-S, the NER trade-off (limit damage vs keep faults detectable), touch and step voltage.Part 2 — earthing
8Power factor correctionReactive power as froth on the beer: kvar, the tariff incentive, capacitor banks, and why VFD harmonics force 7 % detuning.Part 2 — PFC
9Motors & startingThe induction motor as a transformer with a rotating secondary: slip, 1800 rpm at 60 Hz, the 6–7 × DOL inrush problem and the starter family tree up to VFDs.Part 2 — motors
10UPSClean, unbreakable power from a battery and an inverter: double conversion (VFI), autonomy sizing, battery ageing — and why the control system never notices the grid blink.Part 2 — UPS
11Automatic transferThe decision machinery that swaps a dead source for a live one: ATS topologies, open vs closed transition, WCR, and the failure mode that tops the FMECA.Part 2 — ATS
12Diesel generatorsA power station in a box: engine-alternator dynamics, AVR and governor, ISO 8528 ratings (ESP/PRP/COP), step loading and the start-reliability problem.Part 2 — genset
13Lightning protectionCatching a 100 kA, microsecond event: the rolling sphere, LPL classes, down-conductors and the SPD cascade that protects the electronics.Part 2 — lightning
14FMECA & reliabilityFailure as a quantity you can engineer: failure modes, S-O-D scoring and RPN, MTBF/MTTR, and series/parallel availability — the mathematics behind SPOF hunting.Part 3 — FMECA

Suggested reading paths

  • New to power engineering: read companion chapters 1–3 (per-unit, short circuit, transformers) before Part 2 of this manual; everything in the calculations rests on those three.
  • Preparing a site visit: chapters 4–7 (breakers, protection, arc flash, earthing) cover everything you will stand in front of in the switchrooms.
  • Writing the FMECA: chapters 10–12 and 14 (UPS, ATS, genset, reliability) map one-to-one onto the highest-RPN rows of a typical assessment.
  • Refreshing one topic: go straight to the chapter; each is self-contained by design.
Tip — pair the chapters with the appendices. The fastest revision loop for a field engineer is: companion chapter for the concept → Appendix C for the formula with the Rev 3 plant numbers → Appendix A for any unfamiliar term met along the way. Fifteen minutes per concept, fourteen concepts — a complete first-principles refresh of this manual's technical core in under four hours.
Note — keeping the two documents consistent. The companion teaches with illustrative numbers; this manual's appendices (especially Appendix C — Formula Card and the Glossary) carry the binding Rev 3 plant values. If a number in the companion differs from the Plant Data Card values used here (13.8 kV, 60 Hz, 2 × 1600 kVA, 66.9 A / 2309 A FLC, 38.5 kA, DG 1250 kVA, UPS 100 kVA / 30 min, NER 19.9 Ω, PFC ≈ 580 kvar), the manual governs for this plant — and detailed studies govern over both.
Key takeaway: fourteen first-principles chapters in concepts-rev3.html, each mapped above to the manual section that applies it on the Rev 3 plant. Concept first, application second: when a calculation in Part 2 feels like recipe-following, the companion chapter is the missing "why".
